cases.title: Brief blackout then a fading teenager after a fall
cases.chiefComplaint: He passed out for a minute after falling off his skateboard, woke up fine, but now he won't stay awake.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man is brought in by EMS after falling from his skateboard while riding downhill without a helmet. Witnesses say he struck the right side of his head against the curb and was briefly unconscious for about a minute. He then woke up, stood, and seemed his usual self, walking around and complaining only of a mild headache. Roughly 45 minutes later, during transport, he became progressively drowsy and confused and vomited twice. EMS noted transient right-sided pupillary enlargement and worsening responsiveness en route. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and uses no anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he is a college sophomore who drinks alcohol occasionally, denies recreational drug use, and does not smoke. He has had no prior head injuries or seizures. Family history is noncontributory. There is no neck pain reported and no other obvious injuries at the scene.
cases.exam: He opens his eyes to pain, makes incomprehensible sounds, and localizes to stimulation (GCS 9) and is declining. There is a boggy hematoma over the right temporal scalp. The right pupil is dilated and sluggishly reactive; the left is normal. He has a left-sided pronator drift with left-sided weakness (4-/5). Tone is mildly increased on the left. The neck is supple with no midline tenderness, and there are no other external injuries. There is no rhinorrhea or otorrhea. No spontaneous bleeding or bruising elsewhere. Reflexes are brisker on the left, and the left plantar response is extensor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He is now opening eyes only to pain with a GCS of 9 and falling. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Send for MRI brain to better characterize the injury
Perform a lumbar puncture to rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage
Give a dose of IV mannitol and observe for improvement before any imaging
Protect the airway with endotracheal intubation, then obtain an emergent non-contrast CT head
stages.explanation: A rapidly declining GCS (now 9 and dropping) mandates definitive airway control: intubation protects against aspiration and allows ventilation control, followed immediately by an emergent non-contrast CT head to identify a surgical lesion. MRI is too slow and impractical for an unstable, deteriorating patient. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with suspected mass effect and raised ICP because it risks herniation and does not address the problem. Mannitol can temporize impending herniation but is an adjunct, not a substitute for airway control and imaging, and observing without imaging would miss an expanding hematoma. Airway, breathing, and circulation come first, then rapid CT to define and treat the lesion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense extra-axial collection over the right temporal convexity, 18 mm thick, not crossing the coronal suture
Right temporal bone fracture overlying the collection
Midline shift 7 mm to the left with effacement of the right lateral ventricle
INR 1.0, platelets 240 ×10³/µL
Na 140 mEq/L, glucose 108 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the imaging and clinical course?
options: Diffuse axonal injury
Acute subdural hematoma
Acute epidural hematoma
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
stages.explanation: A biconvex (lentiform) hyperdense collection that does not cross suture lines is the hallmark of an epidural hematoma; the overlying temporal fracture and the classic lucid interval (transient recovery before deterioration) clinch it, with the middle meningeal artery as the typical arterial source. A subdural hematoma is crescent-shaped, crosses sutures, and follows the brain surface, usually from bridging veins. Diffuse axonal injury follows high-acceleration trauma and shows little on early CT (small gray-white junction lesions), not a focal biconvex clot. Subarachnoid hemorrhage tracks into sulci and basal cisterns rather than forming a lens-shaped extra-axial collection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the 18 mm clot, 7 mm midline shift, and GCS 9 with anisocoria, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Emergent craniotomy for surgical evacuation of the hematoma
Admit for serial neuro checks and repeat CT in 6 hours
IV tranexamic acid and blood pressure control alone
Endovascular embolization of the middle meningeal artery as the sole therapy
stages.explanation: A large epidural hematoma (>30 mL or thickness >15 mm or midline shift >5 mm) or any epidural with a declining exam and anisocoria requires emergent craniotomy for evacuation; the clinical decline signals herniation, and immediate decompression is life-saving. Observation with serial CT is appropriate only for small (<30 mL, <15 mm, <5 mm shift) epidurals in a neurologically intact, GCS 15 patient, which this is not. Tranexamic acid and blood pressure control do not evacuate a compressing clot. Middle meningeal artery embolization is used adjunctively for chronic subdural collections, not as primary treatment of an acute compressing epidural with herniation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His right pupil is now fixed and dilated. What process explains this finding?
options: Compression of CN VI at the petrous apex
Direct globe trauma
Horner syndrome from carotid injury
Uncal (transtentorial) herniation compressing the ipsilateral CN III
stages.explanation: An expanding right temporal mass pushes the uncus of the temporal lobe over the tentorial edge, compressing the ipsilateral oculomotor nerve (CN III). Because parasympathetic fibers run superficially on CN III, the earliest sign is a sluggish then fixed, dilated pupil on the same side as the lesion, often with contralateral hemiparesis. CN VI compression causes a lateral gaze palsy, not a blown pupil. Direct globe trauma would show external ocular injury and would not evolve with the herniation syndrome. Horner syndrome produces a small (miotic) pupil with ptosis and anhidrosis, the opposite of this dilated pupil. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate decompression.

cases.diagnosis: Acute epidural hematoma from middle meningeal artery laceration, with uncal herniation
pearls: Classic lucid interval: brief loss of consciousness, recovery, then rapid decline as the arterial epidural clot expands.
Epidural hematoma is biconvex and does NOT cross suture lines; subdural is crescentic and crosses sutures.
A temporal bone fracture lacerating the middle meningeal artery is the prototypical cause.
A fixed, dilated pupil ipsilateral to the lesion signals uncal herniation with CN III compression — operate emergently.

cases.title: Weeks of waxing-and-waning confusion in an older man
cases.chiefComplaint: His family says he's been more and more confused and unsteady over the past few weeks.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man is brought in by his daughter for several weeks of fluctuating confusion, increasing forgetfulness, and a gradual decline in his gait, with a near-fall at home this week. The symptoms wax and wane day to day, and she has also noticed mild left-hand clumsiness and intermittent dull headaches. His past medical history includes atrial fibrillation, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. His medications are warfarin 5 mg daily (INR goal 2-3), metoprolol, lisinopril, and metformin. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he lives with his daughter, is a former smoker, and drinks alcohol occasionally. On further questioning, the family recalls that he slipped and struck his head on the bathroom counter about five weeks ago but seemed fine afterward, so they forgot to mention it. He has had no fevers, seizures, or recent infections. Family history is noncontributory. There is no history of recent stroke or prior intracranial bleeding.
cases.exam: He is alert but inattentive and disoriented to the date, with fluctuating attention during the interview. There is a mild left pronator drift and subtle left-sided weakness (4+/5). His gait is slow and wide-based with a tendency to veer left. Pupils are equal and reactive, with no facial droop and intact extraocular movements. The neck is supple, and there is no papilledema appreciated on fundoscopy. There is no neck stiffness, no scalp laceration, and no focal sensory loss. Reflexes are symmetric, and plantar responses are flexor.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic test?
options: Non-contrast CT head
Lumbar puncture
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Electroencephalography (EEG)
stages.explanation: Non-contrast CT is fast, widely available, and first-line for suspected intracranial hemorrhage in an elderly anticoagulated patient with progressive focal signs and a remote head injury; it both detects a collection and characterizes its acuity by density. Lumbar puncture is dangerous with possible mass effect and would not reveal a subdural. Carotid duplex evaluates extracranial stenosis for ischemic stroke or TIA, not an extra-axial collection. EEG assesses seizures and would not be the first study when structural pathology is likely, though it could be considered later if seizures are suspected. The combination of fluctuating cognition, gait decline, and warfarin makes hemorrhage the priority.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: crescent-shaped extra-axial collection over the right cerebral convexity, 14 mm thick, mixed hypo- and hyperdense, crossing suture lines
Midline shift 4 mm to the left with mild sulcal effacement
INR 3.4
Platelets 190 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.8 g/dL
Glucose 142 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the history and CT findings?
options: Acute epidural hematoma
Chronic (acute-on-chronic) subdural hematoma
Bacterial meningitis
Acute ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: A crescentic collection that crosses suture lines and follows the convexity is a subdural hematoma; the mixed density indicates a chronic collection with some acute rebleeding (acute-on-chronic), fitting symptoms that evolved over weeks after a forgotten minor fall in an anticoagulated elder. Epidural hematomas are biconvex, do not cross sutures, and present acutely after arterial injury. Meningitis would cause fever, neck stiffness, and CSF changes, not a crescentic clot with mass effect. Ischemic stroke causes sudden deficits and shows hypodensity in a vascular territory, not an extra-axial crescentic collection. Bridging vein rupture, favored by brain atrophy and anticoagulation, is the typical mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is on warfarin with an INR of 3.4 and has a symptomatic collection with midline shift. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue warfarin and observe with repeat CT in 48 hours
Start a therapeutic heparin infusion to prevent stroke
Reverse anticoagulation with IV vitamin K and 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate, and consult neurosurgery for surgical drainage
Administer tissue plasminogen activator (tPA)
stages.explanation: An anticoagulated patient with intracranial hemorrhage and mass effect needs urgent reversal: IV vitamin K plus 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate rapidly corrects warfarin's effect (PCC is faster and more reliable than fresh frozen plasma). A symptomatic subdural with midline shift warrants neurosurgical evaluation, typically burr-hole drainage for a liquefied chronic collection. Continuing warfarin risks expansion of the bleed. Therapeutic heparin would worsen bleeding; stroke prophylaxis is held until the hemorrhage is stabilized and reconsidered later. tPA is for acute ischemic stroke and is absolutely contraindicated with active intracranial hemorrhage.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After burr-hole drainage he improves, but chronic subdurals can recur. Which adjunctive procedure increasingly reduces recurrence by targeting the membrane blood supply?
options: Carotid endarterectomy
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Bilateral decompressive craniectomy
Middle meningeal artery embolization
stages.explanation: Chronic subdural membranes are fed by fragile neovessels arising from the middle meningeal artery; endovascular MMA embolization devascularizes these membranes and lowers recurrence, used adjunctively or for poor surgical candidates. Carotid endarterectomy treats carotid stenosis for ischemic stroke prevention and is irrelevant here. Ventriculoperitoneal shunting treats hydrocephalus, not a subdural membrane. Decompressive craniectomy is a large operation for refractory raised ICP or massive acute hemorrhage, not a routine measure to prevent chronic subdural recurrence. Standard care remains surgical drainage, with MMA embolization as the emerging adjunct.

cases.diagnosis: Acute-on-chronic subdural hematoma in an anticoagulated patient
pearls: Suspect chronic subdural in elderly or anticoagulated patients with fluctuating confusion and gait decline — the trauma is often trivial or forgotten.
Subdural hematomas are crescentic and cross suture lines; CT density reflects age (hyperdense acute, hypodense chronic, mixed acute-on-chronic).
Reverse warfarin promptly with vitamin K plus 4-factor PCC; PCC works faster than fresh frozen plasma.
Middle meningeal artery embolization is an emerging adjunct to reduce chronic subdural recurrence.

cases.title: Headache and confusion after a fall on blood thinners
cases.chiefComplaint: She fell in the kitchen this morning, hit her head, and now has a bad headache and seems off.
cases.history: A 64-year-old woman presents after a ground-level fall at home, having slipped on a wet kitchen floor and struck the back and left side of her head. She had no loss of consciousness initially, but over the next few hours she developed a worsening headache, nausea, and progressive confusion, with her husband noting that she is 'not making sense.' Her past medical history includes atrial fibrillation, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a prior transient ischemic attack. Her medications are apixaban 5 mg twice daily, amlodipine, and atorvastatin, with her last apixaban dose taken about two hours before the fall. She has no known drug allergies. Socially, she is a retired teacher, a nonsmoker, and drinks alcohol rarely. She has had no seizure activity. Family history is notable for ischemic stroke in her mother. She remains anticoagulated and is now showing new neurologic symptoms, prompting urgent evaluation.
cases.exam: She is somnolent but arousable, with a GCS of 13 (E3 V4 M6) and mildly slurred speech. There is a left facial droop, a left pronator drift, and mild left-sided weakness (4/5). Pupils are equal and reactive, and extraocular movements are intact. There is a tender occipital scalp hematoma without a palpable step-off. The neck is supple with no midline tenderness, and there is no rhinorrhea or otorrhea. Sensation is grossly intact, reflexes are slightly brisker on the left, and the left plantar response is equivocal. No neck stiffness is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Reassure and observe at home with return precautions
Emergent non-contrast CT head
MRI brain with contrast
Start aspirin for a presumed ischemic stroke
stages.explanation: Any patient on an anticoagulant who sustains head trauma and develops new neurologic deficits needs an emergent non-contrast CT to exclude intracranial hemorrhage; the pretest probability of a clinically important bleed is high. Home observation is unsafe with anticoagulation and focal signs. MRI is slower, less available, and not first-line for screening acute hemorrhage. Starting aspirin assumes ischemia and would worsen any hemorrhage; the deficits here may be from a compressing clot, so antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated until bleeding is excluded. CT rapidly identifies and characterizes the lesion to guide reversal and surgery.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: crescent-shaped hyperdense extra-axial collection over the right convexity, 12 mm maximal thickness, crossing suture lines
Midline shift 7 mm to the left with early sulcal effacement
No skull fracture
Na 138 mEq/L, glucose 124 mg/dL, platelets 215 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Acute epidural hematoma
Subarachnoid hemorrhage
Acute subdural hematoma
Hemorrhagic contusion
stages.explanation: A hyperdense, crescent-shaped extra-axial collection that crosses suture lines and conforms to the convexity is an acute subdural hematoma, typically from torn bridging veins, common after falls in older anticoagulated patients. Epidural hematomas are biconvex and bounded by sutures. Subarachnoid hemorrhage fills sulci and basal cisterns rather than forming a crescentic convexity clot. Hemorrhagic contusions are intra-axial (within brain parenchyma), often at the frontal or temporal poles, not an extra-axial crescent. The acute hyperdense appearance fits bleeding within hours of the fall, and the 7 mm shift indicates clinically significant mass effect.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She takes apixaban, a factor Xa inhibitor. What is the preferred anticoagulation reversal strategy?
options: Andexanet alfa (or 4-factor PCC if andexanet is unavailable)
IV vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
Protamine sulfate
Idarucizumab
stages.explanation: Apixaban is a direct factor Xa inhibitor; its specific reversal agent is andexanet alfa, with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate as the widely used alternative when andexanet is unavailable or cost-prohibitive. Vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma reverse warfarin (a vitamin K antagonist), not direct oral anticoagulants. Protamine reverses heparin, not oral Xa inhibitors. Idarucizumab specifically reverses dabigatran, a direct thrombin (IIa) inhibitor, not a factor Xa inhibitor. Rapid reversal limits hematoma expansion and is a priority before or alongside surgical planning.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Reversal agent administered
Repeat neuro exam: GCS now 12, left arm drift more pronounced
Repeat non-contrast CT (1 hour): subdural now 13 mm with midline shift 8 mm
Apixaban anti-Xa level elevated at presentation

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Based on clot thickness, midline shift, and clinical decline, what is the appropriate next step?
options: Continue medical management with serial exams only
Surgical evacuation (craniotomy) given thickness >10 mm and midline shift >5 mm with neurologic worsening
Lumbar drain placement
Resume therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent stroke
stages.explanation: Standard surgical indications for acute subdural hematoma are clot thickness >10 mm or midline shift >5 mm regardless of GCS, plus any subdural with a declining exam or signs of mass effect — all present here, so craniotomy for evacuation is indicated. Medical management with observation suits small (<10 mm), minimal-shift (<5 mm) subdurals in a neurologically stable patient. A lumbar drain is dangerous with a supratentorial mass and risks herniation. Resuming anticoagulation acutely would worsen the hemorrhage; it is deferred and reconsidered later after multidisciplinary risk assessment.

cases.diagnosis: Acute subdural hematoma in a patient anticoagulated with apixaban
pearls: Acute subdural hematoma is hyperdense and crescentic, crosses sutures, and classically arises from torn bridging veins after falls.
Reversal is drug-specific: andexanet alfa (or 4-factor PCC) for factor Xa inhibitors, idarucizumab for dabigatran, vitamin K/PCC for warfarin.
Operative thresholds for acute subdural: clot thickness >10 mm or midline shift >5 mm, or any clot with neurologic decline.
Ground-level falls in anticoagulated older adults carry a high risk of significant intracranial hemorrhage — always image.

cases.title: Dazed and headachy after a soccer collision
cases.chiefComplaint: She got hit going for a header yesterday and has had a headache and trouble focusing since.
cases.history: A 16-year-old girl presents to clinic with her mother one day after a soccer match. While going up for a header she collided head-to-head with another player. She did not lose consciousness but was briefly 'dazed and confused' for about a minute, asking the same questions repeatedly on the sideline. Since then she reports a dull, generalized headache, mild nausea without vomiting, sensitivity to light, difficulty concentrating in class, and a 'foggy' feeling, with slightly disrupted sleep. She denies worsening headache, repeated vomiting, seizures, focal weakness or numbness, and neck pain. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications other than occasional ibuprofen. She has no known drug allergies and no prior concussions. Socially, she is a high school sophomore and competitive soccer player who denies alcohol and drug use. Family history is noncontributory. She is eager to return to play for an important tournament this weekend.
cases.exam: She is alert, fully oriented, and speaking normally, in no acute distress. Cranial nerves are intact, pupils are equal and reactive, and there is no nystagmus. Strength, sensation, and deep tendon reflexes are normal throughout. Routine gait is normal, but tandem gait shows mild imbalance. There is no scalp laceration, skull tenderness, or palpable step-off, and no signs of basilar skull fracture (no Battle sign, raccoon eyes, hemotympanum, or CSF leak). The neck is supple with full range of motion and no midline cervical tenderness. Cognitive and exertional tasks provoke her symptoms. No focal neurologic deficit is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Does she require a non-contrast CT head, and why?
options: Yes — every concussion requires CT to exclude bleeding
No — she has no high-risk features (normal GCS, no focal deficit, no skull-fracture signs, not a dangerous mechanism); CT is not indicated
Yes — MRI is the preferred first-line study for concussion
No — but she needs a lumbar puncture instead
stages.explanation: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis, and neuroimaging is reserved for patients at risk of structural injury. Decision rules (PECARN for children, the Canadian CT Head Rule for adults) reserve CT for features such as GCS <15 at two hours, suspected skull fracture, repeated vomiting, prolonged loss of consciousness, dangerous mechanism, or worsening or focal findings. She has a normal GCS, no focal deficit, no skull-fracture signs, and a low-risk mechanism, so CT is not indicated and avoids unnecessary radiation. Routine CT for every concussion overuses radiation. MRI is not first-line for acute concussion, and lumbar puncture has no role in mild TBI evaluation.

stages.label: OFFICE ASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Sport Concussion Assessment Tool (SCAT5): elevated symptom score — headache, pressure in head, difficulty concentrating, and feeling 'in a fog'
Cognitive screen: mildly impaired delayed recall
Modified Balance Error Scoring System: increased errors on tandem stance
Neurologic exam: no focal deficits; observation over 1 hour shows no deterioration
No CT obtained (no high-risk imaging criteria met)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Concussion (mild traumatic brain injury)
Acute subdural hematoma
Post-traumatic seizure disorder
Cervical spine fracture
stages.explanation: Transient confusion after a head impact with headache, fogginess, photophobia, and difficulty concentrating, in the setting of a normal neurologic exam, defines concussion — a functional disturbance of brain function without structural injury on standard imaging. Acute subdural hematoma would typically produce progressive deficits, declining mental status, or focal findings, none of which are present. There is no witnessed or reported seizure activity, so a seizure disorder is unsupported. The neck is nontender with full range of motion and no midline tenderness, making cervical fracture unlikely; her symptoms localize to brain function rather than the spine.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate initial management in the first 24-48 hours?
options: Strict bed rest in a dark room for 2 weeks with no activity
Immediate return to full contact play since a CT is not needed
A brief period (24-48 h) of relative physical and cognitive rest, then gradual reintroduction of light activity as symptoms allow
Start prophylactic antiepileptic medication
stages.explanation: Current concussion guidance favors a short period (24-48 hours) of relative physical and cognitive rest followed by gradual, symptom-limited return to light activity; prolonged strict rest actually delays recovery. Returning to full contact immediately is unsafe and risks worsening injury or, rarely, second-impact syndrome. Prophylactic antiepileptics are not indicated for uncomplicated concussion without seizures. Symptomatic care (acetaminophen, sleep hygiene, hydration), school accommodations, and monitoring for red flags — worsening headache, repeated vomiting, increasing confusion, or focal deficits — complete early management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She wants to play in the tournament this weekend. What is the correct return-to-play counseling?
options: She may play once she is symptom-free for one hour
She can play now because she never lost consciousness
She should never play soccer again after any concussion
She may return to contact play only after completing a stepwise, symptom-free graded protocol and being cleared — never while still symptomatic
stages.explanation: Return to sport follows a graduated, stepwise protocol: light aerobic exercise, then sport-specific exercise, non-contact drills, full-contact practice, and finally competition, advancing only while she remains symptom-free, typically over at least several days, with medical clearance before contact. Playing while symptomatic — or within a day or two of injury for the tournament — risks recurrent injury and rare second-impact syndrome. Loss of consciousness is not required to diagnose concussion, so its absence does not permit immediate play. A single uncomplicated concussion is not a contraindication to ever playing again, so permanent disqualification is unwarranted.

cases.diagnosis: Concussion (sport-related mild traumatic brain injury)
pearls: Concussion is a clinical diagnosis; CT is normal and reserved for high-risk features per PECARN (children) or the Canadian CT Head Rule (adults).
Loss of consciousness is NOT required to diagnose concussion.
Favor brief relative rest (24-48 h) then gradual symptom-limited activity; prolonged strict rest delays recovery.
Return to contact only after a stepwise, symptom-free graded protocol and clearance — never while symptomatic, to avoid second-impact syndrome.

cases.title: Shooting leg pain down to the foot after lifting
cases.chiefComplaint: I have a sharp pain shooting from my lower back down the back of my leg into my foot.
cases.history: A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with three weeks of low back pain radiating down the posterior right leg to the lateral foot and small toe. The pain began suddenly after lifting a heavy box and is sharp and electric in quality, worse with sitting, coughing, sneezing, and bending forward, and partially relieved by lying down. He reports tingling along the lateral right foot. He denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle numbness, bilateral leg symptoms, fever, or unintentional weight loss. His past medical history includes obesity and mild gastroesophageal reflux. His medications are omeprazole and ibuprofen as needed. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he smokes one pack per day, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does frequent heavy lifting at work. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior back surgery and no night pain or constitutional symptoms suggesting infection or malignancy.
cases.exam: He has an antalgic gait and favors the right leg. The straight-leg raise is positive on the right at about 40 degrees, reproducing radicular pain down the leg; the crossed straight-leg raise is negative. Sensation is decreased over the lateral right foot and small toe. Plantar flexion is mildly weak on the right, with difficulty toe-walking on that side; dorsiflexion and great-toe extension are normal. The right ankle (Achilles) reflex is absent, while the knee reflex is normal and symmetric. There is no saddle anesthesia, rectal tone is normal, and there are no upper motor neuron signs. Paraspinal muscles are tender with reduced lumbar range of motion.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: His absent ankle reflex, weak plantar flexion, and lateral foot numbness localize to which nerve root?
options: L3
L4
L5
S1
stages.explanation: The S1 root mediates the ankle (Achilles) reflex, plantar flexion (gastrocnemius and soleus), and sensation over the lateral foot and small toe — exactly his deficits — and is the root typically compressed by a posterolateral L5-S1 disc herniation. L3 and L4 lesions affect the knee jerk, quadriceps, and medial leg. An L5 lesion causes weak great-toe extension and foot dorsiflexion with dorsal foot numbness but does not abolish the ankle reflex. The preserved knee jerk together with the specific reflex and sensory pattern confirms S1.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no red flags and intact bowel and bladder function. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate MRI and referral for surgery
Conservative management: activity modification, NSAIDs, and physical therapy, with reassurance that most improve in 6-12 weeks
Strict bed rest for 3 weeks
Urgent CT myelogram and epidural abscess workup
stages.explanation: Most lumbar disc herniations with radiculopathy and no red flags improve with conservative care — relative activity modification, NSAIDs, and physical therapy — over 6-12 weeks, so first-line treatment is nonoperative. Immediate MRI and surgery are unnecessary without red flags or a progressive deficit and do not change early management. Prolonged strict bed rest worsens outcomes; staying active as tolerated is preferred. There are no infectious features (no fever, IV drug use, or immunosuppression), so an abscess workup and CT myelogram are not warranted. Early imaging is reserved for red flags, progressive deficit, or failure to improve.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Symptoms persist with ongoing radicular pain after 7 weeks of physical therapy and NSAIDs
MRI lumbar spine: posterolateral disc herniation at L5-S1 compressing the traversing right S1 nerve root
No evidence of infection, tumor, or fracture
Neurologic exam: stable right plantar flexion weakness (4/5), absent right ankle reflex

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: MRI confirms an L5-S1 herniation compressing the S1 root concordant with his exam, and he has failed 7 weeks of conservative therapy. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same conservative regimen indefinitely
Begin long-term opioid therapy
Offer a lumbar microdiscectomy (or consider an epidural steroid injection) for persistent concordant radiculopathy
Recommend lumbar fusion as the first surgical option
stages.explanation: When concordant radiculopathy persists despite six or more weeks of appropriate conservative care and imaging matches the exam, options include a transforaminal epidural steroid injection or surgical microdiscectomy, which speeds relief of leg pain. Continuing the identical regimen that has already failed is unlikely to help and prolongs disability. Chronic opioids are not indicated for radicular pain and carry dependence risk without addressing the cause. Fusion is not first-line for a simple herniation without instability or deformity; microdiscectomy (decompression alone) is the standard surgical procedure here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding, if it developed, would change this from elective to urgent surgical decompression?
options: Pain that is worse when sitting
New saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, or rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness
A positive straight-leg raise
Mild persistent tingling in the foot
stages.explanation: Red-flag features — saddle anesthesia, new urinary retention or incontinence, or rapidly progressive or bilateral motor deficits — suggest cauda equina syndrome or a severe progressive deficit and demand emergent MRI and urgent decompression. Pain worsened by sitting is typical of discogenic radiculopathy and is not an emergency. A positive straight-leg raise simply supports nerve-root tension and is expected with his herniation, not an indication for urgent surgery. Mild persistent paresthesia is common and by itself does not warrant emergency intervention. Monitoring for these red flags is essential during conservative care.

cases.diagnosis: L5-S1 lumbar disc herniation with right S1 radiculopathy
pearls: L5-S1 disc herniation compresses the S1 root: lost ankle reflex, weak plantar flexion, and lateral foot numbness.
An L5 radiculopathy instead causes weak great-toe extension and dorsiflexion with a preserved ankle reflex.
Most lumbar radiculopathies resolve with 6-12 weeks of conservative care; image early only for red flags or progressive deficit.
Offer microdiscectomy for persistent concordant radiculopathy after failed conservative therapy; reserve fusion for instability or deformity.

cases.title: Numb between the legs and can't pass urine
cases.chiefComplaint: My lower back pain spread to both legs, I'm numb around my bottom, and I can't pee.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman presents with two days of severe low back pain that now radiates down BOTH legs. Over the last 12 hours she developed numbness in the perineal and saddle region and noticed she has not urinated since the morning despite feeling that her bladder is full; she did not sense the urge to void. She describes earlier incomplete emptying and now is unable to urinate at all. She also reports reduced sensation when wiping and new constipation. She has a history of chronic low back pain that worsened acutely after moving furniture two days ago. She denies fever, weight loss, IV drug use, or immunosuppression. Her past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Her medications are a combined oral contraceptive and ibuprofen. She has no known drug allergies. Socially, she is a nonsmoker who drinks alcohol occasionally and works at a desk job. Family history is noncontributory. She is anxious and very uncomfortable.
cases.exam: She has bilateral lower-extremity radicular pain with a positive straight-leg raise on both sides. Pinprick sensation is decreased in the saddle (perianal and perineal) distribution. On rectal exam, anal sphincter tone is reduced and the anal wink (bulbocavernosus) reflex is absent. Both ankle reflexes are diminished, and there is mild bilateral foot weakness. The bladder is palpably distended. There is no fever, no spinal step-off, and no upper motor neuron signs (no clonus, no Babinski). Lower-extremity strength is otherwise grossly preserved, and she is tender across the lower lumbar spine.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Prescribe NSAIDs and arrange outpatient physical therapy
Check a post-void residual bladder scan and obtain an emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine
Obtain plain lumbar radiographs and discharge with return precautions
Order a CT head
stages.explanation: Saddle anesthesia, painless urinary retention, bilateral sciatica, and reduced sphincter tone are alarm features for cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency. The immediate steps are to quantify bladder dysfunction with a post-void residual and to obtain an emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine, the test of choice to identify a compressive lesion. Outpatient NSAIDs and physical therapy or discharge would dangerously delay diagnosis. Plain radiographs cannot show neural compression and would miss the diagnosis. CT head is irrelevant because the lesion is in the lumbosacral spine, not the brain. Time to decompression affects neurologic recovery.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Bedside bladder scan: post-void residual 650 mL
MRI lumbosacral spine: large central disc herniation at L4-L5 with severe canal stenosis compressing multiple cauda equina nerve roots
No epidural abscess, mass, or hematoma
Perianal pinprick sensation reduced; anal tone decreased on exam

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Cauda equina syndrome
Conus medullaris infarction
Acute transverse myelitis
Guillain-Barré syndrome
stages.explanation: Compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus by a large central disc herniation, producing saddle anesthesia, painless urinary retention with a high residual, reduced sphincter tone, and bilateral radiculopathy, is cauda equina syndrome — a lower motor neuron, often asymmetric process. Conus medullaris lesions occur higher (T12-L1) and tend to present with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs and early symmetric bladder involvement. Transverse myelitis is a cord (upper motor neuron) lesion with a sensory level, hyperreflexia, and Babinski, not flaccid saddle findings. Guillain-Barré is an ascending areflexic weakness without a sensory level, saddle anesthesia, or a compressive MRI lesion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management?
options: High-dose IV corticosteroids and observation
Intravenous antibiotics
Emergent surgical decompression (e.g., laminectomy and discectomy)
Outpatient epidural steroid injection in 2 weeks
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome from a compressive disc requires emergent surgical decompression to relieve pressure on the nerve roots; earlier surgery, ideally as soon as feasible, improves the chance of recovering bladder, bowel, and motor function. Steroids do not relieve mechanical compression and are not definitive. Antibiotics treat infection such as an epidural abscess, which imaging excluded here. An elective outpatient injection would dangerously delay decompression and risk permanent deficits. This is a true neurosurgical emergency requiring prompt operative referral.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement about timing and prognosis is correct?
options: Surgery can safely wait several weeks since deficits are already established
Once urinary retention occurs, surgery offers no benefit
The procedure should be delayed until sensation returns spontaneously
Earlier decompression is associated with better recovery of bladder, bowel, and motor function, so surgery should not be delayed
stages.explanation: Outcomes in cauda equina syndrome correlate with the degree of deficit at presentation and the time to decompression — earlier surgery gives the best chance of recovering continence and strength, so it should proceed urgently. Waiting weeks risks permanent bladder and bowel dysfunction and weakness. Even with established retention, decompression can still improve or stabilize function and remains warranted. Waiting for spontaneous recovery is inappropriate because the compression is mechanical and progressive. Prompt recognition and operative referral are the priorities.

cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from a large central L4-L5 disc herniation
pearls: Red flags for cauda equina: saddle anesthesia, painless urinary retention (high post-void residual), reduced anal tone, and bilateral sciatica.
Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the diagnostic test of choice.
Treatment is emergent surgical decompression; earlier surgery improves bladder, bowel, and motor recovery.
Painless urinary retention with overflow is often the most reliable objective sign — always check a post-void residual.

cases.title: Leg cramping when walking, better leaning on the cart
cases.chiefComplaint: My legs cramp and go heavy when I walk, but I'm fine if I lean on the cart or sit.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with one year of gradually worsening bilateral leg pain, heaviness, and cramping that comes on with walking and standing upright. The symptoms are relieved by sitting, bending forward, or leaning on a shopping cart, and he can walk much farther when pushing a cart or walking uphill, but worse when walking downhill or standing erect. He also notes numbness and tingling in both thighs and calves with activity. He denies foot rest pain, skin color changes, and non-healing ulcers. He has no bowel or bladder dysfunction and no saddle numbness. His past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and osteoarthritis. His medications are amlodipine and atorvastatin. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he is a former smoker who quit 10 years ago and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no recent trauma, and his symptoms are activity- and posture-dependent.
cases.exam: At rest his neurologic exam is largely normal, with preserved strength, symmetric reflexes, and intact sensation. His gait is normal initially, but prolonged standing and walking reproduce his bilateral leg symptoms. Lumbar extension reproduces the symptoms, while lumbar flexion relieves them. Pedal pulses are 2+ and symmetric, the feet are warm and well-perfused with normal capillary refill, and there are no femoral or popliteal bruits and no trophic skin changes. There is no saddle anesthesia, sphincter tone is normal, and there are no upper motor neuron signs. The straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes his neurogenic claudication from vascular claudication?
options: Symptoms relieved simply by standing still
Symptoms relieved by spinal flexion (sitting, leaning forward) and provoked by standing or walking upright, with normal pedal pulses
Pain on elevation of the legs that improves with dependency
Absent pedal pulses with foot pallor on elevation
stages.explanation: Neurogenic claudication improves with lumbar flexion (sitting, leaning on a cart), which widens the spinal canal, and worsens with extension and standing; pulses are normal because the problem is neural compression, not arterial insufficiency. Relief by merely standing still is typical of vascular claudication, where stopping the muscular work resolves the pain. Pain relieved by leg dependency and worsened by elevation describes critical limb ischemia. Absent pulses and elevation pallor indicate peripheral arterial disease. His preserved pulses, warm feet, and posture-dependent symptoms point to neurogenic claudication from central canal stenosis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the best test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Ankle-brachial index
Plain lumbar radiographs only
Electromyography of both legs
MRI of the lumbar spine
stages.explanation: MRI of the lumbar spine is the test of choice to confirm central canal and lateral recess stenosis, demonstrating degenerative narrowing, ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, and facet and disc changes compressing the cauda equina. The ankle-brachial index screens for peripheral arterial disease and would be normal here, though it is reasonable if vascular disease were suspected. Plain radiographs show alignment and degenerative changes but cannot adequately assess neural compression. EMG can support radiculopathy but is not the primary confirmatory study for canal stenosis. MRI both confirms stenosis and guides treatment planning.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI lumbar spine: multilevel degenerative changes with moderate-to-severe central canal stenosis maximal at L3-L4 and L4-L5
Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and facet arthropathy contributing to the narrowing
No disc herniation with focal nerve-root compression, no tumor, infection, or fracture
Ankle-brachial index: 1.02 on the right, 1.00 on the left (normal)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If you still suspected a vascular cause, which finding would point toward vascular rather than neurogenic claudication?
options: Relief only after stopping activity regardless of posture, with diminished pulses and an abnormal ankle-brachial index
Symptom relief by leaning forward over a cart
Greater walking tolerance uphill than downhill
Provocation of symptoms by lumbar extension
stages.explanation: Vascular claudication resolves with rest in any posture (the muscle simply stops working) and is accompanied by reduced pulses and a low ankle-brachial index, with pain in a fixed muscle group proportional to exertion. Relief by leaning forward is the hallmark of neurogenic claudication, since flexion enlarges the canal. Walking uphill (a flexed posture) is better tolerated than downhill (extension) in neurogenic claudication — the reverse of vascular disease, where uphill is worse. Symptom provocation by lumbar extension is again neurogenic. Only the first option describes the posture-independent, perfusion-limited pattern of vascular claudication.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has moderate symptoms without red flags. What is the appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate decompressive laminectomy
Long-term opioid therapy
Conservative care: flexion-based physical therapy, activity modification, NSAIDs, and consideration of epidural steroid injections, reserving surgery for refractory or disabling cases
Strict bed rest
stages.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis is managed conservatively first — physical therapy emphasizing flexion-based exercises and core strengthening, activity modification, NSAIDs, and sometimes epidural steroid injections; many patients stabilize without surgery. Decompressive laminectomy is effective and offered when symptoms are refractory, progressive, or disabling despite conservative care, not as the immediate first step. Long-term opioids are not recommended and carry dependence risk. Strict bed rest causes deconditioning and worsens function. Surgery is reserved for those who fail nonoperative treatment or develop significant neurologic decline.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication
pearls: Neurogenic claudication improves with flexion (sitting, leaning on a cart) and worsens with extension or standing; the 'shopping cart sign' is classic.
Vascular claudication resolves with rest in any posture and features diminished pulses and a low ankle-brachial index.
Walking uphill (flexed) is better tolerated than downhill in neurogenic claudication — the opposite of vascular disease.
MRI confirms canal stenosis; treat conservatively first and reserve decompressive surgery for refractory or disabling symptoms.

cases.title: Clumsy hands and an unsteady walk
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep dropping things, buttoning my shirt has gotten hard, and I feel unsteady walking.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents with 8 to 10 months of progressive hand clumsiness — trouble buttoning shirts, handling coins, and frequently dropping objects — along with a gradually more unsteady, broad-based gait and several near-falls. He reports neck stiffness and intermittent electric-shock sensations running down his spine when he flexes his neck. He also notes numbness and tingling in both hands without a clear single dermatome. He initially denied bladder symptoms but recently has had mild urinary urgency. His past medical history includes hypertension, osteoarthritis, and known cervical spondylosis. His medications are lisinopril and NSAIDs as needed. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he is a retired machinist and former smoker who drinks alcohol occasionally. He reports no trauma. Family history is noncontributory. His symptoms have been slowly progressive and are now interfering with daily activities and his ability to work with his hands.
cases.exam: He has upper motor neuron signs below the neck: hyperreflexia at the knees and ankles, a positive Hoffmann sign bilaterally, an extensor plantar (Babinski) response, and sustained ankle clonus. His gait is spastic and broad-based, with a positive Romberg sign and impaired tandem gait. Fine motor dexterity and grip strength are reduced, with mild intrinsic hand weakness and early atrophy. Vibration and proprioception are diminished in the feet. The Lhermitte sign is present on neck flexion, and cervical range of motion is reduced. There is no saddle anesthesia, and cranial nerves are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His positive Hoffmann sign, hyperreflexia, Babinski, and spastic gait indicate what type of lesion?
options: A peripheral neuropathy
An upper motor neuron process involving the spinal cord (myelopathy)
A single lumbar nerve root compression
A neuromuscular junction disorder
stages.explanation: Hyperreflexia, a positive Hoffmann sign, extensor plantar responses, clonus, and spastic gait are upper motor neuron findings that localize to the spinal cord above the lumbosacral enlargement — that is, myelopathy, here from cervical cord compression. A peripheral neuropathy produces lower motor neuron signs: hyporeflexia, distal sensory loss, and no Babinski. A single lumbar root lesion causes focal lower motor neuron radicular findings such as an absent ankle jerk, not diffuse upper motor neuron signs. A neuromuscular junction disorder (for example, myasthenia gravis) causes fatigable weakness with normal reflexes and no long-tract signs.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: multilevel degenerative spondylosis with central canal stenosis maximal at C5-C6 and C6-C7
Spinal cord compression with increased T2 signal within the cord at the level of compression
No fracture, mass, or epidural abscess
Reflexes: 3+ at the knees, sustained ankle clonus, Hoffmann positive bilaterally

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
Carpal tunnel syndrome
Lumbar spinal stenosis
Cervical spondylotic myelopathy
stages.explanation: Progressive hand clumsiness, gait imbalance, upper motor neuron signs, the Lhermitte phenomenon, and MRI showing cervical cord compression with intramedullary T2 signal define cervical spondylotic myelopathy — the most common cause of spinal cord dysfunction in older adults. ALS produces mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs without sensory loss and shows no compressive cord lesion, so the sensory findings and imaging argue against it. Carpal tunnel causes median-distribution sensory symptoms and thenar wasting without long-tract signs or gait change. Lumbar stenosis affects the legs with neurogenic claudication and cannot explain hand findings or upper motor neuron signs above the lumbar cord.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has moderate, progressive myelopathy with cord signal change. What is the recommended management?
options: Continue NSAIDs and reassess in one year
Cervical collar immobilization indefinitely
Surgical decompression (e.g., anterior cervical discectomy and fusion, or laminoplasty)
Lumbar epidural steroid injections
stages.explanation: Progressive cervical spondylotic myelopathy with functional decline and cord signal change is best treated with surgical decompression — anterior cervical discectomy and fusion, corpectomy, or posterior laminoplasty or laminectomy with fusion — to halt progression and prevent further deterioration. Watchful waiting for a year risks irreversible cord injury in a progressive case. A cervical collar may transiently limit motion but does not relieve compression or reverse the disease. Lumbar epidural steroid injections target the wrong region entirely and have no role in cervical cord compression. Surgery is the definitive treatment for moderate-to-severe or progressive myelopathy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why is surgery recommended now rather than waiting until he has severe deficits?
options: Surgery reliably reverses all long-standing deficits, so timing does not matter
Myelopathy tends to progress, and neurologic recovery is better when decompression occurs before deficits become severe or long-standing
Surgery is purely cosmetic in this condition
Delaying allows the cord to regenerate on its own
stages.explanation: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy generally follows a stepwise progressive course, and outcomes after decompression are better when surgery is done before deficits become severe or chronic; earlier intervention preserves function and halts decline. Surgery does not reliably reverse long-standing deficits, because established cord damage may be permanent, which is precisely why waiting is harmful. The procedure is functional, not cosmetic. The injured spinal cord does not meaningfully regenerate, so delay risks irreversible loss of hand function and gait.

cases.diagnosis: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy
pearls: Hand clumsiness plus gait imbalance with upper motor neuron signs (Hoffmann, hyperreflexia, Babinski, clonus) in an older adult suggests cervical myelopathy.
MRI showing cord compression with intramedullary T2 signal supports the diagnosis and prognosis.
Progressive or moderate-to-severe myelopathy is treated surgically; recovery is better when decompression precedes severe, long-standing deficits.
Lhermitte sign (an electric shock down the spine on neck flexion) is a classic clue to cervical cord pathology.

cases.title: Neck pain shooting into the arm and middle finger
cases.chiefComplaint: My neck hurts and there's a pain shooting down my arm into my middle finger with tingling.
cases.history: A 48-year-old office worker presents with three weeks of neck pain radiating down the right arm into the forearm and middle finger, accompanied by tingling. The pain is worse with neck extension and rotation toward the right and is relieved somewhat when she rests her hand on top of her head. The onset was gradual without trauma, and she notes occasional weakness when pushing doors open or extending her elbow. She denies gait changes, hand clumsiness, balance problems, and bowel or bladder symptoms, and she has no bilateral arm or leg symptoms. Her past medical history includes migraines. Her medications are ibuprofen as needed and a combined oral contraceptive. She has no known drug allergies. Socially, she is a nonsmoker who drinks alcohol occasionally and works long hours at a computer. Family history is noncontributory. She has had no fever, weight loss, or night pain, and no prior neck surgery.
cases.exam: The Spurling maneuver (neck extension and rotation toward the right with axial compression) reproduces her radicular arm pain. Elbow extension (triceps) and wrist flexion are mildly weak on the right. The right triceps reflex is diminished to absent, while the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes are normal and symmetric. Sensation is decreased over the middle finger. There are no upper motor neuron signs: the Hoffmann sign is negative, there is no hyperreflexia or clonus, the lower-extremity exam and gait are normal, and there is no Lhermitte sign or Babinski. There is no muscle atrophy.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Her triceps weakness, lost triceps reflex, and middle-finger numbness localize to which nerve root?
options: C5
C6
C7
C8
stages.explanation: The C7 root supplies the triceps (elbow extension) and its reflex, contributes to wrist flexion and finger extension, and carries sensation to the middle finger — matching all her findings. C5 mediates the deltoid and biceps and lateral arm sensation with the biceps reflex. C6 affects the biceps and brachioradialis and wrist extension, with sensation to the thumb and the brachioradialis reflex. C8 supplies the intrinsic hand muscles and sensation to the little finger, with no specific deep tendon reflex. The preserved biceps and brachioradialis reflexes with an absent triceps jerk pinpoint C7.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has no myelopathic signs and no red flags. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate MRI and surgical referral
Conservative management: activity modification, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and reassurance, since most cervical radiculopathies improve within weeks
Urgent cervical spine surgery
Long-term oral corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Most cervical radiculopathies resolve with conservative care — relative activity modification, NSAIDs, physical therapy, and reassurance — over several weeks, so nonoperative treatment is first-line in the absence of red flags or myelopathy. Immediate MRI is unnecessary early because findings often do not change initial management and incidental degenerative changes are common. Urgent surgery is reserved for severe or progressive deficits, myelopathy, or failure of conservative care. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are not recommended given limited benefit and significant side effects, though a brief course is sometimes used. Reassess and escalate only if she fails to improve.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Return visit at 7 weeks after physical therapy and NSAIDs
Persistent right arm pain and middle-finger paresthesia, only mildly improved
Exam: triceps strength still 4/5, right triceps reflex remains diminished, Spurling still positive
No new myelopathic signs: Hoffmann negative, normal gait, no hyperreflexia
No bowel or bladder symptoms; no fever or weight loss

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 6-8 weeks of appropriate conservative care she still has bothersome symptoms. What is the best next step?
options: Repeat the same therapy for another year with no further evaluation
Begin chronic opioid therapy
Proceed directly to cervical fusion without imaging
Obtain an MRI of the cervical spine to define the lesion and guide injection or surgical options
stages.explanation: When radiculopathy persists despite six or more weeks of conservative care, MRI of the cervical spine is appropriate to confirm the compressive lesion and correlate it with the exam, guiding an epidural steroid injection or surgical decompression. Simply repeating the same failed therapy indefinitely prolongs disability without new information. Chronic opioids are not recommended for radicular pain and risk dependence. Operating — particularly a fusion — without imaging is unsafe; surgery must be planned around confirmed, concordant pathology. Imaging now logically bridges failed conservative care and procedural options.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which new finding would prompt urgent imaging and change management?
options: Mild persistent tingling in the middle finger
Pain worsened by neck extension
Development of hand clumsiness, gait imbalance, hyperreflexia, or a positive Hoffmann sign
A positive Spurling maneuver
stages.explanation: Signs of myelopathy — hand clumsiness, gait imbalance, hyperreflexia, clonus, or a positive Hoffmann sign — indicate spinal cord compression and warrant urgent MRI and surgical evaluation, a different and more dangerous problem than isolated radiculopathy. Persistent finger tingling is an expected radicular symptom and not alarming by itself. Pain provoked by neck extension is typical of foraminal narrowing in radiculopathy. A positive Spurling maneuver supports radiculopathy and is already present; it is a diagnostic sign, not a red flag. Watching for myelopathic progression is essential during conservative management.

cases.diagnosis: C7 cervical radiculopathy
pearls: C7 radiculopathy: triceps weakness, a lost triceps reflex, and middle-finger numbness; the Spurling maneuver reproduces the arm pain.
Most cervical radiculopathies improve with conservative care in weeks; image only for red flags or symptoms persisting beyond about 6 weeks.
The shoulder-abduction (hand-on-head) relief sign supports a cervical radicular cause.
New upper motor neuron signs (Hoffmann, hyperreflexia, gait change) signal myelopathy and warrant urgent imaging and surgical evaluation.

cases.title: Sudden one-sided weakness and a crushing headache
cases.chiefComplaint: He suddenly couldn't move his right side and started slurring while watching TV.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man is brought in by EMS after the sudden onset, about 60 minutes ago, of right-sided weakness, slurred speech, and a severe headache while sitting at home watching television. His wife reports he became drowsy and vomited once. He has long-standing hypertension and admits he stopped taking his medications months ago and rarely checks his blood pressure. There was no trauma. His past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and tobacco use. He was prescribed amlodipine and lisinopril but has been nonadherent, and he takes metformin. He has no known drug allergies and does not use anticoagulants. Socially, he smokes one pack per day, drinks alcohol several times weekly, and denies recreational drug use. Family history includes hypertension and stroke. He was last known well 60 minutes ago, and his deficits have not improved since onset.
cases.exam: He is drowsy but arousable and notably dysarthric. There is a dense right hemiplegia involving the face, arm, and leg, with right-sided hemisensory loss. His eyes are deviated to the left (a gaze preference toward the side of the lesion and away from the hemiplegia), and there is a right homonymous hemianopia. The right plantar response is extensor. Pupils are equal and reactive, and the neck is supple with no stiffness. There is no external head trauma. His measured deficits are severe, with an elevated NIH Stroke Scale.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He is within the thrombolysis time window. What must be done before considering any treatment?
options: Give IV tissue plasminogen activator immediately based on the deficit
Obtain an emergent non-contrast CT head to distinguish ischemic from hemorrhagic stroke
Start aspirin and clopidogrel
Perform a lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Acute focal deficits may be ischemic or hemorrhagic, and these require opposite treatments, so an emergent non-contrast CT head is mandatory before any therapy; it rapidly detects hemorrhage. Giving tPA without imaging is dangerous and absolutely contraindicated if a bleed is present, which his very high blood pressure, headache, and vomiting raise concern for. Antiplatelet agents would worsen a hemorrhage and are not given before imaging. Lumbar puncture is not indicated and is hazardous with a possible intracranial mass or hemorrhage. CT both confirms the diagnosis and directs management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: hyperdense (acute) hemorrhage centered in the left putamen/basal ganglia, ~25 mL, with minimal surrounding edema
No significant midline shift; ventricles patent
INR 1.0, platelets 230 ×10³/µL
Glucose 168 mg/dL, Na 139 mEq/L
NIH Stroke Scale elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given his uncontrolled hypertension and the CT findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage of the basal ganglia (putaminal bleed)
Acute ischemic stroke
Ruptured saccular aneurysm causing subarachnoid hemorrhage
Brain abscess
stages.explanation: A deep hemorrhage centered in the putamen and basal ganglia in a patient with chronic, poorly controlled hypertension is the classic hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage, caused by rupture of small penetrating (lenticulostriate) arteries weakened by chronic hypertension (lipohyalinosis and Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms). Ischemic stroke shows no hyperdense blood acutely and becomes hypodense over time. Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage tracks into the basal cisterns and sulci with a thunderclap headache, not a focal deep parenchymal clot. A brain abscess is a ring-enhancing lesion with infectious features, not an acute hyperdense bleed. The deep location and hypertensive history are characteristic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of acute medical management for this hemorrhage?
options: Immediate IV thrombolysis
Therapeutic anticoagulation
Controlled blood pressure lowering (e.g., to a systolic around 130-140 mmHg), airway and neuro monitoring, and reversal of any coagulopathy
Lumbar drain placement
stages.explanation: Management of hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage centers on careful blood pressure reduction (guidelines support lowering the systolic toward roughly 130-140 mmHg in eligible patients while avoiding precipitous drops), close neurologic and airway monitoring, normalizing coagulation, and managing intracranial pressure. Thrombolysis is for ischemic stroke and is absolutely contraindicated in hemorrhage. Therapeutic anticoagulation would expand the bleed and is contraindicated. A lumbar drain risks herniation with a supratentorial mass and is not indicated. Blood pressure control limits hematoma expansion, the main driver of early deterioration.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why are most deep (basal ganglia) hypertensive hemorrhages managed medically rather than surgically?
options: Because surgery is technically impossible anywhere in the brain
Because deep hemorrhages never cause symptoms
Because medical therapy reliably dissolves the clot within hours

Because evacuating deep hemorrhages requires cutting through healthy brain and has not shown clear benefit for most patients, so surgery is reserved for selected cases (e.g., cerebellar hemorrhage or deterioration)
stages.explanation: Deep basal ganglia hemorrhages sit beneath functionally important brain, and surgically reaching them damages overlying healthy tissue; trials have not shown a consistent outcome benefit from routine evacuation of most supratentorial deep bleeds, so management is usually medical. Surgery is reserved for selected situations — notably cerebellar hemorrhage greater than 3 cm or with brainstem compression or hydrocephalus, or clinical deterioration from an accessible clot. Surgery is clearly possible in the brain, so the first option is false. Deep hemorrhages plainly cause major deficits, and medical therapy controls pressure and supports the patient but does not rapidly dissolve clot.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage of the left basal ganglia (putaminal hemorrhage)
pearls: Deep hemorrhage in the basal ganglia/putamen, thalamus, pons, or cerebellum with chronic uncontrolled hypertension points to hypertensive intracerebral hemorrhage (lenticulostriate artery rupture).
Always obtain a non-contrast CT before thrombolysis — tPA is contraindicated in hemorrhage.
Cornerstones of care: controlled BP lowering (systolic ~130-140 mmHg), reverse coagulopathy, and monitor for hematoma expansion and raised ICP.
Most deep supratentorial bleeds are managed medically; surgery is reserved for cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm, brainstem compression/hydrocephalus, or deterioration.

cases.title: Word-finding trouble and a weak right hand
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep knowing what I want to say but the words won't come out.
cases.history: A 45-year-old right-handed woman was admitted after three weeks of progressive difficulty producing speech and intermittent right-hand clumsiness. Her husband noticed she pauses mid-sentence, substitutes words, and grows frustrated, though she understands everything said to her. There was no headache initially, but over the past week she has had morning headaches and one witnessed focal seizure with right-arm twitching. No fever, weight loss, or prior cancer. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: a daily multivitamin; levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily was started in the ED. No known drug allergies. She works as an accountant, does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is notable for a mother with breast cancer at 62; no known brain tumors or neurofibromatosis. She is married with two children and is highly anxious about losing her ability to speak. An outpatient MRI obtained before admission showed a left frontal mass, and she was admitted for expedited neurosurgical workup and seizure control. She remains fully oriented and independent in daily activities.
cases.exam: Alert and fully oriented. Spontaneous speech is non-fluent and effortful with frequent word-finding pauses and phonemic paraphasias; comprehension and repetition are relatively preserved while naming is impaired, consistent with an expressive (Broca-type) aphasia. Cranial nerves are intact with no facial droop at rest. The right upper extremity shows subtle pronator drift and 4+/5 grip strength; right lower extremity strength is full. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are symmetric with an equivocal right plantar response. There is no neglect and no visual field cut on confrontation. Gait is normal. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her MRI report describes a left inferior frontal infiltrative mass. Before planning surgery, which study best defines the relationship of the lesion to language-critical cortex and subcortical tracts?
options: Non-contrast head CT
FDG-PET of the brain
Catheter cerebral angiography
Functional MRI with diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) tractography
stages.explanation: Functional MRI lateralizes and localizes language cortex, and DTI tractography maps the arcuate/superior longitudinal fasciculus and corticospinal tract relative to the tumor, directly informing the awake-craniotomy plan and counseling about deficit risk. Non-contrast CT lacks the soft-tissue resolution needed for tract mapping and only screens for hemorrhage or gross mass effect. FDG-PET helps grade gliomas or detect recurrence metabolically but does not map eloquent pathways. Catheter angiography assesses tumor vascularity and is occasionally used for preoperative embolization, but it provides no functional language information. Combining fMRI and DTI gives the anatomic-functional roadmap required when a tumor abuts Broca's area.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with and without contrast: 3.4 cm infiltrative mass in the left inferior frontal gyrus with heterogeneous, irregular rim enhancement and central necrosis; surrounding T2/FLAIR signal abuts the pars opercularis.
MR perfusion: elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) within the enhancing region.
fMRI: expressive-language activation immediately posterior to the lesion margin.
DTI: arcuate/superior longitudinal fasciculus displaced along the deep tumor border.
CBC and BMP: within normal limits.
Glucose: 96 mg/dL.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the imaging features, which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: High-grade glioma (glioblastoma)
Low-grade (IDH-mutant) astrocytoma
Cerebral abscess
Tumefactive demyelination
stages.explanation: Irregular rim enhancement with central necrosis and elevated rCBV in a middle-aged adult is classic for a high-grade glioma/glioblastoma, which also fits the subacute progressive deficit and new seizure. Low-grade gliomas are typically non-enhancing with low perfusion and present more indolently. A cerebral abscess shows a smooth, thin, regular enhancing wall with marked central diffusion restriction and usually systemic or local infection signs, which are absent here. Tumefactive demyelination characteristically forms an incomplete 'open ring' of enhancement facing gray matter, causes less mass effect than expected for size, and shows low rather than high perfusion. The combination of necrosis and high rCBV points firmly to high-grade glioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The tumor abuts Broca's area. What is the optimal surgical strategy to maximize tumor removal while protecting language function?
options: Stereotactic biopsy only, then chemoradiation, to avoid any language risk
Asleep gross-total resection guided by neuronavigation alone
Awake craniotomy with intraoperative cortical and subcortical electrical stimulation mapping
Stereotactic radiosurgery to the enhancing tumor
stages.explanation: Awake craniotomy with direct cortical and subcortical electrical stimulation mapping is the standard for tumors in eloquent (language) cortex: real-time testing identifies positive language sites so resection proceeds to functional boundaries, maximizing extent of resection while minimizing permanent aphasia. Biopsy-only forfeits the well-established survival benefit of maximal safe resection in glioma. Neuronavigation alone is undermined by intraoperative brain shift and cannot identify functional cortex or white-matter tracts. Stereotactic radiosurgery is inappropriate for a large, infiltrative high-grade glioma, provides no tissue for molecular diagnosis, and offers no immediate cytoreduction or mass-effect relief. Awake mapping uniquely balances oncologic aggressiveness against the patient's language.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: During mapping, stimulation at the posterior tumor margin reliably produces speech arrest (a positive language site), but residual enhancing tumor lies just deep to it. What is the best intraoperative decision?
options: Resect through the positive site to achieve gross-total resection
Convert to general anesthesia to remove the remainder
Stop resection at the functional boundary, leave the deep margin, and plan adjuvant therapy
Inject the site with local anesthetic and continue resecting
stages.explanation: A positive stimulation site marks cortex essential for language; resecting through it risks permanent aphasia that outweighs the marginal survival gain of a few extra millimeters of tumor. The correct move is to halt at functional boundaries and treat the residual disease with adjuvant chemoradiation. Resecting through the site, or anesthetizing it to continue, would directly injure language cortex and produce a fixed deficit. Converting to general anesthesia removes the very monitoring that protects function, so the surgeon would be operating blind near eloquent tissue. After subtotal resection, standard adjuvant therapy is the Stupp protocol, concurrent temozolomide with radiation followed by maintenance temozolomide.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype high-grade glioma) abutting Broca's area, managed with awake craniotomy and intraoperative language mapping.
pearls: Maximal safe resection improves survival in glioblastoma, but in eloquent cortex 'safe' is defined intraoperatively by stimulation mapping, not by preoperative imaging alone.
Awake craniotomy with cortical and subcortical mapping lets the surgeon resect to functional boundaries and reduces permanent language deficits.
Positive language sites (speech arrest, anomia) mark tissue that must be preserved even at the cost of residual tumor.
Standard adjuvant therapy after resection is the Stupp protocol: concurrent temozolomide and radiation followed by maintenance temozolomide.

cases.title: Worsening headaches a year after brain tumor treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: My headaches are back and the scan shows something growing again.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man returns to neuro-oncology clinic 13 months after completing concurrent chemoradiation (60 Gy plus temozolomide) for a left parietal IDH-mutant astrocytoma resected last year. He reports two weeks of worsening dull headaches, mild word-finding difficulty, and new right-hand clumsiness. A surveillance contrast MRI shows enlarging enhancement at the resection margin. He has been on maintenance temozolomide. He denies fever, night sweats, or recent seizures. PMH: hypertension, well controlled. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, temozolomide per cycle, and he recently finished a dexamethasone taper. No known allergies. He is a former smoker who quit 10 years ago, drinks alcohol rarely, and uses no illicit drugs. He lives with his wife and works part-time. Family history is noncontributory. He is anxious that the tumor has returned and asks whether he needs another operation. His performance status remains good (KPS 80). He has had no recent infections and no immunosuppression beyond chemotherapy. He has not had any new falls or trauma.
cases.exam: Alert and oriented, fluent speech with occasional word-finding pauses. Cranial nerves are intact and there is no papilledema. There is mild right pronator drift with 4+/5 right-hand grip; other muscle groups are full strength. Fine finger movements are subtly reduced on the right. Sensation is intact and reflexes are symmetric with downgoing plantars. Comprehension is intact and there is no conversational aphasia. Gait is steady. There is no meningismus and no fever. The left parietal craniotomy scar is well healed without erythema, fluctuance, or drainage. There is no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Enlarging enhancement at the prior treatment site one year after chemoradiation. What are the two leading possibilities you must distinguish?
options: Tumor recurrence versus radiation necrosis
Abscess versus metastasis
Stroke versus hemorrhage
Demyelination versus vasculitis
stages.explanation: New or enlarging enhancement appearing months to a year-plus after chemoradiation classically represents either true tumor progression/recurrence or treatment-related radiation necrosis. (The early, sub-3-month variant of treatment effect is termed pseudoprogression.) Both entities produce enhancement, surrounding edema, and focal deficits, and they are frequently indistinguishable on conventional MRI, which is exactly the clinical dilemma. Abscess or metastasis are far less likely without infection or a known second primary and would be unusual at the irradiated bed. Stroke and hemorrhage present acutely with distinct imaging signatures, not as slowly enlarging enhancement. Demyelination and vasculitis do not fit this focal, oncologic, treatment-bed picture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Conventional MRI cannot reliably separate these. Which approach best helps differentiate radiation necrosis from recurrent tumor?
options: Non-contrast CT of the head
MR perfusion (rCBV) with MR spectroscopy, or amino-acid/FDG PET
Repeat MRI in 6 months with no other testing
Lumbar puncture for cytology
stages.explanation: Advanced physiologic imaging is the key discriminator. Recurrent tumor shows elevated relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) on perfusion, an elevated choline-to-NAA ratio on spectroscopy, and increased uptake on amino-acid (FET/MET) or FDG PET, reflecting viable, metabolically active neoplasm. Radiation necrosis instead shows low perfusion and low metabolism. Non-contrast CT lacks both the spatial resolution and the physiologic data needed. Simply waiting six months risks allowing untreated tumor progression and delays appropriate therapy. CSF cytology has low yield for a focal parenchymal high-grade glioma and cannot localize or characterize the enhancing lesion, so it does not resolve the question.

stages.label: ADVANCED IMAGING
stages.content:
MR perfusion: LOW relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV) within the enhancing region.
MR spectroscopy: only mildly elevated choline; no dominant choline:NAA peak.
FET-PET: low amino-acid tracer uptake at the lesion.
MRI: enhancing region with surrounding vasogenic edema and central T1 hypointensity.
CBC and CMP: within normal limits.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
