stages.question: Perfusion and PET favor radiation necrosis, and the patient is symptomatic from edema. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate craniotomy for resection
Corticosteroids, adding bevacizumab if steroid-refractory or steroid-limited
Restart full-dose radiation to the area
Begin broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
stages.explanation: Symptomatic radiation necrosis is first treated with corticosteroids to reduce vasogenic edema and mass effect. For steroid-dependent, steroid-refractory, or progressive disease, bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) has strong evidence for both radiographic and clinical improvement by reducing vascular permeability and edema. Upfront craniotomy is reserved for significant mass effect, diagnostic uncertainty, or failure of medical therapy, and is not first-line when imaging favors necrosis and symptoms are edema-driven. Re-irradiation would worsen the necrotic process. Antibiotics treat infection, which the clinical course and imaging do not support. Starting steroids with bevacizumab escalation is the guideline-concordant pathway here.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Despite steroids and bevacizumab the lesion continues to enlarge and imaging becomes equivocal. What definitively settles whether this is necrosis or recurrent tumor?
options: Another round of perfusion MRI
Empiric second-line chemotherapy
Surgical biopsy or resection for histopathology
Whole-body PET/CT
stages.explanation: When advanced imaging is equivocal and the lesion progresses or threatens function, tissue diagnosis by stereotactic biopsy or resection is the gold standard. Histology distinguishes coagulative necrosis with vascular hyalinization and fibrinoid change from viable, mitotically active tumor, and resection can simultaneously relieve mass effect. Repeating perfusion adds little when prior advanced imaging was already indeterminate. Empiric second-line chemotherapy treats blindly and exposes the patient to toxicity that is pointless if the lesion is pure necrosis. Whole-body PET/CT looks for systemic malignancy and is irrelevant to characterizing this single intracranial lesion. Biopsy is the definitive arbiter.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral radiation necrosis at the prior glioma treatment site, mimicking tumor recurrence, settled by biopsy when imaging is indeterminate.
pearls: Late (>6 month) enlarging enhancement after chemoradiation is either radiation necrosis or true recurrence; pseudoprogression is the early (<12 week) treatment-effect variant.
Recurrent tumor shows high rCBV, high choline:NAA, and increased amino-acid PET uptake; radiation necrosis shows low perfusion and low metabolism.
Symptomatic radiation necrosis is treated with corticosteroids first and bevacizumab for steroid-refractory disease.
When advanced imaging stays equivocal, biopsy is the definitive arbiter between necrosis and recurrence.

cases.title: A first seizure in a healthy young man
cases.chiefComplaint: I had a seizure out of nowhere and they found a spot on my brain.
cases.history: A 29-year-old right-handed man presents to clinic after a single generalized tonic-clonic seizure two weeks ago, witnessed by his partner, with tongue-biting and brief postictal confusion. He had felt entirely well; in retrospect he recalls a few episodes of an odd rising sensation and déjà vu over recent months that he ignored. He denies headaches, weakness, speech trouble, or weight loss. He has never had seizures before. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily, started in the ED after a CT showed a possible abnormality, prompting an outpatient MRI. No known allergies. He is a software developer, a nonsmoker, drinks alcohol socially, and uses no illicit drugs. Family history is unremarkable for tumors or epilepsy. He is engaged to be married and is worried about what this means for his future and his ability to drive. He is fully functional and back at work. He asks whether the lesion can simply be watched rather than operated on, since he feels normal now and has no symptoms day to day.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, fully oriented young man in no distress. Speech is fluent with intact naming, repetition, and comprehension. Cranial nerves II-XII are intact, visual fields are full to confrontation, and there is no papilledema. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with no pronator drift. Sensation is intact to all modalities. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with downgoing plantars. Coordination and tandem gait are normal. There is no neglect or apraxia. The tongue shows a healing lateral bite mark. There are no neurocutaneous stigmata such as café-au-lait macules. Cardiovascular and respiratory exams are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He had a first unprovoked seizure with an abnormality on CT. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Brain MRI with and without contrast
Reassure with no further workup
EEG only
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: A first unprovoked seizure accompanied by a structural abnormality on CT mandates a brain MRI with and without contrast, the most sensitive study to characterize a parenchymal lesion, including its location, enhancement pattern, and infiltrative extent. Reassurance is inappropriate when imaging is already abnormal. EEG helps define the seizure focus and estimate recurrence risk, and is a useful adjunct, but it does not characterize the structural lesion driving the seizure. Lumbar puncture is not indicated without signs of CNS infection or subarachnoid hemorrhage, carries risk if there is mass effect, and would not delineate a brain mass. MRI is the necessary next step.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: 3 cm non-enhancing intra-axial lesion centered in the left insula, T2/FLAIR hyperintense, expanding the cortex with minimal mass effect.
No contrast enhancement.
No diffusion restriction.
MR perfusion: low relative cerebral blood volume.
CBC and CMP: normal.
EEG: occasional left temporal sharp waves.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A non-enhancing, T2/FLAIR-hyperintense insular lesion with low perfusion in a 29-year-old presenting with a seizure. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma
Diffuse low-grade glioma (likely IDH-mutant)
Cerebral abscess
Herpes encephalitis
stages.explanation: A non-enhancing, infiltrative, T2/FLAIR-hyperintense lesion with low perfusion in a young adult presenting with seizures is the classic picture of a diffuse low-grade glioma, which in this age group is usually IDH-mutant, a favorable molecular feature. Glioblastoma typically enhances avidly with central necrosis and high perfusion and occurs in older patients. A cerebral abscess shows rim enhancement with central diffusion restriction and an infectious clinical presentation. Herpes encephalitis causes medial temporal involvement with fever, encephalopathy, and often hemorrhage and restricted diffusion, not an indolent single seizure with a discrete non-enhancing mass. The imaging and demographics point to low-grade glioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He feels well and asks whether the lesion can just be watched. What is the recommended management for a young patient with a suspected diffuse low-grade glioma?
options: Watchful waiting with serial MRI until symptoms worsen
Whole-brain radiation now
Early maximal safe resection
Empiric chemotherapy without tissue diagnosis
stages.explanation: Current evidence favors early maximal safe resection for diffuse low-grade gliomas: greater extent of resection delays malignant transformation, improves seizure control, and prolongs overall survival, while providing tissue for molecular classification (IDH, 1p/19q). Watchful waiting is reserved for selected small, asymptomatic, or truly inoperable lesions and risks ongoing growth and transformation in a young, otherwise healthy patient. Whole-brain radiation is not primary therapy for a focal low-grade glioma and carries significant neurocognitive toxicity. Empiric chemotherapy without histologic and molecular confirmation is inappropriate and forgoes the survival benefit conferred by resection. Early safe resection is the recommended strategy.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Resection pathology returns a WHO grade 2 IDH-mutant astrocytoma. Which statement best guides prognosis and follow-up?
options: IDH-mutant status carries a worse prognosis than IDH-wildtype
IDH mutation is favorable; he needs long-term MRI surveillance and risk-adapted adjuvant therapy based on extent of resection and risk factors
No further imaging is needed once the tumor is resected
He should receive immediate whole-brain radiation regardless of resection
stages.explanation: IDH mutation is a favorable prognostic marker in diffuse gliomas, associated with markedly longer survival than IDH-wildtype tumors. However, even after maximal safe resection low-grade gliomas are not cured; they can recur and transform to higher grade, so patients require long-term serial MRI surveillance and risk-adapted adjuvant therapy (for example, radiation with PCV or temozolomide) determined by age, extent of resection, and other risk features. Stating that IDH-mutant disease is worse is backwards. Abandoning imaging ignores the real recurrence risk. Reflexive whole-brain radiation is not standard and adds cognitive toxicity without indication. Favorable biology still demands vigilant follow-up.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse low-grade (IDH-mutant) glioma of the left insula presenting with a first seizure.
pearls: A first unprovoked seizure plus a structural lesion requires MRI with and without contrast, not reassurance.
Non-enhancing, T2/FLAIR-bright, low-perfusion lesions in young adults are typically IDH-mutant low-grade gliomas, a favorable marker.
Early maximal safe resection delays malignant transformation and improves survival versus watchful waiting.
Extent of resection is the strongest modifiable prognostic factor in low-grade glioma.

cases.title: Ringing ears and trouble hearing on the phone
cases.chiefComplaint: Both my ears ring and I keep switching the phone to my other ear because I can't hear.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college student presents with 18 months of progressive bilateral tinnitus and difficulty understanding speech, especially on the phone and in noisy rooms. She also reports occasional unsteadiness and two episodes of brief facial tingling. She denies vertigo spells, prior ear infections, ototoxic drug exposure, or a loud-noise occupation. She mentions that her father had hearing loss and 'brain tumors' in his 30s and died young, though the details are vague. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none. No known allergies. She is a nonsmoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and uses no drugs. She is a biology major and is frightened by the family history. On review she notes a few painless lumps under the skin of her forearm and a long-standing 'cloudy' spot in one eye noted by an optometrist. She denies facial weakness, swallowing trouble, and headaches, and wears no hearing aids. She had normal developmental milestones and academic performance until the hearing problems began.
cases.exam: Alert young woman. Weber does not clearly lateralize; Rinne shows air greater than bone bilaterally, a pattern of bilateral sensorineural hearing loss that is worse on the right by bedside testing. Tympanic membranes are normal. Facial sensation is intact, facial movement is symmetric, and there is no nystagmus at rest. There is mild bilateral dysmetria on heel-to-shin testing and a wide-based tandem gait. The remaining cranial nerves are intact. A few soft subcutaneous nodules are palpable on the right forearm, and the skin shows two faint café-au-lait macules. There is no spinal tenderness. Motor and sensory exams are otherwise normal. A slit-lamp note in the chart mentions a posterior subcapsular cataract.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young adult has progressive bilateral sensorineural hearing loss and a concerning family history. What is the best initial imaging study?
options: CT temporal bones without contrast
Carotid ultrasound
MRI of the internal auditory canals/brain with and without gadolinium
Plain skull radiographs
stages.explanation: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI with thin cuts through the internal auditory canals is the gold standard for evaluating sensorineural hearing loss and detecting vestibular schwannomas, which enhance avidly and may be only a few millimeters in size. CT temporal bones shows bony anatomy and canal widening but misses small intracanalicular tumors and lacks soft-tissue contrast. Carotid ultrasound evaluates extracranial vascular disease and is irrelevant to a retrocochlear lesion. Plain skull films have no role in characterizing cerebellopontine-angle or canal pathology. Formal audiometry complements imaging by quantifying and localizing the hearing loss, but it does not image the lesion. MRI is the correct first study.

stages.label: AUDIOLOGY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Audiometry: bilateral sensorineural hearing loss; word recognition 72% right, 88% left.
MRI brain/IAC with gadolinium: bilateral avidly enhancing masses in both cerebellopontine angles extending into the internal auditory canals (right 2.2 cm, left 1.1 cm).
Incidental finding: small enhancing extramedullary lesion along the thoracic cord (possible meningioma/schwannoma).
No hydrocephalus.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Bilateral cerebellopontine-angle tumors in a 19-year-old with skin and eye findings and a family history. What is the unifying diagnosis?
options: Sporadic unilateral vestibular schwannoma
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1)
Von Hippel-Lindau disease
Neurofibromatosis type 2 (NF2)
stages.explanation: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic for neurofibromatosis type 2. Supporting features here include the young age, an additional spinal tumor, a posterior subcapsular cataract, and an affected parent, consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance from the NF2 gene on chromosome 22. Sporadic schwannomas are unilateral and present in the fourth to sixth decades. NF1 features cutaneous neurofibromas, numerous café-au-lait macules, Lisch nodules, and optic pathway gliomas, but not bilateral vestibular schwannomas. Von Hippel-Lindau disease causes hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma rather than cerebellopontine-angle schwannomas. The bilateral vestibular tumors clinch NF2.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She still has serviceable hearing bilaterally, worse on the right. What is the guiding principle for managing her tumors?
options: Immediately resect both tumors in a single operation
Radiate both sides emergently
Prioritize hearing preservation: treat strategically (often the larger/worse-hearing side), use hearing-preservation approaches, and consider bevacizumab
Observe indefinitely regardless of growth or hearing
stages.explanation: NF2 care centers on preserving function, especially hearing, over a lifetime of bilateral disease. The strategy is individualized: observe small stable tumors, and intervene on the growing or symptomatic (often larger) side with hearing-preservation microsurgery or, in selected cases, radiosurgery. Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) can improve hearing and shrink schwannomas and is an important option. Resecting both tumors at once risks bilateral deafness and facial palsy and is avoided. Emergent bilateral radiation is not indicated and carries concern for malignant transformation in NF2. Indefinite observation regardless of progression ignores tumor growth and worsening hearing. Function-preserving, sequenced treatment is the principle.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Beyond the brain tumors, what additional steps are essential in this patient?
options: Genetic counseling/testing, whole-neuraxis MRI surveillance, and audiology with planning for hearing rehabilitation (cochlear or auditory brainstem implant)
Annual chest CT for lung cancer screening
Lifelong anticoagulation
Prophylactic bilateral mastectomy
stages.explanation: Because NF2 is autosomal dominant, genetic counseling and testing are essential, along with screening of at-risk relatives. Patients need whole-neuraxis MRI to detect the meningiomas, ependymomas, and additional schwannomas that characterize the syndrome, plus serial audiology to track hearing. When hearing loss becomes inevitable, planning rehabilitation in advance preserves communication: a cochlear implant if the cochlear nerve is intact, or an auditory brainstem implant if it is not. Annual chest CT, lifelong anticoagulation, and prophylactic mastectomy address unrelated conditions and are not part of NF2 management. Genetics, neuraxis surveillance, and hearing rehabilitation planning are the core additional steps.

cases.diagnosis: Neurofibromatosis type 2 with bilateral vestibular schwannomas.
pearls: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are diagnostic of NF2 until proven otherwise; suspect it in young patients with sensorineural hearing loss.
NF2 is autosomal dominant (chromosome 22); offer genetic counseling and screen the whole neuraxis for schwannomas, meningiomas, and ependymomas.
Management prioritizes long-term hearing and facial nerve preservation; bevacizumab can improve hearing and shrink tumors.
Plan hearing rehabilitation early: cochlear implant if the cochlear nerve is intact, auditory brainstem implant if it is not.

cases.title: Personality changes and a fading sense of smell
cases.chiefComplaint: My family says I'm not myself, and I can't smell anything anymore.
cases.history: A 57-year-old woman is brought in by her daughter for roughly a year of gradual personality change: apathy, poor financial judgment, social withdrawal, and disinhibited comments unlike her usual self. She was treated for 'depression' by her primary doctor with an SSRI for six months without benefit. Over the same period she lost her sense of smell, which she attributed to aging, and she recently complained that vision in her left eye is 'dim.' She has mild headaches but no seizures or weakness. PMH: hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and sertraline 100 mg daily. No known allergies. She is a retired teacher, a nonsmoker, and drinks alcohol rarely. Family history is noncontributory. Her daughter notes worsening forgetfulness and recent urinary incontinence. There are no fevers, weight loss, or trauma. She has not had formal vision testing recently. The presentation was initially framed as a primary psychiatric disorder, which delayed neurologic evaluation until the visual symptoms prompted referral.
cases.exam: Pleasant but apathetic, with concrete thinking and impaired judgment on bedside testing; oriented but with poor short-term recall. Anosmia is confirmed bilaterally (she cannot identify coffee or soap). Fundoscopy shows a pale, atrophic left optic disc, while the right disc has blurred, elevated margins consistent with papilledema. Visual acuity is reduced in the left eye with a left relative afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements are intact. There is no facial droop, motor strength is 5/5, sensation is intact, and reflexes are symmetric. Gait is mildly broad-based. A primitive grasp reflex is present. There is no meningismus.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: She has anosmia, left optic atrophy, and right papilledema. This triad localizes to what process?
options: Bilateral retinal disease
A subfrontal/anterior cranial fossa mass (Foster Kennedy syndrome)
Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
Multiple sclerosis
stages.explanation: The Foster Kennedy triad of ipsilateral optic atrophy (from direct optic-nerve compression), contralateral papilledema (from raised intracranial pressure), and anosmia (from olfactory nerve involvement) localizes to a slow-growing subfrontal/anterior cranial fossa mass, classically an olfactory groove or planum sphenoidale meningioma. Bilateral retinal disease would not cause anosmia or this asymmetric optic-nerve picture. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension produces bilateral papilledema in young, often obese women without anosmia or compressive optic atrophy. Multiple sclerosis optic neuritis is typically painful, monocular, and self-limited, and would not explain anosmia with a progressive frontal behavioral syndrome. The triad points to a frontal skull-base tumor.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best imaging to characterize the suspected mass?
options: MRI brain with and without contrast
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
Non-contrast CT only
Amyloid PET for dementia
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI is the study of choice for an anterior fossa mass: meningiomas show avid, homogeneous enhancement and a dural tail, and MRI defines the relationship to the optic nerves, anterior cerebral arteries, and paranasal sinuses for surgical planning. Carotid Doppler evaluates extracranial vascular disease, not an intracranial mass. Non-contrast CT can reveal hyperostosis or calcification but underestimates tumor extent and vascular relationships and is insufficient alone. Amyloid PET addresses neurodegenerative dementia and would miss a surgically treatable structural lesion that is driving this 'pseudo-psychiatric' picture. MRI with contrast both confirms and characterizes the tumor for operative planning.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: 4.5 cm avidly, homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass centered on the olfactory groove/planum sphenoidale with a dural tail and surrounding vasogenic edema in both frontal lobes.
Mass effect compressing the left optic nerve; bowing of the gyrus rectus.
CT: hyperostosis of the cribriform plate.
Labs: CBC, CMP, and TSH normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given an extra-axial, homogeneously enhancing skull-base mass with a dural tail and adjacent hyperostosis, what is the diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma
Pituitary macroadenoma
Olfactory groove meningioma
Metastatic carcinoma
stages.explanation: An extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail and adjacent hyperostosis at the olfactory groove is a classic olfactory groove meningioma; its slow growth explains the insidious frontal-lobe behavioral syndrome and the Foster Kennedy findings. Glioblastoma is intra-axial with irregular enhancement and necrosis, not dural-based. A pituitary macroadenoma is centered in the sella and causes a bitemporal hemianopia, not an olfactory-groove mass with anosmia. Metastases are usually multiple, intra-axial, and arise in a patient with a known primary, and they lack this dural-tailed morphology with hyperostosis. The imaging signature is diagnostic of meningioma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The mass is large with frontal edema and progressive visual decline. What is the definitive management, and what perioperative consideration is key?
options: Observation with repeat MRI in one year
Surgical resection; preoperative embolization may reduce vascularity and corticosteroids reduce edema
Whole-brain radiation as first-line therapy
SSRI optimization and psychiatric admission
stages.explanation: A large, symptomatic olfactory groove meningioma causing visual loss and a progressive frontal syndrome warrants surgical resection (Simpson-grade removal of tumor with involved dura and bone) to decompress the optic apparatus and restore function. Preoperative embolization can reduce intraoperative blood loss in hypervascular tumors, and corticosteroids reduce vasogenic edema perioperatively. Observation risks further irreversible optic atrophy as the optic nerve is already compromised. Primary radiation is reserved for residual, recurrent, or inoperable tumor, not a large compressive mass. Continued psychiatric management alone would miss a surgically curable cause of her decline. Resection with appropriate perioperative measures is definitive.

cases.diagnosis: Olfactory groove meningioma producing Foster Kennedy syndrome.
pearls: Foster Kennedy syndrome is ipsilateral optic atrophy plus contralateral papilledema plus anosmia, from a subfrontal mass (classically an olfactory groove meningioma).
Slow-growing frontal masses can masquerade as depression or dementia; anosmia and visual change are red flags.
Contrast MRI shows a dural-tailed, homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass, and CT may show adjacent hyperostosis.
Definitive treatment is surgical resection; preoperative embolization and steroids help with large, vascular tumors that have surrounding edema.

cases.title: A child who stopped speaking after brain surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: He was talking right after surgery, and now two days later he won't say a word.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is on the neurosurgical ward 48 hours after gross-total resection of a midline cerebellar (vermian) medulloblastoma that had caused weeks of morning vomiting, headache, and unsteady gait. Surgery and the immediate recovery were uneventful; he spoke a few words in the PACU and recognized his parents. Over the past day he has become progressively mute, refusing to speak at all, alternating between irritable crying and emotional lability, and seems to have difficulty initiating movements although he can follow some commands. His parents are distraught, fearing a stroke or that 'something went wrong.' He has remained afebrile. PMH: previously healthy with normal development and speech before the tumor. Medications: a dexamethasone taper, ondansetron, and acetaminophen. No known allergies. He lives with both parents and a sibling, and the family history is noncontributory. An external ventricular drain placed for hydrocephalus is functioning. He has a swallow concern and is kept NPO pending evaluation. His parents ask whether the muteness is permanent.
cases.exam: Awake, makes eye contact, and tracks his parents, but is completely mute with no spontaneous or prompted speech. He is emotionally labile, swinging from crying to calm. He follows simple commands inconsistently with delayed initiation, suggesting akinesia and apraxia features. There is hypotonia and truncal ataxia, with bilateral appendicular dysmetria. Cranial nerves are grossly intact, pupils are equal and reactive, there is no facial asymmetry, and the gag is present. He moves all four limbs to stimulation with normal strength. There is no nuchal rigidity. The posterior fossa incision is clean and dry, and the EVD drains clear CSF at the prescribed level.

stages.label: RECOGNITION
stages.question: A child becomes mute and emotionally labile 1-2 days after midline posterior fossa tumor resection, with ataxia but intact comprehension. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Posterior fossa syndrome (cerebellar mutism)
Postoperative stroke
Nonconvulsive status epilepticus
Psychogenic reaction to hospitalization
stages.explanation: Posterior fossa syndrome (cerebellar mutism) classically appears after a delay of 1-2 days following resection of a midline cerebellar tumor, most often medulloblastoma, with mutism, emotional lability, hypotonia, ataxia, and sometimes apraxia of initiation, while comprehension is preserved. It is attributed to disruption of the proximal efferent cerebellar (dentato-thalamo-cortical) pathways. A postoperative stroke would more typically cause fixed focal deficits with acute imaging changes. Nonconvulsive status epilepticus would impair awareness and show seizures on EEG, not isolated mutism with intact tracking and command-following. Labeling this psychogenic is inappropriate and dangerous before organic causes are excluded. The delayed onset and constellation are characteristic.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before attributing this to posterior fossa syndrome, what should be done to exclude dangerous mimics?
options: Nothing; the diagnosis is purely clinical and needs no workup
Neuroimaging to exclude new hydrocephalus, infarct, or hematoma, confirm EVD function, and add EEG if seizures are suspected
Immediate reoperation
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics empirically
stages.explanation: Although posterior fossa syndrome is ultimately a clinical diagnosis, you must first exclude treatable postoperative complications that can mimic or coexist with it, including obstructive hydrocephalus, a posterior fossa hematoma, and infarction, using CT or MRI, and you must verify that the EVD is functioning. EEG is added when seizures are a concern. Doing nothing risks missing a surgical emergency such as an expanding clot. Reoperation is not indicated without a demonstrated target lesion. Empiric antibiotics treat infection, which is not suggested here given the afebrile course and clean wound, and would not address the syndrome. Imaging plus EVD confirmation is the safe next step.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: expected postoperative changes in the posterior fossa; no new hemorrhage or infarct; ventricles decompressed with the EVD in good position.
MRI brain: postoperative vermian resection cavity; no acute infarct; signal change along the proximal efferent cerebellar pathways.
EEG: no epileptiform activity.
Labs: electrolytes and CBC normal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging excludes complications. The parents ask about treatment and recovery. What is the most accurate counseling and management plan?
options: There is no treatment and he will never speak again
Supportive care with multidisciplinary rehabilitation (speech, OT, PT); symptoms typically improve slowly over weeks to months, though some deficits may persist
Urgent reoperation to relieve the mutism
Lifelong antipsychotic medication
stages.explanation: There is no specific cure for posterior fossa syndrome; management is supportive, with early multidisciplinary rehabilitation, including speech-language therapy and physical and occupational therapy, plus emotional support for the family. Speech characteristically returns gradually over weeks to months, often emerging as dysarthric before normalizing, but a meaningful subset of children have lasting speech, cognitive, or affective deficits, so honest counseling is essential. The syndrome is not improved by reoperation because there is no structural target, and it is not a primary psychiatric disease requiring antipsychotics. Telling the family recovery is impossible is both inaccurate and harmful. Supportive rehabilitation with realistic expectations is correct.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement about the expected course should guide the family's expectations?
options: Recovery is immediate once the syndrome is recognized
Mutism usually resolves within hours
Recovery is typically slow over weeks to months and speech often returns dysarthric before improving; some cognitive or affective deficits may be long-term
The condition inevitably progresses to coma
stages.explanation: Families need realistic expectations: the mutism phase usually lasts days to weeks and then resolves gradually over weeks to months, with speech frequently emerging as slow and dysarthric before further improvement. Long-term neurocognitive, language, and affective sequelae occur in a meaningful subset of children, which is why sustained rehabilitation and neuropsychological follow-up matter. The syndrome does not resolve within hours or immediately upon recognition, nor does it progress to coma. Presenting either an instant recovery or an inexorable decline misrepresents the natural history and would either falsely reassure or needlessly frighten the family. A slow, mostly favorable but variable recovery is the accurate message.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior fossa syndrome (cerebellar mutism) following medulloblastoma resection.
pearls: Cerebellar mutism appears after a 1-2 day delay following midline posterior fossa tumor resection, with mutism, emotional lability, and ataxia; comprehension is preserved.
Always image to exclude hydrocephalus, hematoma, or infarct before attributing new deficits to the syndrome.
It is attributed to injury of the proximal efferent cerebellar (dentato-thalamo-cortical) pathways.
Recovery is slow over weeks to months with rehabilitation; some children have lasting speech, cognitive, or affective deficits.

cases.title: Sudden decline after draining a brain bleed
cases.chiefComplaint: He was waking up nicely after surgery and now he's barely responsive.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man is in the neuro-ICU on postoperative day 1 after burr-hole evacuation of a large chronic subdural hematoma that had caused weeks of headache, gait decline, and mild confusion after a fall. He awoke well from anesthesia, following commands and conversant, with a subdural drain in place. Over the last few hours the bedside nurse reports progressive somnolence, a new severe headache before he became drowsy, and now he is difficult to arouse. PMH: atrial fibrillation on apixaban (held perioperatively), hypertension, and a prior fall. Medications: apixaban (currently held), metoprolol, amlodipine, and perioperative analgesia. No known allergies. He is a former heavy smoker and lives with his wife. The subdural drain has put out a small amount of fluid. He was positioned with the head of the bed flat overnight. He has remained afebrile. The team is called urgently to evaluate the acute decline and orders a stat head CT. He had been neurologically improving until this deterioration.
cases.exam: Somnolent, opening his eyes only to noxious stimulation and no longer following commands (GCS roughly 9, down from 15). Pupils are 3 mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally. He localizes weakly with the left arm and withdraws the right. There is no gaze deviation. Plantar responses are mildly upgoing bilaterally. The subdural drain is in place with scant output, and the surgical dressing is clean. The neck is supple and there are no external signs of bleeding. Respirations are slightly irregular. A repeat exam over 20 minutes shows continued decline, prompting emergent imaging.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient who was improving deteriorates acutely after subdural evacuation. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Reassure that postoperative drowsiness is expected and observe
Stat non-contrast head CT while supporting airway and hemodynamics
Restart apixaban immediately
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Acute neurologic deterioration after intracranial surgery is an emergency that requires an immediate non-contrast head CT to identify a treatable cause, such as re-accumulated or acute hematoma, tension pneumocephalus, hydrocephalus, or infarct, while airway, breathing, and circulation are supported. Attributing the decline to expected drowsiness risks missing a catastrophe. Restarting anticoagulation in a deteriorating postoperative neurosurgical patient could precipitate or worsen intracranial hemorrhage and is contraindicated. Lumbar puncture is dangerous when mass effect or raised intracranial pressure is possible and would not diagnose the surgical cause. The correct first move is emergent imaging with simultaneous resuscitation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast head CT: large subdural air collection bilaterally compressing and separating the frontal lobes, producing the 'Mount Fuji sign' (peaks of separated frontal lobes with widened interhemispheric air).
Mass effect with sulcal effacement; the residual subdural space is largely air-filled.
No new acute blood; ventricles compressed.
Subdural drain tip in place.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows the 'Mount Fuji sign' with frontal lobe compression by air. What is the diagnosis?
options: Benign postoperative pneumocephalus
Tension pneumocephalus
Recurrent subdural hematoma
Cerebral abscess with a gas-forming organism
stages.explanation: The Mount Fuji sign, in which subdural air separates and compresses the frontal lobe tips and widens the interhemispheric space, indicates tension pneumocephalus, where trapped air is under pressure (often via a ball-valve mechanism) and causes mass effect with neurologic decline. Simple, benign postoperative pneumocephalus is common and does not compress or separate the lobes or cause deterioration. A recurrent hematoma would show hyperdense blood, not air, on CT. A gas-forming abscess would produce rim-enhancing parenchymal collections with infectious signs and an appropriate time course, not acute subdural tension air on postoperative day 1. The pressurized air with mass effect defines tension pneumocephalus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is declining from tension pneumocephalus. What is the appropriate management?
options: Emergent decompression (needle aspiration or reopening a burr hole to release the trapped air) plus high-flow 100% oxygen, with appropriate positioning
Observation with serial neuro checks
Start mannitol and begin discharge planning
Immediate therapeutic anticoagulation
stages.explanation: Tension pneumocephalus with neurologic deterioration is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent decompression: releasing the trapped subdural air via needle aspiration or by reopening a burr hole to relieve mass effect. High-flow 100% oxygen accelerates reabsorption by washing out nitrogen and creating a diffusion gradient. Positioning and addressing any persistent source of air entry are important adjuncts. Observation is unsafe in a deteriorating patient with mass effect. Mannitol treats parenchymal edema, not trapped air, and will not fix the problem. Anticoagulation is contraindicated and irrelevant to air decompression. Emergent release of the air plus 100% oxygen is the correct management.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: After successful decompression, what measure reduces recurrence and aids air reabsorption?
options: Keep the patient strictly upright at all times to draw air out, regardless of other factors
Resume nitrous oxide anesthesia
Supplemental high-concentration oxygen, adequate hydration, and avoiding maneuvers that entrain air (such as nose-blowing and Valsalva), while ensuring any dural or skull-base defect is addressed
Place the patient in steep Trendelenburg
stages.explanation: After decompression, supplemental high-concentration oxygen speeds nitrogen washout and air reabsorption, while avoiding Valsalva and nose-blowing prevents re-entrainment of air; identifying and closing any dural or skull-base defect addresses the source. Nitrous oxide is contraindicated because it rapidly expands air-filled spaces and can precipitate or worsen tension pneumocephalus. Rigid positioning rules oversimplify management, since head positioning is individualized to the case. Steep Trendelenburg raises intracranial pressure and is inappropriate. The guiding principles are oxygen therapy, source control, and avoiding air-entraining maneuvers. These measures together limit recurrence and speed resolution.

cases.diagnosis: Tension pneumocephalus after chronic subdural hematoma evacuation (Mount Fuji sign).
pearls: The Mount Fuji sign, with air compressing and separating the frontal lobe tips, signals tension pneumocephalus, not benign postoperative air.
Neurologic decline after intracranial surgery demands an emergent CT before anything else.
Treat tension pneumocephalus with urgent air decompression plus 100% oxygen to accelerate reabsorption.
Avoid nitrous oxide and air-entraining maneuvers (Valsalva, nose-blowing); nitrous expands trapped air and can precipitate tension.

cases.title: Unresponsive after a severe head injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Comatose with fixed pupils after a high-speed motorcycle crash.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man was brought to the ICU after a high-speed motorcycle collision while not wearing a helmet, producing a severe traumatic brain injury with diffuse cerebral edema and a large intraparenchymal hemorrhage. He underwent decompressive surgery and aggressive resuscitation but has had no neurologic improvement over 72 hours. He is now off sedation, mechanically ventilated, and has lost all brainstem reflexes on bedside exam, with fixed dilated pupils. There is no spontaneous movement. PMH: previously healthy. Medications: only resuscitation drugs; sedatives and paralytics have been discontinued long enough to clear. No known allergies. He is an organ-donor-card holder per his license, and his family is at the bedside asking whether he can recover. Toxicology is negative and he has been actively warmed. No barbiturate coma was induced. His core temperature and blood pressure have been corrected to within normal limits before testing. The team prepares to perform a formal brain death evaluation according to institutional protocol.
cases.exam: Comatose with no response to central or peripheral noxious stimulation aside from possible spinally mediated reflexes. Pupils are mid-to-fully dilated and fixed, with no light reflex bilaterally. Corneal reflexes are absent. Oculocephalic and oculovestibular (cold caloric) responses are absent. Gag and cough are absent with deep tracheal suctioning. There is no facial movement and no spontaneous respiratory effort over the ventilator. There is no decerebrate or decorticate posturing. Core temperature is normothermic and there are no clinical signs of drug intoxication. Some deep tendon reflexes may be present, mediated at the spinal level.

stages.label: PREREQUISITES
stages.question: Before any brain death examination, which prerequisites and confounders must be satisfied or excluded?
options: Only that the patient has a documented head injury

A known irreversible catastrophic brain injury, normothermia, normotension, correction of severe metabolic/endocrine derangements, and exclusion of drug or alcohol intoxication and neuromuscular blockade
A normal EEG
Family consent to the diagnosis
stages.explanation: Brain death determination requires first establishing an identifiable, irreversible cause of catastrophic brain injury and eliminating confounders that mimic it. Core temperature must be near normal (typically above 36 degrees C), systolic and mean arterial pressures must be adequate, and severe acid-base, electrolyte, glucose, and endocrine abnormalities must be corrected. CNS depressant drugs, alcohol, and neuromuscular blockade must be excluded. A documented injury alone is insufficient. EEG is an optional ancillary test, not a prerequisite, and a 'normal' EEG would not be expected. Family consent is required for organ donation discussions, not to make the clinical diagnosis of death. These prerequisites must be met before testing.

stages.label: EXAMINATION
stages.question: Which set of findings is required to demonstrate absence of brainstem function?
options: Absent pupillary, corneal, oculocephalic, oculovestibular, gag, and cough reflexes, plus coma with no motor response to noxious stimuli
Absent deep tendon reflexes throughout the body
A single absent corneal reflex
Flaccid paralysis with preserved spontaneous breathing
stages.explanation: The clinical brain death exam documents coma with no cerebral motor response to noxious stimuli and absence of all brainstem reflexes: pupillary light, corneal, oculocephalic (doll's eyes), oculovestibular (cold caloric), gag, and cough. Spinal reflexes, including some deep tendon reflexes and even complex spinally mediated movements, can persist and do not exclude brain death, so their absence is not required and not expected. A single absent reflex is inadequate; the full set must be absent. Preserved spontaneous breathing is fundamentally incompatible with brain death. Demonstrating coma plus the complete absence of brainstem reflexes is what the examination requires.

stages.label: APNEA TEST
stages.content:
Pre-test: core temp 36.9 °C, SBP 110 mmHg, eucapnic baseline ABG pH 7.40, PaCO2 40 mmHg, preoxygenated to PaO2 >200 mmHg.
Ventilator disconnected with O2 delivered via tracheal cannula; observed 8-10 minutes.
No spontaneous respiratory effort observed.
Post-test ABG: PaCO2 68 mmHg (rise >20 mmHg from baseline and >60 mmHg), pH 7.24.
Hemodynamically stable throughout.

stages.label: ANCILLARY TESTING
stages.question: Suppose instead that severe facial trauma had prevented caloric testing, or the patient became unstable during apnea testing. What is the appropriate approach?
options: Declare brain death anyway based on the incomplete exam
Perform a validated ancillary test (cerebral angiography, radionuclide cerebral blood flow scan, or EEG) to confirm absence of brain function or flow
Wait for spontaneous recovery
Disregard the missing components
stages.explanation: When a component of the clinical exam or the apnea test cannot be completed or is confounded, for example facial or cervical trauma preventing oculovestibular testing, or hemodynamic instability or hypoxia aborting apnea testing, a validated ancillary test is required. Options include cerebral angiography (no intracranial flow), a radionuclide cerebral blood flow scan (absent uptake, the 'hollow skull' sign), or EEG (electrocerebral silence). Declaring death on an incomplete exam or disregarding missing data violates protocol and risks error. Waiting for spontaneous recovery contradicts the established irreversible injury. Ancillary testing supplements the clinical exam when it cannot be completed; it does not replace the prerequisites.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The apnea test shows no respiratory effort with PaCO2 rising above 60 mmHg, and all clinical criteria are met. What is the appropriate next step, including communication?
options: Wait several days and repeat the entire process before saying anything to the family
Tell the family he is 'brain dead but might still wake up'
Declare death by neurologic criteria per protocol; communicate clearly and compassionately that the patient has died, then involve the organ procurement organization
Proceed directly to organ recovery without notifying the family or the organ procurement organization
stages.explanation: With prerequisites met, all brainstem reflexes absent, and a positive apnea test (no breathing with PaCO2 at or above 60 mmHg or at least 20 mmHg above baseline), the patient meets criteria for death by neurologic criteria and can be declared per institutional protocol. Communication must be clear and compassionate: brain death is death, not a coma from which one recovers, so telling the family he 'might wake up' is false and cruel. The organ procurement organization must be notified, which is legally required, and trained requestors, not the treating clinicians, approach the family about donation. Clinicians never bypass family notification or proceed to recovery without proper consent and process.

cases.diagnosis: Brain death (death by neurologic criteria) following severe traumatic brain injury.
pearls: Brain death requires a known irreversible cause plus exclusion of confounders: hypothermia, hypotension, drugs or intoxication, neuromuscular blockade, and severe metabolic derangement.
The exam shows coma and absence of ALL brainstem reflexes; spinal reflexes can persist and do not exclude brain death.
A positive apnea test is no respiratory drive with PaCO2 at or above 60 mmHg (or at least 20 above baseline); ancillary tests are used only when the exam or apnea test cannot be completed.
Brain death is legal death; notify the organ procurement organization, but clinicians should not raise donation themselves and must never imply recovery is possible.

cases.title: A growing soft lump on a toddler's head
cases.chiefComplaint: The bump on her head is getting bigger, not smaller, and it pulses.
cases.history: An 18-month-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother for an enlarging soft swelling over the left side of the head. Four months ago she fell from a changing table and sustained a left parietal linear skull fracture; a small scalp hematoma at the time was managed conservatively and she did well. Over the past 6 to 8 weeks the mother noticed a soft, sometimes pulsatile swelling at the old injury site that is slowly enlarging. The child is otherwise developing normally, walking and saying single words, though the mother reports one possible brief staring episode. There has been no vomiting, no obvious weakness, and no fevers. PMH: term birth with normal development. Medications: none. No known allergies. Immunizations are up to date. She lives with both parents, the original injury was witnessed, and there are no historical concerns for non-accidental trauma. Family history is noncontributory. The child did not have routine follow-up imaging after the initial fracture. The mother is worried the lump is 'growing into her brain.'
cases.exam: Alert, interactive toddler at an age-appropriate developmental level. Over the left parietal scalp there is a soft, non-tender, compressible, and mildly pulsatile swelling roughly 3 cm across, with a palpable bony defect or edge beneath it; there is no overlying erythema or warmth. The anterior fontanelle is closed. There is no focal motor deficit and she moves all limbs symmetrically. Pupils are equal and reactive, and there is no setting-sun sign. Cranial nerves are grossly intact and no papilledema is visualized. Grasp and gait are developmentally appropriate. The remainder of the exam is normal, with no other bruises or injuries suggestive of non-accidental trauma.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An enlarging, pulsatile scalp swelling with a palpable skull defect months after a parietal fracture in a toddler. What is the best initial imaging?
options: Plain skull radiograph alone
Scalp ultrasound only, with reassurance
CT head with bone windows, plus MRI to assess for a dural tear and brain involvement
No imaging; observe for another 6 months
stages.explanation: A growing, pulsatile mass over a prior fracture with a palpable bony defect suggests a growing skull fracture (leptomeningeal cyst). CT with bone windows defines the widening fracture and diastasis, and MRI evaluates the dural defect, any herniated leptomeninges or brain, and underlying encephalomalacia, which is needed for surgical planning. A plain film may show diastatic edges but underestimates the dura and brain involvement. Ultrasound alone is insufficient to plan surgery. Continued observation is dangerous because the defect enlarges and brain can herniate, worsening the neurologic outcome and seizure risk. The combination of CT and MRI both confirms the lesion and characterizes the dural and parenchymal involvement.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head (bone windows): left parietal fracture margins are now widely separated (diastatic) compared with the prior study, with scalloped, beveled bony edges.
MRI brain: dural defect at the fracture with herniation of leptomeninges and CSF (and gliotic brain) through the gap; underlying encephalomalacia; a CSF-filled cyst communicating with the subarachnoid space.
No acute hemorrhage.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Widening of an old fracture with herniation of leptomeninges and CSF through a dural defect in a young child. What is the diagnosis?
options: Simple resolving cephalohematoma
Growing skull fracture (leptomeningeal cyst / craniocerebral erosion)
Osteomyelitis of the skull
Langerhans cell histiocytosis
stages.explanation: This is a growing skull fracture, or post-traumatic leptomeningeal cyst: a fracture with an underlying dural tear in a young child allows pulsatile CSF and brain to herniate and progressively erode and widen the bone. A cephalohematoma is a self-limited subperiosteal blood collection that resolves over weeks, not an enlarging pulsatile defect months later. Osteomyelitis presents with infection, including pain, warmth, and fever, and shows lytic changes, not a CSF-filled cyst tied to old trauma. Langerhans cell histiocytosis causes lytic 'punched-out' skull lesions but is unrelated to a prior fracture and lacks the dural-tear mechanism. The trauma history with a diastatic fracture and herniated meninges is diagnostic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for a growing skull fracture?
options: Compression bandage and observation
Aspiration of the cyst in clinic
Surgical repair of the dural defect (watertight duraplasty) with cranioplasty of the bony defect
Antibiotics and steroids
stages.explanation: The defining lesion is the dural tear, so cure requires surgery: a watertight dural repair (duraplasty), typically combined with repair or reconstruction of the bony defect (cranioplasty). The dural closure must extend beyond the bone edges because the dural defect is characteristically larger than the skull gap. Compression or observation will not heal a torn dura and allows continued progression. Clinic aspiration is futile and risks infection or CSF leak because the cyst communicates with the subarachnoid space. Antibiotics and steroids treat infection or inflammation, not a structural dural defect. Watertight duraplasty with cranioplasty is the definitive treatment.

stages.label: TEACHING POINT
stages.question: Why do young children specifically need follow-up after a skull fracture with these features?
options: Because skull fractures in children never need any follow-up

Because rapid brain growth and pulsatile CSF in young children (typically under age 3) can enlarge a fracture through an unrecognized dural tear; early detection and repair prevent progressive bone erosion and neurologic injury
Because all pediatric fractures require lifelong annual CT scans
Because fractures in children always heal without complication
stages.explanation: Growing skull fractures occur almost exclusively in young children, usually under age 3, when the rapidly growing brain and pulsatile CSF can progressively widen a fracture if the dura was torn beneath it. Follow-up, both clinical and, when indicated, with imaging, catches the diastasis early so a duraplasty can be performed before significant bone erosion, brain herniation, or seizures develop. Saying fractures need no follow-up or always heal uneventfully is false and misses this complication. Routine lifelong annual CT for every pediatric fracture is excessive and not standard; targeted follow-up of at-risk fractures, particularly wide or diastatic ones in infants, is what matters.

cases.diagnosis: Growing skull fracture (post-traumatic leptomeningeal cyst) of the left parietal bone.
pearls: A growing skull fracture is a fracture plus an underlying dural tear in a young child (<3 years); pulsatile CSF and brain progressively widen the defect.
Suspect it with an enlarging, pulsatile scalp mass and a palpable bony defect weeks to months after a fracture.
MRI confirms the dural defect and herniated leptomeninges or brain; CT shows diastatic, beveled fracture edges.
Treatment is surgical: watertight duraplasty extending beyond the bone gap plus cranioplasty; observation will not heal a torn dura.

cases.title: A newborn in heart failure with a noisy head
cases.chiefComplaint: Term newborn with respiratory distress, poor feeding, and a loud sound heard over the skull.
cases.history: A term newborn boy, now 4 days old, is in the NICU with tachypnea, poor feeding, sweating with feeds, and failure to thrive emerging in the first days of life. The pregnancy was reportedly uncomplicated, though a late prenatal ultrasound noted a possible intracranial vascular abnormality that was not fully characterized. He was born vaginally at term with normal Apgar scores but developed progressive respiratory distress and signs of congestive heart failure. The neonatology team noted a cranial bruit on auscultation of the head and bounding pulses. There is no cyanosis correcting with oxygen to suggest primary lung disease, and the heart is structurally normal on echocardiography aside from volume overload. There were no maternal infections, and the family history is noncontributory. He has had no seizures. Birth weight was appropriate. He is being supported with respiratory and inotropic measures while the cause of high-output failure is investigated. His head circumference is at the upper limit of normal with prominent scalp veins.
cases.exam: Tachypneic neonate in respiratory distress with subcostal retractions. A loud, continuous cranial bruit is auscultated over the anterior fontanelle and skull. There is a hyperdynamic precordium, bounding peripheral pulses, and a wide pulse pressure, with palpable hepatomegaly. The anterior fontanelle is full but soft, and there are prominent dilated scalp veins. There is no cyanosis that corrects fully with oxygen. The lungs have bilateral crackles. Neurologically, tone is slightly decreased, he moves all limbs, and pupils are reactive, with no overt seizures. There are no dysmorphic features and central capillary refill is brisk. The findings point to a high-output cardiac state with a cranial vascular source.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A term neonate has high-output heart failure, bounding pulses, and a continuous cranial bruit. What intracranial process should top the differential?
options: Neonatal sepsis
Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy
A high-flow intracranial arteriovenous shunt (vein of Galen malformation)
Congenital diaphragmatic hernia
stages.explanation: A continuous cranial bruit with high-output congestive heart failure and bounding pulses and a wide pulse pressure in a neonate is the classic presentation of a high-flow intracranial arteriovenous shunt, a vein of Galen malformation, in which massive arteriovenous shunting overloads the heart. Sepsis can cause shock but not a cranial bruit with a high-output state from shunting. Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy causes encephalopathy and seizures, not high-output failure with a bruit. Congenital diaphragmatic hernia causes respiratory distress with a scaphoid abdomen and bowel in the chest, not a cranial bruit or high-output failure. The bruit plus high-output failure points to an intracranial shunt.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Echocardiogram: structurally normal heart with severe volume overload, dilated right heart, pulmonary hypertension, and retrograde diastolic flow in the descending aorta.
Cranial ultrasound with Doppler: midline posterior dilated venous structure with turbulent high-velocity flow.
MRI/MRA brain: dilated median prosencephalic vein (vein of Galen) with multiple feeding arteries, consistent with a vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation.
No intraparenchymal hemorrhage.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Imaging confirms a dilated median vein fed by multiple arteries. What is the diagnosis?
options: Vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation
Dural sinus thrombosis
Germinal matrix hemorrhage
Choroid plexus papilloma
stages.explanation: A dilated median prosencephalic vein (of Galen) fed by multiple choroidal and pial arteries with high-flow shunting is a vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation, which is the cause of this neonate's high-output failure. Dural sinus thrombosis obstructs venous drainage and does not create a high-flow arteriovenous shunt or a bruit. Germinal matrix hemorrhage occurs mainly in premature infants and presents with hemorrhage and encephalopathy, not high-output cardiac failure. A choroid plexus papilloma overproduces CSF and causes hydrocephalus rather than a high-flow vascular shunt with cardiac failure. The dilated median vein with arterial feeders and retrograde aortic diastolic flow confirms the malformation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How should this neonate be managed initially?
options: Immediate open surgical resection of the malformation
Aggressive medical stabilization of heart failure first, followed by staged endovascular embolization (often timed toward several months of age when feasible)
Emergent single-session embolization of all feeders on day of life 4
Cranial radiation
stages.explanation: Management begins with aggressive medical control of the high-output heart failure, using diuretics, inotropic and ventilatory support, and afterload management. Definitive treatment is staged transarterial endovascular embolization, ideally timed: when the infant is stable, treatment is often deferred toward several months of age to improve technical success and outcomes, though severe, uncontrollable failure can force earlier intervention. Open surgical resection carries prohibitive mortality and is not first-line. Attempting complete single-session embolization risks dangerous hemodynamic shifts and normal perfusion pressure breakthrough. Radiation has no role in acute neonatal management. Stabilize the heart first, then embolize in stages.

stages.label: RATIONALE
stages.question: What is the rationale for STAGED rather than single-session embolization, and what guides timing?
options: Staging is arbitrary and makes no difference
Single-session embolization is always safer in neonates

Gradual, staged occlusion avoids abrupt hemodynamic and cerebral perfusion shifts (normal perfusion pressure breakthrough and hemorrhage); timing balances controlling heart failure against letting the infant grow, using severity scores to set urgency
Embolization should be delayed until adulthood regardless of symptoms
stages.explanation: The shunt is high-flow, so abruptly closing it can cause dangerous swings in cardiac and cerebral hemodynamics, including normal perfusion pressure breakthrough hemorrhage. Staged embolization gradually reduces flow, allowing the heart and brain to adapt. Timing balances the severity of heart failure, which may force earlier treatment, against the technical and developmental advantages of letting the infant grow, and clinical severity scoring such as the Bicetre neonatal score helps decide whether to intervene urgently or stabilize and defer. Calling staging arbitrary, declaring single-session universally safer, or delaying to adulthood all ignore the hemodynamic risks and the danger of uncontrolled neonatal heart failure.

cases.diagnosis: Vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation presenting with neonatal high-output cardiac failure.
pearls: A neonate with high-output heart failure, bounding pulses, and a continuous cranial bruit has a vein of Galen malformation until proven otherwise.
Diagnose with cranial Doppler ultrasound and MRI/MRA; echo shows high-output overload with retrograde aortic diastolic flow.
Stabilize heart failure medically first; staged transarterial endovascular embolization is the definitive treatment, often timed toward a few months of age.
Staged embolization avoids abrupt hemodynamic shifts and normal perfusion pressure breakthrough; severity scores (e.g., Bicetre) guide timing and urgency.

cases.title: Sudden dizziness, vomiting, and unable to stand
cases.chiefComplaint: I got dizzy out of nowhere, threw up over and over, and now I can't stand up without falling.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman is brought in by her daughter after the sudden onset of severe dizziness while preparing lunch about two hours ago. She felt the room spinning violently, became nauseated, and vomited repeatedly. When she tried to walk to the bathroom she veered to the right and fell, unable to keep her balance. She denies chest pain, palpitations, or focal weakness but reports a dull occipital headache that began with the dizziness. There was no loss of consciousness or seizure. PMH: long-standing hypertension that often runs 'high' at home, and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily, though she admits skipping doses. She takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. Allergies: none. Social history: former smoker who quit 10 years ago, drinks wine occasionally, and lives independently. Family history: mother had a stroke at 70. Review of systems is otherwise negative for fever, recent trauma, or hearing loss.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented but appears uncomfortable and cannot sit upright without support. She has prominent gaze-evoked horizontal nystagmus and dysmetria on right finger-to-nose testing with dysdiadochokinesia. Truncal ataxia is severe, and she falls toward the right when seated unassisted. Speech is mildly slurred. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, with a symmetric face and normal visual fields. There is no pronator drift, motor strength is 5/5 in all limbs, and sensation is intact. Reflexes are symmetric with downgoing plantar responses. The neck is supple without meningismus, and there is no facial droop or aphasia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Perform the Dix-Hallpike maneuver and give meclizine for benign positional vertigo
Obtain an emergent non-contrast CT of the head
Order MRI brain with contrast as the first imaging study
Give IV prochlorperazine and observe for symptom resolution
stages.explanation: Sudden vertigo accompanied by an occipital headache, truncal ataxia, and inability to stand points to a central (cerebellar) lesion rather than a peripheral cause. An emergent non-contrast CT is fast, widely available, and immediately detects acute hemorrhage. The Dix-Hallpike maneuver and meclizine target benign positional vertigo, but central red flags (severe truncal ataxia, headache, inability to walk) make that approach dangerous. MRI is more sensitive for ischemia but is slower, less accessible, and does not beat CT for identifying acute blood. An antiemetic alone treats symptoms while missing a potentially lethal posterior fossa catastrophe. Imaging must come before symptomatic management.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: 3.6 cm hyperdense hematoma in the right cerebellar hemisphere
Effacement of the fourth ventricle with early dilation of the temporal horns (hydrocephalus)
Partial effacement of the basal cisterns; cerebellar tonsils near the foramen magnum
Na 140 mEq/L
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
BP on recheck 198/104 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the primary mechanism threatening this patient's life?
options: Embolic occlusion of the posterior cerebral artery
Vasospasm of the basilar artery
Mass effect from the hematoma causing brainstem compression and obstructive hydrocephalus
Seizure-related diffuse cerebral edema
stages.explanation: The posterior fossa is a small, rigid compartment, so a clot larger than 3 cm rapidly produces mass effect: it effaces the fourth ventricle, blocks CSF outflow to cause obstructive hydrocephalus, and compresses the brainstem, which can precipitate sudden coma and death. The CT confirms exactly this. Embolic posterior cerebral artery occlusion is ischemic, not hemorrhagic, and would not produce a hyperdense clot. Basilar vasospasm is a delayed complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage, days after a bleed, not relevant here. Seizure-related edema does not explain an acute hematoma. Recognizing brainstem compression and hydrocephalus drives the urgency of treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for this patient?
options: Blood pressure control and admission for observation only
External ventricular drain placement alone
Emergent suboccipital craniotomy for hematoma evacuation
Intravenous mannitol with a repeat CT in 24 hours
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend emergent surgical evacuation for a cerebellar hemorrhage greater than 3 cm or one causing brainstem compression, neurologic deterioration, or hydrocephalus — all present here. A suboccipital craniotomy directly removes the clot and relieves brainstem compression. An external ventricular drain treats the hydrocephalus but does not decompress the brainstem and may precipitate upward herniation if used alone; it is at best an adjunct. Blood pressure control and observation are appropriate only for small clots (under 3 cm) without compression. Mannitol can temporize raised ICP but is not definitive and must not delay the operation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before going to the OR, the team considers placing an EVD for the hydrocephalus. What is the main risk of relying on an EVD alone in this setting?
options: Upward transtentorial (cerebellar) herniation
Rebleeding from a ruptured aneurysm
Cerebral salt wasting
Communicating hydrocephalus requiring a permanent shunt
stages.explanation: Draining CSF from above while a posterior fossa mass pushes upward can cause upward transtentorial herniation of the cerebellar vermis through the tentorial incisura, compressing the midbrain — which is why an EVD is not definitive and surgical decompression should not be delayed. Rebleeding from an aneurysm is a subarachnoid hemorrhage concern, not hypertensive cerebellar ICH. Cerebral salt wasting is a later electrolyte complication, not the acute risk of CSF diversion. Communicating hydrocephalus may develop later but is not the immediate danger of an EVD in an unrelieved posterior fossa mass.

cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive cerebellar hemorrhage (>3 cm) with fourth-ventricle effacement and obstructive hydrocephalus requiring emergent suboccipital decompression.
pearls: Sudden vertigo with truncal ataxia and inability to stand suggests a central (cerebellar) lesion — image before treating it as peripheral vertigo.
A cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm or with brainstem compression or hydrocephalus is a neurosurgical emergency requiring suboccipital decompression.
An EVD alone can precipitate upward herniation in a posterior fossa mass and does not replace decompression.
Hypertension is the most common cause of spontaneous cerebellar ICH; control the blood pressure but do not delay surgery.

cases.title: The worst headache of my life
cases.chiefComplaint: It felt like I got hit in the back of the head with a bat — the worst headache I have ever had.
cases.history: A 51-year-old woman presents after the abrupt onset of an explosive headache that began about 90 minutes ago while she was lifting a heavy box. She describes it as instantaneous and maximal at onset — 'like getting hit in the back of the head with a bat' — and rates it 10/10, far worse than any prior headache. It is holocephalic and radiates to her neck. She had one episode of vomiting and briefly felt faint but did not lose consciousness. She reports neck stiffness and discomfort looking at bright lights. There was no seizure, weakness, or speech difficulty. PMH: hypertension, not consistently treated. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, taken irregularly. Allergies: none. Social history: current smoker, one pack per day for 25 years; drinks alcohol socially. Family history: her mother had a 'brain aneurysm' with a subarachnoid hemorrhage at age 55. She has had occasional milder headaches over the past two weeks that she attributed to stress.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable, lying still in a dim room, and appears distressed from pain. She is alert and oriented (Glasgow Coma Scale 15) with fluent speech. There is nuchal rigidity with pain on neck flexion and photophobia. Pupils are equal and reactive; fundoscopy shows no papilledema but a small subhyaloid hemorrhage on the right. Cranial nerves are intact, including full extraocular movements without a third-nerve palsy. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout with no pronator drift, sensation is intact, and reflexes are symmetric with downgoing plantars. There is no focal deficit. The clinical picture is Hunt-Hess grade II.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial test in this patient?
options: Non-contrast CT of the head
MRI brain with gadolinium
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
CT angiography of the head and neck
stages.explanation: A thunderclap headache that is maximal at onset is subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise, and a non-contrast head CT is the correct first step — it is fast and highly sensitive (>95%) for SAH within the first 6 hours. Performing a lumbar puncture before imaging is not done; you image first and reserve LP for when the CT is negative. MRI is less available and slower in the acute setting. CT angiography is the next step to localize an aneurysm after SAH is confirmed, but it is not the initial triage study. Speed and sensitivity make non-contrast CT the right opening move.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: diffuse hyperdensity filling the basal cisterns and Sylvian fissures consistent with subarachnoid hemorrhage
No intraparenchymal hematoma; no hydrocephalus
BP 176/95 mmHg
Na 139 mEq/L
Platelets 255 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
ECG: sinus rhythm with diffuse T-wave changes

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: SAH is confirmed. What is the next best step to identify the cause?
options: Begin nimodipine and observe
CT angiography (or digital subtraction angiography) of the cerebral vessels
Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia
MRI brain without contrast
stages.explanation: Once SAH is confirmed, the priority is to localize the bleeding source so it can be secured. CT angiography identifies most aneurysms quickly, and digital subtraction angiography remains the gold standard with the highest resolution. Nimodipine is given to all aneurysmal SAH patients to reduce delayed cerebral ischemia, but it does not identify the source. A lumbar puncture is unnecessary because the CT is already diagnostic. MRI is not used to localize a ruptured aneurysm acutely. Vascular imaging directs the definitive treatment that follows.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Angiography reveals a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm. What is the definitive management?
options: Long-term blood pressure control alone
Repeat CT in 24 hours to monitor
Early securing of the aneurysm by endovascular coiling or microsurgical clipping
Placement of a therapeutic lumbar drain
stages.explanation: The risk of rebleeding is highest in the first 24 hours and carries very high mortality, so the aneurysm must be secured early (within 24-72 hours) by endovascular coiling or microsurgical clipping; coiling is favored when the anatomy is suitable. Blood pressure control reduces the rebleeding risk but does not eliminate the aneurysm. Repeat imaging or observation does nothing to prevent a catastrophic rerupture. A lumbar drain is dangerous before the aneurysm is secured because lowering CSF pressure can precipitate rebleeding or herniation. Securing the aneurysm is the cornerstone of treatment.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Suppose instead the initial non-contrast CT had been read as normal but the history was classic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge with outpatient migraine follow-up
Reassure and treat as a tension headache
Lumbar puncture looking for xanthochromia and elevated red cells
Empiric triptan therapy
stages.explanation: CT sensitivity for SAH falls after about 6 hours, so a negative CT with a convincing thunderclap history mandates a lumbar puncture. Xanthochromia (yellow discoloration from breakdown of red cells into bilirubin) and a persistently elevated red-cell count that does not clear from tube 1 to tube 4 support SAH and help distinguish it from a traumatic tap. Discharge, reassurance, or empiric triptans risk missing a sentinel (warning) bleed before a fatal rerupture. The LP is the safety net that prevents catastrophic misses when imaging is negative.

cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured anterior communicating artery aneurysm.
pearls: A thunderclap 'worst headache of life' is SAH until proven otherwise; non-contrast CT within 6 hours is highly sensitive.
A negative CT with a convincing history mandates a lumbar puncture for xanthochromia before excluding SAH.
Once SAH is confirmed, CTA or DSA localizes the aneurysm, which must be secured early by clipping or coiling to prevent rerupture.
Nimodipine reduces delayed cerebral ischemia and improves outcomes — it does not prevent rebleeding.

cases.title: New trouble speaking a week after a brain bleed
cases.chiefComplaint: ICU nurse reports the patient has become hard to understand and is slow to follow commands on post-bleed day 7.
cases.history: A 47-year-old woman is in the neuro-ICU on post-bleed day 7 after presenting with a Hunt-Hess grade III aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage from a ruptured left middle cerebral artery aneurysm, which was secured by endovascular coiling on day 1. Her early course was stable, and she had been awake, conversant, and following commands. This morning the bedside nurse notes she has become difficult to understand, uses the wrong words, and is slow to follow commands; she also seems to favor her left side. There is no new headache, fever, or reported seizure activity. She has been receiving oral nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours and maintenance isotonic fluids and has been kept normovolemic. Other medications include acetaminophen and pantoprazole. Allergies: none. PMH: hypertension and prior tobacco use. Social history: former smoker, quit at admission. Family history: noncontributory. Her most recent serum sodium has been trending down over the past day.
cases.exam: The patient is awake but exhibits a nonfluent (expressive) aphasia, producing effortful, halting speech with word-finding errors, while comprehension is relatively preserved. She follows axial and some appendicular commands slowly. There is a new mild right pronator drift with subtle right-sided weakness (4+/5), and the right nasolabial fold is slightly flattened. Pupils are equal and reactive, with no fixed gaze preference. There is no neck rigidity beyond her baseline, no papilledema, and no clonus. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is intact. No myoclonus or overt seizure activity is observed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On post-bleed day 7, what is the most likely cause of this new neurologic deficit?
options: Aneurysm rerupture
Cerebral vasospasm causing delayed cerebral ischemia
Acute hydrocephalus
Seizure with postictal Todd paralysis
stages.explanation: Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm typically develops on post-bleed days 4-14 and peaks around days 7-10, and a new focal deficit such as aphasia and arm drift in this window after a secured aneurysm is classic. Rerupture is much less likely after coiling and usually presents with a sudden severe headache and collapse, not isolated aphasia. Acute hydrocephalus causes decreased arousal and headache rather than a focal cortical aphasia. A seizure with Todd paralysis is possible but is a diagnosis of exclusion; ischemia must be ruled out first. Confirmatory studies follow.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Transcranial Doppler: left MCA mean flow velocity 220 cm/s (up from 90 cm/s), Lindegaard ratio 4.5
Non-contrast CT head: no new hemorrhage, no hydrocephalus, no established infarct
CT angiography: focal narrowing of the left M1 segment
Na 132 mEq/L
Glucose 128 mg/dL
Continuous EEG: no seizures

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms vasospasm without infarct. What is the best next step?
options: Induced hypertension to augment cerebral perfusion
Fluid restriction for the hyponatremia
Emergent repeat aneurysm coiling
Start phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Symptomatic vasospasm with delayed cerebral ischemia is treated with hemodynamic augmentation — induced hypertension using vasopressors to raise the mean arterial pressure and improve collateral perfusion — while maintaining euvolemia. Fluid restriction is harmful: hypovolemia worsens DCI, and SAH-associated hyponatremia (often cerebral salt wasting) should be treated with isotonic or hypertonic saline, not restriction.
