Repeat coiling is pointless because the aneurysm is already secured and the problem is arterial narrowing, not the sac. Phenytoin does not treat ischemia, and the EEG shows no seizures. Raising perfusion pressure is the immediate priority.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her aphasia persists despite maximal induced hypertension. What is the next step?
options: Stop nimodipine to allow higher blood pressure
Begin therapeutic hypothermia
Endovascular therapy with intra-arterial vasodilators and/or balloon angioplasty
Perform a decompressive craniectomy
stages.explanation: Refractory symptomatic vasospasm warrants endovascular rescue: intra-arterial calcium channel blockers (verapamil or nicardipine) and balloon angioplasty for accessible proximal large-vessel spasm. Nimodipine should be continued because it improves outcomes; it is only held transiently for hypotension, never abandoned during active spasm. Therapeutic hypothermia is not standard therapy for vasospasm. Decompressive craniectomy is for malignant cerebral edema or herniation, not for the arterial narrowing itself. Endovascular treatment directly targets the spastic vessel when medical augmentation fails.

stages.label: PHARMACOLOGY
stages.question: What best describes the role of nimodipine in this patient?
options: It dissolves the clot in the subarachnoid space
It reliably reverses established vasospasm on angiography
It improves neurologic outcomes despite minimal effect on angiographic vessel caliber
It provides seizure prophylaxis
stages.explanation: Oral nimodipine, an L-type calcium channel blocker, is given to all aneurysmal SAH patients for 21 days because it improves functional outcomes and reduces delayed cerebral ischemia, even though it has little measurable effect on the diameter of spastic vessels on angiography — its benefit is thought to be neuroprotective. It does not lyse subarachnoid clot, does not reliably reverse angiographic spasm, and is not an anticonvulsant. Its main side effect is hypotension, which may require dose adjustment rather than discontinuation. Understanding this distinction prevents the error of stopping it during spasm.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebral vasospasm with delayed cerebral ischemia following aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage.
pearls: Delayed cerebral ischemia from vasospasm appears on post-bleed days 4-14 and peaks around days 7-10 — watch for new focal deficits.
Symptomatic vasospasm is treated with induced hypertension and maintained euvolemia; fluid restriction worsens ischemia.
Oral nimodipine improves outcomes in aneurysmal SAH despite little effect on angiographic vessel caliber.
Refractory vasospasm warrants endovascular rescue with intra-arterial vasodilators or balloon angioplasty.

cases.title: First-ever seizure in a previously healthy man
cases.chiefComplaint: His wife witnessed him suddenly stiffen and convulse at home — the first seizure of his life.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man is brought to the ED by his wife after a witnessed convulsion at home. She describes him suddenly stiffening and then having rhythmic jerking of all four limbs for about 90 seconds, with tongue-biting and brief loss of bladder control, followed by 20 minutes of confusion. This was his first-ever seizure. Over the preceding 5 weeks she had noticed him becoming more withdrawn, with intermittent word-finding difficulty and two morning headaches that improved through the day. He denies fever, recent head trauma, or illicit drug use. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social history: 30-pack-year smoker, drinks two beers on weekends, works as an accountant. Family history: father with coronary disease; no known cancers or seizure disorders. He has had no weight loss, night sweats, or known malignancy.
cases.exam: Postictally the patient is drowsy but rousable and gradually re-orienting. He has a mild expressive aphasia with occasional paraphasic errors. There is a subtle right pronator drift and a mildly flattened right nasolabial fold; strength is otherwise 5/5 and sensation is intact. Reflexes are symmetric, and the right plantar response is equivocal. He has bitten the lateral tongue. Pupils are equal and reactive, extraocular movements are full, and there is no neck stiffness. Fundoscopy shows no frank papilledema. The remainder of the general exam, including the cardiopulmonary exam, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After the postictal period and stabilization, what is the most appropriate initial imaging in the ED?
options: Non-contrast CT of the head
Carotid ultrasound
EEG before any imaging
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: A first-time adult seizure with focal features (aphasia, right drift) demands urgent neuroimaging, and a non-contrast head CT is the fast first study in the ED to screen for hemorrhage, a mass, midline shift, or herniation. An EEG helps characterize seizures later but does not emergently reveal a structural cause. A lumbar puncture is not indicated without signs of meningitis and is risky if there is a mass or raised ICP. Carotid ultrasound evaluates extracranial stenosis and is irrelevant here. MRI is the definitive characterizing study, but CT comes first in the acute setting.

stages.label: CT HEAD
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: ill-defined hypodense mass centered in the corpus callosum extending into both frontal lobes
Surrounding vasogenic edema with mild local mass effect and early effacement of the frontal horns
No acute hemorrhage; no midline shift beyond 3 mm

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best next imaging study to characterize the lesion?
options: Repeat CT in 6 weeks
MRI brain with and without contrast
Whole-body PET scan
Cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: MRI with and without gadolinium best characterizes a brain tumor, defining the enhancement pattern, necrosis, and degree of infiltration, and clarifying the relationship to eloquent structures. A heterogeneously enhancing 'butterfly' lesion crossing the corpus callosum suggests a high-grade glioma (glioblastoma) or CNS lymphoma. Repeating the CT weeks later dangerously delays diagnosis of an aggressive lesion. A whole-body PET for staging is premature before tissue is obtained. Cerebral angiography is not needed to characterize this mass. MRI is the pivotal study that frames the surgical decision.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain: heterogeneously, thick irregular ring-enhancing mass crossing the genu and body of the corpus callosum into both frontal lobes ('butterfly' pattern)
Central necrosis with surrounding T2/FLAIR vasogenic edema
No restricted diffusion in the bulk of the lesion; involves but does not respect midline
MR spectroscopy: elevated choline-to-NAA ratio

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive care, what is appropriate management of his symptoms now?
options: Begin whole-brain radiation immediately
Start empiric high-dose antibiotics
Start dexamethasone for vasogenic edema and an antiseizure medication
Discharge with outpatient neurology follow-up
stages.explanation: A symptomatic tumor with vasogenic edema and a seizure is managed acutely with dexamethasone, which reduces edema and mass effect, and an antiseizure medication such as levetiracetam to prevent recurrence. Radiation and chemotherapy wait until tissue diagnosis and a treatment plan are established. Antibiotics are reserved for a suspected abscess, but the imaging favors tumor. Discharge is unsafe given the mass, edema, and recent seizure. Note that if primary CNS lymphoma is strongly suspected, steroids may transiently shrink the lesion and obscure biopsy — a nuance for the tissue-diagnosis discussion. Tissue diagnosis is the next major step.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How should the diagnosis be established and the lesion treated?
options: Stereotactic needle biopsy only, because the butterfly lesion crosses the midline
Maximal safe surgical resection when feasible, reserving biopsy for deep, eloquent, or extensively bilateral disease
Empiric chemoradiation without obtaining tissue
Observation with serial MRI
stages.explanation: For a suspected glioblastoma, maximal safe surgical resection improves survival and provides tissue for histology and molecular markers (IDH status, MGMT promoter methylation). When a tumor diffusely crosses the corpus callosum into both hemispheres or sits in deep or eloquent cortex, resection may be unsafe, and a stereotactic biopsy is chosen to confirm the diagnosis before chemoradiation. Choosing biopsy purely because the lesion crosses midline is too rigid — resectability depends on the full anatomy. Empiric chemoradiation without tissue is substandard and could mistreat lymphoma or another mimic. Observation is inappropriate for an aggressive lesion.

cases.diagnosis: Glioblastoma (IDH-wildtype, WHO grade 4 astrocytoma) presenting as a butterfly lesion crossing the corpus callosum.
pearls: A first focal seizure in an adult requires neuroimaging to exclude a structural lesion.
A butterfly, thick ring-enhancing lesion crossing the corpus callosum suggests glioblastoma or CNS lymphoma.
Maximal safe resection both improves survival and yields molecular markers in glioblastoma; deep, eloquent, or bilateral lesions are biopsied.
If lymphoma is suspected, recognize that steroids can transiently shrink the lesion and obscure the biopsy.

cases.title: Morning headaches and vomiting in a cancer patient
cases.chiefComplaint: My headaches are worst when I wake up and I keep throwing up.
cases.history: A 59-year-old woman admitted for management of stage IV lung adenocarcinoma reports two weeks of progressively worsening headaches. The headaches are dull, bifrontal, worst on waking in the early morning, and aggravated by lying flat, coughing, or bending over; they ease somewhat after she has been upright for a while. Over the past several days she has had several episodes of vomiting, sometimes without preceding nausea, and her husband notes she seems slightly clumsy with her left hand. She denies fever, neck stiffness, or seizures. Her cancer was diagnosed 8 months ago and she is receiving systemic chemotherapy. PMH: COPD and hypertension. Medications: chemotherapy per oncology, albuterol inhaler, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and ondansetron as needed. Allergies: none. Social history: 40-pack-year smoker, still smoking occasionally; lives with her husband. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is notable for mild fatigue but no focal numbness or visual loss.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented but uncomfortable from headache. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema with blurred disc margins. There is mild left upper-extremity dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing and a subtle left pronator drift, with strength 5-/5 on the left and 5/5 on the right. Sensation is intact and symmetric. Gait is mildly unsteady. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, with no facial droop and full visual fields to confrontation. The neck is supple without meningismus, and there is no fever. Cardiopulmonary exam shows distant breath sounds but no acute distress.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to evaluate her symptoms?
options: Lumbar puncture
EEG
MRI brain with and without contrast
CT of the chest
stages.explanation: New morning-predominant headaches, vomiting, and papilledema indicate raised intracranial pressure, and in a patient with known lung cancer this most likely reflects intracranial metastases. MRI of the brain with and without contrast is the most sensitive test to detect and count metastatic lesions. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated with mass effect and raised ICP because of the herniation risk, and it would not localize the problem. An EEG is not indicated without a seizure. A CT of the chest restages systemic disease but does nothing for the neurologic emergency. Contrast MRI is the right study.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain: multiple (at least 6) ring-enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction of both cerebral hemispheres and the cerebellum
Each lesion surrounded by vasogenic edema
Largest lesion 2.2 cm in the left parietal lobe with mild local mass effect
No uncal or tonsillar herniation; ventricles normal in size

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Multiple brain abscesses
Primary CNS lymphoma
Multiple brain metastases
Multiple sclerosis
stages.explanation: Multiple enhancing lesions at the gray-white junction in a patient with known lung adenocarcinoma are classic for hematogenous metastases — tumor emboli lodge where vessels narrow at the gray-white junction. Brain abscesses are possible but unlikely without fever or other infectious signs, and the oncologic context strongly favors metastases. Primary CNS lymphoma is usually solitary, periventricular, and homogeneously enhancing, typically in immunocompromised patients. Multiple sclerosis produces non-enhancing or ovoid periventricular white-matter lesions, not ring-enhancing masses with edema, and does not fit this demographic or presentation. Metastatic disease is the clear answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best initial treatment for her symptoms?
options: Dexamethasone to reduce vasogenic edema
Immediate whole-brain radiation
Therapeutic lumbar puncture
Mannitol bolus and intubation
stages.explanation: Symptomatic vasogenic edema from brain metastases is treated first with dexamethasone, which rapidly reduces edema and raised ICP and relieves headache. Whole-brain radiation or radiosurgery follows but is not the immediate symptom treatment and requires planning. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated because of the herniation risk with mass lesions. Mannitol and intubation are reserved for acute herniation or impending crisis, which she does not have (she is alert with no herniation on imaging). Steroids are the cornerstone of initial symptom control in metastatic brain disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After steroids, what is the most appropriate definitive management plan?
options: Surgical resection of all the lesions
Hospice referral without further treatment
Stereotactic radiosurgery and/or whole-brain radiation with oncology input and consideration of targeted therapy
Observation with repeat MRI in 3 months
stages.explanation: Multiple metastases are managed with radiation — stereotactic radiosurgery for a limited number or smaller lesions and whole-brain radiation for diffuse disease — coordinated with oncology. Molecular testing for EGFR or ALK alterations may enable CNS-penetrant targeted therapy. Resecting all of many lesions is not feasible; surgery is reserved for a single large symptomatic lesion or when the diagnosis is uncertain. Hospice is premature when effective treatments exist and her performance status allows them. Observation alone risks progressive neurologic decline. The plan should be multidisciplinary.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple brain metastases from lung adenocarcinoma.
pearls: Morning headaches, vomiting, and papilledema signal raised intracranial pressure — image before considering an LP.
Metastases lodge at the gray-white junction and are typically multiple with surrounding vasogenic edema.
Dexamethasone is first-line to reduce vasogenic edema and symptoms from brain metastases.
Multiple metastases are managed with SRS/WBRT and oncology input; surgery is reserved for a solitary large or diagnostically uncertain lesion.

cases.title: Dizziness, and an unexpected scan finding
cases.chiefComplaint: My doctor found something on my head scan and sent me here — I only went in for dizziness.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman presents to clinic to discuss an imaging finding. For the past few months she has had intermittent lightheadedness, especially when standing up quickly, without true spinning vertigo, hearing loss, or syncope. Her primary care physician ordered a non-contrast head CT, which incidentally revealed an extra-axial mass, and she was referred to neurosurgery. She denies headaches, seizures, focal weakness, numbness, visual changes, or personality change. She feels well otherwise and remains fully active at work and home. PMH: hypertension and hypothyroidism. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and levothyroxine 75 µg daily. Allergies: none. Social history: never-smoker, rare alcohol, works as a librarian. Family history: noncontributory, with no known brain tumors. She is anxious about the finding but has no neurologic complaints, and her lightheadedness has improved with better hydration and slower positional changes.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Mental status is normal with fluent speech and intact recall. Cranial nerves II-XII are intact, including normal visual fields to confrontation, a symmetric face, and normal hearing. Motor strength is 5/5 in all extremities with no pronator drift. Sensation is intact to light touch throughout. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with downgoing plantar responses. Coordination is normal on finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin testing. Gait, including tandem gait, is normal, and Romberg is negative. Orthostatic vitals show a mild asymptomatic drop. There are no cutaneous stigmata of neurofibromatosis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An incidental dural-based mass is seen on CT. What is the best next imaging step to characterize it?
options: MRI brain with and without contrast
Lumbar puncture
Cerebral angiography
Whole-body PET scan
stages.explanation: An extra-axial, dural-based mass on CT is best characterized with MRI of the brain with and without gadolinium, which defines enhancement, a dural tail, the brain-tumor interface, surrounding edema, and any venous sinus involvement. A lumbar puncture is irrelevant and risky for a mass lesion. Cerebral angiography is reserved for preoperative embolization planning, not for diagnosis. A PET scan is not a first-line tool here. MRI usually establishes a confident radiographic diagnosis of meningioma without the need for biopsy.

stages.label: MRI BRAIN
stages.content:
MRI brain: 2.0 cm extra-axial, dural-based mass over the right frontal convexity
Homogeneous avid contrast enhancement with a 'dural tail'
Broad dural attachment with a CSF cleft separating it from the cortex
No surrounding vasogenic edema, no mass effect, no midline shift

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Glioblastoma
Brain metastasis
Meningioma
Cerebral abscess
stages.explanation: An extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail and a CSF cleft separating it from the cortex is the classic appearance of a meningioma, which arises from arachnoid cap cells. Glioblastoma is intra-axial, heterogeneous, and infiltrative. Metastases are usually intra-axial at the gray-white junction with prominent edema. An abscess shows ring enhancement with restricted diffusion and accompanying infectious signs. The imaging features here are characteristic enough that biopsy is often unnecessary to make the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is asymptomatic with a normal exam. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate craniotomy and resection
Stereotactic radiosurgery now
Observation with serial MRI surveillance
Chemotherapy
stages.explanation: A small, asymptomatic, incidentally discovered convexity meningioma without edema is best managed with observation and serial MRI surveillance (for example at 6-12 months, then annually), because many never grow or cause symptoms. Upfront surgery is not warranted for a stable asymptomatic lesion and carries operative risk. Stereotactic radiosurgery is an option for growing or surgically difficult tumors, not first-line for a small convexity lesion that is easily resected later if needed. Chemotherapy has no role in a typical benign meningioma. Surveillance lets you act only if the lesion changes.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly prompt surgical resection?
options: Documented interval growth or new neurologic symptoms or edema
Patient age over 50
Presence of a dural tail
Homogeneous contrast enhancement
stages.explanation: Resection is indicated for a symptomatic meningioma, one causing edema or mass effect, or one with documented growth on surveillance; convexity meningiomas are surgically accessible with high cure rates after a complete (Simpson grade I) resection. Age over 50 alone does not dictate surgery. A dural tail and homogeneous enhancement are typical imaging features of meningioma, not surgical indications by themselves. Serial imaging exists precisely to detect the growth or new symptoms that change management from watchful waiting to operation.

cases.diagnosis: Incidental convexity meningioma (presumed WHO grade 1).
pearls: Meningiomas are extra-axial, dural-based, homogeneously enhancing masses with a dural tail and a CSF cleft.
Small, asymptomatic, incidentally found meningiomas without edema are observed with serial MRI.
Surgery is indicated for symptomatic tumors, mass effect or edema, or documented interval growth.
Convexity meningiomas are surgically accessible with high cure rates after complete (Simpson grade I) resection.

cases.title: Gradual hearing loss in one ear with ringing
cases.chiefComplaint: Over the last year my right ear has gotten worse and worse, and it rings all the time.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man presents with about 12 months of gradually progressive hearing difficulty in his right ear. He first noticed trouble understanding callers when holding the phone to that ear and now struggles to follow conversation in noisy restaurants. He has constant high-pitched ringing (tinnitus) in the right ear and occasional mild unsteadiness, but no episodic spinning vertigo, ear pain, drainage, or prior ear infections. Recently he reports subtle numbness over the right cheek. There is no facial weakness and no headaches. PMH: well-controlled GERD. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, social drinker, office worker, with no significant occupational noise exposure. Family history: no neurofibromatosis, no bilateral hearing loss, and no early-onset tumors, which makes a sporadic lesion more likely. He has not had prior ear surgery, and his symptoms have been steadily progressive rather than fluctuating.
cases.exam: On otoscopy the external canals and tympanic membranes are normal. The Weber test lateralizes to the left (better) ear, and the Rinne test shows air conduction greater than bone conduction bilaterally, consistent with a right sensorineural loss. Word recognition is reduced on the right. The corneal reflex is slightly diminished on the right, suggesting early trigeminal involvement. Facial strength is symmetric with no House-Brackmann deficit. Gait is steady, with mild instability on tandem walking; finger-to-nose testing is normal. There is no nystagmus at rest. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test for asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss?
options: Pure-tone audiometry
CT of the temporal bones without contrast
Tympanometry only
Electronystagmography
stages.explanation: Unilateral or asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss should first be documented and characterized with pure-tone audiometry; asymmetric loss with word recognition out of proportion to the pure-tone thresholds suggests retrocochlear pathology. A CT of the temporal bone is better for bony or middle-ear disease, not for soft-tissue lesions of the cerebellopontine angle. Tympanometry assesses middle-ear function, which is normal here. Electronystagmography evaluates vestibular function but is not the first diagnostic step for hearing loss. The audiogram guides the decision to obtain an MRI.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Audiometry confirms asymmetric right sensorineural loss with reduced word recognition. What is the best next step?
options: Reassure and repeat the audiogram in 1 year
Trial of a hearing aid only
MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium
CT of the head without contrast
stages.explanation: Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss with poor speech discrimination is retrocochlear until proven otherwise, and MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium is the gold standard for detecting a vestibular schwannoma, even a small intracanalicular one. Reassurance with a repeat audiogram delays diagnosis. A hearing aid may improve function but does not address the underlying cause. A non-contrast CT misses small intracanalicular tumors. Contrast-enhanced MRI is the definitive study to evaluate the cerebellopontine angle and canal.

stages.label: MRI IAC
stages.content:
MRI of the internal auditory canals with gadolinium: 1.6 cm avidly enhancing mass at the right cerebellopontine angle
Extends into and widens the right internal auditory canal ('ice cream cone' shape)
Arises from the vestibular portion of cranial nerve VIII
No brainstem compression; the fourth ventricle remains patent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Meningioma of the cerebellopontine angle
Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma)
Epidermoid cyst
Facial nerve hemangioma
stages.explanation: An enhancing cerebellopontine angle mass that extends into and widens the internal auditory canal in an 'ice cream cone' shape, centered on cranial nerve VIII with progressive unilateral hearing loss and tinnitus, is a vestibular schwannoma. A CPA meningioma is dural-based with a dural tail, forms an obtuse angle with the petrous bone, and tends not to widen the canal. An epidermoid cyst is non-enhancing with restricted diffusion. Facial nerve lesions follow cranial nerve VII. A sporadic unilateral schwannoma is most common, whereas bilateral tumors suggest neurofibromatosis type 2.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 1.6 cm tumor with serviceable hearing and no brainstem compression, which is a reasonable management option?
options: Immediate whole-brain radiation
Chemotherapy
Cochlear implant
Observation with serial MRI, stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection depending on size, hearing, and patient preference
stages.explanation: Vestibular schwannoma management is individualized. Small, slow-growing tumors can be observed with serial MRI; stereotactic radiosurgery (such as Gamma Knife) controls growth for small-to-medium tumors; and microsurgery is preferred for larger tumors or those causing brainstem compression. The choice weighs tumor size, growth rate, hearing status, age, and patient preference. Whole-brain radiation and chemotherapy have no role in this benign tumor. A cochlear implant addresses hearing but does not treat the mass. Tailoring therapy to the individual is the standard of care.

cases.diagnosis: Sporadic vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) of the right cerebellopontine angle.
pearls: Asymmetric sensorineural hearing loss with poor word recognition is retrocochlear until proven otherwise — get an MRI of the IACs with contrast.
A CPA mass extending into and widening the internal auditory canal ('ice cream cone') is a vestibular schwannoma.
Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic for neurofibromatosis type 2; unilateral lesions are usually sporadic.
Management options include observation, stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgery based on size, growth, and hearing.

cases.title: Bumping into things on the sides
cases.chiefComplaint: My eye doctor said I'm losing my side vision — I keep clipping door frames and missing cars.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man is referred after his optometrist detected peripheral vision loss during a routine exam for new glasses. Over the past several months he has been bumping into door frames, missing cars merging from the sides while driving, and knocking over objects at the edges of his vision. He also reports mild, dull headaches and, on questioning, decreased energy, reduced libido, and erectile difficulty over the past year. He denies galactorrhea, changes in hand or shoe size, easy bruising, or weight change. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, works as an engineer. Family history: noncontributory, with no endocrine tumors. He has had no episodes of severe sudden headache, no vision loss with eye pain, and no collapse. His visual symptoms have been slowly progressive rather than sudden.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing without acromegalic or cushingoid features. Confrontation visual field testing reveals a bitemporal hemianopsia, with loss of the temporal fields in both eyes; central acuity is grossly preserved. Pupils are equal and reactive without a relative afferent pupillary defect; fundoscopy shows no papilledema, though early temporal disc pallor is questioned. Extraocular movements are full without diplopia, and the remaining cranial nerves are intact. Motor, sensory, and coordination exams are normal. There is no galactorrhea on examination. The thyroid is normal, and there are no skin changes of hormonal excess.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best next step to evaluate the bitemporal field defect?
options: Prescribe new corrective glasses
MRI of the sella/pituitary with and without contrast plus formal visual field testing
CT of the head without contrast
Carotid Doppler ultrasound
stages.explanation: A bitemporal hemianopsia localizes to the optic chiasm, and the classic cause is a sellar mass compressing the decussating nasal fibers. MRI of the sella with and without contrast best demonstrates pituitary anatomy and the mass's relation to the chiasm, and formal perimetry quantifies and tracks the deficit. New glasses cannot correct a neurologic field cut. A non-contrast CT poorly visualizes the chiasm and pituitary. A carotid Doppler is unrelated to this localization. Dedicated sellar imaging plus perimetry defines both the lesion and its visual impact.

stages.label: MRI & LABS
stages.content:
MRI sella: 2.4 cm sellar mass with suprasellar extension elevating and compressing the optic chiasm
No cavernous sinus invasion; pituitary stalk displaced
Formal perimetry: bitemporal hemianopsia
Prolactin 28 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
Morning cortisol 14 µg/dL; TSH 1.6 mIU/L, free T4 normal
Total testosterone low; IGF-1 normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Prolactinoma
Craniopharyngioma
Nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma
Tuberculum sellae meningioma
stages.explanation: A sellar macroadenoma (over 1 cm) with suprasellar extension compressing the chiasm and only a mildly elevated prolactin is a nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma; the mild prolactin rise reflects 'stalk effect' rather than tumor secretion. A true prolactinoma of this size would typically produce prolactin well above 150-200 ng/mL. A craniopharyngioma is usually suprasellar, often calcified and cystic, with bimodal age peaks. A tuberculum sellae meningioma is dural-based, but here the epicenter is the sella with stalk displacement, favoring an adenoma. The low testosterone reflects compressive hypopituitarism.

stages.label: ENDOCRINE WORKUP
stages.question: Why is checking the prolactin level critical before planning surgery?
options: To distinguish a prolactinoma (treated medically) from a nonfunctioning adenoma
Because prolactin itself causes the visual field defect
To diagnose acromegaly
Because prolactin predicts surgical bleeding risk
stages.explanation: A true prolactinoma — even a large one — is treated first with dopamine agonists such as cabergoline, which shrink the tumor and can relieve chiasmal compression without an operation. A markedly elevated prolactin (often above 200 ng/mL) signals a prolactinoma, whereas a mild elevation reflects stalk effect from a nonfunctioning adenoma that needs surgery. Distinguishing the two completely changes management. Prolactin does not directly cause the field defect (the mass does), does not diagnose acromegaly (that requires IGF-1 and growth hormone), and does not predict surgical bleeding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given chiasmal compression and visual loss from a nonfunctioning macroadenoma, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Dopamine agonist therapy
Transsphenoidal surgical resection
Observation with repeat MRI in 1 year
Whole-sella radiation as first-line therapy
stages.explanation: A nonfunctioning macroadenoma causing chiasmal compression with visual field loss is treated with transsphenoidal resection to decompress the optic apparatus; vision often improves, particularly when the symptom duration is short. Dopamine agonists treat prolactinomas, not nonfunctioning tumors. Observation is appropriate only for small asymptomatic incidentalomas without chiasm contact, not for progressive visual loss. Radiation is an adjuvant for residual, recurrent, or unresectable disease, not first-line when surgery can rapidly relieve compression. Pituitary hormone deficits should also be assessed and replaced perioperatively.

cases.diagnosis: Nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma with chiasmal compression.
pearls: Bitemporal hemianopsia localizes to the optic chiasm and is classically caused by a pituitary macroadenoma compressing the decussating nasal fibers.
Always measure prolactin in a sellar mass: a prolactinoma is treated medically, whereas a nonfunctioning adenoma is operated.
Mildly elevated prolactin (stalk effect) suggests a nonfunctioning adenoma; levels above 150-200 ng/mL suggest a prolactinoma.
A nonfunctioning macroadenoma with chiasmal compression and visual loss is treated with transsphenoidal resection.

cases.title: Missed periods and milky breast discharge
cases.chiefComplaint: I haven't had a period in five months and my breasts leak a milky fluid, but I'm not pregnant.
cases.history: A 28-year-old woman presents with 5 months of absent menstrual periods and bilateral milky nipple discharge. Her cycles were previously regular. She is sexually active using condoms and has a negative home pregnancy test. She also reports decreased libido and occasional mild headaches but no visual changes. She denies recent pregnancy or breastfeeding, and she is not taking antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or other dopamine-blocking medications. She has no symptoms of hypothyroidism such as cold intolerance or weight gain. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none; no herbal supplements. Allergies: none. Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, graduate student, with no recent major stressors or excessive exercise. Family history: noncontributory. She is concerned about fertility, as she and her partner hope to conceive within a year. The galactorrhea is spontaneous and occurs from both breasts.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Expressible white discharge is present from both nipples, with no breast mass, skin changes, or lymphadenopathy. Visual fields are full to confrontation, consistent with a small lesion not reaching the optic chiasm. Pupils are equal and reactive, extraocular movements are full, and fundoscopy is normal. The thyroid is non-enlarged without nodules, and there are no signs of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. Cranial nerves are intact and the remaining neurologic exam is normal. There are no acromegalic or cushingoid features and no hirsutism or acne.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After a negative pregnancy test, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Serum prolactin and TSH, and review of her medications
Start combined oral contraceptives
MRI of the brain immediately
Refer for endometrial biopsy
stages.explanation: Amenorrhea with galactorrhea and a negative pregnancy test should prompt measurement of prolactin and TSH and a review of her medications, because hyperprolactinemia and hypothyroidism are leading causes; in primary hypothyroidism, elevated TRH stimulates prolactin release. Starting combined oral contraceptives would mask the problem without a diagnosis. MRI is appropriate only after biochemical confirmation of hyperprolactinemia. An endometrial biopsy is for abnormal uterine bleeding in older women, not this presentation. Establishing the biochemical cause directs everything that follows.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Serum prolactin 182 ng/mL (normal <25)
TSH 2.0 mIU/L, free T4 normal
β-hCG: negative
Renal and hepatic function normal
Not taking antipsychotics, metoclopramide, or other dopamine antagonists

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the best next step?
options: Repeat the prolactin in 6 months
Start cabergoline empirically without imaging
MRI of the pituitary/sella with contrast
Measure IGF-1
stages.explanation: Confirmed marked hyperprolactinemia — a level over roughly 100-150 ng/mL strongly suggests a prolactin-secreting tumor — warrants MRI of the sella with contrast to identify and size an adenoma as micro- or macroadenoma. Repeating the prolactin in 6 months needlessly delays diagnosis. Treating before imaging forfeits the baseline tumor size and could miss a macroadenoma that needs closer monitoring of the optic chiasm. IGF-1 screens for acromegaly, which is not suggested here. Imaging defines the lesion before therapy begins.

stages.label: MRI SELLA
stages.content:
MRI sella with contrast: 7 mm intrasellar enhancing lesion (microadenoma)
No suprasellar extension; optic chiasm not compressed
Normal pituitary stalk position
No cavernous sinus involvement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma
Prolactinoma (prolactin-secreting microadenoma)
Physiologic hyperprolactinemia of pregnancy
Primary hypothyroidism
stages.explanation: Marked hyperprolactinemia, where the level roughly correlates with tumor size, together with a pituitary microadenoma and classic galactorrhea and amenorrhea, is a prolactinoma (a lactotroph adenoma). A nonfunctioning adenoma causes only mild prolactin elevation through stalk effect, not a level of 182 ng/mL with a discrete enhancing lesion. Pregnancy is excluded by the negative β-hCG. Normal TSH and free T4 exclude hypothyroidism. The biochemical and radiographic findings match, confirming a prolactin-secreting tumor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment?
options: Transsphenoidal surgical resection
Radiation therapy
Dopamine agonist therapy (cabergoline)
Observation only
stages.explanation: Prolactinomas are uniquely medically treated: dopamine agonists, with cabergoline preferred over bromocriptine, lower prolactin, restore menses and fertility, and shrink the tumor — including most macroadenomas — by acting on D2 receptors on lactotrophs. Because medical therapy works so well, surgery is reserved for dopamine-agonist intolerance or resistance, apoplexy, or progressive visual loss despite treatment. Radiation is a third-line option. Observation alone leaves symptoms such as infertility and estrogen-deficiency bone loss untreated. Since cabergoline can restore fertility, counsel her on contraception and pregnancy planning.

cases.diagnosis: Prolactinoma (prolactin-secreting pituitary microadenoma).
pearls: Work up amenorrhea plus galactorrhea by first excluding pregnancy, then checking prolactin and TSH and reviewing medications.
Marked hyperprolactinemia roughly correlates with tumor size; levels above 100-150 ng/mL strongly suggest a prolactinoma.
Prolactinomas — even macroadenomas — are treated first with dopamine agonists (cabergoline), not surgery.
Surgery is reserved for dopamine-agonist resistance or intolerance, apoplexy, or progressive visual loss.

cases.title: Headache and vomiting in a boy with a shunt
cases.chiefComplaint: Parents report 2 days of headache and vomiting, and today he is very sleepy with his eyes 'looking down.'
cases.history: A 7-year-old boy with congenital hydrocephalus and a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt placed in infancy is brought to the ED by his parents for 2 days of worsening headache and repeated vomiting. He has been increasingly sleepy today and harder to wake from naps, and his parents noticed that his eyes seem to be 'looking down.' His last shunt revision was at age 4. There has been no fever, neck stiffness, recent illness, abdominal pain, or trauma. He is up to date on vaccinations. PMH: prematurity with post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: lives with both parents, attends second grade, developmentally appropriate at baseline. Family history: noncontributory. His parents say these symptoms feel similar to a prior shunt malfunction he had as a toddler that required a revision. He has had no recent seizures.
cases.exam: The child is lethargic but arousable to voice, irritable when stimulated, and prefers to keep his eyes closed. There is bilateral upward-gaze paresis with both eyes deviated downward (the 'sunsetting' sign), and fundoscopy reveals early papilledema. The shunt reservoir behind the right ear pumps sluggishly and refills slowly. The shunt tubing tract is palpable along the neck and anterior chest. There is no neck stiffness and no fever. Pupils are equal and reactive. Tone and strength are grossly symmetric, and he moves all four limbs. No focal motor deficit is seen, but he is too drowsy for detailed coordination testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Lumbar puncture
EEG
Non-contrast CT head (or rapid-sequence MRI) and a shunt series
Reassure and observe
stages.explanation: A shunted child with headache, vomiting, lethargy, and sunsetting eyes has raised intracranial pressure from shunt malfunction until proven otherwise. The workup is to image the ventricles — a non-contrast CT or a low-dose rapid-sequence MRI to limit radiation — compared with the child's baseline, plus a shunt series (skull, neck, chest, and abdominal radiographs) to look for disconnection, fracture, or migration. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated in obstructive hydrocephalus because of the herniation risk and does not assess the shunt. An EEG is irrelevant. Observation alone risks rapid deterioration.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: ventricles markedly enlarged compared with the baseline scan from 1 year ago
Effacement of cortical sulci with transependymal CSF flow
Shunt series: discontinuity of the catheter at the level of the clavicle
Temp 37.0 °C, no fever
WBC 8.5 ×10³/µL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Shunt infection (ventriculitis)
Migraine
Mechanical VP shunt malfunction (obstruction/disconnection)
Viral gastroenteritis
stages.explanation: Ventricles that are enlarged compared with baseline plus a catheter discontinuity on the shunt series, in a child with signs of raised ICP, confirm mechanical shunt failure — the disconnection here interrupts CSF diversion and causes acute hydrocephalus. The absence of fever and a normal white count argue against shunt infection, though the two can coexist. Migraine and gastroenteritis do not enlarge the ventricles or cause sunsetting eyes; the vomiting here is from raised ICP. Sunsetting eyes (Parinaud sign) reflect dorsal midbrain compression by the dilated third ventricle.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Discharge with antiemetics
Start IV antibiotics and discharge
Outpatient MRI in 1 week
Urgent neurosurgical shunt revision; a shunt tap can assess function and ICP and temporarily relieve pressure
stages.explanation: A confirmed shunt malfunction with raised ICP is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent revision of the failed system. A shunt tap — a sterile needle into the reservoir — can measure the opening pressure, assess proximal and distal flow, sample CSF for culture, and temporarily drain CSF to relieve pressure while the OR is arranged. Discharge with antiemetics or a delayed outpatient MRI risks herniation and death. Antibiotics alone do not relieve the obstruction, and infection is not supported here, though CSF is sampled at the tap or revision. Definitive treatment is operative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting the OR he becomes more somnolent with a newly dilated right pupil and worsening bradycardia. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Emergent CSF drainage (shunt tap or ventricular access) with immediate neurosurgical intervention
Lumbar puncture to relieve pressure
Administer a triptan
Obtain a repeat shunt series first
stages.explanation: A dilating pupil with bradycardia signals impending herniation (the Cushing response) from acute hydrocephalus, so emergent CSF diversion is needed: tapping the shunt reservoir or obtaining ventricular access buys time, alongside immediate neurosurgical intervention. A lumbar puncture is contraindicated because draining below an obstruction can drive downward herniation. A triptan is irrelevant to raised ICP. Repeating the shunt series wastes critical minutes when the cause and the need for decompression are already established. Temporize ICP with head elevation and hyperosmolar therapy en route to definitive surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt malfunction from catheter disconnection causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus.
pearls: In any shunted child with headache, vomiting, and lethargy, assume shunt malfunction with raised ICP until proven otherwise.
Work up with ventricular imaging (CT or rapid-sequence MRI) compared to baseline, plus a shunt series for disconnection or fracture.
Sunsetting eyes (Parinaud sign) reflect dorsal midbrain compression from a dilated third ventricle.
LP is contraindicated in obstructive hydrocephalus; a shunt tap can assess and temporarily relieve pressure before urgent revision.

cases.title: Fever and irritability six weeks after surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: She's been hot, cranky, and not herself since this morning.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is brought in by her parents for two days of fever and worsening fussiness. She was born at 32 weeks and developed post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus, for which a ventriculoperitoneal shunt was placed six weeks ago through a right parieto-occipital incision. Since yesterday she has been increasingly irritable, feeding poorly, and vomited twice this morning. Her parents note she is sleepier than usual and difficult to console. Mom points out a reddened, slightly swollen area over the tubing on the side of her neck. There has been no cough, rhinorrhea, diarrhea, or sick contacts at daycare. She has no headache history and no seizures. Past medical history is notable for prematurity and hydrocephalus; the shunt is her only surgery. She takes no medications and has no known drug allergies. Immunizations are up to date. She lives with both parents and attends daycare. Family history is noncontributory.
cases.exam: The child is irritable but briefly consolable in her mother's arms. She is warm and flushed. The anterior fontanelle is closed. There is erythema, mild swelling, and tenderness tracking along the subcutaneous shunt tubing over the right neck and clavicle, without frank pus. The shunt reservoir refills sluggishly when depressed. The neck is supple without true rigidity. Pupils are equal and reactive; she tracks and moves all four limbs symmetrically with no focal deficit. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness and no peritoneal signs. The skin shows no petechiae or rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After stabilization and blood cultures, what is the most appropriate test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Lumbar puncture to sample cerebrospinal fluid
Aspiration of cerebrospinal fluid from the shunt reservoir
Empiric oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up
Abdominal ultrasound looking for a pseudocyst
stages.explanation: Aspirating CSF directly from the shunt reservoir is the diagnostic test of choice for a suspected shunt infection: it samples the very fluid in contact with the colonized hardware and provides cell count, glucose, protein, Gram stain, and culture to guide therapy. A lumbar puncture is far less reliable, because CSF may not communicate freely with the shunt system, and in the setting of possible distal obstruction it carries risk; it is not the standard sampling route. Empiric oral antibiotics with discharge would undertreat a potentially life-threatening hardware infection and obscure cultures. Abdominal ultrasound can reveal a distal CSF pseudocyst and is a useful adjunct, but it neither confirms infection nor identifies the organism.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Shunt reservoir CSF: WBC 1,850/µL (88% neutrophils)
CSF glucose 28 mg/dL; CSF protein 210 mg/dL
CSF Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in clusters
Serum WBC 17.6 ×10³/µL; CRP 96 mg/L
CT head: ventricle size stable compared with prior, no new obstruction
Shunt series: hardware intact, no disconnection or migration
Blood cultures: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which organism is most likely responsible?
options: Coagulase-negative staphylococcus (Staphylococcus epidermidis)
Streptococcus pneumoniae
Escherichia coli
Cryptococcus neoformans
stages.explanation: Coagulase-negative staphylococci, chiefly Staphylococcus epidermidis, are the most common cause of CSF shunt infections, especially those presenting within weeks to a few months of surgery, because skin flora are inoculated onto the hardware at implantation and form biofilm. Staphylococcus aureus is the next most common and tends to cause more aggressive wound and tract infections. Streptococcus pneumoniae causes community-acquired meningitis but is not a typical shunt pathogen. Escherichia coli and other gram-negatives appear more often with distal bowel perforation or in neonates, not in this early postoperative skin-tracking picture. Cryptococcus causes meningitis in immunocompromised hosts and would not produce gram-positive cocci in clusters on Gram stain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Continue antibiotics through the existing shunt and observe
Treat with IV antibiotics alone, leaving the hardware in place
Remove the infected shunt, place an external ventricular drain, and start IV vancomycin
Immediately replace it with a new ventriculoperitoneal shunt in a single stage
stages.explanation: The standard of care for a confirmed shunt infection is complete removal of the colonized hardware combined with temporary CSF diversion via an external ventricular drain and culture-directed intravenous antibiotics, typically empiric vancomycin (covering methicillin-resistant staphylococci) until sensitivities return. Biofilm on retained hardware shields organisms from antibiotics, so leaving the shunt in place and infusing antibiotics through it, or giving IV antibiotics alone, reliably fails and risks ventriculitis. Single-stage replacement with a brand-new shunt during active infection has unacceptably high reinfection rates. The EVD also permits serial CSF sampling to confirm clearance and manages the underlying hydrocephalus in the interim.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: When is it most appropriate to reimplant a new permanent shunt?
options: As soon as the child becomes afebrile, regardless of cultures
Reimplantation is unnecessary; the EVD can remain permanently
After documented sterilization of CSF with repeat negative cultures plus a course of IV antibiotics
Only after a fixed 6 weeks of antibiotics in every case
stages.explanation: A new permanent shunt should be implanted only after the CSF is documented sterile, generally several consecutive negative cultures following an adequate course of IV therapy, which minimizes reinfection of fresh hardware. Reimplanting as soon as the child defervesces ignores that cultures can still be positive and is premature. A fixed 6-week course for every patient is not required; duration is tailored to organism and clearance, often roughly 7 to 14 days after cultures turn negative for coagulase-negative staph. Leaving the EVD permanently is not an option, as external drains carry their own infection risk and cannot provide durable diversion.

cases.diagnosis: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt infection (coagulase-negative staphylococcal shunt-associated ventriculitis)
pearls: Tap the shunt reservoir, not the lumbar space, to diagnose a shunt infection.
Coagulase-negative staphylococci predominate in infections within months of shunt placement.
Treat with hardware removal, an EVD, and IV vancomycin; antibiotics alone fail against biofilm.
Reimplant a new shunt only after CSF cultures are confirmed sterile.

cases.title: Unsteady shuffling walk and memory lapses
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep shuffling, and it feels like my feet are stuck to the floor.
cases.history: A 76-year-old man is brought to clinic by his daughter for roughly ten months of progressive walking difficulty. He describes his feet feeling 'glued to the floor,' takes short shuffling steps, and has had three falls in the past two months. Turning is especially hard. Over the last three months he has developed urinary urgency progressing to frank incontinence, which embarrasses him. His daughter adds that he has become forgetful and 'slower' in conversation, though he still recognizes everyone and manages basic finances with help. There is no resting tremor, no limb stiffness, and no visual hallucinations. He denies headache, recent head trauma, or prior meningitis. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He is a retired accountant, drinks alcohol rarely, and never smoked. Family history is notable for coronary disease but no Parkinson or Alzheimer disease.
cases.exam: He is alert and pleasant. Gait is wide-based and magnetic, with short shuffling steps, reduced foot clearance, diminished arm swing, and en-bloc turning; initiation is hesitant. There is no resting tremor and no cogwheel rigidity, and tone is normal. Romberg is negative. Strength is 5/5 throughout, reflexes are symmetric, and plantar responses are flexor. Cognitive testing shows psychomotor slowing with mild impairment in attention and recall; he scores 23/30 on the MoCA. Cranial nerves are intact, and there is no nystagmus. Visual fields are full. Cardiovascular and the remaining examination are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the clinical picture, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Order MRI of the brain
Begin a levodopa trial for presumed Parkinson disease
Start donepezil for Alzheimer disease
Obtain an EEG
stages.explanation: The triad of magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and cognitive slowing suggests normal pressure hydrocephalus, and MRI of the brain is the appropriate next step to document ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy and to exclude mimics such as tumor or chronic ischemia. A levodopa trial is premature, since he lacks parkinsonian rest tremor and rigidity and his gait is magnetic rather than festinating with reduced arm swing alone. Donepezil treats Alzheimer dementia, but cognition is the least prominent feature here and the diagnosis is not yet established. An EEG is not indicated in the absence of seizures or paroxysmal spells.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: ventriculomegaly with Evans index 0.36
Disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid-space hydrocephalus (DESH): tight high-convexity sulci with widened Sylvian fissures
No periventricular tumor or obstructing mass
Mild deep white-matter ischemic changes
Narrowed callosal angle (about 70°)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which symptom is most likely to improve with treatment and best predicts a good response?
options: Cognitive impairment
Urinary incontinence
Gait disturbance
All three improve equally
stages.explanation: Gait disturbance is the symptom that most reliably and most fully improves after CSF diversion and is the best predictor of shunt responsiveness; it is also typically the first symptom to appear. Dementia is the least reversible feature, especially when advanced, because it may reflect coexisting neurodegeneration. Urinary incontinence improves to an intermediate degree. The three components of the Hakim triad do not respond equally, so selecting patients largely on gait improvement is the standard approach. This is why objective gait assessment, not memory testing, anchors the decision to shunt.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to predict whether a shunt will help?
options: High-volume lumbar puncture (large-volume tap test) with gait assessment before and after
Dopamine transporter (DaT) scan
Lumbar puncture to measure CSF protein
Cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: A high-volume (large-volume) tap test, removing roughly 30 to 50 mL of CSF and reassessing timed gait, is the standard bedside predictor; objective gait improvement strongly suggests the patient will benefit from a shunt, and extended lumbar drainage can be used when the tap is equivocal. Notably, opening pressure is normal, hence 'normal pressure' hydrocephalus. A DaT scan evaluates Parkinsonian syndromes and would not guide shunting. Measuring CSF protein adds nothing diagnostic here. Cerebral angiography has no role in evaluating NPH.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After a positive tap test, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Lifelong acetazolamide
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement
Repeated serial lumbar punctures indefinitely
Physical therapy alone
stages.explanation: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt placement, typically with a programmable valve, is the definitive treatment for NPH, and a positive tap test predicts a favorable response. Acetazolamide reduces CSF production but is not effective durable therapy for NPH. Serial lumbar punctures are impractical and do not provide lasting benefit. Physical therapy is a helpful adjunct for gait and fall prevention but does not address the underlying disturbance in CSF dynamics. Shunt complications such as subdural collections, infection, and overdrainage must be weighed, but in well-selected tap-positive patients the functional benefit is substantial.

cases.diagnosis: Normal pressure hydrocephalus (idiopathic)
pearls: The Hakim triad, magnetic gait, urinary incontinence, and cognitive slowing ('wet, wobbly, wacky'), defines NPH.
Gait improves most after shunting; dementia is the least reversible feature.
A high-volume tap test (or extended lumbar drainage) predicts shunt responsiveness.
Opening pressure is normal; MRI shows ventriculomegaly out of proportion to atrophy (DESH, Evans >0.3).

cases.title: Worsening headache and arm weakness with fever
cases.chiefComplaint: My head's been killing me for a week, and now my arm is weak.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents with one week of progressively severe, constant headache and two days of weakness in his left arm. This morning his girlfriend witnessed a one-minute episode of left-sided face and arm twitching followed by confusion. He has had subjective fevers, drenching night sweats, and poor appetite. He injects heroin several times daily and shares equipment. Three weeks ago he was told at another hospital that he had a 'heart valve infection' but left against medical advice before completing treatment. He reports no recent travel, no sick contacts, and no prior seizures. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no prescription medications and has no known drug allergies. He is unhoused, smokes one pack of cigarettes daily, and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for a 5-kg weight loss and intermittent chills but negative for neck stiffness, photophobia, or visual changes.
cases.exam: He is ill-appearing and diaphoretic but oriented. There is a left pronator drift with 4/5 strength in the left upper extremity; the right side and both legs are full strength. Reflexes are mildly brisker on the left with an upgoing left plantar response. Cranial nerves are intact and there is no neck rigidity. Fundoscopy shows early papilledema. Cardiac exam reveals a soft holosystolic murmur at the apex. There are several nontender erythematous macules on the palms and soles. No actively draining skin abscesses are seen. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT head only
MRI of the brain with contrast and diffusion-weighted imaging
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
Carotid ultrasound
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain with contrast and diffusion-weighted imaging is the best study to characterize a suspected intracranial mass and, critically, to use diffusion characteristics to distinguish an abscess from a tumor. A non-contrast CT can screen quickly and detect mass effect or herniation risk, but it lacks the soft-tissue and diffusion detail needed here. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when a focal mass with edema and papilledema is suspected, because of herniation risk, and it is rarely diagnostic for a parenchymal abscess. Carotid ultrasound is irrelevant to this presentation.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: 2.8-cm right frontal ring-enhancing lesion with surrounding vasogenic edema and mass effect
DWI: marked central diffusion restriction (bright on DWI, dark on ADC)
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL; CRP 142 mg/L
Blood cultures: Staphylococcus aureus in 4/4 bottles
Transthoracic echocardiogram: 1.2-cm mitral valve vegetation with mild regurgitation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On MRI, which finding best distinguishes this lesion from a necrotic high-grade tumor?
options: Ring enhancement after contrast
Surrounding vasogenic edema
Central restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC
Mass effect with midline shift
stages.explanation: Central restricted diffusion, bright on DWI with low ADC, is the hallmark that separates a pyogenic abscess from a necrotic tumor. Abscess cavities contain viscous, cellular pus that restricts water movement, whereas the necrotic core of a high-grade tumor shows facilitated diffusion. Ring enhancement reflects a breakdown of the blood-brain barrier and occurs in both abscess and tumor, so it does not discriminate. Surrounding vasogenic edema and mass effect with midline shift likewise occur with either pathology. Diffusion imaging is therefore the key sequence when an abscess is in the differential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of this 2.8-cm lesion?
options: Empiric antibiotics alone without any procedure
Immediate high-dose corticosteroids as primary therapy
Stereotactic aspiration for culture plus prolonged IV antibiotics
Whole-brain radiation
stages.explanation: A pyogenic brain abscess larger than about 2.5 cm, or one causing a neurologic deficit and mass effect, warrants stereotactic aspiration, which is both diagnostic (yielding organism and sensitivities) and therapeutic, combined with a prolonged course of IV antibiotics, commonly 6 to 8 weeks. Antibiotics alone are reserved for small lesions under roughly 2.5 cm, early cerebritis, or surgically inaccessible sites. Corticosteroids are used only to control significant edema and mass effect, not as primary curative therapy, and may blunt enhancement. Whole-brain radiation treats malignancy, not infection.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What additional management is essential given the echocardiogram findings?
options: Start anticoagulation for the vegetation
Discharge on oral antibiotics once afebrile
Place an inferior vena cava filter
Coordinate prolonged IV antibiotics with cardiology and cardiac surgery for infective endocarditis
stages.explanation: The brain abscess is a septic embolus from Staphylococcus aureus endocarditis, so the patient needs full endocarditis management: prolonged IV antibiotics, repeat blood cultures to document clearance, and surgical evaluation of the valve, coordinated with cardiology and cardiac surgery. Anticoagulation is generally avoided in infective endocarditis, particularly with an intracranial lesion, because of the risk of hemorrhagic transformation of septic emboli. Oral antibiotics with discharge would dangerously undertreat both the abscess and the endocarditis. An IVC filter addresses lower-extremity venous thromboembolism and is irrelevant here.

cases.diagnosis: Pyogenic brain abscess from septic embolization in Staphylococcus aureus infective endocarditis
pearls: Restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC distinguishes a pyogenic abscess from a necrotic tumor.
Avoid lumbar puncture when a focal ring-enhancing mass with edema is present, given herniation risk.
Abscesses larger than ~2.5 cm or causing a deficit need stereotactic aspiration plus weeks of IV antibiotics.
In an IV drug user, a brain abscess is a septic embolus until endocarditis is excluded.

cases.title: Fever and worsening back pain with leg weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: My back is on fire and my legs are giving out.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with five days of worsening mid-back pain that has become severe and constant, unrelieved by acetaminophen and worse at night. Since yesterday his legs feel heavy and he has stumbled twice; this morning he could not climb his porch steps. He also reports difficulty starting his urinary stream. He has had subjective fevers and chills for several days. He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and injects methamphetamine and heroin. He recalls a 'boil' on his forearm two weeks ago that drained on its own. He denies recent trauma, prior back surgery, or known cancer. Past medical history includes diabetes and hypertension. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily and lisinopril, with poor adherence. He has no known drug allergies. He lives alone, smokes, and has injected drugs for years. Review of systems is positive for fever, focal back tenderness, and progressive leg weakness, but he denies saddle numbness until today.
cases.exam: He is uncomfortable and febrile. There is exquisite point tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic spinous processes. Motor exam shows 3/5 strength in both lower extremities with preserved upper-extremity strength. Lower-extremity reflexes are hyperactive with bilateral upgoing toes, and there is a T10 sensory level to pinprick. The bladder is palpable with an elevated post-void residual, consistent with retention. Anal sphincter tone is reduced. There is a healing skin lesion on the right forearm. No meningismus is present. Cardiac exam reveals no murmur. The remainder of the examination, including the upper limbs and cranial nerves, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test?
options: Plain radiographs of the thoracic spine
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies
Urgent contrast-enhanced MRI of the entire spine
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Urgent gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the entire spine is the diagnostic test of choice. It defines the extent of an epidural collection, demonstrates cord compression, and detects non-contiguous skip lesions, all essential given his progressing deficit. Plain radiographs may show vertebral changes late but miss epidural abscesses. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies have no role in an acute compressive emergency. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated: it can seed the epidural infection into the subarachnoid space, may be unrevealing, and risks neurologic worsening if there is a complete block. Imaging the whole spine, not just the symptomatic level, is critical.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 17.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
ESR 112 mm/hr; CRP 188 mg/L
Glucose 318 mg/dL; HbA1c 10.4%
MRI spine with contrast: rim-enhancing dorsal epidural collection from T8–T11 compressing the cord with cord T2 signal change
Blood cultures: methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement about the classic clinical triad of this condition is correct?
options: The complete triad is often absent, so a high index of suspicion is required
The triad of fever, back pain, and neurologic deficit is present in the majority of patients
Back pain is rarely a feature
Fever reliably distinguishes it from vertebral metastasis
stages.explanation: The classic triad of fever, focal back pain, and neurologic deficit is complete in only a minority of patients with spinal epidural abscess, so a high index of suspicion in at-risk groups, such as diabetics and IV drug users, is essential to avoid catastrophic delays. Back pain is actually the most common presenting symptom, not a rare one, and elevated inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) are a more sensitive clue than fever, which is variably present. Fever does not reliably separate infection from malignancy, since both can produce systemic symptoms; imaging and cultures make the distinction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given progressive weakness and cord compression, what is the most appropriate management?
options: IV antibiotics alone with bed rest
Outpatient oral antibiotics and a follow-up MRI in one week
Corticosteroids and physical therapy
Emergent surgical decompression and drainage plus IV antibiotics
stages.explanation: A neurologic deficit with cord compression makes this a surgical emergency: emergent decompressive laminectomy with drainage of the abscess, plus culture-directed IV antibiotics (empiric vancomycin for MRSA), offers the best chance of neurologic recovery. Every hour of delay risks permanent paralysis. Antibiotics alone are reserved for patients without a deficit, or poor surgical candidates under close monitoring with serial exams. Outpatient oral therapy is dangerous and inappropriate for cord compression. Corticosteroids and physical therapy do not treat the abscess and would delay definitive care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which organism is the most common cause, guiding empiric therapy?
options: Escherichia coli
Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA)
Streptococcus pneumoniae
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus, increasingly methicillin-resistant, causes roughly two-thirds of spinal epidural abscesses, especially with a skin or soft-tissue source and in IV drug users, which is why empiric vancomycin is standard until sensitivities return. Gram-negative rods such as Escherichia coli are more often linked to a urinary source. Pseudomonas aeruginosa has a historical association with IV drug use but is far less common than S. aureus overall. Streptococcus pneumoniae is an uncommon cause of epidural abscess. Tailoring antibiotics to blood and operative cultures is essential once data return.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal epidural abscess (T8–T11) with cord compression, MRSA
pearls: The classic triad (fever, back pain, neurologic deficit) is complete in a minority; suspect it in diabetics and IV drug users.
Urgent whole-spine contrast MRI is the diagnostic test of choice; skip lesions are common.
Neurologic deficit with cord compression mandates emergent surgical decompression plus IV antibiotics.
S. aureus (often MRSA) is the leading pathogen; start empiric vancomycin.

cases.title: Numb, tingling hands that wake her at night
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands go numb and tingly at night and I have to shake them out.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman comes to clinic with three months of numbness and tingling in both hands, worse on the right. The sensations involve the thumb, index, and middle fingers and wake her several times a night; she shakes her hands out to relieve them (the 'flick sign'). Symptoms also appear while driving and holding her phone. She has begun dropping coffee mugs and finds buttons difficult. She works as a data-entry clerk and crochets daily. She denies neck pain, arm radiation, or symptoms in the little finger. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism and prediabetes. Medications are levothyroxine 75 µg daily. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol socially. Family history includes a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. Review of systems is otherwise negative; specifically she reports no fever, weight change, or bowel or bladder symptoms, and no weakness proximal to the hand.
cases.exam: Sensation to light touch is diminished over the palmar surfaces of the right thumb, index, and middle fingers and the radial half of the ring finger; importantly, sensation over the thenar eminence skin is preserved. There is mild thenar atrophy on the right with 4+/5 thumb abduction; the remaining hand muscles are strong. The Phalen maneuver reproduces paresthesias within 40 seconds, and Tinel sign over the volar wrist is positive. Two-point discrimination is mildly widened at the index fingertip. The neck has full range of motion without radicular reproduction, and the Spurling test is negative. Reflexes and the contralateral examination are normal.

stages.label: ANATOMY
stages.question: Why is sensation over the thenar eminence skin preserved despite median nerve compression?
options: The thenar skin is supplied by the ulnar nerve
The palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the carpal tunnel and bypasses it
The thenar skin has no cutaneous innervation
Compression spares only motor fibers
stages.explanation: The palmar cutaneous branch of the median nerve arises about 5 cm proximal to the wrist and runs superficial to the flexor retinaculum, so it is not compressed within the carpal tunnel. This is why sensation over the thenar eminence skin is preserved, a classic feature distinguishing carpal tunnel syndrome from a more proximal median nerve lesion. The thenar skin is median-innervated, not ulnar. It certainly has cutaneous innervation. Compression within the tunnel affects both sensory and motor median fibers traveling through it, so it is not motor-sparing; the preserved thenar sensation is purely an anatomical bypass.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis and grade severity?
options: MRI of the cervical spine
Wrist radiographs
Rheumatoid factor and ANA testing
Nerve conduction studies and electromyography
stages.explanation: Nerve conduction studies and electromyography confirm a median neuropathy at the wrist, grade its severity (prolonged distal latency, reduced conduction velocity, and, when severe, denervation of the abductor pollicis brevis), and guide the decision to operate. The diagnosis is fundamentally clinical, but electrodiagnostics provide objective confirmation and a baseline. Cervical MRI evaluates radiculopathy, but her exam localizes to the median nerve with no neck signs. Wrist radiographs and serologies do not diagnose carpal tunnel syndrome, although they may be relevant for contributing conditions such as arthritis.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: prolonged right median distal motor latency 5.8 ms (normal <4.2 ms)
Prolonged median sensory latency with reduced amplitude across the wrist
Ulnar nerve conduction studies normal
EMG: mild chronic denervation in the abductor pollicis brevis
Impression: moderate right median neuropathy at the wrist

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For her current symptoms, what is the most appropriate initial treatment?
options: Immediate carpal tunnel release surgery
Long-term oral opioids
Nocturnal neutral wrist splinting with activity modification
Wrist arthrodesis
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for mild-to-moderate carpal tunnel syndrome is a neutral wrist splint worn at night plus ergonomic and activity modification, often combined with a corticosteroid injection; many patients improve without surgery. Immediate surgical release is not first-line for mild-to-moderate disease lacking severe signs. Opioids are inappropriate for a compressive neuropathy and do not address the mechanical problem. Wrist arthrodesis is a treatment for arthritis or instability, not carpal tunnel syndrome. If conservative measures fail or red-flag signs emerge, surgical decompression is the next step.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which finding most strongly indicates surgical carpal tunnel release?
options: Progressive thenar atrophy with denervation on EMG
Symptoms only at night
A positive Phalen test
Bilateral symptoms
stages.explanation: Thenar (abductor pollicis brevis) atrophy and electrodiagnostic evidence of denervation mark severe, advanced median neuropathy and are clear indications for surgical decompression to prevent permanent motor loss; failure of conservative therapy is another. Symptoms only at night and a positive Phalen test are typical of mild disease that is managed conservatively first. Bilateral involvement is common in carpal tunnel syndrome and is not, by itself, an indication for surgery. The key is recognizing motor compromise, which signals that the window for full recovery is closing.

cases.diagnosis: Carpal tunnel syndrome (median neuropathy at the wrist)
pearls: CTS spares thenar eminence skin because the palmar cutaneous branch arises proximal to the tunnel.
Diagnosis is clinical (nocturnal paresthesias, Phalen, Tinel); NCS/EMG grades severity.
Start with nocturnal neutral wrist splinting; most mild-to-moderate cases improve.
Thenar atrophy or denervation on EMG mandates surgical release to avoid permanent deficit.

cases.title: Tingling little finger and a weakening grip
cases.chiefComplaint: My ring and pinky fingers are numb and my hand feels weak.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man reports two months of numbness and tingling along the little finger and the ulnar half of the ring finger of his right hand. The symptoms intensify when he leans on his elbows at his desk and when he holds the phone with the elbow bent for long periods, and they sometimes wake him at night. He has grown clumsy, dropping his keys and struggling to open jars, and he notices the hand 'looks different.' He works as a long-haul truck driver and rests his right elbow on the windowsill for hours. He denies neck pain, shoulder symptoms, or numbness of the thumb. He has no history of wrist trauma or repetitive gripping injury. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack daily and drinks beer on weekends. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no neck stiffness, leg weakness, or bowel or bladder changes.
cases.exam: Inspection shows mild clawing of the right ring and little fingers (hyperextension at the MCP joints with flexion at the interphalangeal joints) and visible wasting of the first dorsal interosseous and hypothenar muscles. Sensation is diminished over the little finger, the ulnar half of the ring finger, and, importantly, the dorsal ulnar aspect of the hand. Froment sign is positive: he flexes the thumb interphalangeal joint to grip paper, compensating for a weak adductor pollicis. Finger abduction and adduction are weak.
