Tinel sign is positive over the cubital tunnel behind the medial epicondyle, and sustained elbow flexion reproduces symptoms. Thenar bulk and median-innervated sensation are normal. Neck range of motion is full with a negative Spurling test.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Which examination finding best localizes the lesion to the elbow rather than the wrist (Guyon canal)?
options: Weakness of the interosseous muscles
Sensory loss over the dorsal ulnar aspect of the hand
Clawing of the ring and little fingers
A positive Froment sign
stages.explanation: The dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch leaves the ulnar nerve about 5 to 7 cm proximal to the wrist, so sensory loss over the dorsal ulnar hand implies a lesion at or above the forearm, that is, at the elbow (cubital tunnel). A lesion at the wrist in Guyon canal spares this territory because the branch has already departed. Interosseous weakness, clawing of the ring and little fingers, and a positive Froment sign all reflect ulnar motor dysfunction but occur with lesions at either the elbow or the wrist, so they do not localize the level. Dorsal hand sensation is the discriminating clue.

stages.label: EXAM SIGN
stages.question: What does the positive Froment sign demonstrate?
options: Weak adductor pollicis compensated by flexor pollicis longus (median nerve)
Weak abductor pollicis brevis compensated by the ulnar nerve
Median nerve sensory loss
Radial nerve motor weakness
stages.explanation: Froment sign reveals weakness of the adductor pollicis, an ulnar-innervated muscle. When asked to pinch a sheet of paper between thumb and index finger, the patient substitutes the flexor pollicis longus, supplied by the anterior interosseous branch of the median nerve, causing visible flexion of the thumb interphalangeal joint. It is not a test of the abductor pollicis brevis or of median sensation, and the radial nerve, which controls wrist and finger extension, is unrelated. A related concept is the 'ulnar paradox,' in which a more proximal lesion produces less clawing.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: ulnar motor conduction velocity slowed across the elbow (38 m/s) with focal conduction block
Reduced ulnar sensory nerve action potential amplitude; median studies normal
EMG: chronic denervation in the first dorsal interosseous and abductor digiti minimi
Impression: ulnar neuropathy localized to the elbow (cubital tunnel)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After electrodiagnostic confirmation, what is the most appropriate initial management for mild-to-moderate symptoms?
options: Immediate ulnar nerve transposition
Wrist splinting in a neutral position
Long-term gabapentin alone
Activity modification, elbow padding, night-time extension splinting, and avoiding prolonged elbow flexion
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome, conservative therapy is first-line: avoiding leaning on the elbow and sustained elbow flexion, using an elbow pad, and wearing a night splint that keeps the elbow extended to reduce traction and pressure on the nerve. Wrist splinting in neutral targets carpal tunnel syndrome, not the cubital tunnel. Surgery is reserved for failure of conservative measures or for severe or progressive deficits. Gabapentin may ease dysesthesias but does not relieve the underlying compression and is not adequate stand-alone therapy. Most early cases improve with behavioral and splinting measures.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which scenario best justifies surgical decompression or transposition?
options: Symptoms only when leaning on the elbow, present for two weeks
A positive Tinel sign alone
Progressive intrinsic muscle wasting and weakness despite conservative measures
Bilateral elbow discomfort without weakness
stages.explanation: Progressive motor loss, manifest as intrinsic muscle wasting and weakness, or failure of an adequate trial of conservative therapy is the clearest indication for surgery, either in situ decompression or anterior transposition of the ulnar nerve, to halt ongoing axonal loss. Brief, position-dependent symptoms and an isolated positive Tinel sign warrant conservative management first. Bilateral discomfort without any weakness is also managed conservatively. The decision to operate hinges on demonstrable, progressive motor compromise rather than sensory symptoms alone, because muscle denervation may not fully recover.

cases.diagnosis: Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow (cubital tunnel syndrome)
pearls: Dorsal ulnar hand sensory loss localizes the lesion to the elbow; the wrist (Guyon canal) spares it.
Froment sign reveals adductor pollicis weakness compensated by median-innervated flexor pollicis longus.
Ulnar paradox: a more proximal lesion causes less clawing because the FDP to the ring and little fingers is also weak.
Treat mild cubital tunnel conservatively; operate for progressive wasting or failed conservative care.

cases.title: Electric face pains triggered by chewing
cases.chiefComplaint: I get sudden electric shocks in my cheek and jaw when I chew.
cases.history: A 61-year-old woman presents with six weeks of recurrent, excruciating right facial pain. She describes sudden, electric-shock-like jolts shooting across her cheek and lower jaw, each lasting only a few seconds but recurring in clusters. Attacks are triggered by chewing, brushing her teeth, washing her face, and even a cold breeze on the right side. Between paroxysms she is pain-free. She has lost weight because she fears eating, and a dentist who found no cause extracted a healthy tooth without relief. She has no facial numbness, weakness, hearing change, or rash. Past medical history includes hypertension. Medications are amlodipine and hydrochlorothiazide. She has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for headache, visual symptoms, fever, and limb weakness; she specifically denies any constant burning pain or a prior shingles outbreak on the face.
cases.exam: She appears anxious and guards the right side of her face. Cranial nerve examination is entirely normal: facial sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact and symmetric, the corneal reflex is present bilaterally, there is no facial weakness, and masseter bulk and strength are normal. Light tactile stimulation of the right nasolabial region provokes a brief paroxysm of pain. There is no vesicular rash, no facial swelling, and no temporal artery tenderness. The remainder of the neurologic examination, including the other cranial nerves, motor, sensory, and gait testing, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
options: Reassure and treat empirically without any imaging
MRI of the brain to evaluate for vascular compression or secondary causes
Temporal artery biopsy
CT of the sinuses
stages.explanation: Although trigeminal neuralgia is a clinical diagnosis, MRI of the brain, often a dedicated high-resolution posterior fossa protocol, is recommended to identify neurovascular compression (most commonly by the superior cerebellar artery) and to exclude secondary causes such as a multiple sclerosis plaque or a cerebellopontine angle tumor. This matters especially with atypical features, younger age, bilateral symptoms, or any sensory deficit. Empiric treatment without imaging risks missing a structural lesion. Temporal artery biopsy targets giant cell arteritis, which features jaw claudication, headache, and an elevated ESR. Sinus CT addresses sinusitis, not this lancinating neuralgia.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain (high-resolution posterior fossa): a loop of the superior cerebellar artery contacts and indents the right trigeminal nerve root entry zone
No cerebellopontine angle mass
No demyelinating plaques in the brainstem or elsewhere
Impression: neurovascular compression of the right trigeminal nerve

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line pharmacologic treatment?
options: Carbamazepine
Ibuprofen
Sumatriptan
Prednisone
stages.explanation: Carbamazepine (or oxcarbazepine) is first-line and highly effective for trigeminal neuralgia, substantially reducing paroxysms; baseline and periodic monitoring of sodium and the complete blood count is advised because of hyponatremia and marrow suppression. NSAIDs such as ibuprofen are ineffective for this neuropathic pain. Triptans like sumatriptan treat migraine, not trigeminal neuralgia. Prednisone is used in giant cell arteritis and acute cluster headache, not in trigeminal neuralgia. A clear response to carbamazepine also supports the diagnosis, whereas a poor response should prompt re-evaluation for secondary causes.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If pain becomes refractory to maximally tolerated medication and MRI shows an artery looping onto the nerve, what offers the best durable relief in a fit surgical candidate?
options: Repeat dental extractions
Microvascular decompression
Indefinite opioid therapy
Botulinum toxin to the masseter
stages.explanation: Microvascular decompression, which interposes a Teflon pad between the offending vessel and the trigeminal root entry zone, provides the highest long-term pain-free rates and is preferred for healthy surgical candidates with demonstrated neurovascular conflict. Percutaneous rhizotomy and stereotactic radiosurgery are reasonable options for poor surgical candidates but are generally less durable and carry a higher rate of recurrence or sensory loss. Repeat dental extractions are futile and harmful. Chronic opioids are ineffective for trigeminal neuralgia and inappropriate. Botulinum toxin is, at best, an adjunct and not a definitive cure.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which vessel most commonly compresses the trigeminal nerve root in this condition?
options: Anterior inferior cerebellar artery
Posterior inferior cerebellar artery
Superior cerebellar artery
Basilar artery
stages.explanation: The superior cerebellar artery is the classic offending vessel compressing the trigeminal root entry zone in primary trigeminal neuralgia, and it is the structure most often addressed at microvascular decompression. The anterior inferior cerebellar artery is more often implicated in hemifacial spasm and in compression of the facial-vestibulocochlear complex. The posterior inferior cerebellar artery relates to the lower cranial nerves and lateral medulla. A dolichoectatic basilar artery can occasionally contribute, but the superior cerebellar artery is by far the most common culprit, which is why surgeons look for it first.

cases.diagnosis: Trigeminal neuralgia (V2-V3 distribution), likely neurovascular compression
pearls: Trigeminal neuralgia is paroxysmal, lancinating V2/V3 pain with trigger zones and a normal exam.
Carbamazepine is first-line; obtain MRI to exclude MS or a CPA tumor, especially if atypical or young.
The superior cerebellar artery is the usual compressive vessel at the root entry zone.
Microvascular decompression gives the most durable relief in fit surgical candidates.

cases.title: Cough-triggered headaches at the back of the head
cases.chiefComplaint: Coughing sets off a sharp headache at the back of my head.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents with eight months of headaches localized to the back of her head and upper neck. The pain is brought on abruptly by coughing, sneezing, laughing, and straining, lasting seconds to a couple of minutes before easing. Over the same period she has noticed that she frequently burns her fingertips on the stove without realizing it and has dropped hot pans, yet she can still feel light touch normally. She reports mild hand clumsiness and occasional neck stiffness. There is no loss of consciousness, visual change, or leg weakness. Past medical history is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and a daily multivitamin and has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, recent trauma, and bowel or bladder dysfunction; she notes the headaches are clearly worse with Valsalva-type maneuvers and are not throbbing or associated with photophobia or aura.
cases.exam: Mental status and cranial nerves are intact except for subtle downbeat nystagmus on lateral gaze. The hallmark finding is a dissociated, suspended sensory loss: pinprick and temperature sensation are reduced over both shoulders and the upper arms in a cape-like distribution, while light touch, vibration, and proprioception are preserved. There is mild intrinsic hand weakness with early loss of bulk, and upper-extremity reflexes are diminished. Lower-extremity strength is full with normal reflexes and downgoing toes. A mild thoracic scoliosis is noted. Gait and Romberg are normal. There is no neck rigidity or fever, and straight-leg raise is negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic study?
options: Non-contrast CT head
MRI of the brain and cervical spine
Cervical spine radiographs
Electromyography of the upper limbs
stages.explanation: MRI of the brain and cervical spine is the study of choice. It measures the degree of cerebellar tonsillar descent below the foramen magnum and detects an associated syrinx, which is the lesion responsible for the cape-like, dissociated sensory loss (disrupted crossing spinothalamic fibers in the central cord). CT poorly visualizes the craniocervical junction and the cord. Plain radiographs cannot show the tonsils or a syrinx. Electromyography might document lower motor neuron changes in the hands but neither localizes the pathology nor explains the headaches and sensory pattern. MRI ties the whole picture together.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: cerebellar tonsils extending 8 mm below the foramen magnum with a peg-like configuration
MRI cervical spine: fluid-filled syrinx spanning C3–C6
No hydrocephalus or posterior fossa mass
No abnormal enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which patient with this radiographic finding most clearly warrants posterior fossa decompression?
options: An asymptomatic person with 4-mm tonsillar ectopia found incidentally
A patient with isolated migraines and normal cord imaging
This symptomatic patient with a syrinx and progressive neurologic signs
Any patient with tonsils 3 mm below the foramen magnum
stages.explanation: Surgery is indicated for symptomatic Chiari I malformation, particularly when there is a syrinx, progressive myelopathic signs, or disabling cough-induced headaches, because decompression halts neurologic decline and usually promotes syrinx resolution. Incidental, asymptomatic tonsillar ectopia, especially less than 5 mm, is observed, not operated on. Isolated migraine with normal cord imaging is not an indication. The radiographic diagnosis itself generally requires at least 5 mm of tonsillar descent, so 3 mm is not even diagnostic. The combination of symptoms plus a syrinx is what drives the decision to operate here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard surgical procedure?
options: Ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Direct drainage of the syrinx alone (syringosubarachnoid shunt) as the first step
Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion
Suboccipital craniectomy with C1 laminectomy and duraplasty to decompress the foramen magnum
stages.explanation: Posterior fossa decompression, a suboccipital craniectomy with C1 laminectomy and usually a duraplasty to expand the cisterna magna, restores normal CSF flow at the foramen magnum and typically leads the syrinx to shrink without the cord being entered directly. Primary syrinx shunting is reserved for syrinxes that persist after adequate decompression. A ventriculoperitoneal shunt treats hydrocephalus, which is absent here. Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion addresses degenerative anterior cervical disc disease, unrelated to this craniocervical junction pathology. Decompressing the obstruction, rather than draining the cyst, is the standard first operation.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What typically happens to the syrinx after adequate decompression?
options: It usually enlarges and requires immediate shunting
It often decreases in size as normal CSF dynamics are restored
It is unaffected by decompression
It converts into hydrocephalus
stages.explanation: Restoring CSF flow across the craniocervical junction commonly allows the syrinx to collapse or shrink over the following months, with stabilization or improvement of the sensory and motor deficits; serial MRI is used to document this response. Progressive enlargement requiring secondary syrinx shunting is the exception and signals a failed or inadequate decompression. The syrinx is not unaffected, because relieving the obstruction is precisely the mechanism that resolves it. It does not transform into hydrocephalus. Patients are counseled that established deficits may not fully reverse even as the syrinx regresses.

cases.diagnosis: Chiari I malformation with cervical syringomyelia
pearls: Chiari I causes cough/Valsalva-induced occipital headaches; a syrinx produces cape-like dissociated sensory loss.
Diagnose with MRI: tonsillar descent of 5 mm or more below the foramen magnum, plus look for a syrinx.
Operate on symptomatic patients (syrinx, myelopathy, disabling headache); observe incidental ectopia.
Suboccipital decompression with duraplasty restores CSF flow and usually shrinks the syrinx.

cases.title: Daily morning headaches and graying vision
cases.chiefComplaint: My head pounds every morning and my vision keeps graying out.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with three weeks of worsening daily headache and intermittent visual disturbances. The headache is diffuse, worse in the mornings and when she lies down or bends over, and is accompanied by a whooshing sound in her ears that pulses with her heartbeat. Several times a day her vision 'grays out' in both eyes for a few seconds when she stands. Today she developed horizontal double vision. She has gained about 14 kg over the past year. She takes a combined oral contraceptive pill and recently finished a course of doxycycline for acne. She denies fever, neck stiffness, weakness, or numbness. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and she has no known drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for nausea and the transient visual obscurations described, but negative for trauma, rash, and limb weakness; she reports no prior similar headaches.
cases.exam: She is alert and in no acute distress; body habitus is obese. Fundoscopic examination reveals bilateral papilledema with blurred disc margins and engorged retinal veins. Central visual acuity is preserved, but confrontation fields show enlarged blind spots and early peripheral constriction. There is a right abduction deficit producing horizontal diplopia on right gaze, consistent with a sixth-nerve palsy; pupils are equal and reactive without an afferent defect. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. Motor, sensory, coordination, and gait testing are normal, and there is no neck stiffness. The sixth-nerve palsy is recognized as a non-localizing (false-localizing) sign of raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first step before lumbar puncture?
options: Lumbar puncture immediately without imaging
Neuroimaging, preferably MRI brain with MR venography
Empiric acetazolamide and discharge
Optic nerve sheath fenestration
stages.explanation: With papilledema and a focal sixth-nerve palsy, neuroimaging must precede lumbar puncture to exclude a mass and, crucially, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, a key mimic best evaluated with MR venography, before attributing the findings to idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Performing the LP first risks missing these diagnoses and, with an unsuspected mass, herniation. Acetazolamide is appropriate therapy but only after the diagnosis is established and dangerous causes are excluded; discharging her now would be unsafe. Optic nerve sheath fenestration is a treatment, far premature before any diagnosis or formal ophthalmologic assessment.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: normal parenchyma; empty sella, flattening of the posterior globes, and distended optic nerve sheaths
MR venography: patent dural venous sinuses (no thrombosis)
Lumbar puncture (lateral decubitus): opening pressure 32 cm H2O
CSF analysis: normal cell count, protein, and glucose

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings best satisfies the diagnostic criteria for this condition?
options: Elevated opening pressure with abnormal CSF composition
Normal opening pressure with papilledema
Elevated opening pressure (>25 cm H2O) with normal CSF, papilledema, and neuroimaging without a mass
A mass lesion on MRI with elevated opening pressure
stages.explanation: The modified Dandy criteria require signs and symptoms of raised intracranial pressure (headache, papilledema, possibly a sixth-nerve palsy), an elevated opening pressure above 25 cm H2O in adults, normal CSF composition, and neuroimaging without a mass or hydrocephalus, though secondary signs of high pressure such as an empty sella may be present, exactly this patient's profile. Abnormal CSF would suggest infection or inflammation. A normal opening pressure excludes the diagnosis. A mass lesion would indicate a secondary cause rather than idiopathic intracranial hypertension. Meeting all criteria, including a normal MRV, confirms the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Therapeutic lumbar puncture only, with no medication
Begin warfarin anticoagulation
High-dose IV antibiotics
Acetazolamide, weight-loss counseling, and discontinuation of the oral contraceptive
stages.explanation: First-line management combines the carbonic anhydrase inhibitor acetazolamide, which lowers CSF production, with weight reduction, the most important long-term driver, and removal of contributing agents such as combined oral contraceptives, tetracyclines, and excess vitamin A. Serial therapeutic lumbar punctures are not durable monotherapy. Anticoagulation treats cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, which the MR venogram excluded. Antibiotics are unnecessary because the CSF is normal and there is no infection. Close ophthalmologic monitoring of visual fields is essential throughout, because vision, not headache, determines how aggressively therapy must be escalated.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She returns one week later with rapidly worsening visual fields despite therapy. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Increase caffeine intake
Continue the current medication unchanged
Surgical intervention: optic nerve sheath fenestration (or CSF diversion) to preserve vision
Reassure and schedule routine follow-up in three months
stages.explanation: Progressive or fulminant vision loss despite maximal medical therapy is a surgical emergency. Optic nerve sheath fenestration is favored when failing vision is the primary threat, while CSF-diverting shunts (ventriculoperitoneal or lumboperitoneal) are better when intractable headache and global raised pressure dominate; either intervention can arrest visual decline. Doing nothing or scheduling routine follow-up risks permanent blindness. Increasing caffeine and leaving therapy unchanged do nothing for the deteriorating optic nerves. Urgent escalation, guided by serial perimetry and the optic nerve appearance, is what preserves sight.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri)
pearls: IIH classically affects obese women of childbearing age; OCPs, tetracyclines, and vitamin A are triggers.
Image (with MRV) before LP; papilledema with a CN VI palsy demands exclusion of venous sinus thrombosis.
Diagnosis: opening pressure >25 cm H2O with normal CSF and no mass (modified Dandy criteria).
Acetazolamide plus weight loss first; escalate to nerve sheath fenestration or shunt for failing vision.

cases.title: A gray curtain over one eye that lifted in minutes
cases.chiefComplaint: My right eye went dark like a curtain came down, then cleared up.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents to clinic after three episodes over the past ten days in which the vision in his right eye went dark, as though 'a gray curtain came down from the top.' Each episode lasted two to three minutes and then lifted completely, with no pain. He had no associated headache, weakness, or speech difficulty. He did not seek care initially, but his wife insisted after the third event. He has hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hyperlipidemia, and he has smoked a pack a day for 40 years. Medications are amlodipine, metformin, and a low-dose statin he admits to taking irregularly. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two beers most evenings. Family history includes a brother with a stroke at 70. Review of systems is otherwise negative; he specifically denies palpitations, chest pain, prior limb transient ischemic attacks, or any current visual or neurologic symptoms at the time of the visit.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and in no distress. A right carotid bruit is audible on auscultation of the neck. Cardiac rhythm is regular without murmurs, and there is no irregularity to suggest atrial fibrillation. The neurologic examination is normal: visual acuity and fields are full at present, extraocular movements are intact, there is no facial droop or dysarthria, and motor, sensory, and cerebellar testing as well as gait are normal. Dilated fundoscopy shows a bright, refractile (Hollenhorst) plaque in a branch retinal arteriole of the right eye. Peripheral pulses are intact, and there are no carotid thrills.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test to evaluate the suspected cause?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasonography
Echocardiography only
Temporal artery biopsy
24-hour Holter monitor only
stages.explanation: Transient monocular vision loss (amaurosis fugax) with an ipsilateral carotid bruit and a Hollenhorst plaque points to carotid artery disease, so carotid duplex ultrasonography is the appropriate, noninvasive first test to quantify the stenosis; it is typically confirmed with CTA or MRA before any intervention. Echocardiography and Holter monitoring evaluate cardioembolic sources such as atrial fibrillation and are complementary, but they are not the first step given a clearly lateralizing retinal event with an audible bruit. Temporal artery biopsy evaluates giant cell arteritis, which causes vision loss but with systemic symptoms, jaw claudication, and an elevated ESR.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Carotid duplex: 80% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery; 30% on the left
CTA neck: confirms high-grade right ICA stenosis with ulcerated plaque
ECG: normal sinus rhythm
LDL cholesterol 138 mg/dL
HbA1c 7.8%

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this clinical event best classified?
options: A migrainous visual aura
An ipsilateral retinal transient ischemic attack from carotid disease (symptomatic stenosis)
Central retinal vein occlusion
An asymptomatic carotid stenosis
stages.explanation: Amaurosis fugax is a retinal transient ischemic attack, and because the high-grade stenosis is on the same side as the affected eye, this is symptomatic carotid stenosis, a category with much higher stroke risk and clearer surgical benefit than asymptomatic disease. A migrainous aura typically produces positive, marching visual phenomena in younger patients. Central retinal vein occlusion causes painless vision loss but is usually persistent with characteristic fundus hemorrhages, not transient. By definition the lesion is symptomatic, not asymptomatic, which is the distinction that changes management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 80% symptomatic stenosis, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Best medical therapy alone with no revascularization
Carotid endarterectomy plus optimal medical therapy (antiplatelet and high-intensity statin)
Anticoagulation with warfarin
Observation with repeat ultrasound in one year
stages.explanation: For symptomatic stenosis of 70 to 99% (and selected 50 to 69% lesions), carotid endarterectomy added to best medical therapy markedly reduces ipsilateral stroke risk, as established by NASCET; endarterectomy is generally preferred over stenting in older patients. Best medical therapy alone is insufficient when high-grade symptomatic stenosis is surgically amenable. Anticoagulation with warfarin is for cardioembolic sources, not atherosclerotic carotid disease, for which antiplatelet therapy is used. Year-long observation dangerously delays a high-yield intervention during the period of greatest recurrent stroke risk.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the optimal timing of carotid endarterectomy after this event?
options: Within 2 weeks of the symptomatic event
Delay at least 3 months to let the plaque stabilize
Only after a completed disabling stroke
Timing does not affect outcomes
stages.explanation: The benefit of carotid endarterectomy is greatest when it is performed early, ideally within 2 weeks of the index TIA or minor stroke, because the risk of recurrent stroke is highest in that window and the number needed to treat falls sharply with early surgery. A 3-month delay is an outdated approach for symptomatic disease and forfeits much of the benefit. Waiting for a completed disabling stroke defeats the entire preventive purpose. Timing clearly matters; early revascularization in a stable, non-disabled patient is the standard of care.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic high-grade (80%) right internal carotid artery stenosis with amaurosis fugax
pearls: Amaurosis fugax is an ipsilateral retinal TIA; evaluate the carotid with duplex ultrasound.
Symptomatic carotid stenosis of 70-99% benefits from CEA plus antiplatelet and statin therapy (NASCET).
Revascularize early, ideally within 2 weeks of the event, when recurrent stroke risk peaks.
Use antiplatelets, not anticoagulation, for atherosclerotic carotid disease.

cases.title: Weak, burning hands after a forward fall
cases.chiefComplaint: I fell on my face and now my arms feel weak and my hands are burning.
cases.history: A 74-year-old retired schoolteacher is brought to the ED after tripping over a curb and falling forward onto his face. He struck his forehead on the pavement and did not lose consciousness. Immediately afterward he noticed marked weakness and clumsiness in both hands with burning, electric dysesthesias in his fingers; his legs felt comparatively strong and he was able to stand. He has long-standing cervical spondylosis with chronic neck stiffness, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, and tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He lives with his wife, drinks a glass of wine occasionally, and has never smoked. Family history is noncontributory. He denies bowel or bladder incontinence, chest pain, or headache, and reports no prior episodes of arm weakness.
cases.exam: There is a 3 cm forehead abrasion with surrounding ecchymosis. He is alert and fully oriented; speech and cranial nerves are normal. Motor testing shows symmetric upper-extremity weakness graded 3/5 proximally and 2/5 in the hand intrinsics, with relatively preserved lower-extremity strength at 4+/5. Pinprick sensation is diminished in both hands with prominent burning dysesthesia; light touch is variably preserved. Deep tendon reflexes are brisk with bilateral Hoffmann signs. Rectal tone and perianal sensation are intact, and the bulbocavernosus reflex is present. There is no midline cervical step-off, but he guards against neck movement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management in the emergency department?
options: Obtain flexion-extension cervical radiographs to assess for instability
Maintain rigid cervical immobilization and complete the ATLS primary survey
Administer a high-dose methylprednisolone bolus and infusion
Transport directly to MRI before any other evaluation
stages.explanation: In any trauma with suspected spinal cord injury, the first priorities are cervical immobilization and a systematic ATLS primary survey (airway, breathing, circulation, disability) to identify life threats; imaging follows stabilization. Flexion-extension films are dangerous and uninformative in the acute setting with a potentially unstable spine and a guarding patient. High-dose methylprednisolone is not standard of care for acute spinal cord injury and carries significant complications, so it should not be given reflexively. MRI is important but only after the patient is stabilized and immobilized; rushing to the scanner bypasses identification of concurrent injuries and risks the airway and hemodynamics.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT cervical spine: no acute fracture or dislocation; multilevel degenerative spondylosis with congenital and acquired canal stenosis maximal at C3-C6
CT head: no intracranial hemorrhage
MRI cervical spine: T2 hyperintense intramedullary signal centered at C4-C5 within the central cord; ligaments intact; no epidural hematoma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which spinal cord syndrome best explains this presentation?
options: Anterior cord syndrome
Brown-Sequard syndrome
Central cord syndrome
Complete spinal cord injury
stages.explanation: Central cord syndrome produces disproportionate upper- versus lower-extremity weakness (hands worst), variable sensory loss with burning dysesthesias, and typically follows hyperextension in a spondylotic, stenotic neck without fracture, because corticospinal fibers to the arms and hands lie more medially. Anterior cord syndrome causes loss of motor and pain/temperature below the lesion with preserved proprioception, usually from anterior spinal artery infarct, and tends to affect the legs. Brown-Sequard is a hemicord injury with ipsilateral motor/proprioception loss and contralateral pain/temperature loss; this patient is symmetric. A complete cord injury has no motor or sensory function below the level including loss of sacral sparing, but here sacral sensation and rectal tone are preserved.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate acute management?
options: Emergent surgical decompression within 6 hours is mandatory in all cases
Maintain mean arterial pressure at 85-90 mmHg, supportive care, and neurosurgical consultation for decompression timing
High-dose methylprednisolone for 48 hours
Apply a halo vest for external immobilization
stages.explanation: Central cord syndrome without bony instability is managed with hemodynamic augmentation (MAP 85-90 mmHg for about 5-7 days to optimize cord perfusion), supportive care, and neurosurgical evaluation; decompression is offered with timing individualized. A rigid 6-hour mandate overstates the evidence, and many older patients are medically stabilized first. Methylprednisolone is not standard and carries infection, GI, and wound complications. A halo vest is for unstable upper-cervical bony or ligamentous injury; this patient has T2 cord signal without fracture or instability, so external rigid fixation is inappropriate and would not address cord perfusion.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the expected pattern of neurologic recovery?
options: Lower extremities tend to recover first, with hand and fine-motor function recovering last and often incompletely
Hands recover first while the legs remain permanently weak
No meaningful recovery is expected in any region
Bladder function is the last to recover, after the hands
stages.explanation: Central cord syndrome generally carries a relatively favorable prognosis, especially in younger patients; the classic recovery sequence is legs first, then bladder, then proximal arms, with intrinsic hand function recovering last and frequently remaining impaired. Advanced age, as in this patient, predicts less complete recovery. Saying the hands recover first inverts the known pattern. Expecting no recovery is overly pessimistic for an incomplete injury with preserved sacral function. Bladder function typically returns before fine hand function, not after, so the final option misorders the expected sequence of return.

cases.diagnosis: Acute traumatic central cord syndrome
pearls: Central cord syndrome classically follows a hyperextension injury in an older patient with pre-existing cervical spondylosis, often without fracture.
Weakness is worse in the upper extremities (especially the hands) than the lower extremities, with variable sensory loss and burning dysesthesias.
Preserved sacral sensation and rectal tone distinguish an incomplete injury from a complete cord injury.
Maintain MAP 85-90 mmHg to optimize cord perfusion; recovery proceeds legs-first with hand function last.

cases.title: Motionless after a dive into shallow water
cases.chiefComplaint: He dove into the shallow end, hit the bottom, and hasn't moved his arms or legs since.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college student is admitted to the ICU after a witnessed diving accident at a pool party. He dove headfirst into the shallow end, struck the bottom with the crown of his head, and floated face-up until friends pulled him out. He has been unable to move his arms or legs since impact. Bystanders denied prolonged submersion, and he was breathing on arrival of EMS, who immobilized his cervical spine. He had been drinking alcohol. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known allergies. He does not smoke. Family history is unremarkable. In the trauma bay he was hypotensive and bradycardic with warm, dry, well-perfused extremities. There is no external hemorrhage, no abdominal distension, and no long-bone deformity. He reports he cannot feel or move his legs and has tingling in his forearms.
cases.exam: He is awake and frightened, breathing with a diaphragmatic (abdominal) pattern. Cranial nerves are intact. Motor exam shows 4/5 shoulder abduction and elbow flexion (C5) but 0/5 strength in the wrists, hands, and bilateral lower extremities. There is a sensory level at the clavicles and lateral arms with loss of pinprick and light touch below. Reflexes are absent throughout and tone is flaccid; rectal tone is absent and the bulbocavernosus reflex is not elicited. Priapism is present. The skin is warm and dry without diaphoresis. The abdomen is soft and nondistended; the pelvis is stable and the bedside FAST exam is negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this hypotensive, bradycardic trauma patient, what is the most important initial step?
options: Exclude hemorrhagic causes of shock with FAST, pelvic, and long-bone assessment while resuscitating
Immediately start a norepinephrine infusion without further workup
Administer atropine and observe
Give a 2-unit packed red blood cell transfusion empirically
stages.explanation: Even when neurogenic shock is strongly suspected (hypotension with paradoxical bradycardia and warm, perfused skin), hemorrhage remains the most immediately lethal and reversible cause of shock in trauma and must be actively excluded with FAST, pelvic, and extremity evaluation alongside resuscitation. Starting vasopressors before excluding hemorrhage can mask ongoing blood loss. Atropine alone treats the bradycardia but ignores occult bleeding and the underlying distributive process. Empiric transfusion without evidence of hemorrhage exposes the patient to unnecessary transfusion risk and does not address neurogenic vasodilation; transfusion should be guided by evidence of blood loss, which is absent here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L
FAST exam: negative; pelvis stable on imaging
CT cervical spine: C5-C6 fracture-dislocation with severe canal compromise
MRI: cord signal change and edema spanning C5-C6 with loss of normal cord architecture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the cause of his hemodynamic instability?
options: Hemorrhagic shock
Neurogenic shock
Cardiogenic shock
Septic shock
stages.explanation: Neurogenic shock results from disruption of sympathetic outflow (T1-L2) after a high cervical cord injury, producing unopposed vagal tone: hypotension with bradycardia and warm, dry, vasodilated skin. The normal hemoglobin, normal lactate, negative FAST, and stable pelvis exclude hemorrhagic shock, which classically causes tachycardia and cool, clammy skin. Cardiogenic shock would show pump failure (elevated JVP, pulmonary edema) and is unsupported. Septic shock is unlikely given the acute traumatic context, absence of fever or source, and the warm-but-bradycardic picture pointing to loss of sympathetic tone. Note that spinal shock (areflexia/flaccidity) is a distinct phenomenon from neurogenic shock.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What hemodynamic target is recommended after acute traumatic spinal cord injury?
options: Permissive hypotension with systolic BP 80-90 mmHg
MAP < 65 mmHg to limit cord edema
MAP 85-90 mmHg for the first 5-7 days
Systolic BP > 180 mmHg to maximize perfusion
stages.explanation: Guidelines recommend maintaining MAP at 85-90 mmHg for the first 5-7 days after acute spinal cord injury to optimize cord perfusion and limit secondary ischemic injury, achieved with fluids first and then vasopressors. Permissive hypotension is appropriate for uncontrolled hemorrhage but harmful to an injured cord that needs perfusion. A MAP below 65 mmHg risks cord ischemia and is below even general resuscitation targets. Driving systolic pressure above 180 mmHg offers no added benefit and increases cardiovascular complications; the goal is a defined MAP range, not extreme hypertension, balanced against the patient's overall trauma status.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He remains bradycardic to the 40s with hypotension despite adequate fluids. Which agent best addresses both problems?
options: Phenylephrine infusion
Norepinephrine (or dopamine) infusion
Vasopressin infusion
Esmolol infusion
stages.explanation: Norepinephrine provides both alpha (vasoconstriction) and beta-1 (chronotropic and inotropic) activity, making it ideal in neurogenic shock where vasodilation and bradycardia coexist; dopamine is an alternative for the same reason. Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-agonist and can reflexively worsen the bradycardia, so it is suboptimal as a single agent. Vasopressin raises vascular tone but lacks a chronotropic effect and is not first-line for neurogenic shock. Esmolol is a beta-blocker that would dangerously exacerbate both the bradycardia and hypotension. Atropine or temporary pacing may be needed for symptomatic bradycardic episodes during tracheal suctioning.

cases.diagnosis: Complete (ASIA A) cervical spinal cord injury with neurogenic shock
pearls: Neurogenic shock = hypotension + bradycardia + warm, well-perfused skin from loss of sympathetic tone in high cord injuries; hemorrhagic shock causes tachycardia and cool skin.
Always exclude hemorrhage before attributing trauma hypotension to neurogenic shock.
Target MAP 85-90 mmHg for 5-7 days to perfuse the injured cord.
Choose a vasopressor with chronotropy (norepinephrine or dopamine); avoid pure alpha-agonists that worsen bradycardia.

cases.title: Severe upper neck pain after a fall at home
cases.chiefComplaint: I fell in the kitchen and hit my chin, and now the top of my neck is killing me.
cases.history: An 81-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her daughter after a ground-level fall in her kitchen. She tripped on a rug and fell forward, striking her chin and forehead on the countertop. She had no loss of consciousness and denies weakness, numbness, or trouble breathing, but reports severe pain at the top of her neck that is worse with any movement. She has osteoporosis treated with alendronate, hypertension, atrial fibrillation managed with apixaban, and mild cognitive impairment. Additional medications include metoprolol and lisinopril. She has no known drug allergies. She lives with her daughter, does not drink or smoke, and was previously independent in her activities of daily living. Family history is notable for osteoporosis. She is anticoagulated, raising concern for intracranial hemorrhage, and her age plus the high-risk mechanism preclude clinical clearance of the cervical spine.
cases.exam: She is alert and conversant, oriented to person and place. She holds her head carefully and has marked tenderness over the high posterior cervical midline (C1-C2 region) with severe pain on attempted rotation. There is a small chin abrasion. Cranial nerves are intact. Motor strength is 5/5 in all extremities and sensation is intact throughout; reflexes are normal and there are no long-tract signs. Gait is not formally tested given immobilization. No respiratory distress is noted. There is no scalp hematoma, and the remainder of the trauma survey is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging strategy?
options: Clinically clear the cervical spine and discharge with analgesia
Non-contrast CT of the head and cervical spine
Plain cervical radiographs (AP, lateral, odontoid views)
MRI of the cervical spine as the sole study
stages.explanation: This elderly patient on anticoagulation with a high-risk mechanism cannot be clinically cleared under NEXUS or the Canadian C-spine rule (age >= 65 and a dangerous mechanism are high-risk features), so imaging is mandatory. Non-contrast CT of both the head and cervical spine evaluates for intracranial hemorrhage (she takes apixaban) and characterizes bony injury with far greater sensitivity than radiographs. Clinical clearance is unsafe here. Plain films miss many odontoid and upper-cervical fractures, especially in osteoporotic bone with degenerative overlay. MRI alone is excellent for ligaments and cord but is not the first study for acute bony trauma and is less accessible; it complements rather than replaces CT.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: no acute intracranial hemorrhage
CT cervical spine: fracture through the base of the odontoid process (dens) at its junction with the C2 body, with 4 mm posterior displacement
No other cervical fractures; diffuse osteopenia noted
CTA: no vertebral artery injury

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Anderson and D'Alonzo classification, what type of odontoid fracture is this, and why is it significant?
options: Type I - avulsion of the dens tip, generally stable
Type II - fracture through the base of the dens, with the highest nonunion rate
Type III - fracture extending into the C2 body, which usually heals well
Jefferson fracture - burst of the C1 ring
stages.explanation: A fracture through the base of the dens at its junction with the body is a Type II odontoid fracture, the most common odontoid fracture and the one with the highest nonunion rate, because the narrow fracture line crosses a watershed area with limited cancellous surface and is subject to shear. Type I is an avulsion at the dens tip (alar ligament) and is usually stable. Type III extends into the cancellous C2 body, has a broad bleeding surface, and typically heals with immobilization. A Jefferson fracture is a C1 ring burst injury at a different level entirely, not an odontoid fracture.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Considering her age and bone quality, which management option is generally LEAST appropriate?
options: Hard cervical collar with close follow-up
Posterior C1-C2 (Goel-Harms) fusion
Halo vest immobilization
Shared decision-making weighing surgical risk against nonunion
stages.explanation: Halo vest immobilization is poorly tolerated in the elderly and is associated with markedly increased morbidity and mortality (pneumonia, aspiration, pin complications, falls), making it the least appropriate option in an 81-year-old. A hard collar is reasonable, accepting a higher nonunion rate, particularly in frail patients or poor surgical candidates. Posterior C1-C2 fusion offers the best fusion rates and is increasingly favored for displaced Type II fractures in elderly patients who can tolerate surgery. Because each option carries real trade-offs, shared decision-making is essential. Note that anterior odontoid screw fixation is often contraindicated here due to osteoporosis and fracture obliquity.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which factor most increases this patient's risk of fracture nonunion?
options: Displacement greater than 5 mm, advanced age, and posterior angulation
Her use of a proton pump inhibitor
The presence of atrial fibrillation
A chin abrasion at the impact site
stages.explanation: Established risk factors for odontoid nonunion include fracture displacement greater than 5 mm, advanced age (and especially the elderly), posterior displacement or angulation, treatment delay, and the inherent biology of the Type II location. Her near-threshold displacement and age place her at substantial risk, which is precisely why immobilization-only strategies carry high nonunion rates and surgical fusion is considered. A proton pump inhibitor is unrelated to cervical fracture healing. Atrial fibrillation affects anticoagulation and perioperative risk but does not itself impair bony union. The superficial chin abrasion marks the impact mechanism, not a determinant of healing.

cases.diagnosis: Type II (Anderson-D'Alonzo) odontoid fracture
pearls: Type II odontoid fractures (through the dens base) are the most common and have the highest nonunion rate; Type III extends into the body and heals well.
Elderly patients with high-risk features cannot be clinically cleared - get a CT, and remember concurrent intracranial bleed risk if anticoagulated.
Halo immobilization is poorly tolerated and increases mortality in the elderly; posterior C1-C2 fusion or a hard collar are the practical choices.
Nonunion risk rises with displacement > 5 mm, posterior angulation, advanced age, and treatment delay.

cases.title: Deeply comatose after a highway crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Unresponsive 19-year-old, restrained driver, high-speed rollover.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after a high-speed motor vehicle rollover. He was a restrained driver; the vehicle was traveling at highway speed and rolled multiple times. At the scene his Glasgow Coma Scale was 5 (E1 V2 M2) and he was intubated for airway protection before transport. There was no hypoxia or prolonged hypotension documented by EMS. He has no significant past medical history per family, takes no regular medications, and has no known allergies. He does not use tobacco; family reports occasional alcohol use. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival a trauma evaluation excluded major extracranial hemorrhage, and an initial CT head was performed. Notably, his profound depression in consciousness appears strikingly out of proportion to a nearly normal initial head CT, prompting concern for a diffuse injury not well captured on CT.
cases.exam: Off sedation, he opens his eyes to no stimulus, is intubated (no verbal response), and shows flexion (decorticate) posturing to noxious stimulation, consistent with GCS 5. Pupils are 3 mm and equally reactive to light; corneal and gag reflexes are present. There is no pupillary asymmetry, no gaze deviation, and no focal lateralizing weakness - movements are symmetric. Tone is increased symmetrically. There is no scalp laceration or palpable skull deformity and no obvious cervical step-off in the collar. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. The remainder of the trauma exam reveals only minor abrasions without long-bone deformity or hemodynamic instability.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the profound coma with a near-normal CT, which study best explains his neurologic state?
options: Repeat non-contrast CT in 6 hours only
CT angiography of the head and neck
Continuous EEG
MRI brain with gradient-echo (GRE/SWI) and diffusion-weighted sequences
stages.explanation: The dissociation between a severely depressed GCS and a near-normal CT is the hallmark of diffuse axonal injury, and MRI with gradient-echo/susceptibility-weighted imaging (to detect microhemorrhages) plus diffusion-weighted imaging (to detect axonal edema) is far more sensitive than CT for petechial lesions at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem. A repeat CT may show evolving contusions but commonly remains unrevealing in DAI. CT angiography evaluates vascular injury or dissection, not diffuse parenchymal axonal damage. Continuous EEG helps exclude nonconvulsive seizures as a contributor but does not image the structural injury responsible for the coma; MRI is the key diagnostic test here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Initial CT head: no mass lesion, no significant midline shift; a few scattered punctate hyperdensities
MRI brain (GRE/SWI): multiple punctate foci of susceptibility (microhemorrhage) at the gray-white junctions bilaterally, the splenium of the corpus callosum, and the dorsolateral midbrain
DWI: corresponding foci of restricted diffusion
No surgical mass lesion identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the diagnosis?
options: Diffuse axonal injury
Diffuse hypoxic-ischemic (anoxic) brain injury
Nonconvulsive status epilepticus
Cerebral fat embolism syndrome
stages.explanation: The mechanism (high-speed rotational and deceleration forces), the clinical-radiographic dissociation, and the MRI pattern of microhemorrhages and restricted diffusion at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, and dorsolateral brainstem are diagnostic of diffuse axonal injury. Anoxic injury is unlikely given the documented absence of hypoxia or hypotension and tends to affect watershed zones, basal ganglia, and cortex diffusely rather than this shearing distribution. Nonconvulsive status can depress consciousness but would not produce these structural lesions and is excluded with EEG. Fat embolism typically follows long-bone fractures (absent here) and presents with hypoxia, petechiae, and altered mentation in a different imaging pattern.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for this injury?
options: Emergent surgical decompression of the lesions
Prevention of secondary brain injury with ICP monitoring and avoidance of hypoxia and hypotension
High-dose corticosteroids
Therapeutic hypothermia to 32 degrees C as standard care
stages.explanation: Diffuse axonal injury has no focal surgical target, so management centers on neurocritical care to prevent secondary insults: maintaining oxygenation and cerebral perfusion (avoiding hypoxia and hypotension), and ICP monitoring is indicated given GCS <= 8 with an abnormal scan per Brain Trauma Foundation criteria. There is no mass lesion to evacuate, so decompression is not indicated unless ICP becomes refractory. Corticosteroids are contraindicated in TBI - the CRASH trial showed increased mortality. Prophylactic deep therapeutic hypothermia has not improved outcomes as routine care and carries complications; targeted temperature management is used selectively, not as standard therapy.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: How should the team approach prognosis with the family?
options: Predict a full recovery within days given the normal CT
Declare the injury uniformly fatal
Counsel that recovery is often prolonged and variable, with brainstem and corpus callosum involvement portending worse outcomes, and avoid early definitive prognostication
Defer all discussion until the patient is extubated
stages.explanation: Diffuse axonal injury outcomes are highly variable and recovery is frequently prolonged; lesion burden and location matter, with corpus callosum and especially dorsolateral brainstem involvement associated with worse outcomes. Early definitive prognostication is discouraged because meaningful recovery can occur over weeks to months, so the team should counsel honestly about uncertainty while supporting the family. Promising full recovery in days ignores the severity implied by GCS 5 and brainstem lesions. Declaring it uniformly fatal is inaccurate. Deferring all discussion is poor communication; families need ongoing, honest updates even amid uncertainty.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse axonal injury (severe traumatic brain injury)
pearls: Suspect diffuse axonal injury when coma is far worse than a near-normal CT would predict after a high-energy rotational mechanism.
MRI with GRE/SWI and DWI reveals microhemorrhages and edema at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem.
There is no surgical fix - management is prevention of secondary injury; steroids are harmful in TBI.
Brainstem and corpus callosum involvement predict worse outcomes; avoid premature prognostication.

cases.title: Bruising behind the ear and fluid leaking from the ear
cases.chiefComplaint: I got hit in the head, and now there's clear fluid dripping out of my ear and my hearing is muffled.
cases.history: A 26-year-old man presents to the ED after being struck on the side of the head during an altercation outside a bar. He had a brief loss of consciousness and now reports a headache, muffled hearing on the right, and clear fluid trickling from his right ear. He denies neck pain, weakness, or visual changes. He had been drinking alcohol. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known drug allergies. He works in construction, smokes occasionally, and uses no other substances. Family history is noncontributory. He is hemodynamically stable and protecting his airway. Notably, the combination of post-traumatic clear otorrhea and periauricular bruising raises concern for a skull base injury, and the team must avoid maneuvers that could introduce infection or instrumentation through a basilar fracture.
cases.exam: He is alert and oriented. There is retroauricular ecchymosis over the right mastoid (Battle sign) and bilateral periorbital ecchymosis (raccoon eyes) without globe injury. Otoscopy shows blood behind an intact tympanic membrane (hemotympanum) and clear fluid in the canal; dripped fluid on gauze produces a double-ring halo sign. Hearing is decreased on the right. Facial movements are symmetric (CN VII intact) and the remaining cranial nerves are normal. There is no focal motor or sensory deficit, no neck tenderness, and no rhinorrhea. The remainder of the exam, including the trauma survey, is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best accounts for the Battle sign, raccoon eyes, hemotympanum, and clear otorrhea?
options: Isolated tympanic membrane perforation
Basilar (temporal bone) skull fracture
Frontal sinus fracture
Mandibular condyle fracture
stages.explanation: Battle sign (mastoid ecchymosis), raccoon eyes (periorbital ecchymosis), hemotympanum, and CSF otorrhea are classic external signs of a basilar skull fracture, most often involving the temporal bone; the clear otorrhea with a halo sign indicates a dural breach allowing CSF egress. An isolated tympanic membrane perforation would cause bloody otorrhea but not periorbital and mastoid ecchymoses or a CSF leak. A frontal sinus fracture more likely produces forehead injury and CSF rhinorrhea, not otorrhea with mastoid bruising. A mandibular condyle fracture causes malocclusion and jaw pain, not these cranial base findings.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head/temporal bone (bone windows): longitudinal fracture of the right temporal bone extending toward the petrous apex; small amount of pneumocephalus
No intracranial hematoma or midline shift
Fluid sample sent for beta-2 transferrin (pending)
No cervical spine fracture on CT

stages.label: PRECAUTIONS
stages.question: He becomes nauseated and the team considers gastric decompression. What is the key precaution?
options: Place a nasogastric tube as usual
Perform blind nasotracheal intubation if the airway worsens
Pack the ear canal tightly to stop the leak
Avoid nasogastric tube placement and use an orogastric tube instead
stages.explanation: With a basilar skull fracture and possible anterior skull base or cribriform involvement, blind passage of a nasogastric tube risks intracranial migration through the fracture into the brain - a catastrophic complication - so an orogastric tube is used instead. For the same reason, blind nasotracheal intubation is contraindicated; orotracheal intubation is preferred if an airway is needed. Tightly packing the ear canal is discouraged because it can obstruct drainage and promote infection or meningitis; the leak is generally left to drain freely. The safe maneuver is to avoid all nasal instrumentation and decompress the stomach via the oral route.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How should the CSF otorrhea be managed initially?
options: Immediate surgical dural repair
Conservative management with head-of-bed elevation and observation, as most leaks resolve spontaneously
Start prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics for all patients
Insert an ear wick and apply pressure to seal the leak
stages.explanation: Most traumatic CSF otorrhea resolves spontaneously within several days, so initial management is conservative: head-of-bed elevation, avoidance of straining, nose-blowing, and Valsalva, and observation, with neurosurgical follow-up. Immediate surgical repair is reserved for leaks that persist (typically beyond about 7-10 days) or are high-flow or complicated. Routine prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended - meta-analyses show no reduction in meningitis and they may select resistant organisms; vigilance for meningitis is preferred. Packing or pressure-sealing the ear is avoided because obstructing drainage can raise the risk of ascending infection.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication should the team monitor for most closely?
options: Meningitis
Deep vein thrombosis
Pneumothorax
Acute kidney injury
stages.explanation: A dural breach with CSF leak creates a direct communication between the subarachnoid space and the contaminated external and middle ear, so meningitis (often pneumococcal) is the principal infectious risk and warrants close monitoring for fever, meningismus, and altered mentation; pneumococcal vaccination is recommended. While immobilized trauma patients have some DVT risk, that is not the complication specifically driven by this injury. A pneumothorax relates to chest trauma, not a temporal bone fracture. Acute kidney injury is not a direct consequence of a basilar fracture. Delayed complications can also include facial nerve palsy and hearing loss from temporal bone involvement.

cases.diagnosis: Basilar skull fracture (temporal bone) with CSF otorrhea
pearls: Battle sign, raccoon eyes, hemotympanum, and CSF oto/rhinorrhea are the external signatures of a basilar skull fracture.
Never place a nasogastric tube (or attempt nasotracheal intubation) - it can pass intracranially through the fracture; use the oral route.
Most traumatic CSF leaks close spontaneously with conservative measures; prophylactic antibiotics are not routinely recommended.
Meningitis is the feared complication of a CSF leak - monitor closely and vaccinate against pneumococcus.

cases.title: Scalp gash and arm weakness after being struck with a tool
cases.chiefComplaint: He was hit in the head with a hammer and now his right arm is weak.
cases.history: A 31-year-old man is brought to the ED after being struck on the head with a hammer during an assault. There was a brief loss of consciousness at the scene. He now has a bleeding scalp laceration over the left side of his head and notes weakness of his right arm. He complains of headache but denies seizures, vomiting, or visual changes. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and reports no allergies. His tetanus immunization status is uncertain. He drinks alcohol socially and does not use illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He is awake and protecting his airway. The mechanism (a focal high-energy blow from a small object), an overlying open wound, and a focal neurologic deficit referable to the motor cortex raise concern for a penetrating or depressed cranial injury requiring operative evaluation.
cases.exam: He is alert and oriented (GCS 15). There is a 4 cm stellate scalp laceration over the left parietal region with a palpable bony depression and visible debris at the wound base; active bleeding is controlled with pressure. Pupils are equal and reactive. Motor exam reveals 3/5 strength in the right upper extremity with a mild right facial droop, and 4+/5 in the right lower extremity; the left side is full strength. Sensation is grossly intact. There is no neck tenderness. No CSF leak is appreciated. The remainder of the trauma survey is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT head with bone windows
Skull radiographs
MRI brain without contrast
CT angiography of the head
stages.explanation: Non-contrast CT of the head with bone windows is the study of choice: it defines the depth of bony depression, detects intracranial hemorrhage or contusion, identifies intracranial air or foreign bodies, and demonstrates the relationship of the fracture to underlying structures. Skull radiographs are largely obsolete for this purpose - they underestimate depression and miss intracranial injury. MRI is not the acute first-line study for bony trauma and is slower and less accessible, though it may be adjunctive. CT angiography is indicated if vascular injury (such as venous sinus or arterial involvement) is suspected, but it is not the initial study to characterize the fracture itself.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head (bone windows): depressed left parietal skull fracture overlying the motor strip; the inner table fragment is depressed greater than the full thickness of the adjacent skull
The fracture communicates with the overlying scalp laceration (open/compound)
Small underlying cortical contusion; no large hematoma or midline shift
Scattered intracranial air; visible debris within the wound

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given an open depressed fracture over eloquent cortex with contamination and a neurologic deficit, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Suture the scalp laceration and observe with serial exams
Operative wound debridement, elevation of the depressed fragment, and dural repair with IV antibiotics
Elective outpatient elevation in 2 weeks
Mannitol and discharge once the deficit improves
stages.explanation: An open (compound) depressed skull fracture that is depressed beyond the cranial thickness, contaminated, associated with a dural breach and intracranial air, and accompanied by a neurologic deficit warrants operative management: debridement of devitalized tissue and debris, elevation of the depressed fragment, repair of any dural laceration, and broad-spectrum IV antibiotics to reduce infection risk. Simply suturing the scalp leaves contamination and depression unaddressed, risking infection and seizures. Delaying elevation electively is inappropriate for a contaminated open fracture. Mannitol treats raised ICP but does not address the open wound or depressed bone, and discharge is unsafe.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the appropriate seizure prophylaxis strategy?
options: No prophylaxis is indicated
Lifelong antiepileptic therapy
A 7-day course of levetiracetam (or phenytoin) to reduce early post-traumatic seizures
Start phenobarbital indefinitely
stages.explanation: For severe TBI and high-risk lesions such as depressed skull fractures with cortical injury, a 7-day course of an antiepileptic (levetiracetam or phenytoin) is recommended to reduce early (within 7 days) post-traumatic seizures; prophylaxis beyond 7 days does not prevent late post-traumatic epilepsy and is not recommended. Giving no prophylaxis ignores the elevated early-seizure risk in this penetrating cortical injury. Lifelong or indefinite therapy is not supported because prolonged prophylaxis offers no benefit for late seizures and adds side effects. Phenobarbital is not preferred given its sedative and cognitive effects; levetiracetam is generally favored for tolerability.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Beyond antibiotics, which additional measure is most important in this contaminated wound?
options: Ensure tetanus immunization is up to date
Begin therapeutic anticoagulation
Administer prophylactic antifungal therapy
Start corticosteroids to reduce swelling
stages.explanation: This is a contaminated open wound of uncertain immunization status, so tetanus prophylaxis (tetanus toxoid, with immunoglobulin depending on history) is an essential adjunct alongside debridement and antibiotics. Therapeutic anticoagulation is contraindicated with an intracranial contusion and would risk hemorrhage. Prophylactic antifungals are not indicated for a typical traumatic wound; bacterial coverage is the priority. Corticosteroids are not used for traumatic brain injury - they failed to improve outcomes and increased mortality in the CRASH trial - and they would further impair wound healing. Infection (osteomyelitis, abscess, meningitis) is the chief delayed risk to prevent.

cases.diagnosis: Open (compound) depressed skull fracture over the motor cortex
pearls: Indications to elevate a depressed skull fracture: depression greater than the cranial thickness, an open/contaminated wound, dural penetration, neurologic deficit, or frontal sinus involvement.
Open (compound) depressed fractures need debridement, elevation, dural repair, and IV antibiotics.
Give a 7-day antiepileptic course to prevent early post-traumatic seizures - longer courses don't prevent late epilepsy.
Don't forget tetanus prophylaxis in contaminated cranial wounds; avoid steroids in TBI.

cases.title: Climbing pressures after a severe head injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Intubated teenager with a severe head injury and rising intracranial pressure.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy is in the ICU after sustaining a severe traumatic brain injury in a high-speed motor vehicle collision. He arrived with a GCS of 6, was intubated, and underwent CT showing diffuse cerebral edema with bilateral frontal contusions and effaced basal cisterns but no surgically evacuable hematoma. An external ventricular drain with an ICP monitor was placed. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no known allergies. He does not use tobacco, alcohol, or drugs per family. Family history is noncontributory. Over the past hour, his intracranial pressure has risen to 25-30 mmHg despite initial sedation, with corresponding decreases in cerebral perfusion pressure. The team must escalate therapy in a stepwise, tiered fashion to control the intracranial hypertension while preserving cerebral perfusion.
cases.exam: He is intubated and sedated. Off sedation he localizes minimally on the left and withdraws on the right; pupils are 4 mm and sluggishly reactive bilaterally without anisocoria. There is no spontaneous eye opening. The ICP monitor reads 28 mmHg with a calculated CPP of 58 mmHg. The head is currently turned partially to one side with the cervical collar slightly tight. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft. Core temperature is mildly elevated at 38.1 degrees C. There is no obvious posturing at rest, but ICP surges with stimulation and suctioning.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What first-tier measures should be optimized before escalating pharmacologic therapy?
options: Immediately proceed to decompressive craniectomy
Elevate the head of bed to 30 degrees, ensure midline head/neck positioning, loosen the cervical collar, and optimize sedation/analgesia
Start an aggressive hyperventilation protocol to PaCO2 25 mmHg
Induce barbiturate coma
stages.explanation: First-tier ICP control optimizes cerebral venous outflow and metabolic demand: elevate the head of bed to about 30 degrees, keep the head midline and loosen a tight cervical collar (a turned head or constrictive collar impedes jugular venous drainage and raises ICP), ensure adequate sedation and analgesia, and treat fever and seizures. Decompressive craniectomy is a late-tier salvage therapy, not a first step. Aggressive prophylactic hyperventilation to PaCO2 25 risks cerebral ischemia and is reserved as a brief temporizing measure for herniation. Barbiturate coma is a high-tier therapy with significant hemodynamic risk, used only after lesser measures fail.

stages.label: MONITORING
stages.content:
After head-of-bed elevation, neck repositioning, collar loosening, and deeper sedation: ICP remains 28-32 mmHg, CPP 55 mmHg
ABG: PaCO2 38 mmHg, PaO2 110 mmHg
Serum sodium 138 mEq/L, serum osmolality 295 mOsm/kg
Repeat CT: diffuse edema, no new surgical mass lesion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: ICP remains elevated despite first-tier measures. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Begin scheduled prophylactic hyperventilation to PaCO2 25 mmHg
Withdraw the ICP monitor and observe clinically
Start a corticosteroid infusion
Drain CSF via the EVD and administer a hyperosmolar agent (hypertonic saline or mannitol)
stages.explanation: When first-tier measures fail, the next steps are CSF drainage through the EVD (which directly lowers intracranial volume) and hyperosmolar therapy with hypertonic saline or mannitol to draw water out of the brain; these are mainstay second-tier interventions. Prophylactic deep hyperventilation risks ischemia and is only a brief bridge during herniation, not a scheduled therapy. Removing the monitor abandons the data guiding therapy during active intracranial hypertension. Corticosteroids are contraindicated in TBI (the CRASH trial showed increased mortality). Mannitol requires monitoring of serum osmolality and volume status; hypertonic saline is often preferred when hypovolemia or hypotension is a concern.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite CSF drainage and hyperosmolar therapy, ICP remains refractory at 30 mmHg. What is the appropriate escalation?
options: Discontinue all sedation to assess the exam
Advance to higher-tier therapy: consider decompressive craniectomy and/or barbiturate coma, with brief hyperventilation only as a temporizing bridge
Permit ICP up to 40 mmHg as acceptable
Administer a fluid bolus to raise ICP-driven perfusion
stages.explanation: Refractory intracranial hypertension despite optimized first- and second-tier therapy calls for higher-tier (salvage) measures: decompressive craniectomy to relieve the volume constraint and/or barbiturate (pentobarbital) coma to suppress cerebral metabolism, with short-duration hyperventilation used only as a temporizing bridge or during impending herniation. Stopping all sedation would cause ICP surges and is unsafe during a crisis. Tolerating an ICP of 40 mmHg is dangerous - sustained ICP above 22 mmHg is associated with worse outcomes and risks herniation. A nonspecific fluid bolus does not lower ICP and could worsen edema; the goal is to reduce intracranial volume and metabolic demand.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding decompressive craniectomy for refractory ICP, what does the evidence (e.g., RESCUEicp) show?
options: It reliably restores normal neurologic function in nearly all patients
It lowers ICP and reduces mortality but increases the proportion of survivors with severe disability, so a goals-of-care discussion is essential
It has no effect on intracranial pressure
It is contraindicated in all pediatric and adolescent patients
stages.explanation: Trials such as RESCUEicp demonstrate that decompressive craniectomy effectively lowers refractory ICP and reduces mortality, but a greater proportion of survivors live with severe disability or a vegetative outcome, making honest goals-of-care discussions with the family essential before proceeding. It does not restore normal function in nearly all patients - that overstates the benefit. It clearly lowers ICP by relieving the volume constraint, so claiming no effect is false. It is not categorically contraindicated in adolescents; decompressive craniectomy is used across age groups, including pediatrics, when ICP is refractory, with decisions individualized.

cases.diagnosis: Severe traumatic brain injury with refractory intracranial hypertension
pearls: Tiered ICP therapy starts simple: head of bed 30 degrees, midline neck, loosen the collar, sedation/analgesia, normothermia, and treat seizures.
Second tier: CSF drainage via EVD and hyperosmolar therapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol).
Reserve aggressive hyperventilation for brief herniation rescue; avoid steroids entirely in TBI.
Decompressive craniectomy lowers ICP and mortality but increases severe-disability survival - discuss goals of care.

cases.title: A persistent clear drip from one nostril
cases.chiefComplaint: Ever since my accident, clear watery fluid keeps dripping from my right nostril when I lean forward.
cases.history: A 40-year-old man is on the inpatient ward recovering from a motor vehicle collision four days ago that caused facial and anterior skull base fractures. He now reports persistent clear, watery drainage from his right nostril that worsens when he leans forward or strains and is accompanied by a salty taste in the back of his throat. He denies fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, or worsening headache. He has a history of well-controlled hypertension and takes amlodipine; he has no known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. He is neurologically intact and afebrile. The unilateral clear rhinorrhea following an anterior skull base fracture raises concern for a cerebrospinal fluid leak, and the team must confirm the fluid's nature before deciding between conservative management and surgical repair.
cases.exam: He is alert, oriented, and comfortable. Inspection reveals clear, thin fluid dripping from the right nostril, which increases when he leans forward (reservoir sign). When collected on tissue, the fluid spreads with a clear halo around a central blood-tinged spot. There is resolving periorbital ecchymosis. Cranial nerves, including olfaction and extraocular movements, are grossly intact, and there is no facial weakness. The neck is supple without meningismus. Neurologic exam shows full strength, intact sensation, and normal reflexes. There is no photophobia and no fever, arguing against active meningitis at this time.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most specific test to confirm that the nasal fluid is cerebrospinal fluid?
options: Bedside glucose oxidase (glucose stick) testing of the fluid
Beta-2 transferrin assay of the fluid
Gram stain and culture of the fluid
Serum sodium measurement
