stages.explanation: Beta-2 transferrin (and beta-trace protein) is found almost exclusively in CSF, perilymph, and ocular fluid, making its assay highly sensitive and specific for confirming a CSF leak; it is the gold-standard test for suspected rhinorrhea or otorrhea. Bedside glucose testing is unreliable - nasal secretions can contain glucose and contamination causes false positives and negatives - so it should not be the confirmatory test. Gram stain and culture evaluate for infection, not whether the fluid is CSF. Serum sodium reflects systemic electrolyte status and says nothing about the fluid's origin. High-resolution CT and CT cisternography help localize the defect once CSF is confirmed.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Fluid beta-2 transferrin: POSITIVE
High-resolution CT skull base: fracture through the right cribriform plate/fovea ethmoidalis with an adjacent bony defect
No expanding pneumocephalus; no collection requiring drainage
WBC 8.1 x10^3/uL; afebrile - no current evidence of meningitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beta-2 transferrin confirms a CSF leak. What is the appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate endoscopic surgical repair on day 4
Conservative management: bed rest, head-of-bed elevation, stool softeners, and avoidance of nose-blowing, straining, and Valsalva
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to drain CSF to dryness
Bilateral nasal packing to tamponade the leak
stages.explanation: Most traumatic CSF rhinorrhea closes spontaneously within about 7 days, so initial management is conservative: bed rest with head-of-bed elevation, stool softeners, and avoidance of nose-blowing, sneezing with a closed mouth, straining, and Valsalva to lower CSF pressure across the defect. Immediate surgery on day 4 is premature given the high spontaneous closure rate; repair is reserved for leaks that persist or recur. A single large-volume LP is not a treatment; if drainage is needed, a controlled lumbar drain is used, not draining to dryness. Nasal packing is discouraged because it can promote ascending infection and meningitis by obstructing flow.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Should prophylactic antibiotics be started to prevent meningitis while the leak is managed conservatively?
options: No - prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended; monitor for meningitis and ensure pneumococcal vaccination
Yes - start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for the duration of the leak
Yes - give a single prophylactic dose of vancomycin daily
Yes - start oral antibiotics only if the leak lasts more than 24 hours
stages.explanation: Evidence including Cochrane reviews does not support prophylactic antibiotics for CSF leaks - they do not reduce meningitis and may select for resistant organisms - so the recommended approach is vigilant monitoring for signs of meningitis (fever, neck stiffness, altered mentation) and ensuring pneumococcal vaccination, since Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common pathogen. Continuous broad-spectrum antibiotics for the duration of the leak is exactly the practice the evidence argues against. A daily prophylactic vancomycin dose is likewise unsupported and promotes resistance. Starting antibiotics based purely on leak duration is arbitrary; antibiotics are for treating documented infection, not prophylaxis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Under what circumstances should surgical repair be pursued?
options: Never - all traumatic CSF leaks resolve on their own
Only if the patient develops a fever, regardless of the leak
If the leak persists beyond about 7-10 days of conservative management (often with a trial of lumbar drainage), is high-flow, or is complicated by meningitis
Immediately in every patient with a confirmed leak
stages.explanation: Surgical repair (commonly endoscopic endonasal) is indicated when a leak persists despite about 7-10 days of conservative measures, sometimes after a trial of lumbar drainage, or when the leak is high-flow, associated with significant pneumocephalus, or complicated by meningitis. Saying leaks never need repair is false - a meaningful minority persist. Fever alone signals possible infection requiring evaluation but is not by itself the criterion for structural repair. Immediate repair in every patient ignores the high spontaneous closure rate and exposes many patients to unnecessary surgery; a period of conservative management is appropriate first.

cases.diagnosis: Post-traumatic cerebrospinal fluid rhinorrhea from an anterior skull base fracture
pearls: Confirm a suspected CSF leak with beta-2 transferrin (or beta-trace protein); glucose-stick testing is unreliable.
Most traumatic CSF rhinorrhea resolves within about 7 days with bed rest, head elevation, stool softeners, and Valsalva avoidance.
Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended; monitor for meningitis and vaccinate against pneumococcus.
Repair (often endoscopic) is reserved for leaks persisting beyond about 7-10 days, high-flow leaks, or those complicated by meningitis.

cases.title: An unusually sleepy, poorly feeding infant
cases.chiefComplaint: My baby has been really sleepy, throwing up, and not eating since this morning.
cases.history: A 4-month-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother's boyfriend for lethargy, repeated vomiting, and poor feeding that began this morning. The caregiver states the infant rolled off the couch onto a carpeted floor, but the reported low-height mechanism seems inconsistent with the severity of symptoms, and the account changes during questioning. There is no reported fever, cough, diarrhea, or sick contacts. The infant was born full-term without complications, has met developmental milestones, and is up to date on vaccinations. He takes no medications and has no known allergies. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. Social history is notable for multiple caregivers in the home and a prior unexplained bruise noted at a well-child visit. The combination of depressed consciousness, vomiting, and a mechanism that does not fit the clinical picture raises concern for inflicted injury.
cases.exam: The infant is lethargic with a weak cry and is difficult to arouse. The anterior fontanelle is full and tense. Pupils are equal and reactive. There is no external scalp bruising, no palpable skull deformity, and no signs of external trauma to the torso or extremities on initial inspection. Dilated funduscopic examination by ophthalmology reveals multilayered retinal hemorrhages extending to the periphery in both eyes. Tone is mildly decreased and the infant has intermittent episodes of staring. There is no fever, nuchal rigidity is difficult to assess, and the abdomen is soft. Capillary refill is brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT of the head
Skull radiograph series only
Outpatient MRI in 1 week
Cranial ultrasound only
stages.explanation: In a lethargic infant with a full fontanelle and concern for intracranial injury, an urgent non-contrast head CT is the appropriate first study to rapidly detect hemorrhage (subdural, subarachnoid), edema, mass effect, and herniation that may require emergent intervention. A skull radiograph series can show fractures and is part of the skeletal survey, but it does not evaluate the intracranial contents and cannot exclude bleeding. An outpatient MRI in a week is dangerously inappropriate for an acutely altered infant, though MRI is valuable later to date and characterize injuries. Cranial ultrasound can suggest collections but is operator-dependent and insensitive for the full extent of injury; CT is the acute test of choice.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: bilateral subdural hematomas of differing densities - a hyperdense (acute) component layered with a hypodense (chronic/subacute) component - indicating collections of different ages
No skull fracture identified on CT
Dilated eye exam: bilateral multilayered retinal hemorrhages
Coagulation studies (PT/PTT/INR), CBC, and fibrinogen: within normal limits
No metabolic abnormality identified

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical and imaging picture?
options: Benign enlargement of the subarachnoid spaces of infancy
Abusive head trauma (inflicted traumatic brain injury)
Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn from vitamin K deficiency
Bacterial meningitis
stages.explanation: Bilateral subdural hematomas of different ages, multilayered retinal hemorrhages, and depressed consciousness in an infant - with a history (a low-height fall) that does not explain the severity and that changes on questioning - are highly concerning for abusive head trauma. Benign enlargement of the subarachnoid spaces can predispose to benign subdurals but does not cause retinal hemorrhages or this acute encephalopathy. Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn is excluded by normal coagulation studies and the older age with completed vaccinations. Bacterial meningitis is unlikely given the afebrile state, normal labs, and structural findings; subdurals of differing ages plus retinal hemorrhages point to inflicted trauma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate next step in management and workup?
options: Reassure the caregiver and discharge with close follow-up
Obtain a skeletal survey, formal dilated ophthalmologic exam, and consult child protective services with a mandated report of suspected abuse
Order genetic testing for osteogenesis imperfecta before taking any other action
Repeat the CT in 48 hours and decide then
stages.explanation: Suspected abusive head trauma mandates a structured child-abuse evaluation: a skeletal survey to detect occult or healing fractures, a formal dilated retinal exam by ophthalmology to document hemorrhages, screening labs to exclude bleeding and metabolic mimics, and, critically, a report to child protective services, since clinicians are mandated reporters who must report reasonable suspicion (not proof) to protect the child and siblings. Discharging the infant risks returning him to an unsafe environment. Pursuing genetic testing first delays protection and the broader workup; mimics are screened in parallel, not as a prerequisite. Simply repeating the CT in 48 hours fails to address safety and the required multidisciplinary evaluation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding the mandated report, which statement is correct?
options: A report is required only after the diagnosis of abuse is definitively proven
Reporting requires the consent of the parent or guardian
A reasonable suspicion of abuse is sufficient and legally required to trigger a report, and the clinician is protected when reporting in good faith
Reporting should be deferred to the outpatient pediatrician
stages.explanation: Mandated reporters are legally required to report a reasonable suspicion of child abuse - definitive proof is not required, and the determination of whether abuse occurred is made by child protective services and investigators, not solely by the clinician. Good-faith reporting is legally protected. Waiting for proof endangers the child and violates reporting laws. Caregiver consent is not needed and could tip off a perpetrator or delay protection. Deferring the report to an outpatient provider is inappropriate when suspicion arises acutely; the evaluating clinician must report and ensure the child's immediate safety, including assessment of siblings in the home.

cases.diagnosis: Abusive head trauma (inflicted traumatic brain injury)
pearls: Subdural hematomas of differing ages plus multilayer retinal hemorrhages in an infant strongly suggest abusive head trauma.
A history that doesn't fit the injury severity (or that changes) is a major red flag.
Workup: head CT acutely, skeletal survey, dilated retinal exam, and labs to exclude bleeding/metabolic mimics.
Clinicians are mandated reporters - a reasonable suspicion is enough; report to CPS in good faith and protect siblings.

cases.title: A long, narrow head shape with a midline ridge
cases.chiefComplaint: His head has looked long and narrow since birth, and we can feel a ridge down the middle.
cases.history: A 5-month-old boy is brought to the outpatient clinic by his parents, who are concerned that his head has appeared long and narrow since birth and that they can feel a firm ridge running down the middle of the top of his head. They report no flattening that comes and goes, and the shape has not improved as he has grown. He was born full-term by vaginal delivery without instrumentation, and there were no perinatal complications. He is feeding well, smiling, reaching for objects, and meeting developmental milestones. He sleeps on his back per safe-sleep guidance. He takes no medications and has no allergies. Family history is unremarkable, with no known syndromes or craniofacial anomalies. The fixed, long-narrow head shape present from birth with a palpable midline ridge - rather than a positional flattening that shifts - raises concern for premature fusion of a cranial suture.
cases.exam: The infant is alert, interactive, and age-appropriate. The head is elongated front-to-back and narrow side-to-side (scaphocephaly/dolichocephaly), with frontal bossing and a prominent occiput. A firm bony ridge is palpable along the sagittal midline. The anterior fontanelle is open and soft. Head circumference is appropriate for age and tracking along its curve. There is no occipital flattening that shifts the ears or forehead into a parallelogram shape, and no neck mass or torticollis is present. Cranial nerves, tone, and primitive reflexes are normal, and the eyes show no proptosis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding best distinguishes this condition from positional plagiocephaly?
options: A palpable ridge over a suture with a long-narrow (scaphocephalic) head fixed since birth
Occipital flattening that improves with repositioning
An ear and forehead shifted forward on the flattened side, forming a parallelogram
A soft, mobile occiput that flattens only during sleep
stages.explanation: A palpable bony ridge over a suture with a fixed scaphocephalic (long, narrow) head shape present from birth indicates premature suture fusion (craniosynostosis), here of the sagittal suture - the most common single-suture synostosis. Positional (deformational) plagiocephaly instead produces a parallelogram-shaped head: unilateral occipital flattening with the ipsilateral ear and forehead pushed forward, no suture ridge, and improvement with repositioning and tummy time. The options describing flattening that improves with repositioning, a parallelogram deformity, or positional flattening during sleep all describe positional plagiocephaly, which is benign and does not require surgery. The ridge and the head shape are the key discriminators.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: How is the diagnosis best confirmed when needed?
options: Routine lumbar puncture
Genetic karyotype as the first test
Cranial imaging - low-dose CT with 3D reconstruction (or ultrasound of the suture in young infants)
EEG
stages.explanation: Craniosynostosis is often a clinical diagnosis, but when confirmation or surgical planning is needed, cranial imaging is used: low-dose CT with 3D reconstruction clearly demonstrates the fused suture and skull morphology, and high-resolution ultrasound of the suture is a radiation-sparing option in young infants. A lumbar puncture has no role in diagnosing suture fusion. A genetic karyotype is not the first test for isolated single-suture synostosis; genetic evaluation is reserved for multisuture or syndromic presentations such as Apert or Crouzon. EEG evaluates seizures, not cranial sutures, and is irrelevant here. Imaging confirms the suture status and guides the operative approach.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Low-dose cranial CT with 3D reconstruction: complete fusion of the sagittal suture with a scaphocephalic calvarial shape
Other cranial sutures patent
No hydrocephalus; ventricles normal in size
No intracranial mass or signs of raised intracranial pressure

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate management of confirmed sagittal craniosynostosis at this age?
options: Reassurance and a cranial repositioning/helmet program with no surgical referral
Referral to a craniofacial/neurosurgical team for surgical correction (endoscopic strip craniectomy with postoperative helmeting if early, or open cranial vault remodeling if older)
Observation alone, as the suture will reopen with growth
Lifelong avoidance of tummy time
stages.explanation: True craniosynostosis is treated surgically, so referral to a craniofacial/neurosurgical team is appropriate. Options include endoscopic strip craniectomy (best performed early, typically before about 3-6 months, followed by helmet therapy to remodel the skull) or open cranial vault remodeling for older infants; surgery corrects the shape and prevents the rare but real risks of restricted brain growth and raised intracranial pressure, especially with multisuture disease. Helmeting alone treats positional plagiocephaly, not a fused suture, and will not correct synostosis. A fused suture does not reopen with growth, so observation alone is inadequate. Tummy time is beneficial and should be encouraged, not avoided.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If this infant instead had positional plagiocephaly, what would be the correct management?
options: Urgent surgical suturectomy
Helmet therapy mandatory for every infant
Conservative measures - repositioning, increased supervised tummy time, and reassurance - with no surgery needed
Cranial CT every month to monitor
stages.explanation: Positional (deformational) plagiocephaly is a benign condition caused by external molding (often from back-sleeping) and is managed conservatively: active repositioning, increased supervised tummy time while awake, limiting time in car seats and swings, and reassurance, with most cases improving over time; physical therapy is added if torticollis is present. Surgery has no role because no suture is fused. A helmet (cranial orthosis) may be considered for moderate-to-severe or persistent cases but is not mandatory for every infant and is a second-line measure. Serial monthly CT scans are unnecessary, expose the infant to radiation, and play no role in this clinical diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Sagittal (single-suture) craniosynostosis
pearls: Sagittal craniosynostosis causes scaphocephaly (long, narrow head) with a palpable midline ridge present from birth - the most common single-suture synostosis.
Positional plagiocephaly is a parallelogram deformity (ear/forehead shifted forward) that improves with repositioning and has no suture ridge - no surgery needed.
Confirm synostosis with low-dose 3D CT or suture ultrasound; reserve genetic workup for multisuture/syndromic cases.
True synostosis is treated surgically (early endoscopic strip craniectomy with helmeting, or later open vault remodeling); positional plagiocephaly is managed conservatively.

cases.title: Morning vomiting and unsteady walking in a young boy
cases.chiefComplaint: My son keeps throwing up in the mornings and has been walking like he is drunk.
cases.history: The boy's mother describes about six weeks of intermittent vomiting, usually shortly after waking and sometimes projectile, which often relieves a headache he says is worst in the morning. Over the last two weeks his walking has become progressively unsteady; he widens his stance, lists from side to side, and has fallen several times. He has grown more irritable and sleepy during the day, and he recently complained of seeing two televisions. There has been no fever, seizure, recent trauma, or travel. Birth and developmental milestones were normal and immunizations are current. He takes no regular medications and has no known allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sister and attends first grade, where teachers have noted deteriorating handwriting and increasing clumsiness over the past month. Family history is unremarkable, with no known cancer-predisposition syndromes or childhood tumors.
cases.exam: The child is alert but irritable. Gait is markedly wide-based and unsteady with inability to tandem walk and a tendency to sway in all directions rather than fall consistently to one side. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There is a right abducens palsy with horizontal diplopia on right lateral gaze. Strength is full and symmetric in all limbs, with normal symmetric reflexes and downgoing toes. Finger-to-nose shows only mild dysmetria, and rapid alternating movements are preserved. The neck is mildly stiff with a slight head tilt. There is no nystagmus at rest, no skin stigmata, and no lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the signs of raised intracranial pressure, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure and obtain CSF
Prescribe oral ondansetron and discharge with pediatric clinic follow-up
Obtain an urgent non-contrast CT head
Schedule an outpatient brain MRI in two weeks
stages.explanation: A child with morning headache, projectile vomiting, papilledema, an abducens palsy, and truncal ataxia has raised intracranial pressure until proven otherwise. The fastest, most accessible study in the emergency department is a non-contrast CT head, which will reveal a posterior fossa mass and hydrocephalus and guide emergent management. Lumbar puncture is dangerous before imaging because a posterior fossa mass obstructing CSF flow can precipitate herniation. Discharging with an antiemetic ignores clear localizing signs and risks rapid deterioration. An outpatient MRI in two weeks is far too slow for evolving signs of raised pressure, though MRI will ultimately be required for definitive characterization.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: hyperdense midline cerebellar (vermian) mass filling the fourth ventricle
Marked dilation of the lateral and third ventricles with periventricular hypodensity
Effacement of the fourth ventricle and basal cisterns
Na 138 mEq/L
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next imaging step to characterize the lesion and stage the disease?
options: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis to search for a primary tumor
MRI of the brain and entire spine with gadolinium
Whole-body PET-CT
Technetium bone scan
stages.explanation: Medulloblastoma readily disseminates through the cerebrospinal fluid, so staging requires contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain and the entire spine, ideally before surgery to avoid postoperative artifact, plus CSF cytology. This identifies drop metastases that change risk stratification and treatment. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis and PET-CT are low yield because medulloblastoma rarely metastasizes outside the central nervous system. A bone scan is not part of standard staging and would miss the relevant leptomeningeal disease. Imaging the whole neuraxis is the key principle for any tumor prone to CSF seeding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After stabilization, what is the cornerstone of initial treatment?
options: Maximal safe surgical resection of the tumor
Permanent ventriculoperitoneal shunt as definitive treatment
Immediate whole-brain radiation
Primary chemotherapy with deferral of surgery
stages.explanation: Maximal safe surgical resection is the cornerstone of initial treatment: it relieves the obstruction, provides tissue for diagnosis and molecular subgrouping, and is the strongest modifiable prognostic factor. Hydrocephalus frequently resolves once the tumor is removed, so many children avoid a permanent shunt. A permanent VP shunt alone treats neither the tumor nor establishes diagnosis and carries a theoretical risk of peritoneal seeding. Whole-brain radiation upfront is not appropriate before resection and staging. Primary chemotherapy with deferred surgery is reserved for special circumstances and is not standard for an operable posterior fossa medulloblastoma.

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: Pathology confirms a WHO grade 4 embryonal tumor. Which adjuvant strategy is standard for this 6-year-old after resection?
options: Craniospinal irradiation plus chemotherapy
Observation with serial MRI alone
Focal posterior fossa radiation only, no chemotherapy
Chemotherapy alone, avoiding all radiation because of his age
stages.explanation: For a child older than three with medulloblastoma, standard adjuvant therapy after maximal resection is craniospinal irradiation, which treats the whole neuraxis given the high seeding risk, with a posterior fossa or tumor-bed boost, combined with chemotherapy. Observation alone is inadequate for a WHO grade 4 malignancy. Focal radiation only fails to address microscopic dissemination throughout the neuraxis. Chemotherapy alone while avoiding all radiation is the strategy reserved for children under three, in whom craniospinal irradiation causes devastating neurocognitive injury; at age six the neurocognitive risks are accepted in exchange for the survival benefit of radiation.

cases.diagnosis: Medulloblastoma (WHO grade 4 embryonal tumor) of the cerebellar vermis with obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Morning headache with vomiting plus truncal ataxia in a child points to a midline posterior fossa tumor causing obstructive hydrocephalus.
Medulloblastoma is the most common malignant brain tumor of childhood and classically arises in the cerebellar vermis.
It seeds CSF readily, so stage with contrast MRI of the entire neuraxis and CSF cytology before assuming localized disease.
Craniospinal irradiation is standard in children older than 3 but is avoided in infants because of severe neurocognitive injury.

cases.title: Months of clumsiness and headaches in a school-age girl
cases.chiefComplaint: She has been getting headaches and keeps dropping things with her right hand.
cases.history: The girl's parents describe several months of gradually worsening headaches with occasional morning nausea, plus increasing clumsiness of the right hand and deteriorating handwriting. They have noticed she veers to the right when walking, and her soccer coach commented on her poor coordination and frequent stumbles. There has been no recent vomiting, seizure, fever, or trauma, and no clear visual complaints, though she now seems unsteady on her feet. She was a full-term infant with normal development and current immunizations. She takes no medications and has no known allergies. She lives with both parents and a younger brother and has done well academically, although teachers have recently noted a decline in neatness and attention. Family history is noncontributory, with no neurofibromatosis, brain tumors, or other cancer-predisposition syndromes. There is no history of café-au-lait macules or skin lesions.
cases.exam: She is alert and well-appearing in no distress. Cranial nerves are intact except for mild gaze-evoked nystagmus on right lateral gaze. There is prominent right appendicular ataxia: dysmetria on right finger-to-nose testing, dysdiadochokinesia, and an intention tremor of the right hand. Gait is steady but consistently veers to the right, and she cannot tandem walk. Fundoscopy shows borderline disc margins without frank papilledema. Strength is full and symmetric, sensation is intact, and deep tendon reflexes are normal with downgoing toes. There is no truncal ataxia predominance and no meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluating this child?
options: Reassure the family and monitor symptoms for another month
Obtain an MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Order an electroencephalogram
Obtain plain skull radiographs
stages.explanation: Progressive headache with lateralizing cerebellar signs, including right-sided dysmetria, intention tremor, and a gait that veers right, warrants prompt neuroimaging, and contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain is the study of choice for posterior fossa lesions. It characterizes the cyst, mural nodule, and any mass effect far better than CT. Continued observation is unsafe given clear focal deficits and possible early disc changes. An EEG evaluates seizures, which this child does not have, and would not visualize a structural lesion. Plain skull radiographs cannot assess the brain parenchyma and have no role in this workup.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: well-circumscribed cystic mass in the right cerebellar hemisphere (4.2 cm)
Avidly enhancing mural nodule along the cyst wall
Minimal surrounding vasogenic edema
Mild mass effect on the fourth ventricle without significant hydrocephalus
No leptomeningeal enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Hemangioblastoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
stages.explanation: A well-circumscribed cystic cerebellar mass with an avidly enhancing mural nodule in a child is the classic picture of pilocytic astrocytoma, the most common pediatric brain tumor overall. Medulloblastoma is typically a solid, hyperdense midline vermian mass that fills the fourth ventricle, not a cyst-with-nodule. Ependymoma arises within the fourth ventricle and tends to extrude through its foramina. Hemangioblastoma has an identical cyst-plus-nodule appearance but occurs in adults, often with von Hippel-Lindau disease, rather than in a nine-year-old. The patient's age and imaging pattern make pilocytic astrocytoma the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the treatment of choice for this lesion?
options: Gross total surgical resection
Stereotactic biopsy followed by craniospinal irradiation
Primary chemotherapy
Stereotactic radiosurgery alone
stages.explanation: Gross total surgical resection is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is the treatment of choice for cerebellar pilocytic astrocytoma. Because this is a WHO grade 1 tumor, complete removal is generally curative and usually requires no adjuvant therapy. Biopsy followed by craniospinal irradiation is inappropriate, since radiation is not needed after complete resection and would expose a child to needless toxicity. Primary chemotherapy is reserved for unresectable or progressive disease, such as some optic-pathway tumors. Radiosurgery alone is not standard for an accessible, resectable cerebellar lesion where open resection offers a cure.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: After complete resection, what is the expected outcome?
options: It is uniformly fatal within two years
It requires craniospinal irradiation regardless of resection
Complete resection is essentially curative, with long-term survival over 90%
It commonly disseminates through the CSF, so the entire neuraxis must be irradiated
stages.explanation: Pilocytic astrocytoma is a WHO grade 1 tumor, and after gross total resection the prognosis is excellent, with long-term survival exceeding ninety percent, which is why complete resection is considered essentially curative. The tumor is not uniformly fatal; that describes high-grade gliomas. It does not require craniospinal irradiation after complete resection, since grade 1 tumors are not treated like medulloblastoma. Unlike medulloblastoma and ependymoma, pilocytic astrocytoma rarely disseminates through the CSF, so neuraxis irradiation is not warranted. Histology classically shows a biphasic pattern with Rosenthal fibers and eosinophilic granular bodies.

cases.diagnosis: Pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO grade 1) of the right cerebellar hemisphere
pearls: A cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule in a child is classic for pilocytic astrocytoma.
Pilocytic astrocytoma is WHO grade 1; gross total resection is essentially curative with long-term survival over 90%.
Histology shows a biphasic pattern with Rosenthal fibers and eosinophilic granular bodies.
Optic pathway pilocytic astrocytomas are strongly associated with neurofibromatosis type 1.

cases.title: Falling off his growth curve with new vision trouble
cases.chiefComplaint: He is the shortest in his class now and keeps bumping into things on the sides.
cases.history: An 11-year-old boy is referred by his pediatrician for poor growth: he has crossed percentiles downward over the past two years and now sits below the third percentile, with a delayed bone age. For several months he has had intermittent headaches, and his parents and teachers note that he bumps into door frames and misses objects approaching from the sides, including the soccer ball. Over the last month he has developed increased thirst and frequent urination, including waking at night to drink. He also reports fatigue and feeling cold all the time. There has been no vomiting, seizure, or trauma. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He was a healthy full-term infant with previously normal growth. He lives with both parents, and family history is noncontributory, with no endocrine or tumor syndromes. He has not yet shown any signs of puberty.
cases.exam: He is short for age and prepubertal, well below the third percentile for height, with no signs of puberty. Confrontation visual fields reveal a bitemporal hemianopia, and visual acuity is mildly reduced bilaterally. Fundoscopy shows mild optic disc pallor without papilledema. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There is no focal limb weakness, and strength, tone, reflexes, and coordination are normal. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, and there is no thyromegaly or goiter. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in the workup?
options: MRI of the brain and sella with contrast plus baseline pituitary hormone testing
Begin growth hormone therapy empirically
Reassure and continue plotting growth for one year
Obtain plain skull radiographs only
stages.explanation: Growth failure with a bitemporal field cut points to a sellar or suprasellar lesion compressing the optic chiasm and disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, so the right step is contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain and sella plus baseline pituitary hormone testing (IGF-1, free T4 and TSH, morning cortisol and ACTH, gonadotropins, and prolactin). Empiric growth hormone is dangerous before excluding a mass and could accelerate tumor growth. Continued growth monitoring ignores a localizing visual field defect and endocrine red flags. Skull radiographs might show calcification but cannot evaluate the chiasm, hypothalamus, or hormonal status, making MRI with labs the correct comprehensive workup.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI: suprasellar cystic and solid mass with coarse calcification, extending above the sella and compressing the optic chiasm
Cystic component contains dark 'motor-oil' fluid
IGF-1 low for age
Free T4 low with inappropriately low TSH (central hypothyroidism)
08:00 cortisol 2.1 µg/dL with low ACTH
Prolactin mildly elevated; FSH and LH low
Serum Na 147 mEq/L with dilute urine (urine osm 110 mOsm/kg)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this clinical and imaging picture?
options: Pituitary macroadenoma
Rathke cleft cyst
Craniopharyngioma
Pineal germinoma
stages.explanation: A calcified, cystic, suprasellar mass in a child with a cyst containing dark 'motor-oil' fluid is the classic adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma, which has a bimodal age distribution peaking in childhood. Pituitary macroadenoma is uncommon in children and usually neither calcifies nor presents with this cyst pattern. A Rathke cleft cyst is typically a small, non-calcified intrasellar cyst without a solid enhancing component. Pineal germinoma arises in the pineal or suprasellar region but is not calcified and is associated with elevated germ cell markers. The calcification plus cystic suprasellar location in an 11-year-old makes craniopharyngioma the best answer.

stages.label: ENDOCRINE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before any surgery, how should his hormone deficiencies be addressed?
options: Start levothyroxine immediately to correct the hypothyroidism
Start desmopressin and withhold all other hormones
No hormone replacement is needed before surgery
Replace glucocorticoids before giving thyroid hormone or proceeding to surgery
stages.explanation: In panhypopituitarism, glucocorticoids must be replaced before thyroid hormone or any surgery. Giving levothyroxine first increases metabolic demand and cortisol clearance and can precipitate an adrenal crisis in a cortisol-deficient patient. Desmopressin alone while withholding other hormones treats the diabetes insipidus but leaves the dangerous cortisol deficiency uncorrected. Assuming no replacement is needed ignores the documented central hypothyroidism and hypocortisolism. The correct sequence, glucocorticoid replacement first and then thyroid hormone, prevents life-threatening adrenal crisis and is a classic, high-yield principle in managing hypopituitarism before definitive treatment.

stages.label: SURGICAL STRATEGY
stages.question: Imaging shows the tumor is adherent to the hypothalamus. What is the preferred surgical philosophy?
options: Attempt aggressive gross total resection in all cases regardless of hypothalamic involvement
Favor planned subtotal resection plus radiotherapy when the hypothalamus is involved, to limit morbidity
Treat with radiation alone without obtaining any tissue
Use primary chemotherapy as definitive treatment
stages.explanation: When a craniopharyngioma involves the hypothalamus, aggressive attempts at gross total resection risk catastrophic hypothalamic injury: intractable obesity, memory impairment, temperature dysregulation, and worsened endocrinopathy. For these tumors, a planned subtotal resection followed by radiotherapy achieves comparable tumor control with far less morbidity. Pursuing aggressive total resection in all cases maximizes the risk of devastating hypothalamic damage. Radiation alone without tissue forgoes needed decompression of the chiasm and histologic confirmation. Primary chemotherapy has no established role for craniopharyngioma. Balancing tumor control against hypothalamic preservation is the central surgical decision in these cases.

cases.diagnosis: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma of the suprasellar region with panhypopituitarism and central diabetes insipidus
pearls: A calcified cystic suprasellar mass in a child is craniopharyngioma until proven otherwise; the cyst classically contains dark 'motor-oil' fluid.
Always replace glucocorticoids before thyroid hormone in panhypopituitarism; giving levothyroxine first can precipitate adrenal crisis.
Bitemporal hemianopia localizes to chiasmatic compression, classically from a mass pushing up from below.
Hypothalamic involvement makes aggressive resection risky (obesity, memory and temperature dysregulation); planned subtotal resection plus radiation reduces morbidity.

cases.title: A toddler admitted for vomiting now with a head tilt
cases.chiefComplaint: She has been vomiting for weeks and now holds her head to one side.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl was admitted to the pediatric ward after weeks of intermittent vomiting initially attributed to gastroenteritis and treated as reflux at prior outpatient visits. The vomiting has become persistent and now occurs mostly in the morning, accompanied by daytime lethargy, a new head tilt, and neck stiffness. Her parents report progressive unsteadiness and a regression in her walking over the past two weeks. There has been no diarrhea, fever, or sick contacts, and no trauma. On the ward she had a witnessed episode of bradycardia during an episode of vomiting. She takes only an as-needed antiemetic and has no allergies. Developmental milestones were previously normal. She lives with her parents and an infant sibling. Family history is noncontributory, with no known tumor or neurocutaneous syndromes. Immunizations are up to date.
cases.exam: She is lethargic but rousable. She holds her head tilted toward the left shoulder and resists neck flexion with nuchal rigidity. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There are lower cranial nerve findings: a diminished gag reflex and a hoarse, weak cry. She has truncal ataxia with a wide-based gait and falls, plus mild appendicular dysmetria. Gaze-evoked nystagmus is present. Strength is grossly intact in the limbs with symmetric reflexes. There is no focal long-tract weakness, but the head tilt and lower cranial nerve signs raise concern for tonsillar herniation and brainstem compromise.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step on the ward?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture
Obtain an abdominal ultrasound for presumed gastroenteritis
Continue oral rehydration and observe overnight
Obtain an urgent non-contrast CT head and consult neurosurgery
stages.explanation: This toddler has weeks of vomiting now accompanied by lethargy, a head tilt, nuchal rigidity, papilledema, lower cranial nerve signs, and relative bradycardia, all signs of a posterior fossa mass with raised intracranial pressure and impending herniation. The immediate step is an urgent non-contrast CT head with neurosurgical consultation. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated and could trigger fatal herniation. An abdominal ultrasound and continued rehydration with observation both anchor on the discarded gastroenteritis diagnosis and dangerously delay recognition of a neurosurgical emergency. Rapid imaging plus early neurosurgical involvement is essential when a child shows localizing signs and evidence of brainstem compromise.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: heterogeneous fourth ventricular mass with punctate calcification and obstructive hydrocephalus
MRI brain: mass arising from the floor of the fourth ventricle (rhomboid fossa)
Tumor extends laterally through the foramina of Luschka into the cerebellopontine angle cisterns and inferiorly through the foramen of Magendie toward the foramen magnum
Heterogeneous enhancement; effacement of the fourth ventricle
Dilation of the third and lateral ventricles

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which tumor best explains this growth pattern through the fourth ventricular foramina?
options: Medulloblastoma
Ependymoma
Pilocytic astrocytoma
Brainstem glioma
stages.explanation: A fourth ventricular tumor that arises from the floor (rhomboid fossa) and squeezes out through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie, the so-called plastic tumor behavior, with calcification, is characteristic of ependymoma. Medulloblastoma typically arises from the vermis and fills the fourth ventricle but does not characteristically extrude through the lateral foramina. Pilocytic astrocytoma is a hemispheric cyst-with-nodule lesion, not a floor-based fourth ventricular mass. Brainstem glioma infiltrates the pons rather than filling the ventricle and extruding through its outlets. The growth pattern through the foramina and the brainstem floor origin make ependymoma the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: How should the obstructive hydrocephalus be managed in this acutely deteriorating child?
options: Place an external ventricular drain for temporary CSF diversion
Place a permanent ventriculoperitoneal shunt immediately
Give high-dose mannitol alone and observe
Perform a therapeutic lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: In an acutely deteriorating child with obstructive hydrocephalus, temporary cerebrospinal fluid diversion with an external ventricular drain rapidly lowers intracranial pressure, stabilizes the patient, and can be managed peri-operatively; hydrocephalus often resolves after tumor removal. An immediate permanent VP shunt is premature, frequently unnecessary after resection, and carries infection and seeding risks. High-dose mannitol alone is only a temporizing measure and will not relieve the mechanical obstruction. A therapeutic lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a posterior fossa mass because it can precipitate herniation. An EVD, or endoscopic third ventriculostomy, is the appropriate bridge to definitive surgery.

stages.label: OPERATIVE LIMITS
stages.question: What is the principal factor limiting the extent of resection of this tumor?
options: Risk of postoperative CSF leak
The calcified content of the tumor
Proximity to the optic chiasm
Adherence to the floor of the fourth ventricle, risking cranial nerve and cardiorespiratory injury
stages.explanation: The principal factor limiting resection of a fourth ventricular ependymoma is its adherence to the floor of the fourth ventricle, the dorsal brainstem, where the lower cranial nerve nuclei (IX, X, XII) and cardiorespiratory centers lie. Aggressive removal of tumor densely adherent to the floor risks permanent cranial nerve palsies, swallowing and airway compromise, and autonomic instability. Postoperative CSF leak is a complication but not the main constraint on resection extent. The tumor's calcium content does not limit resection. Proximity to the optic chiasm is irrelevant in the posterior fossa. Extent of resection is the strongest prognostic factor, but it must be balanced against brainstem injury.

stages.label: ADJUVANT THERAPY
stages.question: After maximal safe resection, what is the standard adjuvant approach for this localized ependymoma?
options: Observation alone after resection
Craniospinal irradiation for all patients regardless of staging
Focal conformal radiotherapy to the tumor bed
Chemotherapy alone, which is curative
stages.explanation: After maximal safe resection, standard adjuvant therapy for intracranial ependymoma is focal conformal radiotherapy to the tumor bed, which improves local control even in young children when the alternative is recurrence. Observation alone is inadequate given high local recurrence rates. Craniospinal irradiation for all patients is reserved for documented dissemination; most ependymomas recur locally rather than seed, so routine neuraxis irradiation adds toxicity without benefit. Chemotherapy alone is not curative; it is mainly used to delay radiation in infants. Staging with neuraxis MRI and CSF cytology guides whether craniospinal coverage is needed.

cases.diagnosis: Fourth ventricular ependymoma arising from the floor of the fourth ventricle with extension through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie and obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Ependymoma classically arises from the floor of the fourth ventricle and squeezes ('plastic tumor') through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie.
Lower cranial nerve signs such as a poor gag and a hoarse cry reflect tumor adherence to the brainstem floor and limit safe resection.
Extent of resection is the strongest prognostic factor; focal conformal radiotherapy follows maximal safe removal.
Stage with neuraxis MRI and CSF cytology; reserve craniospinal irradiation for documented dissemination.

cases.title: A crooked smile and worsening balance over a month
cases.chiefComplaint: Her face looks uneven and she has been tripping a lot.
cases.history: A 7-year-old girl is brought in for four to six weeks of progressive neurologic symptoms. Her parents first noticed a left-sided facial droop and a crooked smile, then intermittent double vision, slurred speech, and drooling with some difficulty swallowing. Over the same period she has become increasingly clumsy, tripping and falling, and has developed weakness of the right arm and leg. The decline has been steady and relatively rapid. She has had no headaches, no early-morning vomiting, and no seizures, and her cognition and personality appear preserved. There is no fever or recent illness. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She was previously healthy with normal development. She lives with her parents and attends second grade. Family history is noncontributory, with no neurocutaneous or cancer-predisposition syndromes. There has been no recent travel or trauma.
cases.exam: She is alert and cognitively intact for age. There are multiple cranial nerve palsies: a left peripheral-pattern facial weakness with forehead involvement, bilateral abducens palsies with esotropia and impaired abduction, and a diminished gag reflex with dysarthria. Long-tract signs are present, with a right hemiparesis, hyperreflexia, and an upgoing right toe. Cerebellar testing shows an ataxic, wide-based gait and right-sided dysmetria. Fundoscopy reveals no papilledema. Sensation is intact. The combination of multiple cranial neuropathies, long-tract signs, and cerebellar findings without raised intracranial pressure is the key pattern.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test?
options: Non-contrast CT head only
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Lumbar puncture for cytology first
Nerve conduction studies
stages.explanation: A child with multiple cranial neuropathies, long-tract signs, and cerebellar findings evolving over weeks has a brainstem process, and contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain is the definitive test, exquisitely demonstrating the pons. Non-contrast CT alone poorly visualizes the posterior fossa and would miss the infiltrative changes. Lumbar puncture first is not the initial step, is unhelpful, and is potentially risky before imaging. Nerve conduction studies assess the peripheral nervous system and have no role in a central brainstem syndrome. MRI both localizes and characterizes the lesion, typically revealing the diagnosis without the need for tissue.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: expansile, infiltrative T2/FLAIR-hyperintense mass centered in the pons
The lesion engulfs and encases the basilar artery
Little to no contrast enhancement
Effacement of the fourth ventricle without hydrocephalus
No discrete cyst, hemorrhage, or separate demyelinating lesions

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical and imaging picture?
options: Brainstem encephalitis
Pontine cavernous malformation
Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma
Multiple sclerosis
stages.explanation: An expansile, infiltrative, T2/FLAIR-hyperintense mass centered in the pons that engulfs the basilar artery and shows little enhancement is the classic picture of diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma. Brainstem encephalitis would usually present with fever and CSF pleocytosis and a more inflammatory, less expansile pattern. A pontine cavernous malformation is a discrete lesion with blood products and a popcorn appearance, not diffuse infiltration. Multiple sclerosis is rare at this age and would typically show multiple discrete demyelinating lesions with a relapsing course. The diffusely expansile pontine mass engulfing the basilar artery is diagnostic of DIPG.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC STRATEGY
stages.question: How is the diagnosis of this lesion typically established?
options: The imaging is characteristic, so diagnosis is radiographic; biopsy is reserved for atypical cases or research and molecular protocols
Open surgical resection is required to establish the diagnosis
CSF cytology is diagnostic
PET scanning is diagnostic
stages.explanation: DIPG has such a characteristic appearance, a diffusely expansile, infiltrative pontine mass, that the diagnosis is traditionally made radiographically without biopsy, with tissue reserved for atypical features or enrollment in research and molecular-profiling protocols. Mandatory open resection is not performed because the tumor diffusely infiltrates eloquent brainstem and cannot be safely removed. CSF cytology is not diagnostic for this infiltrative glioma. PET is not a diagnostic test here. Modern trials increasingly obtain stereotactic biopsy to identify the H3 K27M alteration, but classic radiographic features remain sufficient for diagnosis in routine care.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard intervention for this tumor?
options: Maximal surgical resection
Focal radiotherapy for temporary palliation
Curative chemoradiation
Immediate ventriculoperitoneal shunt
stages.explanation: DIPG is not surgically resectable because it diffusely infiltrates the pons, which houses cranial nerve nuclei and cardiorespiratory centers; any attempt at resection would be devastating. The only intervention that reliably, if temporarily, improves symptoms is focal radiotherapy, which is palliative rather than curative. Maximal surgical resection is contraindicated for the reasons above. Curative chemoradiation overstates reality, as no regimen cures DIPG. An immediate VP shunt is unnecessary because DIPG usually does not cause early hydrocephalus, since the fourth ventricle is effaced only late. Radiotherapy buys time and symptom relief while the family is counseled.

stages.label: GOALS OF CARE
stages.question: How should the team approach goals of care with the family?
options: Promise a cure with aggressive chemotherapy
Pursue repeated debulking surgeries
Withhold the prognosis from the family to maintain hope
Communicate honestly that the tumor is incurable, involve palliative care early, and frame radiotherapy as palliative
stages.explanation: DIPG is incurable, with median survival under a year, so the standard of care includes honest prognostic communication, early palliative care involvement, and framing radiotherapy as palliation rather than cure. Promising a cure with chemotherapy is false and erodes trust. Repeated debulking surgeries are impossible and harmful for a diffuse, unresectable brainstem tumor. Withholding the prognosis to preserve hope violates the principles of honest, family-centered communication and impairs informed decision-making. Compassionate, truthful counseling with early palliative support is the appropriate disposition for this devastating diagnosis.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse intrinsic pontine glioma (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered), WHO grade 4
pearls: The DIPG triad is cranial neuropathies, long-tract signs, and cerebellar signs evolving over weeks, typically without early hydrocephalus.
DIPG is diagnosed radiographically by its expansile, infiltrative pontine appearance and is not surgically resectable.
Focal radiotherapy provides temporary symptom relief but is not curative; median survival is under a year.
Most DIPGs are diffuse midline gliomas with the H3 K27M alteration, WHO grade 4.

cases.title: A teen who cannot look up and has pounding headaches
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep getting bad headaches and my eyes feel stuck looking down.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents with several weeks of progressively worsening headaches, now accompanied by nausea and a few episodes of vomiting. He has noticed increasing difficulty looking upward, with trouble reading the scoreboard and watching things above eye level, and over the last week he has had intermittent double vision. His parents say his eyes sometimes appear to be looking downward. There has been no trauma, fever, or weight change, and no seizures. He has been more tired than usual and napped frequently this week. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He lives with his parents and plays on the basketball team. Family history is noncontributory, with no tumor syndromes. In the emergency department he appears drowsy and was difficult to fully arouse during triage, which prompted urgent evaluation.
cases.exam: He is drowsy but rousable and oriented. Eye examination shows a vertical upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus on attempted upward gaze, bilateral lid retraction (Collier sign), and pupils that are large and react poorly to light but constrict to near targets (light-near dissociation). Together these constitute Parinaud, or dorsal midbrain, syndrome. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilledema. There is no focal limb weakness, and strength, tone, sensation, reflexes, and gait are normal. There is no meningismus. The relative bradycardia and hypertension suggest a Cushing response from raised intracranial pressure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform a lumbar puncture
Reassure and arrange outpatient follow-up
Obtain an urgent non-contrast CT head
Schedule an outpatient MRI in two weeks
stages.explanation: This teenager has Parinaud syndrome and signs of raised intracranial pressure (headache, vomiting, papilledema, drowsiness, relative bradycardia) from a pineal-region lesion obstructing the cerebral aqueduct. The fastest emergency study is an urgent non-contrast CT head to confirm hydrocephalus and the mass and guide emergent care. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus because it risks herniation. Reassurance with routine follow-up is unsafe given progressive neurologic signs. An outpatient MRI in two weeks is far too slow for a patient with evolving raised pressure, although MRI with tumor markers will follow to characterize the lesion.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: pineal-region mass compressing the tectal plate with obstructive hydrocephalus
MRI brain: heterogeneously enhancing pineal mass obstructing the cerebral aqueduct
Marked dilation of the lateral and third ventricles
No other parenchymal lesions identified
Tumor markers pending

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which test best helps characterize the lesion and guide management?
options: Serum and CSF beta-hCG and alpha-fetoprotein
Thyroid function panel
Antinuclear antibody
Serum ceruloplasmin
stages.explanation: Pineal-region masses are most often germ cell tumors, and serum and CSF beta-hCG and alpha-fetoprotein are essential to characterize them and direct therapy. Pure germinomas have minimal or no marker elevation, whereas a markedly elevated beta-hCG or any elevated AFP indicates a non-germinomatous germ cell tumor, which has a worse prognosis and requires more aggressive multimodal therapy. A thyroid panel, ANA, and ceruloplasmin are unrelated to pineal germ cell tumors and would not influence diagnosis or treatment. Marker testing, alongside imaging and tissue, is the key branch point in pineal tumor management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best approach to manage the hydrocephalus and obtain a diagnosis?
options: Immediate gross total resection of the pineal mass
Endoscopic third ventriculostomy with biopsy and CSF sampling
Permanent ventriculoperitoneal shunt alone
Empiric radiation without tissue diagnosis
stages.explanation: Endoscopic third ventriculostomy simultaneously relieves the obstructive hydrocephalus without a shunt and allows biopsy and CSF sampling, making it the elegant first move for a pineal-region tumor with hydrocephalus. Immediate gross total resection is not first-line because germinomas are exquisitely radiosensitive and radical surgery is usually unnecessary and morbid. A permanent VP shunt alone misses the chance for tissue and carries a theoretical risk of peritoneal tumor seeding. Empiric radiation without tissue is inappropriate because one must first exclude a non-germinomatous tumor that would require different treatment. ETV with biopsy addresses pressure and diagnosis together.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms a pure germinoma. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Aggressive gross total resection
Observation
Alpha-interferon therapy
Radiotherapy, often with chemotherapy, sparing radical resection because of exquisite radiosensitivity
stages.explanation: Pure germinomas are exquisitely radiosensitive and chemosensitive, so the definitive treatment is radiotherapy, often combined with chemotherapy to reduce the radiation dose and field, with excellent long-term cure rates, and radical surgery is spared. Aggressive gross total resection is unnecessary and exposes the patient to avoidable brainstem and deep venous injury. Observation is inappropriate for a malignant tumor. Alpha-interferon has no role. Recognizing the exquisite radiosensitivity of germinoma is the key teaching point: it allows cure with radiation-based therapy rather than morbid attempts at complete resection.

cases.diagnosis: Pineal germinoma causing Parinaud syndrome and obstructive hydrocephalus
pearls: Parinaud syndrome (upgaze palsy, convergence-retraction nystagmus, light-near dissociation) localizes to the dorsal midbrain and pineal region.
Pineal-region germinomas obstruct the cerebral aqueduct; ETV can relieve the hydrocephalus and provide biopsy without a shunt.
Check serum and CSF AFP and beta-hCG: pure germinomas have minimal markers, while elevated AFP or high hCG signals a non-germinomatous germ cell tumor.
Germinomas are exquisitely radiosensitive, so cure is achieved with radiation (often plus chemotherapy), sparing radical resection.

cases.title: Sudden severe headaches that vanish when he lies down
cases.chiefComplaint: I get these crushing headaches that come out of nowhere, and a couple of times my legs just gave out.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man presents with several months of intermittent, severe headaches that come on suddenly, sometimes when he bends forward or changes position, peak within seconds, and last minutes to an hour before resolving, often relieved by lying down. He has had two frightening episodes in which his legs abruptly buckled and he dropped to the ground without losing consciousness, recovering within seconds. He reports occasional nausea during the headaches but feels completely normal between episodes. There has been no fever, neck stiffness, visual loss, weakness, or seizure. He is otherwise healthy, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He works as an accountant, drinks alcohol socially, and does not use tobacco or illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He came to the emergency department after another sudden, severe headache today that frightened him.
cases.exam: Between episodes he is comfortable and neurologically intact. Mental status is normal, with intact attention and memory. Cranial nerves are normal, fundoscopy shows sharp disc margins without papilledema, and there is no meningismus. Strength is full and symmetric in all limbs, sensation is intact, deep tendon reflexes are normal with downgoing toes, and gait, including tandem gait, is normal. There are no cerebellar signs and no focal deficits. The notably normal interictal examination, despite dramatic episodic symptoms, is a pertinent feature.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Carotid ultrasound
Non-contrast CT head
Electroencephalography
Lumbar puncture
stages.explanation: Intermittent, positional headaches with transient leg buckling (drop attacks) suggest intermittent obstruction of CSF flow at the foramen of Monro. In the emergency department, a non-contrast CT head is the fastest first study and readily shows a hyperdense lesion at the foramen of Monro with ventricular enlargement; MRI follows for definitive characterization. Carotid ultrasound evaluates extracranial vessels and is irrelevant here. EEG assesses seizures, but these positional, consciousness-preserving episodes are not seizures. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when an obstructing intraventricular lesion may be present because of herniation risk. CT first, then MRI, is the appropriate path.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT head: well-defined, round, hyperdense mass (1.3 cm) at the foramen of Monro in the anterior third ventricle
Mild symmetric enlargement of both lateral ventricles
MRI brain: spherical lesion at the foramen of Monro, T1 hyperintense, sitting between the columns of the fornix
No restricted diffusion; no enhancement of the brain parenchyma

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this lesion?
options: Craniopharyngioma
Central neurocytoma
Subependymoma
Colloid cyst of the third ventricle
stages.explanation: A well-defined, round, hyperdense lesion sitting at the foramen of Monro between the columns of the fornix, causing intermittent obstruction, is the classic colloid cyst of the third ventricle. Craniopharyngioma is suprasellar and typically calcified and cystic, not at the foramen of Monro. Central neurocytoma usually arises in the lateral ventricle attached to the septum pellucidum, often in young adults, but does not have this classic round foramen-of-Monro position. Subependymoma more commonly occurs in the fourth or lateral ventricles and is usually asymptomatic. The location and imaging make colloid cyst the best answer.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most feared complication of this lesion?
options: Sudden death from acute obstructive hydrocephalus
Malignant transformation and metastasis
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
stages.explanation: The feared complication of a colloid cyst is acute obstruction at the foramen of Monro causing sudden obstructive hydrocephalus, which can lead to rapid neurologic deterioration and even sudden death; the patient's positional headaches and drop attacks reflect transient ball-valve obstruction. Malignant transformation and metastasis do not occur, as colloid cysts are benign. Cavernous sinus thrombosis is unrelated to this midline intraventricular lesion. SIADH is not a characteristic complication. The risk of acute decompensation is precisely why symptomatic cysts with ventriculomegaly warrant timely surgical treatment rather than watchful waiting.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best definitive management for this symptomatic patient with ventriculomegaly?
options: Observation with annual MRI
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt alone
Surgical removal of the cyst (endoscopic or microsurgical)
Radiotherapy
stages.explanation: This patient is symptomatic with ventriculomegaly, so the definitive treatment is surgical removal of the cyst, achievable endoscopically or via a microsurgical transcallosal or transcortical approach, which relieves obstruction and prevents life-threatening recurrence of hydrocephalus. Observation with annual MRI is appropriate only for small, incidental, asymptomatic cysts without hydrocephalus. A VP shunt alone does not remove the cyst and leaves the patient dependent on diversion, though bilateral diversion is occasionally used adjunctively. Radiotherapy has no role for this benign lesion. Symptomatic colloid cysts with hydrocephalus are a clear indication for resection.

cases.diagnosis: Colloid cyst of the third ventricle causing intermittent obstruction at the foramen of Monro
pearls: A round, hyperdense mass at the foramen of Monro causing positional headaches and drop attacks is a colloid cyst until proven otherwise.
Intermittent ball-valve obstruction can become acute, causing sudden obstructive hydrocephalus and even sudden death.
Drop attacks, sudden leg buckling without loss of consciousness, are a classic colloid cyst symptom.
Symptomatic cysts or those with hydrocephalus warrant surgical removal; small incidental cysts can be monitored.

cases.title: A flushed man with headaches and a new clumsy hand
cases.chiefComplaint: I have had headaches for months, and lately my right hand is shaky and my face is always red.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man presents with several months of posterior, occipital headaches that have recently become worse in the mornings with occasional nausea. Over the past few weeks he has developed clumsiness and a tremor of the right hand and feels unsteady on his feet. He mentions that he has always looked ruddy and that at a recent blood drive he was told his blood was too thick to donate. In his twenties he had a reddish lesion in one eye treated with laser by an ophthalmologist. He takes no medications, has no allergies, and does not smoke. He works as an engineer. Family history is striking: his father died young of kidney cancer, and a paternal aunt had a brain cyst removed. He reports no abdominal pain, no episodic sweating spells, and no palpitations, though he is not always sure.
cases.exam: He has a plethoric, ruddy complexion. Cranial nerves are intact except for mild gaze-evoked nystagmus. There is right-sided appendicular ataxia with dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, dysdiadochokinesia, and an intention tremor of the right hand. Gait is steady but veers to the right, and tandem gait is impaired. Fundoscopy reveals a discrete reddish retinal vascular lesion without papilledema. Strength is full and symmetric, sensation is intact, and reflexes are normal. There is no abdominal mass or bruit. The combination of cerebellar signs, plethora, and a retinal vascular lesion is the salient pattern.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial test to characterize the lesion?
options: MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Non-contrast CT head only
Electroencephalography
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: Progressive headache with lateralized cerebellar signs warrants contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain, the best test to define a posterior fossa lesion and its cyst-and-nodule architecture and vascularity. A non-contrast CT only would miss the enhancing nodule and underestimate the lesion. EEG evaluates seizures, which are absent, and cannot image structure. Carotid duplex assesses extracranial carotid disease and is irrelevant to a cerebellar mass. MRI with contrast, combined with the patient's plethora and history of a treated retinal lesion, will point toward the diagnosis and prompt the appropriate laboratory and genetic workup.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: cystic right cerebellar mass with an intensely enhancing mural nodule abutting the pial surface
Prominent flow voids within the nodule indicating high vascularity
CBC: Hgb 19.5 g/dL, Hct 58%
Serum erythropoietin elevated
Serum and urine studies otherwise pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this adult patient?
options: Pilocytic astrocytoma
Hemangioblastoma
Cerebellar metastasis
Medulloblastoma
stages.explanation: In an adult, a cystic cerebellar mass with an intensely enhancing, highly vascular mural nodule, especially with secondary polycythemia and a prior retinal vascular lesion, is a hemangioblastoma. Pilocytic astrocytoma has an identical cyst-plus-nodule look but is a pediatric tumor, not one of a 36-year-old with this systemic picture. Cerebellar metastasis is possible in adults but would not explain the erythropoietin-driven polycythemia or the retinal lesion. Medulloblastoma is a solid midline childhood tumor. The combination of adult age, cyst-with-vascular-nodule, polycythemia, and retinal findings strongly indicates hemangioblastoma, often within von Hippel-Lindau disease.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the mechanism of his elevated hematocrit?
options: JAK2 V617F mutation
Chronic hypoxia from obstructive sleep apnea
Dehydration causing hemoconcentration
Ectopic erythropoietin production by the tumor
stages.explanation: Hemangioblastomas can produce erythropoietin ectopically, driving secondary polycythemia with an elevated hematocrit and a high serum erythropoietin level; the polycythemia typically resolves after the tumor is resected. The JAK2 V617F mutation causes polycythemia vera, a primary process characterized by a low erythropoietin level, the opposite of this patient. Chronic hypoxia from sleep apnea can raise hematocrit but does not fit this clinical picture. Dehydration causes relative, not true, erythrocytosis and would not elevate erythropoietin. The high erythropoietin with a vascular cerebellar tumor points clearly to ectopic hormone production by the hemangioblastoma.

stages.label: GENETIC WORKUP
stages.question: Given the full constellation, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Send VHL germline genetic testing and screen for renal cell carcinoma and pheochromocytoma
Reassure the patient that this is a sporadic, isolated tumor
Start hydroxyurea for the elevated hematocrit
Proceed directly to splenectomy
stages.explanation: A CNS hemangioblastoma plus a retinal hemangioblastoma and a family history of renal cell carcinoma strongly suggests von Hippel-Lindau disease, so the next step is VHL germline genetic testing and surveillance for associated tumors: renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic and endolymphatic sac tumors. Reassuring the patient that this is sporadic ignores the multi-organ pattern. Starting hydroxyurea misattributes the polycythemia to a primary marrow disorder. Splenectomy has no role. Importantly, screening for pheochromocytoma with plasma metanephrines must precede any surgery to avoid an intraoperative hypertensive crisis under anesthesia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best management of the symptomatic cerebellar lesion?
options: Whole-brain radiation
Therapeutic phlebotomy alone
Surgical resection of the enhancing mural nodule after excluding pheochromocytoma
Observation
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of a symptomatic cerebellar hemangioblastoma is surgical resection of the enhancing mural nodule, which is the neoplastic component; the cyst wall is non-neoplastic and need not be removed. Resection is curative for the lesion, and the secondary polycythemia resolves afterward. Crucially, pheochromocytoma must be excluded with plasma metanephrines before surgery to prevent an intraoperative hypertensive crisis. Whole-brain radiation is not standard for a resectable solitary hemangioblastoma. Therapeutic phlebotomy alone treats only the hematocrit, not the tumor. Observation is inappropriate for a symptomatic lesion causing cerebellar dysfunction and mass effect.

cases.diagnosis: Cerebellar hemangioblastoma with secondary polycythemia, in the setting of von Hippel-Lindau disease
pearls: A cystic cerebellar mass with an enhancing mural nodule in an adult is a hemangioblastoma (the pediatric mimic is pilocytic astrocytoma).
Hemangioblastomas can secrete erythropoietin, producing secondary polycythemia that resolves after resection.
Hemangioblastoma plus a retinal lesion and a family history of renal cell carcinoma should trigger VHL germline testing.
Screen for pheochromocytoma with plasma metanephrines before surgery in suspected VHL to avoid an intraoperative hypertensive crisis.

cases.title: Weeks of confusion and a weak left side in an older man
cases.chiefComplaint: He has been getting confused and his left arm is not working right.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man is admitted for three weeks of progressive cognitive decline, with his family describing personality change, slowed thinking, intermittent headaches, and difficulty using his left arm. There have been no seizures, fevers, or night sweats, and no recent infections or weight loss that they have noticed. His past medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes, and he is not known to be immunocompromised. His medications are lisinopril and metformin, and he has no allergies. He is a retired schoolteacher, lives with his wife, and does not use tobacco, alcohol, or illicit drugs. Family history is noncontributory, with no known cancers or neurologic disease. On admission, an MRI revealed a brain mass, and the covering team is considering starting empiric dexamethasone for presumed vasogenic edema before any procedure. He has not yet received corticosteroids.
cases.exam: He is alert but inattentive and disoriented to time, with slowed processing and poor short-term recall. There is a left pronator drift and a mild left hemiparesis (4/5) with left hyperreflexia and an upgoing left toe. A subtle central left facial droop is present. Visual fields are full to confrontation, and fundoscopy shows no papilledema. There is no meningismus, no rash, and no lymphadenopathy. The deficits localize to the right cerebral hemisphere. The remainder of the general examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial test to characterize the lesion?
options: Non-contrast CT head only
Lumbar puncture before any imaging
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
Electroencephalography
stages.explanation: Subacute cognitive decline with a focal right-hemisphere deficit in an older adult requires contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain, the most sensitive study to characterize a mass, its enhancement pattern, and its diffusion characteristics. A non-contrast CT only may show a hyperdense lesion but cannot adequately characterize enhancement or diffusion. Lumbar puncture before imaging is unsafe when a mass with potential mass effect is suspected. EEG evaluates seizures and would not characterize a structural lesion. MRI with contrast guides the differential and the safest route to tissue diagnosis, and is the appropriate first study here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: homogeneously enhancing mass in the right basal ganglia extending across the corpus callosum
The lesion crosses the midline and shows restricted diffusion
Relatively little central necrosis with surrounding vasogenic edema
Hyperdense on the prior non-contrast CT
HIV antibody negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits this picture?
options: Primary CNS lymphoma
Glioblastoma
Bacterial brain abscess
Meningioma
stages.explanation: A homogeneously enhancing periventricular mass that crosses the corpus callosum, shows restricted diffusion, and is hyperdense on CT in an older adult is classic for primary CNS lymphoma. Glioblastoma typically enhances heterogeneously with central necrosis and ring enhancement rather than homogeneous enhancement. A bacterial abscess is ring-enhancing with central restricted diffusion and is usually accompanied by fever and a source, which this patient lacks. Meningioma is an extra-axial, dural-based mass, not an infiltrative periventricular lesion. The homogeneous enhancement, periventricular location, callosal crossing, and restricted diffusion make primary CNS lymphoma the best answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The team wants to start dexamethasone for the edema. What is the best advice?
options: Give high-dose dexamethasone now and biopsy in one week
Hold corticosteroids until biopsy is obtained, because steroids can render the biopsy nondiagnostic
Corticosteroids have no effect on lymphoma, so timing does not matter
Start mannitol and proceed to gross total resection
stages.explanation: Corticosteroids are lymphotoxic and can cause primary CNS lymphoma to shrink dramatically or transiently disappear, yielding a nondiagnostic biopsy, the so-called ghost tumor. Therefore steroids should be held until tissue is obtained, unless herniation is imminent. Giving high-dose dexamethasone now and biopsying in a week risks exactly this nondiagnostic result. Claiming steroids have no effect is incorrect and is the crux of the teaching point. Starting mannitol and proceeding to resection is wrong because resection offers no survival benefit in PCNSL and mannitol does not address diagnosis. Withholding steroids preserves the diagnostic yield of biopsy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate procedure to establish the diagnosis?
options: Gross total surgical resection
Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue
Craniotomy for debulking
Stereotactic needle biopsy
