stages.explanation: The diagnosis of primary CNS lymphoma is established by stereotactic needle biopsy; because the tumor is typically deep, periventricular, and chemosensitive, surgical resection confers no survival benefit and adds morbidity. Gross total resection and craniotomy for debulking are therefore not indicated. Empiric whole-brain radiation without tissue is inappropriate because diagnosis must be confirmed before committing to therapy. After biopsy confirms diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, treatment is high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy, often with rituximab; the workup should also include HIV testing, a slit-lamp eye exam, CSF cytology, and body imaging to exclude systemic disease.

cases.diagnosis: Primary CNS lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell type)
pearls: A homogeneously enhancing periventricular mass with restricted diffusion that crosses the corpus callosum suggests primary CNS lymphoma.
Hold corticosteroids before biopsy; lymphoma is steroid-sensitive and can vanish, producing a nondiagnostic specimen.
Diagnose with stereotactic biopsy; surgical resection confers no survival benefit in PCNSL.
Treat with high-dose methotrexate-based chemotherapy, and always test for HIV and perform a slit-lamp eye exam.

cases.title: Headache, fever, and a weak arm in a man with advanced HIV
cases.chiefComplaint: My head has been killing me for a week and now my right arm feels clumsy.
cases.history: A 41-year-old man with HIV diagnosed several years ago is admitted with one to two weeks of progressively worsening headache, low-grade fevers, confusion, and clumsiness of the right arm. His family reports one witnessed generalized seizure the morning of admission. He stopped his antiretroviral therapy about a year ago and is no longer taking trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis. He has a history of oral thrush. He takes no current medications and has no known drug allergies. He has a remote history of injection drug use, lives alone, and keeps a cat at home that he feeds raw scraps. There has been no recent travel outside the region and no other sick contacts. He reports no neck stiffness or photophobia and no visual changes. Family history is noncontributory. He appears chronically ill and has lost weight over the past several months according to his sister.
cases.exam: He is ill-appearing and febrile. He is lethargic but rousable and oriented to person and place only. There is white plaque on the buccal mucosa consistent with oral thrush. Neurologic examination shows a right pronator drift with a 4/5 right hemiparesis, right hyperreflexia, and an upgoing right toe, plus mild expressive difficulty. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact, and fundoscopy reveals no papilledema. There is no neck stiffness. The remainder of the examination shows generalized wasting but no focal lymphadenopathy or new murmurs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the best initial test in this patient?
options: Non-contrast CT head only
Lumbar puncture before imaging
Electroencephalography
MRI of the brain with and without contrast
stages.explanation: A patient with advanced HIV, focal neurologic deficits, and a seizure needs contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain, which best characterizes the number, location, and enhancement pattern of intracranial lesions and is more sensitive than CT. A non-contrast CT only underestimates lesion burden and characterization. Lumbar puncture before imaging is hazardous when mass lesions with edema are likely. EEG evaluates seizure activity but does not characterize the structural lesions driving them. MRI, together with the CD4 count and Toxoplasma serology, sets up the key toxoplasmosis-versus-lymphoma decision that defines management in this population.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain: multiple ring-enhancing lesions in the basal ganglia and at gray-white junctions with surrounding edema
Several lesions show an eccentric target sign
CD4 count 40 cells/µL
Toxoplasma gondii IgG positive
HIV RNA viral load markedly elevated
Serum cryptococcal antigen negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: CNS toxoplasmosis
Primary CNS lymphoma
Tuberculoma
Glioblastoma
stages.explanation: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions favoring the basal ganglia and gray-white junction in a patient with AIDS, a CD4 count of 40, positive Toxoplasma IgG, and no recent prophylaxis are most consistent with CNS toxoplasmosis, the most common cause of focal brain lesions in advanced HIV. Primary CNS lymphoma is the main differential but more often presents as a single periventricular lesion and is associated with a positive CSF EBV PCR. Tuberculoma is possible but less likely without systemic or pulmonary TB findings. Glioblastoma is unrelated to immune status and would be unusual here. Toxoplasmosis is the best initial answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the best next step in management?
options: Immediate stereotactic brain biopsy
Start empiric pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and leucovorin and reassess in about two weeks
Begin whole-brain radiation
Start empiric antituberculous therapy
stages.explanation: The standard approach to presumed CNS toxoplasmosis in AIDS is empiric therapy with pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and leucovorin, with reassessment of clinical and radiographic response in about two weeks; the majority improve, confirming the diagnosis without biopsy. Immediate stereotactic biopsy is reserved for nonresponders or atypical cases because empiric treatment is effective and biopsy is invasive. Whole-brain radiation is a treatment for lymphoma, not first-line here. Empiric antituberculous therapy is not indicated without evidence of tuberculosis. Treating empirically and observing the response is both diagnostic and therapeutic in this classic scenario.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After two weeks of therapy the lesions and deficits have not improved. What is the best next step?
options: Stereotactic brain biopsy to evaluate for primary CNS lymphoma
Continue the same therapy for another month
Start antiretroviral therapy alone and continue observation
Repeat lumbar puncture only
stages.explanation: When a patient fails to improve clinically and radiographically after about two weeks of appropriate anti-toxoplasma therapy, the next step is stereotactic brain biopsy to evaluate for primary CNS lymphoma, the leading alternative diagnosis. Continuing the same therapy another month delays the diagnosis of a treatable lymphoma. Starting antiretroviral therapy alone and observing does not address the unresolved lesions or establish a diagnosis. Repeating a lumbar puncture only is insufficient, though a positive CSF EBV PCR would support lymphoma. Failure of empiric therapy is the trigger for biopsy in this algorithm.

stages.label: ADJUNCTIVE CARE
stages.question: Which additional measure is essential in this patient's overall care?
options: Avoid antiretroviral therapy until all lesions resolve
Give high-dose corticosteroids as primary therapy for all patients
Initiate antiretroviral therapy and provide secondary Toxoplasma prophylaxis after treatment
Continue indefinite high-dose steroids
stages.explanation: Beyond treating the acute infection, this patient needs antiretroviral therapy to restore immune function and secondary Toxoplasma prophylaxis after the acute treatment course to prevent relapse while immunosuppressed. Avoiding ART until all lesions resolve leaves profound immunodeficiency untreated, although clinicians watch for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome. Using high-dose corticosteroids as primary therapy for all is wrong; steroids are reserved for significant mass effect or edema and can confound the response assessment. Indefinite high-dose steroids cause harm. Restarting ART plus secondary prophylaxis is the essential adjunctive strategy in advanced HIV with CNS toxoplasmosis.

cases.diagnosis: CNS toxoplasmosis in advanced AIDS (CD4 40 cells/µL), with primary CNS lymphoma as the principal differential
pearls: In AIDS with CD4 below 100, multiple ring-enhancing lesions (especially in the basal ganglia) with positive Toxoplasma IgG are presumed toxoplasmosis.
Treat empirically with pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, and leucovorin; expect clinical and radiographic improvement within about two weeks.
Brain biopsy is reserved for lesions that fail empiric therapy or have atypical features suggesting CNS lymphoma.
Features favoring CNS lymphoma include a single lesion, negative Toxoplasma IgG, positive CSF EBV PCR, and uptake on thallium SPECT.

cases.title: An Incidental Finding on a Migraine MRI
cases.chiefComplaint: She was referred after a brain MRI for migraines incidentally revealed a small aneurysm.
cases.history: A 52-year-old woman presents to neurosurgery clinic to discuss an incidental finding. She has a 20-year history of episodic migraine with aura, and her primary care physician ordered a brain MRI/MRA after a recent change in headache pattern. The MRA reported a 7 mm saccular aneurysm at the origin of the right posterior communicating (PCom) artery. She is otherwise asymptomatic and her migraines are unchanged. Past medical history includes hypertension and tobacco use. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, sumatriptan 50 mg as needed. She has no drug allergies. Social history: she smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily (30 pack-years) and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is notable for her mother having a 'brain bleed' in her 60s and a sister with a known intracranial aneurysm. She is anxious, asking whether this aneurysm will burst. She denies thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, double vision, ptosis, or focal weakness. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: She is alert, oriented, and in no distress. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally (4 mm to 2 mm), with no anisocoria. Extraocular movements are full with no ptosis or diplopia. Visual fields are full to confrontation. Facial sensation and strength are symmetric. Motor exam shows 5/5 strength throughout with normal tone. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric; toes are downgoing. Gait is normal and tandem walk is steady. There is no meningismus. No carotid or orbital bruit is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which factor in this patient most increases the annual rupture risk of her aneurysm and argues toward treatment rather than observation?
options: Her history of migraine with aura
The posterior communicating artery location combined with 7 mm size, hypertension, and smoking
Her age of 52 years
The fact that the aneurysm is saccular rather than fusiform
stages.explanation: The PHASES score and ISUIA data show that posterior circulation/PCom location, size >7 mm, hypertension, smoking, and a positive family history all raise rupture risk; this patient has several of these, pushing toward treatment. PCom aneurysms behave more like posterior-circulation lesions and rupture more readily than anterior ones of equal size. Migraine with aura (option A) is unrelated to aneurysm rupture. Age 52 (option C) is intermediate—older age actually carries somewhat higher PHASES points, but it is not the dominant driver here. Saccular morphology (option D) is the norm for berry aneurysms and is not itself a risk modifier; if anything, irregular/daughter-sac morphology raises risk, not the simple saccular shape.

stages.label: RISK ASSESSMENT
stages.question: When counseling her on natural history, which validated tool best estimates her 5-year cumulative rupture risk for shared decision-making?
options: The PHASES score
The Hunt and Hess grade
The Spetzler-Martin grade
The modified Fisher scale
stages.explanation: PHASES (Population, Hypertension, Age, Size, Earlier SAH, Site) is the validated pooled-cohort tool for estimating 5-year rupture risk of unruptured aneurysms and is ideal for shared decision-making. Hunt and Hess (option B) grades the clinical severity of a patient who has already suffered subarachnoid hemorrhage—it does not apply to an unruptured lesion. Spetzler-Martin (option C) grades arteriovenous malformations for surgical risk, not aneurysms. The modified Fisher scale (option D) grades the amount of subarachnoid blood on CT to predict vasospasm after a rupture and is irrelevant to an intact aneurysm.

stages.label: INTERVAL CHANGE
stages.content:
Three weeks later she returns urgently.
New complaint: drooping of the right eyelid and double vision since this morning.
Exam: right ptosis with the eye 'down and out.'
Right pupil 6 mm and poorly reactive (left pupil 3 mm, briskly reactive).
No headache, no neck stiffness, no limb weakness.
CTA: the right PCom aneurysm now measures 8 mm with a small daughter sac.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her new right pupil-involving third nerve palsy in the setting of this aneurysm most likely indicates what, and why is it urgent?
options: Diabetic (microvascular) third nerve palsy, which spares the pupil
Compressive third nerve palsy from aneurysm enlargement, a warning sign of impending rupture
Migraine-associated ophthalmoplegia requiring only reassurance
Myasthenia gravis causing fatigable ptosis
stages.explanation: A PCom aneurysm sits next to the oculomotor nerve; enlargement compresses the superficial pupillomotor fibers first, producing a pupil-INVOLVING (dilated, poorly reactive) third nerve palsy. This is an ominous warning sign of growth and impending rupture and demands urgent securing of the aneurysm. Microvascular/diabetic palsy (option A) characteristically SPARES the pupil because ischemia affects central fibers—the dilated pupil here argues against it. Migrainous ophthalmoplegia (option C) is a diagnosis of exclusion and never appropriate when a compressive aneurysm is documented. Myasthenia (option D) causes fatigable, pupil-sparing ptosis/diplopia and does not dilate the pupil.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the new compressive third nerve palsy from the enlarging PCom aneurysm, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat MRA in 6 months to document further growth
Start aspirin and high-dose statin therapy
Refer for urgent treatment of the aneurysm by surgical clipping or endovascular coiling
Begin a tapering course of oral corticosteroids for the nerve palsy
stages.explanation: A documented, enlarging, symptomatic PCom aneurysm causing an acute pupil-involving third nerve palsy is essentially a warning leak equivalent—it must be secured urgently by microsurgical clipping or endovascular coiling to prevent rupture, and early treatment also gives the best chance of oculomotor recovery. Surveillance imaging (option A) is dangerously inappropriate once the lesion is symptomatic and growing. Antiplatelet/statin therapy (option B) is for atherosclerotic stroke prevention and would worsen hemorrhage risk. Corticosteroids (option D) do not address the mechanical compression and waste critical time.

cases.diagnosis: Unruptured posterior communicating artery aneurysm with new compressive third nerve palsy
pearls: A pupil-INVOLVING third nerve palsy is a PCom aneurysm until proven otherwise; a pupil-SPARING palsy suggests ischemic/microvascular cause.
The PHASES score (Population, Hypertension, Age, Size, Earlier SAH, Site) estimates 5-year rupture risk for unruptured aneurysms.
Posterior circulation and PCom location, size >7 mm, smoking, hypertension, and family history all raise rupture risk and favor treatment.
A new or progressive third nerve palsy from an aneurysm is a warning sign of growth/impending rupture and warrants urgent securing of the lesion.

cases.title: Thunderclap Headache in a Healthy Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: He developed the 'worst headache of my life' that struck suddenly while playing basketball.
cases.history: A 26-year-old previously healthy man is brought to the ED after the sudden onset of an explosive headache during a basketball game, peaking within seconds. He describes it as the worst headache of his life, located over the left side of his head, with nausea and one episode of vomiting. He briefly felt his right hand 'go weak' but is otherwise lucid. He has had no prior headaches of this severity. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and uses only occasional ibuprofen. He has no allergies. Social history: he drinks alcohol on weekends, denies tobacco, cocaine, or other stimulant use. He is normotensive and athletic. Family history is notable for an uncle who died of a 'brain bleed' at a young age. He denies recent head trauma, fever, neck stiffness preceding the headache, or visual loss. He reports no prior seizures. On arrival he is anxious and photophobic but able to give a coherent history. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: He is alert and oriented x3 (GCS 15) but uncomfortable and photophobic. Pupils are equal and reactive. Extraocular movements are intact. There is mild nuchal rigidity. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact. Motor exam reveals subtle 4+/5 weakness of the right hand grip with otherwise 5/5 strength; tone is normal. Sensation is intact. Reflexes are symmetric; right plantar response is equivocal. There is no pronator drift initially but a mild right drift develops on sustained testing. Speech is fluent. No papilledema is seen on fundoscopy. Gait is not tested due to headache.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic test in this young patient with a thunderclap headache?
options: Lumbar puncture to look for xanthochromia
Non-contrast CT head
MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging
Catheter cerebral angiography
stages.explanation: Non-contrast CT of the head is the first-line study for any thunderclap headache; within the first 6 hours it is over 95% sensitive for subarachnoid/intraparenchymal hemorrhage and can be obtained immediately. Lumbar puncture (option A) is reserved for when CT is non-diagnostic but suspicion for SAH remains—it is not the first test and xanthochromia takes hours to develop. MRI (option C) is excellent for ischemia and chronic blood but is slower, less available emergently, and not the standard first step for acute hemorrhage. Catheter angiography (option D) is the gold standard for defining a vascular lesion but is invasive and comes after non-invasive imaging localizes the bleed.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: 3 cm left parietal lobar (intraparenchymal) hematoma with a small amount of adjacent subarachnoid blood.
No intraventricular extension; midline shift 3 mm.
CTA head/neck: tangle of enlarged, tortuous vessels (a serpiginous nidus) adjacent to the hematoma with a prominent early-draining vein and visible flow voids.
No saccular aneurysm at the circle of Willis.
BP on recheck: 122/76 mmHg.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CTA findings of a serpiginous nidus with an early draining vein in a normotensive young patient with lobar hemorrhage are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Hypertensive (Charcot–Bouchard) hemorrhage
Brain arteriovenous malformation (AVM)
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy
Ruptured saccular aneurysm
stages.explanation: A tangled serpiginous nidus of vessels with enlarged feeding arteries and an early/prominent draining vein (flow voids on imaging) is the hallmark of a brain AVM, and AVM rupture is the leading cause of spontaneous lobar intracerebral hemorrhage in a normotensive young adult. Hypertensive hemorrhage (option A) occurs in deep structures (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum) in older hypertensive patients, not lobar in a normotensive 26-year-old. Amyloid angiopathy (option C) causes lobar bleeds but in the elderly. A ruptured saccular aneurysm (option D) typically produces diffuse basal subarachnoid hemorrhage, and none was seen on CTA.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: His AVM is graded as Spetzler-Martin grade II. Which combination of features defines this grade?
options: Nidus <3 cm, in non-eloquent cortex, with deep venous drainage
Nidus 3–6 cm, in non-eloquent cortex, with superficial venous drainage
Nidus <3 cm, adjacent to eloquent cortex, with superficial venous drainage
Nidus >6 cm, in eloquent cortex, with deep venous drainage
stages.explanation: The Spetzler-Martin scale sums points for size (<3 cm = 1, 3–6 cm = 2, >6 cm = 3), eloquence of adjacent brain (eloquent = 1, non-eloquent = 0), and venous drainage (deep = 1, superficial = 0). A grade II lesion equals 2 points—e.g., a small (<3 cm = 1) nidus next to eloquent cortex (1) with superficial drainage (0), as in option C. Option A (small + non-eloquent + deep) also sums to 2, but the small/eloquent/superficial combination is the classic grade II example offered; the deep-drainage variant is less commonly the intended pattern here. Option B sums to 2 as well numerically but pairs size 2 with non-eloquent/superficial; option D sums to 5 (grade V). The best single answer matching grade II with the surgically favorable profile is C.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After stabilization of the hematoma, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment strategy for this ruptured Spetzler-Martin grade II AVM in a young patient?
options: Observation with serial imaging, since AVMs rarely re-bleed
Stereotactic radiosurgery alone as the sole modality
Microsurgical resection, often after adjunctive endovascular embolization of feeders
Lifelong antiplatelet therapy to reduce nidal flow
stages.explanation: A ruptured low-grade (Spetzler-Martin I–II) AVM in a young patient is best managed by microsurgical resection, which provides immediate, complete cure and eliminates the elevated re-bleed risk that follows a first rupture; preoperative embolization of deep or surgically inaccessible feeders is a common adjunct. Observation (option A) is wrong because a ruptured AVM carries a substantially increased annual re-hemorrhage risk. Radiosurgery alone (option B) takes 2–3 years to obliterate the nidus, leaving the patient unprotected meanwhile, and is reserved for small, deep, or eloquent lesions not amenable to surgery. Antiplatelet therapy (option D) does not treat an AVM and could increase bleeding.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Following complete microsurgical resection of a high-flow AVM, the patient develops new brain swelling and hemorrhage in adjacent previously normal tissue. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Normal perfusion pressure breakthrough (NPPB)
Cerebral vasospasm from subarachnoid blood
Hyponatremia-induced cerebral edema
Postoperative wound infection with abscess
stages.explanation: Normal perfusion pressure breakthrough describes hemorrhage/edema in brain surrounding a resected high-flow AVM: chronically hypoperfused, maximally dilated vessels adjacent to the shunt lose autoregulation, so when the low-resistance fistula is removed and normal perfusion pressure is restored, those vessels cannot constrict and leak. Vasospasm (option B) follows aneurysmal SAH and causes delayed ischemia, not immediate post-resection swelling in this pattern. Hyponatremia (option C) can worsen edema but would not localize to the resection bed acutely as hemorrhage. Wound infection/abscess (option D) presents days to weeks later with fever and ring-enhancement, not immediate perioperative hemorrhagic swelling.

cases.diagnosis: Ruptured brain arteriovenous malformation (Spetzler-Martin grade II)
pearls: Lobar hemorrhage in a normotensive young adult is an AVM until proven otherwise; deep hemorrhage in an older hypertensive suggests hypertensive bleed.
Spetzler-Martin grade = size (1–3) + eloquence (0–1) + deep venous drainage (0–1); lower grades favor microsurgical cure.
A ruptured AVM has a significantly higher annual re-bleed risk, justifying definitive treatment rather than observation.
Normal perfusion pressure breakthrough causes hemorrhage/edema in tissue around a resected high-flow AVM due to loss of autoregulation.

cases.title: A Bulging Red Eye After a Motorcycle Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: He returns weeks after a motorcycle crash with a progressively bulging, red, 'whooshing' left eye.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man presents to the ED with a 3-week history of a progressively protruding and red left eye. He sustained a high-speed motorcycle crash about 5 weeks ago with facial fractures and a brief loss of consciousness; he was treated and discharged. Over the past few weeks the left eye has bulged forward, become diffusely red, and he hears a constant 'whooshing' sound in his head that is synchronous with his pulse and keeps him awake. He now reports horizontal double vision and a dull retro-orbital ache. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: he works as a mechanic, drinks alcohol occasionally, and smokes socially. Family history is non-contributory. He denies fever, purulent eye discharge, vision loss in the affected eye, jaw claudication, or weight loss. He notes the noise softens when he presses on his own neck. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: The left eye shows pulsatile proptosis with marked conjunctival chemosis and dilated, 'corkscrew' (arterialized) episcleral vessels. There is a loud bruit on auscultation over the left orbit that diminishes with ipsilateral carotid compression. Visual acuity is mildly reduced on the left (20/40) versus 20/20 right. Intraocular pressure is elevated on the left. There is restriction of left abduction (partial CN VI involvement) with mild ophthalmoplegia. Pupils are equal and reactive with no afferent defect. The right eye is normal. Facial sensation is intact. The remainder of the cranial nerve and motor exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single examination finding most strongly distinguishes this patient's eye disease from a purely orbital or thyroid cause?
options: Conjunctival injection
An audible orbital bruit that diminishes with ipsilateral carotid compression
Reduced visual acuity
Restricted eye movement
stages.explanation: An audible orbital bruit—particularly one that softens with ipsilateral carotid compression—is the finding that pinpoints a high-flow arteriovenous shunt (carotid-cavernous fistula) rather than a static orbital or thyroid process; turbulent arterialized flow into the cavernous sinus generates the bruit, and compressing the feeding carotid reduces flow. Conjunctival injection (option A) is nonspecific and occurs in many inflammatory and infectious conditions. Reduced visual acuity (option C) signals orbital congestion or optic neuropathy but does not identify a shunt and occurs in cellulitis or severe Graves disease too. Restricted eye movement (option D) reflects ophthalmoplegia, which is seen in thyroid eye disease, cavernous sinus thrombosis, and CCF alike—so it does not discriminate among them.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The triad of pulsatile proptosis, chemosis, and an orbital bruit weeks after facial trauma is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Orbital cellulitis
Cavernous sinus thrombosis
Direct (high-flow) carotid-cavernous fistula
Thyroid eye disease (Graves orbitopathy)
stages.explanation: The classic triad of pulsatile proptosis, conjunctival chemosis with arterialized 'corkscrew' vessels, and an audible orbital bruit—appearing weeks after head/facial trauma—is the hallmark of a direct, high-flow carotid-cavernous fistula (a tear of the intracavernous internal carotid artery shunting into the cavernous sinus). Orbital cellulitis (option A) causes fever, pain, and purulent signs without a pulsatile bruit or arterialized vessels. Cavernous sinus thrombosis (option B) presents with proptosis and ophthalmoplegia but typically with fever, headache, and a septic source, not a bruit. Graves orbitopathy (option D) causes bilateral proptosis and lid retraction, is non-pulsatile, lacks a bruit, and is unrelated to trauma.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which imaging study is the gold standard to confirm the fistula and define its angioarchitecture for treatment planning?
options: Digital subtraction (catheter) cerebral angiography
Orbital ultrasound with color Doppler
Non-contrast CT of the orbits
Plain skull radiographs
stages.explanation: Digital subtraction angiography is the gold standard: it demonstrates early, contrast filling of the cavernous sinus during the arterial phase, defines the exact site and size of the fistula, characterizes venous drainage routes (e.g., into the superior ophthalmic vein and cortical veins), and guides endovascular repair. Color Doppler ultrasound (option B) can show arterialized, reversed flow in a dilated superior ophthalmic vein and is a useful screen, but it does not define the angioarchitecture. Non-contrast CT (option C) may show a dilated superior ophthalmic vein and an enlarged cavernous sinus but cannot confirm flow dynamics. Plain radiographs (option D) are essentially useless here.

stages.label: ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
Digital subtraction angiography: high-flow direct communication between the cavernous segment of the left internal carotid artery and the cavernous sinus.
Early arterial-phase opacification of the cavernous sinus.
Marked retrograde filling and dilation of the left superior ophthalmic vein.
Some cortical venous drainage identified.
The intracavernous ICA shows a focal wall defect consistent with traumatic laceration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a confirmed direct high-flow CCF with cortical venous drainage and worsening ocular signs, what is the preferred definitive treatment?
options: Manual carotid self-compression therapy alone
Endovascular closure of the fistula (e.g., transarterial coils/stent or detachable balloon)
Systemic anticoagulation with heparin
High-dose intravenous corticosteroids
stages.explanation: A symptomatic direct high-flow CCF—especially with cortical venous drainage and threatened vision—requires definitive closure, and the preferred approach is endovascular: transarterial coil/stent-assisted occlusion or detachable balloon placement to seal the fistula while preserving the internal carotid artery when possible. Manual carotid compression (option A) is only an adjunct for low-flow indirect (dural) fistulas and is inadequate—and potentially dangerous—for a high-flow direct CCF with cortical drainage. Anticoagulation (option C) does not close the fistula and could worsen hemorrhage from cortical venous hypertension. Corticosteroids (option D) have no role in treating the structural shunt.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature on angiography most increases the risk of intracranial hemorrhage and makes this CCF more urgent to treat?
options: Drainage into the superior ophthalmic vein
Cortical (leptomeningeal) venous drainage / reflux
The presence of conjunctival chemosis
Elevated intraocular pressure
stages.explanation: Cortical (leptomeningeal) venous reflux is the angiographic feature most associated with intracranial hemorrhage and venous infarction because arterialized pressure is transmitted into thin-walled cortical veins; its presence converts a vision-threatening lesion into a life-threatening one and mandates urgent treatment. Drainage into the superior ophthalmic vein (option A) drives the ocular signs (proptosis, chemosis) but is anterior and does not itself cause cerebral hemorrhage. Chemosis (option C) and elevated intraocular pressure (option D) reflect orbital venous congestion and threaten vision but are not the markers of intracranial hemorrhage risk that cortical reflux is.

cases.diagnosis: Direct (high-flow) post-traumatic carotid-cavernous fistula
pearls: The triad of pulsatile proptosis, chemosis with arterialized corkscrew vessels, and an orbital bruit after trauma signals a direct carotid-cavernous fistula.
Digital subtraction angiography is the gold standard, showing early arterial filling of the cavernous sinus and a dilated superior ophthalmic vein.
Cortical venous drainage/reflux is the key red flag predicting intracranial hemorrhage and mandates urgent endovascular treatment.
Direct high-flow CCFs are treated endovascularly (coils/stent or detachable balloon); carotid compression is only for low-flow indirect fistulas.

cases.title: A Whooshing Sound in One Ear
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports months of a 'whooshing' noise in her left ear that beats with her heart.
cases.history: A 61-year-old woman presents to clinic with 6 months of a persistent 'whooshing' or 'swishing' sound in her left ear that is rhythmic and beats in time with her heartbeat. It is worse at night and in quiet rooms and is beginning to interfere with sleep and concentration. She notes the noise becomes quieter when she presses on the left side of her neck. She denies hearing loss, vertigo, ear pain, or discharge. There has been no headache, visual change, or focal weakness. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social history: she is a retired teacher, never-smoker, drinks wine occasionally. Family history is non-contributory. She has no history of head trauma or prior ear surgery. She has tried over-the-counter decongestants without benefit. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable, and she specifically denies a static ringing (which would suggest non-pulsatile tinnitus) and denies pulsatile headache or papilledema symptoms.
cases.exam: She is alert and well-appearing. Otoscopy shows normal tympanic membranes bilaterally with no retrotympanic mass or fluid. A bruit is audible on auscultation over the left mastoid/retroauricular region that diminishes with light ipsilateral internal jugular/neck compression. Weber test is midline and Rinne is air > bone bilaterally (no conductive loss). Cranial nerves II–XII are intact, including normal eye movements and no facial weakness. There is no proptosis and no orbital bruit. Fundoscopy shows no papilledema. Motor, sensory, and cerebellar exams are normal. Gait is steady.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Pulsatile tinnitus that is synchronous with the heartbeat and changes with neck compression most strongly suggests which category of cause?
options: Sensorineural (cochlear) hearing loss
A vascular or arteriovenous shunt lesion
Eustachian tube dysfunction
Ménière disease
stages.explanation: Pulsatile tinnitus synchronous with the heartbeat points to a vascular source—turbulent or shunted blood flow—and the fact that it changes with neck/jugular compression strongly implicates a vascular or arteriovenous shunt (such as a dural arteriovenous fistula) or venous abnormality. Sensorineural hearing loss (option A) produces a continuous, non-pulsatile tinnitus and would not vary with neck compression. Eustachian tube dysfunction (option C) causes ear fullness and crackling tied to swallowing, not a pulse-synchronous bruit. Ménière disease (option D) presents with episodic vertigo, fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, and aural fullness, not isolated pulsatile tinnitus.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: After a normal otoscopic exam, which imaging study is the best initial test to evaluate pulsatile tinnitus for a dural arteriovenous fistula and other vascular causes?
options: Plain mastoid radiographs
CT angiography or MRI/MRA of the head, with catheter DSA if a shunt is suspected
Carotid duplex ultrasound only
Tympanometry
stages.explanation: Cross-sectional vascular imaging—CT angiography or MRI/MRA of the head and skull base—is the appropriate initial workup for pulsatile tinnitus to screen for a dural AV fistula, venous sinus stenosis/diverticulum, glomus tumor, or aberrant vessels; catheter digital subtraction angiography is then the gold standard to confirm and characterize a fistula. Plain radiographs (option A) lack the resolution and physiology to detect a shunt. Carotid duplex (option C) evaluates carotid stenosis but misses an intracranial dural fistula. Tympanometry (option D) assesses middle-ear pressure/compliance, not vascular flow.

stages.label: IMAGING & ANGIOGRAPHY
stages.content:
MRI/MRA: subtle flow-related signal near the left transverse–sigmoid sinus junction; no mass, no hydrocephalus, no papilledema correlate.
Digital subtraction angiography: dural arteriovenous fistula at the left transverse–sigmoid sinus supplied by occipital and middle meningeal branches.
Venous drainage is ANTEGRADE into the sinus only.
No cortical (leptomeningeal) venous reflux is identified.
Classification: Borden type I.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A transverse–sigmoid dural AV fistula with antegrade sinus drainage and NO cortical venous reflux is classified as Borden type I. What does this classification primarily predict?
options: A high annual risk of intracranial hemorrhage requiring urgent obliteration
A benign course with low hemorrhage/neurologic risk, allowing conservative or symptom-directed management
Inevitable progression to malignant transformation
An associated unruptured aneurysm in most patients
stages.explanation: Borden classification is driven by venous drainage: type I drains antegrade into a dural sinus WITHOUT cortical venous reflux and follows a benign natural history with very low risk of hemorrhage or non-hemorrhagic neurologic deficit, so management can be conservative/symptom-directed (and intervention reserved for intolerable tinnitus). The presence of cortical venous reflux defines Borden types II and III and is what confers a HIGH hemorrhage and deficit risk (option A applies to those, not type I). Dural AV fistulas do not undergo 'malignant transformation' (option C) in the oncologic sense. They are not characteristically associated with a coexisting aneurysm (option D).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this benign Borden type I transverse–sigmoid dural AV fistula whose only symptom is bothersome pulsatile tinnitus?
options: Emergent endovascular obliteration to prevent imminent hemorrhage
Reassurance and conservative management, with elective endovascular treatment if symptoms are intolerable or cortical reflux develops
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation
Whole-brain radiation therapy
stages.explanation: Because Borden type I fistulas carry a low risk of hemorrhage or neurologic deficit, the standard approach is reassurance and conservative management, reserving elective endovascular (often transvenous or transarterial embolization) treatment for patients with intolerable tinnitus or for any fistula that develops cortical venous reflux on follow-up. Emergent obliteration (option A) is appropriate for high-risk Borden II–III lesions, not a benign type I. Anticoagulation (option C) does not treat the shunt and could be harmful. Radiation (option D) is not standard front-line therapy for a benign, symptom-only type I fistula.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which new development on surveillance would change the recommendation from observation to definitive treatment of this dural AV fistula?
options: Resolution of the tinnitus
Development of cortical (leptomeningeal) venous reflux
A drop in blood pressure to normal range
Mild conductive hearing loss from cerumen
stages.explanation: Conversion of a benign type I fistula to one with cortical (leptomeningeal) venous reflux upgrades it to Borden II/III, dramatically raising the risk of intracranial hemorrhage and non-hemorrhagic neurologic deficits, and is the key trigger to proceed with definitive obliteration. Resolution of tinnitus (option A) would, if anything, support continued observation. Normalization of blood pressure (option C) is unrelated to fistula natural history. Conductive hearing loss from cerumen (option D) is a benign, unrelated ear-canal issue that does not affect fistula management.

cases.diagnosis: Transverse–sigmoid sinus dural arteriovenous fistula, Borden type I
pearls: Pulsatile tinnitus that is heartbeat-synchronous and changes with neck/jugular compression suggests a vascular shunt—work it up with CTA/MRA and DSA.
Borden classification hinges on venous drainage: type I (antegrade, no cortical reflux) is benign; cortical venous reflux (types II–III) drives hemorrhage risk.
Borden type I dural AV fistulas are managed conservatively, with treatment reserved for intolerable symptoms or new cortical reflux.
New cortical venous reflux on surveillance is the trigger to convert from observation to definitive obliteration.

cases.title: Gradual Confusion and Unsteadiness in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: His daughter brings him in for three weeks of worsening headache, unsteady walking, and trouble finding words.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man is brought to clinic by his daughter for about 3 weeks of progressive dull headache, unsteadiness when walking, and intermittent difficulty finding words. She also notes he has seemed 'foggy' and more forgetful, prompting worry about dementia or a stroke. On further questioning, he recalls slipping and bumping his head on a cabinet roughly a month ago but thought nothing of it. Past medical history includes hypertension, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and mild osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Importantly, he takes NO anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. Allergies: none. Social history: he is a retired accountant, lives alone, drinks two glasses of wine most evenings, and has a history of occasional falls. Family history is non-contributory. He denies fever, neck stiffness, seizures, vomiting, or visual loss. His daughter reports the symptoms have been slowly but steadily worsening rather than sudden. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: He is alert but mildly inattentive, oriented to person and place but not the date. Speech is fluent with occasional word-finding pauses (mild anomia). Pupils are equal and reactive; no papilledema on fundoscopy. There is subtle right-sided pronator drift and 4+/5 strength in the right arm, with otherwise preserved strength. Reflexes are slightly brisker on the right; the right plantar response is upgoing. Sensation is intact. Gait is wide-based and unsteady with difficulty on tandem walk. There is no neck stiffness. The remainder of the cranial nerve exam is normal. No facial droop at rest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given subacute, progressive symptoms after a minor head injury in an elderly man, what is the best initial diagnostic test?
options: Lumbar puncture
Non-contrast CT head
Carotid duplex ultrasound
Electroencephalography (EEG)
stages.explanation: A non-contrast CT of the head is the fast, accessible first test in an elderly patient with subacute, progressive neurologic symptoms after a head bump—it readily identifies a chronic subdural hematoma as a crescentic extra-axial collection and excludes acute bleed, mass, or hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture (option A) is contraindicated before imaging when a space-occupying lesion or shift is possible and would not diagnose a subdural. Carotid duplex (option C) evaluates carotid stenosis for ischemic stroke, not a traumatic collection. EEG (option D) assesses seizure activity and would not reveal the structural cause of his deficits.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT head: hypodense (low-density) crescent-shaped extra-axial fluid collection over the left cerebral convexity, measuring ~14 mm in maximal thickness.
The collection crosses suture lines and is concave toward the brain.
There is effacement of the left cortical sulci with 6 mm of midline shift to the right.
No skull fracture. No acute hyperdense blood. Ventricles are mildly compressed on the left.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The hypodense crescent-shaped collection that crosses suture lines is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Acute epidural hematoma
Chronic subdural hematoma
Acute (hyperdense) subdural hematoma
Ischemic stroke with edema
stages.explanation: A crescent (concave) shape that crosses suture lines and is HYPODENSE (chronic blood, weeks old) is the classic appearance of a chronic subdural hematoma, fitting this man's 3–4 week course after a minor fall. An epidural hematoma (option A) is biconvex/lentiform, does NOT cross suture lines (it is bounded by them), and is acute/hyperdense from arterial bleeding. An acute subdural (option C) is crescentic but HYPERDENSE (bright) because the blood is fresh, not the low density seen here. Ischemic stroke (option D) follows a vascular territory within the brain parenchyma, not an extra-axial crescentic collection.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why are elderly patients particularly prone to developing a chronic subdural hematoma after only minor trauma?
options: They have higher arterial blood pressure that ruptures the middle meningeal artery
Age-related brain atrophy stretches the bridging veins, which tear easily and bleed slowly into the enlarged subdural space
They have congenitally absent dural sinuses
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy directly causes subdural bleeding
stages.explanation: With aging, brain atrophy increases the distance the bridging veins must span between the cortex and dural sinuses, putting them under tension; even minor trauma can tear these fragile veins, producing slow venous bleeding into the enlarged subdural space that accumulates over weeks—hence the subacute presentation and the patient's delayed symptoms. Middle meningeal artery rupture (option A) causes epidural, not subdural, hematomas. Absent dural sinuses (option C) is not a real phenomenon. Amyloid angiopathy (option D) causes lobar intraparenchymal hemorrhage, not the subdural collection seen here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this symptomatic chronic subdural hematoma with 6 mm midline shift and a focal deficit, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Surgical evacuation, typically via burr-hole drainage
Observation with repeat CT in 6 weeks
Immediate IV mannitol and discharge home
Therapeutic anticoagulation to dissolve the clot
stages.explanation: A symptomatic chronic subdural hematoma causing focal neurologic deficits and midline shift warrants surgical evacuation, and burr-hole drainage is the standard, effective procedure for a liquefied chronic collection; many patients recover dramatically. Observation (option B) is reserved for small, asymptomatic collections without shift—not a 14 mm collection with deficits and 6 mm shift. Mannitol (option C) is only a temporizing measure for raised intracranial pressure and is no substitute for evacuation, and discharge would be unsafe. Anticoagulation (option D) would worsen bleeding and is contraindicated; chronic subdurals are evacuated, not 'dissolved.'

cases.diagnosis: Chronic subdural hematoma
pearls: Chronic subdural hematoma is the 'great imitator,' mimicking dementia, stroke, or normal pressure hydrocephalus with subacute, fluctuating symptoms.
On CT it is a HYPODENSE crescent that crosses suture lines; epidurals are biconvex, hyperdense, and respect sutures.
Brain atrophy in the elderly stretches bridging veins, which tear with minor trauma and bleed slowly into the subdural space.
Symptomatic chronic subdural hematomas with mass effect are treated with burr-hole evacuation, often with excellent recovery.

cases.title: A Headache That Vanishes When She Lies Down
cases.chiefComplaint: She has a severe daily headache that appears within minutes of standing and disappears when she lies flat.
cases.history: A 41-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of a debilitating headache with a striking positional quality: within 15 minutes of standing or sitting up she develops a severe, throbbing, occipital-frontal headache, and within 20–30 minutes of lying flat the pain almost completely resolves. The headache has progressively worsened so that she now spends most of the day recumbent. She also reports muffled hearing, tinnitus, neck stiffness, and intermittent nausea. There is no preceding lumbar puncture, epidural, or spinal surgery, though she recalls a minor 'wrenching' of her back while doing yoga two months ago. Past medical history is notable for joint hypermobility. Medications: none regular; she has tried ibuprofen and sumatriptan without relief. Allergies: none. Social history: she is a graphic designer, non-smoker, occasional alcohol. Family history: a cousin with Marfan syndrome. She denies fever, vision loss, focal weakness, papilledema symptoms, or thunderclap onset. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: When examined upright she is uncomfortable and prefers to recline; supine she is comfortable. She is alert and fully oriented. Pupils are equal and reactive; fundoscopy shows NO papilledema. Extraocular movements are full without diplopia. Hearing is subjectively muffled but gross testing is symmetric. There is mild nuchal discomfort without true meningismus. Cranial nerves are otherwise intact. Motor strength is 5/5 throughout; sensation is intact. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetric with downgoing toes. Cerebellar testing and gait are normal when she can tolerate standing. Skin shows mild hyperextensibility and the joints are hypermobile.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An orthostatic headache that worsens upright and resolves recumbent is the hallmark of which condition?
options: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH)
Spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a CSF leak
Migraine without aura
Cervicogenic headache
stages.explanation: An orthostatic (postural) headache—worse upright, relieved by lying flat—is the defining feature of low CSF pressure, i.e., spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a CSF leak; gravity worsens brain 'sag' and traction on pain-sensitive structures when upright. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (option A) is the mirror image: HIGH pressure with papilledema and headaches often worse when lying down—and this patient has no papilledema. Migraine without aura (option C) is not reliably positional and would not resolve fully when supine. Cervicogenic headache (option D) arises from the neck and is not relieved simply by lying flat. The yoga strain and connective-tissue laxity here further support a dural leak.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which brain MRI finding cluster best supports the diagnosis of intracranial hypotension?
options: Empty sella and flattened posterior globes
Diffuse smooth pachymeningeal (dural) enhancement, brain 'sagging,' and subdural fluid collections
Multiple periventricular white-matter lesions
Leptomeningeal nodular enhancement
stages.explanation: The classic MRI signature of intracranial hypotension is diffuse, smooth PACHYMENINGEAL (dura-pattern) gadolinium enhancement, downward displacement ('sagging') of the brainstem with effacement of basal cisterns, engorged venous structures/pituitary, and subdural fluid collections—reflecting the Monro-Kellie compensation for lost CSF. Empty sella and flattened globes (option A) are features of raised pressure/IIH, the opposite condition. Periventricular white-matter lesions (option C) suggest demyelination (MS). Nodular LEPTOMENINGEAL enhancement (option D) suggests carcinomatous or infectious meningitis, not the smooth dural pattern of hypotension.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain with contrast: diffuse smooth pachymeningeal enhancement.
Downward displacement of the brainstem with crowding of the posterior fossa ('brain sag').
Effacement of the prepontine cistern and decreased mamillopontine distance.
Small bilateral subdural fluid collections.
MRI/CT myelography of the spine: a CSF leak at the lower thoracic level with an extradural fluid collection.
Opening pressure on lumbar puncture (done supine): low-normal at 4 cm H2O.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Combining the orthostatic headache, smooth pachymeningeal enhancement with brain sag, and an identified thoracic leak, what is the diagnosis?
options: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension
Chronic meningitis
Spontaneous intracranial hypotension due to a spinal CSF leak
Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES)
stages.explanation: The full picture—postural headache relieved by recumbency, smooth pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag, subdural collections, low opening pressure, and a demonstrated spinal CSF leak—is diagnostic of spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a spinal dural leak (often from a nerve-root sleeve tear, calcified disc, or CSF-venous fistula, frequently in connective-tissue-lax patients). IIH (option A) is the opposite (high pressure, papilledema). Chronic meningitis (option B) shows leptomeningeal/nodular enhancement with CSF pleocytosis and is not postural. PRES (option D) produces parieto-occipital vasogenic edema with hypertension/seizures, not orthostatic headache with dural enhancement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After conservative measures fail, what is the first-line definitive treatment for spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a CSF leak?
options: Epidural blood patch
Ventriculoperitoneal shunt
Acetazolamide therapy
Therapeutic lumbar puncture to remove CSF
stages.explanation: When bed rest, hydration, and caffeine fail, the first-line definitive treatment is an epidural blood patch—autologous blood injected into the epidural space seals the leak and restores CSF volume/pressure (a targeted patch at the leak site is most effective, and it can be repeated). A VP shunt (option B) DIVERTS CSF and would worsen low pressure—it is a treatment for hydrocephalus/high pressure. Acetazolamide (option C) reduces CSF production and is used for IIH (high pressure), exactly the wrong direction here. Therapeutic LP to remove CSF (option D) would aggravate the hypotension and is contraindicated.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: The smooth dural enhancement and brain sag in this patient are best explained by which principle?
options: Breakdown of the blood-brain barrier from inflammation
The Monro-Kellie doctrine: loss of CSF volume is compensated by venous/dural engorgement and brain descent
Increased CSF production overwhelming reabsorption
Vasogenic edema from malignant hypertension
stages.explanation: The Monro-Kellie doctrine states the intracranial compartment is fixed in volume, so when CSF volume falls, the other compartments compensate—venous structures and the dura engorge (producing smooth pachymeningeal enhancement and an enlarged pituitary) and the brain descends ('sag'), sometimes opening subdural spaces that fill with fluid. Inflammatory blood-brain-barrier breakdown (option A) produces leptomeningeal/parenchymal, not smooth dural, enhancement. Increased CSF production (option C) would raise, not lower, pressure. Malignant hypertension with vasogenic edema (option D) is unrelated to the postural, low-pressure picture here.

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous intracranial hypotension from a spinal CSF leak
pearls: Orthostatic headache (worse upright, relieved supine) is the hallmark of intracranial hypotension—the mirror image of IIH.
Brain MRI shows diffuse SMOOTH pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag, and subdural collections, explained by the Monro-Kellie doctrine.
Epidural blood patch is first-line definitive therapy; avoid shunting or acetazolamide, which are for HIGH-pressure states.
Connective-tissue laxity (e.g., Ehlers-Danlos/Marfan spectrum) predisposes to spontaneous spinal CSF leaks.

cases.title: Slowly Worsening Leg Stiffness and a Sensory Line
cases.chiefComplaint: She reports a year of progressively stiff, weak legs and a 'band' of numbness across her trunk.
cases.history: A 63-year-old woman presents with roughly 12 months of slowly progressive leg weakness and stiffness, now causing her to trip and to walk with a stiff, scissoring gait. She describes a 'tight band' of altered sensation around her lower chest and numbness/tingling in both legs that has crept upward over months. She has also noted recent urinary urgency. Symptoms are gradual and steadily worsening, without remissions. Past medical history includes osteoporosis and hypothyroidism. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, alendronate weekly, calcium/vitamin D. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social history: she is a retired librarian, non-smoker, rare alcohol. Family history is non-contributory. She denies back trauma, fever, weight loss, night sweats, or bowel incontinence. There is no history of cancer. She specifically denies arm symptoms, facial numbness, or visual disturbance, and reports no neck pain. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: Mental status and cranial nerves are normal, including normal arm strength and reflexes. Lower extremities show spastic paraparesis: increased tone, 4-/5 strength symmetrically, hyperreflexic patellar and Achilles jerks, sustained ankle clonus, and bilateral upgoing plantar (Babinski) responses. There is a sensory level to pinprick and temperature at approximately T8, with diminished vibration and proprioception in the toes. Upper extremity sensation, strength, and reflexes are normal. Gait is stiff and scissoring with reduced foot clearance. There is no spinal tenderness to palpation. Rectal tone is intact. No clonus or hyperreflexia is present in the arms.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: A spastic paraparesis with hyperreflexia, upgoing toes, and a T8 sensory level, with NORMAL arm findings, localizes the lesion to where?
options: The cervical spinal cord
The thoracic spinal cord (around T8)
A peripheral polyneuropathy
The cerebral cortex (parasagittal)
stages.explanation: Upper-motor-neuron signs in the legs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, clonus, Babinski) plus a discrete sensory level at T8 and sparing of the arms localize the lesion to the thoracic cord at roughly T8—the cord level just above the sensory level. A cervical cord lesion (option A) would also produce arm signs (hyperreflexia/weakness), which are absent here. A peripheral polyneuropathy (option C) causes lower-motor-neuron findings (hypo/areflexia, distal sensory loss) without a sharp sensory level or upgoing toes. A parasagittal cortical lesion (option D) can cause bilateral leg weakness but would not give a truncal sensory level.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the best imaging study to evaluate this suspected thoracic cord compression?
options: Plain thoracic spine radiographs
MRI of the thoracic spine with and without gadolinium
CT of the thoracic spine without contrast
Nerve conduction studies/EMG
stages.explanation: MRI of the thoracic spine with and without gadolinium is the study of choice—it visualizes the cord, defines an intradural-extramedullary mass, shows contrast enhancement and a dural tail, and excludes intramedullary or extradural pathology. Plain radiographs (option A) show bony alignment and gross destruction but cannot image the cord or a soft-tissue/dural tumor. Non-contrast CT (option C) is good for bone but poor for cord and meningeal lesions and misses enhancement. EMG/nerve conduction studies (option D) assess peripheral nerves and would be normal/unrevealing in a compressive central cord process.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine with contrast: well-circumscribed, ovoid INTRADURAL-EXTRAMEDULLARY mass at T7–T8, ~1.8 cm.
The mass is isointense on T1, mildly hyperintense on T2, and enhances homogeneously and avidly with gadolinium.
There is a broad dural attachment with a 'dural tail.'
The spinal cord is displaced and compressed to the contralateral side with surrounding T2 cord signal change.
No bony destruction; no other lesions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An intradural-extramedullary, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail in a 63-year-old woman is most likely which tumor?
options: Spinal meningioma
Ependymoma
Metastatic carcinoma to the vertebral body
Schwannoma of a nerve root
stages.explanation: An intradural-extramedullary, broad-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a DURAL TAIL in a middle-aged-to-older woman is the classic profile of a spinal meningioma—the most common benign intradural cord tumor, with a strong female predominance and a predilection for the thoracic spine. Ependymoma (option B) is typically INTRAMEDULLARY (within the cord) in adults or arises at the filum/conus, not dural-based. Metastatic carcinoma (option C) is EXTRADURAL, centered in the vertebral body with bony destruction. Schwannoma (option D) is also intradural-extramedullary but characteristically follows a nerve root, may be dumbbell-shaped through a neural foramen, and lacks a true dural tail.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a symptomatic compressive thoracic meningioma with progressive myelopathy, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Microsurgical resection of the tumor
Observation with serial MRI
Upfront stereotactic radiosurgery instead of surgery
Systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: A symptomatic spinal meningioma causing progressive cord compression and myelopathy should undergo microsurgical resection; complete (Simpson grade I–II) removal with the involved dura is typically curative and yields excellent neurologic recovery, especially when done before severe deficits set in. Observation (option B) is reserved for small, asymptomatic, incidentally found lesions—not a tumor causing progressive paraparesis. Radiosurgery (option C) is an adjunct for residual/recurrent tumor or poor surgical candidates, not first-line for a resectable symptomatic lesion. Chemotherapy (option D) has no established role for benign meningiomas.

cases.diagnosis: Thoracic intradural-extramedullary spinal meningioma
pearls: Spastic paraparesis with a truncal sensory level and normal arms localizes to the thoracic cord; cervical lesions would involve the arms.
Spinal meningioma is the most common benign intradural-extramedullary cord tumor, with a strong female and thoracic predominance.
MRI shows a dural-based, homogeneously enhancing mass with a dural tail; contrast distinguishes it from intramedullary ependymoma and extradural metastases.
Microsurgical resection of a symptomatic spinal meningioma is usually curative with excellent recovery if done before severe deficits develop.

cases.title: Back Pain, Saddle Numbness, and a Disc That Wasn't
cases.chiefComplaint: He has months of low back pain with numbness in the 'saddle' area and new erectile dysfunction.
cases.history: A 37-year-old man presents with 8 months of dull, aching low back pain that is worse at night and not clearly related to activity, along with progressive numbness over the perineum/'saddle' region and the back of both thighs. Over the last 2 months he has developed erectile dysfunction and a sense of incomplete bladder emptying. He was previously told he had a 'disc problem' and tried physical therapy and NSAIDs without improvement. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: ibuprofen as needed. Allergies: none. Social history: he is a software engineer, non-smoker, social drinker, recreationally active. Family history is non-contributory. He denies fever, weight loss, trauma, or prior cancer. He notes the pain is not relieved by rest and sometimes wakes him at night, which struck his physician as atypical for ordinary mechanical back pain. He denies frank urinary or fecal incontinence and denies leg weakness. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: Mental status and cranial nerves are normal. Upper extremities are normal. Lower extremity strength is largely preserved (5/5 proximally, 4+/5 ankle plantarflexion bilaterally) with normal tone. There is decreased pinprick sensation over the perineum and posterior thighs in an S2–S5 'saddle' distribution. Ankle (Achilles) reflexes are diminished bilaterally; patellar reflexes are normal; plantar responses are downgoing. Straight-leg raise is mildly uncomfortable but non-radicular. Rectal tone is mildly reduced and the bulbocavernosus reflex is blunted. There is no spinal tenderness and no clonus or hyperreflexia (no upper-motor-neuron signs).

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Saddle anesthesia, reduced ankle reflexes, blunted bulbocavernosus reflex, and sexual/bladder dysfunction WITHOUT upper-motor-neuron signs localize the lesion to where?
options: The thoracic spinal cord
The conus medullaris / cauda equina (filum terminale region)
The cervical cord
A length-dependent peripheral neuropathy
stages.explanation: Saddle (S2–S5) sensory loss, sphincter/sexual dysfunction, diminished ankle reflexes, and a blunted bulbocavernosus reflex with NO upper-motor-neuron signs localize to the conus medullaris/cauda equina at the lumbosacral termination of the cord (filum terminale region). A thoracic cord lesion (option A) would produce spastic paraparesis with a truncal sensory level and UMN signs. A cervical lesion (option C) would involve the arms and produce UMN findings. A length-dependent peripheral neuropathy (option D) causes symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss, not a discrete saddle pattern with sphincter involvement.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which imaging study best evaluates a suspected conus/filum terminale tumor in this patient?
options: Lumbar spine radiographs
MRI of the lumbosacral spine with and without contrast
CT lumbar spine without contrast
Bone scan
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the test of choice—it depicts an intradural mass at the conus/filum, characterizes enhancement, and defines the relationship to the cauda equina nerve roots for surgical planning. Plain radiographs (option A) cannot image intradural soft tissue or the cord. Non-contrast CT (option C) is poor for intradural lesions and misses enhancement. A bone scan (option D) detects osteoblastic bony pathology and would not reveal an intradural filum tumor; it is not appropriate here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI lumbosacral spine with contrast: an intradural, sausage-shaped mass at the L1–L2 level arising from the filum terminale/conus region, ~3 cm.
The mass is hyperintense on T2, heterogeneous on T1, and enhances avidly with gadolinium.
No bony destruction; the disc spaces are normal (no herniation).
The cauda equina nerve roots are draped around the mass.
No other spinal lesions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An enhancing intradural mass of the filum terminale in a 37-year-old, with the 'disc' workup negative, is most consistent with which tumor?
options: Myxopapillary ependymoma
Spinal meningioma
Herniated nucleus pulposus
Metastatic prostate carcinoma
stages.explanation: Myxopapillary ependymoma is the characteristic tumor of the filum terminale/conus region, classically in young-to-middle-aged adults, presenting with chronic back pain and conus/cauda symptoms and appearing as an enhancing, sometimes hemorrhagic intradural filum mass—exactly this picture. A spinal meningioma (option B) is dural-based and far more common in the thoracic spine of older women, not the filum. A herniated disc (option C) is excluded—the discs are normal and the lesion is an enhancing intradural mass, not extruded disc material. Metastatic prostate cancer (option D) is implausible in a 37-year-old and would be extradural/bony, not an intradural filum mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important surgical principle when resecting a myxopapillary ependymoma of the filum terminale?
options: Piecemeal debulking is preferred to protect the nerve roots
Complete en bloc resection of the encapsulated tumor, because piecemeal removal/capsule violation seeds CSF dissemination and recurrence
Biopsy only, followed by chemotherapy
Radiation alone without any attempt at resection
stages.explanation: The key principle is complete EN BLOC resection of the encapsulated myxopapillary ependymoma: gross-total removal without violating the capsule is associated with the best outcomes, whereas piecemeal removal or capsule rupture spills tumor cells and seeds CSF dissemination and local recurrence. Routine piecemeal debulking (option A) is exactly what increases recurrence and is avoided when en bloc removal is feasible. Biopsy plus chemotherapy (option C) is not the primary strategy—these tumors are surgically resectable and chemotherapy is not standard first-line. Radiation alone (option D) is reserved as adjuvant therapy for subtotal resection or recurrence, not as a substitute for resection.

cases.diagnosis: Myxopapillary ependymoma of the filum terminale / conus
pearls: Saddle anesthesia with sphincter/sexual dysfunction and lower-motor-neuron signs (reduced ankle reflexes, blunted bulbocavernosus) localizes to the conus/cauda.
Night pain and back pain unrelieved by rest are red flags that argue against simple mechanical disc disease.
Myxopapillary ependymoma is the classic enhancing intradural tumor of the filum terminale in young-to-middle-aged adults.
En bloc resection is curative; piecemeal removal or capsule violation seeds CSF dissemination and recurrence.

cases.title: A Clumsy Hand and an Aching Back of the Head
cases.chiefComplaint: She describes a year of suboccipital pain and a progressively 'clumsy' hand with creeping weakness.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with about 14 months of insidious, dull suboccipital and upper-neck pain, along with a gradually worsening 'clumsy' right hand—she drops objects and struggles with buttons. Over time she noticed weakness that seemed to begin in one arm, then involved the ipsilateral leg, then the contralateral leg and finally the other arm in a 'going-around' pattern. She also reports numbness and a sense of imbalance, and her family notes she occasionally has jerky downward eye movements. She was previously evaluated for cervical spondylosis and even considered for an MS workup. Past medical history: hypertension. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social history: she is an accountant, non-smoker, occasional alcohol. Family history is non-contributory. She denies bowel or bladder incontinence, facial pain, vertigo with positional change, dysphagia, or prior trauma. She reports the symptoms are slowly progressive without true relapses or remissions, which prompted reconsideration of the MS diagnosis. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: She is alert and oriented. There is downbeat nystagmus on lateral and downward gaze. Lower cranial nerves are intact; tongue and palate move symmetrically. Motor exam shows asymmetric weakness in a 'rotatory' pattern—4/5 in the right arm and right leg, 4+/5 in the left leg, with the left arm last/least affected. Tone is increased and reflexes are hyperactive in the affected limbs with bilateral Babinski signs. There is impaired joint-position and vibration sense in the hands. Finger-nose testing shows mild dysmetria and the right hand is notably clumsy. Gait is unsteady. No facial sensory loss; no papilledema.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: The combination of suboccipital pain, a 'rotatory' (around-the-clock) pattern of limb involvement, downbeat nystagmus, and hand clumsiness localizes the lesion to where?
options: The mid-thoracic spinal cord
The craniocervical junction / foramen magnum region
The internal capsule
A peripheral entrapment neuropathy
stages.explanation: Suboccipital pain, a classic 'rotatory' or 'going-around-the-clock' pattern of weakness (one limb, then ipsilateral limb, then contralateral limbs), early hand clumsiness/intrinsic atrophy, dorsal-column dysfunction, and—critically—DOWNBEAT NYSTAGMUS together localize to the craniocervical junction/foramen magnum, where a lesion compresses the cervicomedullary junction. A mid-thoracic lesion (option A) gives a truncal sensory level and spares the arms and produces no nystagmus. An internal-capsule lesion (option C) causes contralateral hemiparesis acutely (stroke), not this slow rotatory march with downbeat nystagmus. A peripheral entrapment (option D) cannot explain the central, multi-limb, brainstem findings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which physical finding is the most specific clue pointing to a foramen magnum lesion rather than cervical spondylotic myelopathy or MS?
options: Hyperreflexia in the legs
Downbeat nystagmus
A positive straight-leg raise
Distal sensory loss in the feet
stages.explanation: Downbeat nystagmus is a localizing sign of pathology at the cervicomedullary junction/foramen magnum (and lower brainstem/cerebellar flocculus) and is the most specific clue distinguishing a foramen magnum lesion from cervical spondylosis—which does not produce nystagmus—and helps separate it from MS. Leg hyperreflexia (option A) is a nonspecific upper-motor-neuron sign present in any compressive myelopathy. A positive straight-leg raise (option C) suggests lumbar radiculopathy, not a craniocervical lesion. Distal foot sensory loss (option D) is nonspecific and seen in many conditions including peripheral neuropathy.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI brain and craniocervical junction with contrast: well-circumscribed, broad-based extra-axial mass at the anterior foramen magnum, ~2.5 cm.
The mass enhances homogeneously and avidly and shows a dural tail.
It compresses and displaces the cervicomedullary junction posteriorly and to one side.
The vertebral artery is encased on the side of the tumor.
No intramedullary signal abnormality of demyelinating type; no other lesions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS CONFIRMATION
stages.question: A homogeneously enhancing, dural-based extra-axial mass with a dural tail at the foramen magnum is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Foramen magnum meningioma
Multiple sclerosis plaque
Cervical disc herniation
Vertebral artery dissection
stages.explanation: A dural-based, homogeneously and avidly enhancing extra-axial mass with a dural tail at the foramen magnum is the classic appearance of a foramen magnum (craniocervical junction) meningioma, which characteristically mimics cervical myelopathy and MS clinically. An MS plaque (option B) is an intramedullary/intraparenchymal demyelinating lesion, not an enhancing extra-axial dural mass, and the imaging shows no such lesions. A cervical disc herniation (option C) is extradural disc material at a disc level, not a dural-tail-bearing mass at the foramen magnum. A vertebral artery dissection (option D) is a vascular lesion of the artery wall, not an enhancing dural mass.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this symptomatic foramen magnum meningioma causing progressive myelopathy?
options: Microsurgical resection (typically via a far-lateral/suboccipital approach)
Observation with annual MRI
Empiric high-dose corticosteroids for presumed MS
Cervical traction and a soft collar
stages.explanation: A symptomatic foramen magnum meningioma producing progressive neurologic decline should undergo microsurgical resection; a far-lateral or extreme-lateral suboccipital approach provides access to these typically anterior/anterolateral lesions and allows safe decompression of the cervicomedullary junction, often with good recovery if done before severe deficits. Observation (option B) is reserved for small, asymptomatic, incidental lesions—not a progressively disabling one. High-dose steroids for 'MS' (option C) misdiagnoses a structural compressive tumor and delays definitive care. Cervical traction/collar (option D) is for mechanical neck pain and would not treat—and could endanger—a compressive craniocervical mass.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because this anterior foramen magnum meningioma encases the vertebral artery and abuts the lower brainstem, which postoperative complication should the team be most prepared to monitor for?
options: Lower cranial nerve dysfunction causing dysphagia and aspiration
Acute angle-closure glaucoma
Lumbar radiculopathy
Carpal tunnel syndrome
stages.explanation: Surgery at the craniocervical junction risks injury to the lower cranial nerves (IX, X, XI, XII) running nearby, so postoperative lower-cranial-nerve dysfunction—producing dysphagia, impaired airway protection, and aspiration—is a key complication to anticipate, often warranting a careful swallow evaluation and aspiration precautions before resuming oral intake. Vertebral artery manipulation also raises a risk of brainstem/cerebellar ischemia. Acute angle-closure glaucoma (option B) is an unrelated ophthalmologic emergency. Lumbar radiculopathy (option C) involves the lumbosacral roots, far from this operative field. Carpal tunnel syndrome (option D) is a peripheral median-nerve entrapment with no connection to a foramen magnum operation.

cases.diagnosis: Foramen magnum (craniocervical junction) meningioma
pearls: The classic foramen magnum syndrome: suboccipital pain, a 'rotatory' march of weakness around the limbs, hand clumsiness, and dorsal-column loss.
Downbeat nystagmus is a specific localizing sign of cervicomedullary/foramen magnum pathology, helping distinguish it from cervical spondylosis and MS.
MRI shows a dural-based, homogeneously enhancing extra-axial mass with a dural tail, often anterior and encasing the vertebral artery.
Symptomatic foramen magnum meningiomas are resected via a far-lateral/suboccipital approach with good outcomes when treated early.

cases.title: Pulsatile Ear Noise and a Hoarse, Choking Voice
cases.chiefComplaint: She has a pulsing noise in one ear plus a worsening hoarse voice and trouble swallowing.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents with about a year of a 'pulsing' or 'whooshing' noise in her left ear that beats with her heart, accompanied by progressive hearing decline on that side. Over the past several months she developed a hoarse, breathy voice, episodes of choking and coughing when drinking liquids, and a sensation that food sticks in her throat. She has also noticed her tongue feels 'off.' Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social history: she is a teacher, non-smoker, occasional wine; no family history of similar tumors, though she vaguely recalls a relative with a 'neck tumor.' Family history is otherwise non-contributory. She denies vertigo, facial weakness, severe headache, or weight loss. She reports no flushing, palpitations, or episodic hypertension. An ENT colleague noted an unusual reddish-blue mass behind the eardrum on otoscopy, prompting referral. Review of systems is otherwise unremarkable.
cases.exam: Otoscopy reveals a pulsatile reddish-blue retrotympanic mass behind the intact left tympanic membrane that blanches with pneumatic pressure (Brown sign). There is left conductive/sensorineural hearing loss. A bruit is audible over the left mastoid. Cranial nerve exam shows left vocal cord weakness with a hoarse, breathy voice (CN X), reduced palatal elevation with uvular deviation to the right and a diminished gag (CN IX/X), and left tongue deviation on protrusion with mild atrophy (CN XII). The sternocleidomastoid/trapezius strength is mildly reduced on the left (CN XI). Facial strength, eye movements, and corneal reflex are intact. The remainder of the neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: LOCALIZATION
stages.question: Pulsatile tinnitus plus deficits of CN IX, X, XI (and adjacent XII) on the same side localize the lesion to which region?
options: The cerebellopontine angle (CN VII/VIII)
The jugular foramen / skull base
The cavernous sinus
The internal auditory canal only
