The hindbrain (Chiari II) abnormality does not simply resolve and can cause brainstem symptoms such as stridor, apnea, and swallowing difficulty. AFP is a fetal protein marker unrelated to thyroid function. Lifelong multidisciplinary care also addresses neurogenic bladder and bowel, orthopedic deformities, and later tethered cord. Anticipating hydrocephalus is the key early postoperative concern.

cases.diagnosis: Myelomeningocele (open neural tube defect) with Chiari II malformation
pearls: Open myelomeningocele: protect the placode with a moist sterile dressing, position prone, use latex-free precautions, and close within ~48 hours.
Elevated maternal serum/amniotic AFP and amniotic acetylcholinesterase signal an open neural tube defect; prenatal US shows 'lemon' and 'banana' signs.
Nearly all have a Chiari II malformation; hydrocephalus commonly develops after closure and may need a VP shunt.
Folic acid before and during pregnancy prevents neural tube defects; lifelong multidisciplinary care is needed.

cases.title: Stridor, blue spells, and choking feeds in a shunted infant
cases.chiefComplaint: NICU nurse reports high-pitched noisy breathing, choking with feeds, and two apneic episodes with desaturation overnight.
cases.history: This 2-month-old boy was born at 37 weeks with a lumbar myelomeningocele diagnosed on the prenatal anatomy scan; the defect was closed on day of life 1, and a ventriculoperitoneal shunt was placed on day 5 for progressive ventriculomegaly. He has remained in the NICU for feeding support. Over the past week the nursing staff has documented progressive inspiratory stridor that worsens with agitation, a newly weak and hoarse cry, prolonged feeds with nasal regurgitation of formula, and coughing or choking with swallowing. Overnight he had two apneic spells lasting 20-30 seconds with desaturation to the 70s and bradycardia to the 80s, requiring tactile stimulation. His mother has also noticed intermittent backward arching of the head and neck. He has had no fever, no vomiting between feeds, and no seizure-like activity. He takes no medications other than vitamin D; he has no known allergies. Immunizations are up to date for age. Family history is noncontributory; his mother did not take periconceptional folate.
cases.exam: Alert but irritable infant with audible inspiratory stridor at rest and a weak, hoarse cry. Anterior fontanelle is soft and flat; head circumference tracks along its prior curve. The shunt valve depresses and refills briskly; the tract is without swelling. Lumbar wound is well healed. Gag reflex is absent and oral secretions pool. Suck is weak. Intermittent opisthotonic posturing is observed. Tone is mildly increased in the upper extremities with spontaneous antigravity movement; lower extremities move at the hips with the known L4-level deficit below. No fever, no abdominal distension or pseudocyst on palpation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A shunted infant with myelomeningocele develops stridor, apneic spells, and feeding difficulty. What is the most appropriate first step?
options: Proceed directly to posterior fossa decompression
Evaluate the shunt with a shunt series, cranial imaging, and assessment of shunt function
Refer for flexible laryngoscopy alone and treat as laryngomalacia
Begin thickened feeds and acid suppression for presumed reflux
Perform a lumbar puncture to measure opening pressure
stages.explanation: In any shunted myelomeningocele infant who develops brainstem symptoms, shunt malfunction must be excluded first — raised intracranial pressure can drive the hindbrain caudally and produce identical stridor, apnea, and dysphagia, and a shunt revision is far safer than an unnecessary posterior fossa operation. A shunt series, cranial imaging compared with priors, and functional assessment (tap if needed) are the correct opening moves. Immediate decompression (option A) is premature before shunt failure is excluded. Laryngoscopy (C) is a useful adjunct to document vocal cord function but does not address the cause. Reflux therapy (D) ignores the apnea and absent gag, which signal lower cranial nerve dysfunction. Lumbar puncture (E) is contraindicated through a repaired myelomeningocele and risks herniation with hindbrain crowding.

stages.label: SHUNT WORKUP & IMAGING
stages.content:
Shunt series: catheter in continuity, no disconnection or kink
Rapid-sequence MRI brain: slit-like ventricles, unchanged from prior; shunt tip well positioned
MRI craniocervical junction: cerebellar vermis and medulla herniated below foramen magnum to C4, medullary kink, tectal beaking, small C2-C4 syrinx
Shunt tap: brisk proximal flow, opening pressure 8 cm H2O, clear CSF
CSF: WBC 2 /µL, glucose 48 mg/dL, protein 32 mg/dL, Gram stain negative
Flexible laryngoscopy: bilateral vocal cord abductor paresis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: With a functioning shunt, stable small ventricles, and these MRI and laryngoscopy findings, what best explains this infant's stridor, apnea, and dysphagia?
options: Gastroesophageal reflux with recurrent microaspiration
Occult shunt failure with intermittently raised intracranial pressure
Congenital laryngomalacia with positional airway collapse
Brainstem dysfunction from hindbrain herniation (Chiari II malformation)
Subclinical seizures with ictal apnea
stages.explanation: The triad of inspiratory stridor (vagal/abductor paresis), central apneic spells, and neurogenic dysphagia in a myelomeningocele infant is the classic symptomatic Chiari II presentation: the caudally displaced medulla and lower cranial nerves are compressed and intrinsically dysmorphic, and MRI confirms vermian and medullary herniation with a medullary kink. Bilateral abductor cord paresis on laryngoscopy localizes to the tenth nerve nuclei. Reflux (A) does not cause bilateral cord paresis or opisthotonus. Shunt failure (B) has been reasonably excluded by stable ventricles, a patent system, and normal tap pressure. Laryngomalacia (C) causes stridor but not absent gag, apnea, or arching. Ictal apnea (E) would not explain the cranial nerve findings or the structural abnormality demonstrated.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Symptoms progress over 48 hours despite the confirmed working shunt, with more frequent apneic spells. What is the most appropriate intervention?
options: Cervicomedullary (posterior fossa and upper cervical) bony decompression
Empiric shunt revision despite the normal workup
Tracheostomy and gastrostomy alone, deferring any cranial surgery indefinitely
High-dose dexamethasone and observation in the NICU
Continued apnea monitoring with caffeine therapy only
stages.explanation: Once shunt malfunction is convincingly excluded, a progressively symptomatic Chiari II infant needs urgent cervicomedullary decompression — suboccipital bony decompression with cervical laminectomies to the level of the herniated tissue — because brainstem dysfunction in infancy can be rapidly fatal, and earlier surgery before prolonged or fixed deficits gives the best chance of recovery. Empiric shunt revision (B) is reasonable only when shunt function is uncertain; here flow and pressure were verified. Tracheostomy and gastrostomy (C) palliate the airway and nutrition but do not treat the progressive compression and may still be needed adjunctively. Steroids (D) have no role in this structural problem. Caffeine and monitoring (E) address apnea of prematurity, not symptomatic brainstem compression.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which preoperative finding most strongly predicts a poor outcome even after technically successful decompression?
options: Size of the small cervical syrinx
Age at myelomeningocele closure
Bilateral vocal cord paralysis with prolonged apneic spells
Degree of tectal beaking on MRI
Slit-like ventricular size
stages.explanation: Neonates and young infants with established bilateral vocal cord paralysis and recurrent prolonged apnea have the worst prognosis: these findings often reflect intrinsic, dysplastic brainstem nuclei and ischemic or hemorrhagic injury rather than purely extrinsic compression, so decompression may not reverse them, and mortality in this subgroup remains substantial. Rapid progression and need for ventilatory support compound the risk. Syrinx size (A) at this scale does not drive infant survival. Timing of back closure (B) influences infection risk, not later brainstem outcome. Tectal beaking (D) is a near-universal anatomic marker of Chiari II without independent prognostic weight. Small ventricles (E) here simply confirm the shunt is working and carry no adverse prognostic meaning.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After decompression his stridor improves, but at two weeks he still has a weak suck, intermittent nasal regurgitation, and occasional brief desaturations with feeds. What is the best next step?
options: Discharge home with ad lib oral feeding and routine follow-up
Gastrostomy tube placement after a formal swallow evaluation, with continued cardiorespiratory monitoring
Immediate re-exploration and wider decompression
Nissen fundoplication alone without addressing feeding route
Long-term parenteral nutrition via central line
stages.explanation: Lower cranial nerve function recovers slowly and incompletely after Chiari II decompression, and ongoing neurogenic dysphagia carries a high aspiration risk in infants. A videofluoroscopic swallow study to document the deficit followed by gastrostomy feeding protects the airway while allowing growth, and continued monitoring captures residual central apnea; many infants later regain enough function for oral feeding. Discharging with oral feeding (A) invites aspiration pneumonia and failure to thrive. Re-exploration (C) is not indicated when the patient is improving and imaging shows adequate decompression. Fundoplication alone (D) treats reflux but not the unsafe swallow. Chronic parenteral nutrition (E) carries line sepsis and liver risks when the gut works and enteral access solves the problem.

cases.diagnosis: Symptomatic Chiari II malformation with brainstem (lower cranial nerve) dysfunction in a shunted myelomeningocele infant, after exclusion of shunt malfunction
pearls: In a shunted myelomeningocele infant with new stridor, apnea, or dysphagia, always exclude shunt malfunction before blaming the Chiari II hindbrain hernia.
Symptomatic Chiari II in infancy is a neurosurgical emergency: progressive brainstem signs warrant urgent cervicomedullary decompression.
Bilateral vocal cord paralysis with prolonged apnea predicts poor outcome because the brainstem nuclei are often intrinsically dysplastic, not just compressed.
Chiari II is essentially confined to patients with myelomeningocele — vermian herniation, medullary kink, and tectal beaking are its MRI signature.

cases.title: Neck pain after diving into a shallow pool
cases.chiefComplaint: "I dove into the shallow end and hit the top of my head — now my neck kills and I feel like I have to hold my head up with my hands."
cases.history: A 20-year-old college swimmer arrives by EMS after diving headfirst into the shallow end of a backyard pool roughly 45 minutes ago. He struck the crown of his head on the pool floor with his neck in neutral position. He did not lose consciousness and climbed out himself, but immediately developed severe upper neck pain and a sensation of instability, describing the urge to support his head with both hands. He reports a pressure-like occipital headache and pain radiating from the suboccipital region toward the vertex. He denies numbness, tingling, weakness, electric-shock sensations, difficulty breathing, or trouble swallowing. He has no double vision, dizziness, or vomiting. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He drank two beers earlier in the afternoon but is clinically sober. He smokes occasionally and denies other drug use. Family history is noncontributory. EMS placed a rigid cervical collar at the scene and transported him on a backboard, which has since been removed per protocol.
cases.exam: Awake, alert, anxious young man in a rigid collar, supporting his occiput against the stretcher. There is marked tenderness over the upper cervical midline and suboccipital region without palpable step-off; an abrasion covers the vertex of the scalp. Motor strength is 5/5 in deltoids, biceps, triceps, grip, hip flexors, and ankle dorsiflexors bilaterally. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact in all dermatomes, including greater occipital distribution. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses are flexor. Rectal tone is normal. Cranial nerves are intact. Range-of-motion testing is deliberately not performed.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for this patient?
options: Three-view plain radiographs including an open-mouth odontoid view
MRI of the cervical spine
Noncontrast CT of the cervical spine with multiplanar reconstructions
Flexion-extension lateral radiographs
CT angiography of the neck as the sole study
stages.explanation: A high-risk axial-load mechanism with midline tenderness mandates imaging, and multidetector CT with sagittal and coronal reconstructions is the modern study of choice — it detects upper cervical fractures with far greater sensitivity than radiographs and directly measures lateral mass displacement. Plain films with an open-mouth view (A) are the historical basis of the rule of Spence but miss a meaningful fraction of C1 injuries. MRI (B) is invaluable later for ligamentous assessment but is not the first test and is poor for bone detail. Flexion-extension views (D) are dangerous before bony injury is excluded in an acutely painful neck. CT angiography (E) screens for vertebral artery injury once a qualifying fracture is found, but it does not replace the diagnostic spine CT.

stages.label: CT FINDINGS
stages.content:
CT cervical spine: four-part burst fracture of the atlas — bilateral fractures of the anterior and posterior arches of C1
Coronal reconstruction: combined lateral mass overhang of C1 on C2 measuring 8.5 mm
Atlantodental interval: 4 mm
No fracture of C2 or the subaxial spine; occipital condyles intact
CT angiography neck: vertebral and carotid arteries patent, no dissection

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: What is the significance of the 8.5 mm combined lateral mass overhang on the coronal CT?
options: Combined displacement above roughly 7 mm suggests transverse atlantal ligament disruption, indicating a potentially unstable injury
It indicates atlantoaxial rotatory subluxation requiring derotation under anesthesia
Any C1 burst fracture is unstable regardless of displacement, so the measurement adds nothing
The overhang quantifies the risk of vertebral artery injury rather than ligament integrity
It is a normal variant in young athletes with ligamentous laxity
stages.explanation: The rule of Spence holds that combined lateral mass displacement of C1 over C2 greater than about 6.9 mm (classically 8.1 mm on radiographs after magnification correction) implies the transverse atlantal ligament has ruptured, converting a typically stable C1 burst into an unstable atlantoaxial injury; the elevated atlantodental interval of 4 mm supports this. The spreading lateral masses can only displace that far if the ligament tethering the dens has failed. Rotatory subluxation (B) is a different entity with asymmetric lateral mass-dens relationships, usually in children. Most isolated C1 fractures with an intact ligament are stable and heal in a collar, so option C is false. The overhang says nothing about arterial injury (D), which CTA already excluded. An 8.5 mm overhang is never a normal variant (E).

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the CT findings, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Apply a halo vest immediately without further imaging
Obtain upright flexion-extension radiographs to stress the ligament
Begin cervical traction at the bedside
Obtain MRI of the cervical spine to directly assess transverse ligament integrity
Discharge in a soft collar with outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: The rule of Spence is an indirect, insensitive surrogate; modern management hinges on the actual state of the transverse atlantal ligament, which MRI demonstrates directly and which determines collar versus fusion. MRI also classifies the injury (Dickman type I midsubstance tear versus type II bony avulsion from the lateral mass tubercle), a distinction with directly opposite treatment implications. Committing to a halo (A) before knowing ligament status risks both over- and under-treatment. Flexion-extension films (B) deliberately stress a potentially unstable atlantoaxial segment and are unsafe acutely. Traction (C) has no role in an isolated C1 burst and can distract the occipitocervical junction. Discharge in a soft collar (E) would be negligent with evidence of ligamentous incompetence.

stages.label: MRI FINDINGS
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: discontinuity of the transverse atlantal ligament at its midsubstance (Dickman type I)
No bony avulsion of the lateral mass tubercle
Spinal cord: normal caliber and signal, no compression
Prevertebral edema from C1 to C3, no epidural hematoma

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management of this injury?
options: Rigid cervical collar for 8-12 weeks with serial upright radiographs
Posterior C1-C2 instrumented fusion
Halo vest immobilization with the expectation that the ligament will heal
Anterior odontoid screw fixation
Six weeks of inpatient cervical traction followed by collar
stages.explanation: A midsubstance (Dickman type I) transverse ligament tear is a purely ligamentous injury with essentially no capacity to heal, so external immobilization of any kind leaves the atlantoaxial joint permanently unstable; posterior C1-C2 instrumented fusion (for example Goel-Harms screw fixation) is the standard of care. A collar (A) suffices only for stable C1 fractures with an intact ligament. A halo (C) is a reasonable trial for Dickman type II bony avulsions, which can unite, but not for intrasubstance tears. An odontoid screw (D) treats type II dens fractures — the dens here is intact. Prolonged traction (E) is an obsolete strategy that adds immobility complications without producing ligament healing.

cases.diagnosis: Jefferson burst fracture of C1 with transverse atlantal ligament rupture (Dickman type I) — unstable, requiring C1-C2 fusion
pearls: Jefferson fractures result from axial loading (diving, spearing); patients are usually neurologically intact because the bursting arch widens the canal.
Rule of Spence: combined C1 lateral mass overhang >6.9 mm (≈8.1 mm on magnified radiographs) implies transverse ligament rupture and instability.
MRI directly classifies transverse ligament injury: midsubstance tears (Dickman I) need C1-C2 fusion; bony avulsions (Dickman II) may heal in rigid immobilization.
C1-C3 fractures meet Denver criteria for vertebral artery injury screening — add CT angiography.

cases.title: Severe neck pain after a head-on collision
cases.chiefComplaint: "My chin slammed into the dashboard when I hit the truck — my upper neck hurts like crazy."
cases.history: A 44-year-old delivery driver presents after a frontal motor vehicle collision at approximately 35 mph. He was unbelted; on impact he was thrown forward and his chin struck the dashboard, forcing his head into hyperextension. There was no loss of consciousness and the airbag did not deploy. He self-extricated and was ambulatory at the scene, but EMS placed a rigid collar for severe upper posterior neck pain that worsens with any attempted movement. He describes the pain as sharp, localized just below the skull, radiating into the occiput, rated 8/10. He denies weakness, numbness, paresthesias, electric-shock sensations down the spine, difficulty breathing, dysphagia, or change in voice. He has a small chin laceration. Past medical history includes hypertension treated with lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. He has no allergies. He smokes half a pack per day for 20 years and drinks socially; he denies drug use. Family history is unremarkable. His last meal was two hours before the crash.
cases.exam: Anxious man in a rigid cervical collar with a 2 cm sutured chin laceration. There is significant tenderness over the upper cervical spine, maximal at C2, without palpable step-off or interspinous gap. No torticollis. Motor examination shows 5/5 strength in all four extremities; sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact C2 through S1. Reflexes are 2+ throughout with downgoing plantar responses. Rectal tone is normal and perianal sensation intact. Cranial nerves II-XII are normal; no Horner syndrome. No carotid bruit or expanding neck hematoma. Chest, abdomen, pelvis, and extremities are without significant injury on secondary survey.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial study to evaluate this patient's cervical spine?
options: Noncontrast CT of the cervical spine with reconstructions
MRI of the cervical spine as the first test
Active flexion-extension radiographs in the ED
Clinical clearance by NEXUS criteria without imaging
Lateral cervical radiograph alone
stages.explanation: A high-energy hyperextension mechanism with focal midline upper cervical tenderness requires CT, which is the gold-standard screening study after blunt trauma — fast, highly sensitive for fracture, and able to characterize displacement and angulation that drive treatment decisions. MRI first (B) is reserved for neurologic deficit, suspected ligamentous or disc injury, or an obtunded patient; it images bone poorly and delays diagnosis. Flexion-extension views (C) are contraindicated before fracture is excluded — actively moving a potentially unstable C2 injury risks cord damage. NEXUS clearance (D) fails immediately because he has midline tenderness and a distracting mechanism. A single lateral film (E) misses a substantial proportion of axis fractures, particularly minimally displaced pars fractures.

stages.label: CT FINDINGS
stages.content:
CT cervical spine: bilateral fractures through the C2 pars interarticularis (pedicle region)
Anterolisthesis of C2 on C3: 2 mm; angulation: 2 degrees
C2-C3 disc space height preserved; facets aligned, no perched or locked facet
Spinal canal at C2 appears widened rather than narrowed
No other cervical fracture; atlantodental interval 2 mm
CT angiography neck: bilateral vertebral arteries patent, no dissection or occlusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following best describes this injury?
options: Type II odontoid fracture
Jefferson burst fracture of C1
Traumatic spondylolisthesis of the axis (hangman's fracture), Levine-Edwards type I
Bilateral C2-C3 facet dislocation
Clay-shoveler fracture of the C7 spinous process
stages.explanation: Bilateral pars interarticularis fractures of C2 with anterolisthesis define traumatic spondylolisthesis of the axis — the hangman's fracture — produced here by hyperextension with axial load from the chin-on-dashboard mechanism. With less than 3 mm of translation and no significant angulation, this is a Levine-Edwards type I injury, the most stable subtype. A type II odontoid fracture (A) traverses the base of the dens, which CT shows to be intact. A Jefferson fracture (B) involves the C1 ring, also intact with a normal atlantodental interval. Facet dislocation (D) is a flexion-distraction injury and the facets are explicitly aligned. A clay-shoveler fracture (E) is an avulsion of a lower cervical spinous process and is irrelevant to these findings.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Despite a fracture through both pars of C2, the patient is neurologically intact. What best explains this?
options: Cord injuries at C2 are uniformly fatal, so all survivors are intact
Bilateral pars fractures separate the posterior elements from the body, effectively widening the canal and auto-decompressing the cord
The spinal cord does not extend as high as the C2 level
Hyperextension mechanisms are incapable of injuring the spinal cord
The vertebral arteries shield the cord from bony fragments at this level
stages.explanation: When both pars fracture, the neural arch of C2 dissociates from the vertebral body, so anterolisthesis of the body actually enlarges rather than compromises the canal — the same biomechanics intended in judicial hanging, where death came from cord distraction, not compression. The canal at C2 is also generously wide relative to the cord. This is why most patients with hangman's fractures present neurologically intact, as the CT comment about a widened canal reflects. Option A is wrong — high cord injuries are survivable, especially incomplete ones. The cord plainly traverses C2 (C); it extends from the foramen magnum to the conus. Hyperextension can certainly injure the cord (D), classically causing central cord syndrome in spondylotic spines. The vertebral arteries (E) offer no mechanical protection and are themselves at risk.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this Levine-Edwards type I injury?
options: Anterior C2-C3 discectomy and fusion
Halo vest application with reduction traction
Posterior C1-C3 instrumented fusion
Rigid cervical orthosis for 6-12 weeks with upright radiographic surveillance
stages.explanation: Type I hangman's fractures — less than 3 mm of translation, minimal angulation, intact C2-C3 disc — are stable injuries that unite reliably with external immobilization alone; a rigid collar for 6-12 weeks with early upright films and interval imaging to confirm maintained alignment and healing is standard, with union rates well above 90%. Anterior C2-C3 fusion (A) is reserved for irreducible type II or type III patterns or failed nonoperative care. A halo with traction (B) is unnecessary for a nondisplaced injury, and traction is specifically dangerous in type IIa fractures, where it distracts the disc and worsens angulation — a reason to characterize the pattern before ever applying weight. Posterior C1-C3 fusion (C) sacrifices substantial rotation for an injury that heals in a collar.

cases.diagnosis: Hangman's fracture — traumatic spondylolisthesis of C2, Levine-Edwards type I — managed with rigid cervical orthosis
pearls: Hangman's fracture is a bilateral C2 pars fracture from hyperextension with axial load (chin-on-dashboard); most patients are neurologically intact because the canal widens.
Levine-Edwards type I (<3 mm translation, no angulation) heals in a rigid collar; type II needs reduction, and traction is contraindicated in type IIa (flexion-distraction) patterns.
C2 fractures trigger vertebral artery injury screening with CT angiography.
Surgery (C2-C3 anterior fusion or posterior fixation) is reserved for type III injuries, irreducible displacement, or failure of external immobilization.

cases.title: Weak arms and legs after going over the handlebars
cases.chiefComplaint: "I flipped over my bike and landed on my head — I can barely move my legs and my hands feel useless."
cases.history: A 28-year-old mountain biker is brought in by EMS after launching over his handlebars on a downhill trail roughly an hour ago and striking the crown of his helmet against the ground with his neck flexed. He did not lose consciousness. Immediately afterward he was unable to stand and noticed weakness in both hands and severe neck pain. EMS immobilized him with a collar. He reports band-like numbness around both forearms and patchy sensation in his legs; he can feel his feet being touched. He denies breathing difficulty but feels his breaths are shallow. There is no headache, vision change, or facial weakness. Past medical history is notable only for childhood asthma; he takes no daily medications and has no allergies. He works as a software engineer, does not smoke, drinks occasionally, and uses no drugs. He was wearing a helmet, now cracked at the vertex. Family history is noncontributory. Tetanus status is current. He is anxious but fully alert, cooperative, and answering questions appropriately throughout transport and arrival.
cases.exam: Alert, cooperative man in rigid collar on full spinal precautions. GCS 15. Motor: deltoids and biceps 4/5 bilaterally; wrist extension 3/5; triceps and grip 2/5; hip flexors and knee extensors 2/5; ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion 1/5. Sensory: diminished pinprick below C6 with patchy preservation of light touch in the legs; perianal sensation intact, voluntary anal contraction weak but present. Reflexes 1+ in arms, absent at knees and ankles; plantar responses mute. Priapism absent. Marked posterior midline tenderness at the mid-cervical spine with a palpable interspinous gap. Skin warm, bradycardic with relative hypotension. Chest, abdomen, pelvis, and long bones without apparent injury.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After the primary survey, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: MRI of the cervical spine as the first imaging study
Five-view plain radiographs of the cervical spine
Bolus of high-dose IV methylprednisolone before any imaging
Maintain rigid immobilization and obtain an urgent noncontrast CT of the cervical spine
Bedside flexion-extension views to assess stability
stages.explanation: This man has an incomplete cervical cord injury after an axial-load flexion mechanism; the priority after ABCs (noting his neurogenic bradycardia and hypotension, which need MAP support) is rapid, accurate definition of the bony injury, and multidetector CT delivers that in minutes. CT defines fracture-dislocation anatomy and guides the reduction strategy. MRI first (A) costs critical time, requires a potentially unstable patient to lie remote from resuscitation, and is not needed before closed reduction in an awake, examinable patient. Plain films (B) are slower and far less sensitive than CT. High-dose methylprednisolone (C) is no longer standard of care for acute spinal cord injury and never precedes imaging. Flexion-extension views (E) in an unstable, neurologically injured spine are frankly dangerous.

stages.label: CT & INITIAL DATA
stages.content:
CT cervical spine: bilateral jumped (locked) facets at C5-C6 with approximately 50% anterolisthesis of C5 on C6
Fractures: small fractures of the superior articular processes of C6 bilaterally
C5-C6 disc space narrowed; canal severely compromised at C5-C6
CT angiography: left vertebral artery with nonocclusive intimal irregularity (grade I injury); right patent
Neurologic status: ASIA C incomplete tetraplegia, sensory level C6
MAP maintained 85-90 mmHg on low-dose norepinephrine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which statement best characterizes this injury?
options: Unilateral facet dislocation, typically causing about 25% anterolisthesis
Bilateral cervical facet dislocation from flexion-distraction, classically displacing the vertebra about 50% with a high rate of disc disruption and cord injury
Burst fracture of C5 from pure axial compression
Hyperextension teardrop injury with intact posterior ligaments
Atlantoaxial rotatory fixation
stages.explanation: Bilateral jumped facets are the hallmark of severe flexion-distraction: both inferior articular processes of C5 have ridden up, over, and in front of the C6 superior facets, locking there. This requires complete failure of the posterior ligamentous complex and usually the disc and longitudinal ligaments, producing the classic ~50% vertebral translation seen here — twice the ~25% typical of a unilateral dislocation (A). The associated disc disruption is precisely why a retropulsed fragment can endanger the cord during reduction, and spinal cord injury accompanies most bilateral dislocations. A burst fracture (C) is a compression injury of the vertebral body without facet dislocation. A hyperextension teardrop (D) is the wrong mechanism and spares posterior ligaments. Rotatory fixation (E) occurs at C1-C2, not C5-C6.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient remains awake, cooperative, and reliably examinable with an incomplete deficit. Regarding reduction, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Obtain MRI before any reduction attempt in all patients with jumped facets
Take the patient directly to the OR for open posterior reduction without a closed attempt
Proceed with immediate awake closed reduction using cranial tongs with sequential weight and serial neurologic examinations
Apply a halo vest in the dislocated position and admit for observation
Continue vasopressors alone and reassess in 24 hours
stages.explanation: In an awake, alert, examinable patient with a cervical facet dislocation and spinal cord injury, immediate closed reduction with Gardner-Wells tongs is the standard: traction is applied in increments with a neurologic exam and lateral fluoroscopy or radiograph after each weight change, and decompression of the cord is achieved fastest this way — speed of reduction correlates with neurologic recovery. Large series show awake closed reduction is safe without pre-reduction MRI because the conscious patient serves as his own monitor; this is the crux of the MRI-first debate. Mandating MRI first (A) applies to obtunded or unexaminable patients, before open reduction, or after failed closed reduction — delaying decompression in an examinable patient with a deteriorating-risk incomplete injury is the greater harm. Primary open reduction (B) skips a faster, effective step. Halo immobilization in situ (D) leaves the cord compressed. Watchful waiting (E) abandons a reversible injury.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: At 40 lb of traction the facets remain perched, and the patient reports new worsening numbness in both hands with a subjective decrease in grip. What should you do?
options: Abort the reduction, remove weight to baseline, and obtain an immediate MRI
Add weight more rapidly to complete the reduction before the deficit progresses
Sedate and intubate the patient so traction can continue without his discomfort
Proceed straight to open posterior reduction without further imaging
stages.explanation: Any new or worsening neurologic finding during closed reduction is an absolute stop signal: weights are reduced, the spine is stabilized, and urgent MRI is obtained to look for the lesion responsible — most importantly a herniated disc fragment that can be driven into the cord as the vertebra translates back. This is exactly the scenario that makes the awake exam the essential safety monitor. Pushing more weight faster (B) risks converting an incomplete injury to a complete one. Sedating and intubating (C) destroys the only monitoring that made awake reduction safe, and is precisely the wrong response to a neurologic change. Open reduction without imaging (D) repeats the same hazard under anesthesia, blind to a fragment MRI would reveal; pre-reduction MRI is mandatory once the patient can no longer be examined or has deteriorated.

stages.label: MRI FINDINGS
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: large extruded C5-C6 disc fragment displaced posterior to the C5 body, compressing the ventral cord
Cord: T2 hyperintensity from C5 to C6 without hemorrhage
Complete disruption of the posterior ligamentous complex and interspinous ligaments at C5-C6
Neurologic exam after weight removal: returned to baseline ASIA C

stages.label: DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the MRI findings, what is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Posterior open reduction first, then assess the disc afterward
Resume tong traction with higher weights now that the MRI is done
Anterior cervical discectomy to remove the extruded fragment, followed by open reduction and instrumented fusion
C5-C6 laminectomy alone without fusion
Halo vest immobilization for 12 weeks without surgery
stages.explanation: With a large ventral disc fragment already compressing the cord, the fragment must be removed anteriorly before the spine is realigned — reducing first, whether closed (B) or posteriorly open (A), can plow the fragment into the cord and cause catastrophic deterioration, the very complication his transient worsening foreshadowed. The standard sequence is anterior cervical discectomy, then reduction under direct vision, then anterior plating and fusion; supplemental posterior fixation is added when the posterior ligamentous disruption demands extra stability, as is likely here. Laminectomy alone (D) neither removes the ventral compression nor addresses gross three-column instability and invites kyphotic collapse. Halo immobilization (E) cannot maintain reduction of a fully ligamentous bilateral facet injury and leaves the cord compressed; these injuries require internal fixation.

cases.diagnosis: Bilateral C5-C6 facet dislocation with incomplete spinal cord injury (ASIA C) and a trapped extruded disc fragment, treated with anterior discectomy, open reduction, and instrumented fusion
pearls: Bilateral jumped facets imply complete posterior ligamentous and disc failure and classically show ~50% anterolisthesis (unilateral ≈ 25%).

Awake, examinable patients can undergo immediate closed tong reduction without pre-reduction MRI — the serial neurologic exam is the safety monitor; MRI is mandatory for the obtunded, before open reduction, or after any deterioration.
New deficit during traction means stop, remove weight, and image — a retropulsed disc fragment may be entering the canal.
A ventral disc fragment on MRI mandates anterior discectomy before reduction; reduce first and you risk driving the fragment into the cord.

cases.title: Back pain after a fall from scaffolding
cases.chiefComplaint: "I came off the scaffold and landed hard on my feet — my lower back is killing me."
cases.history: A 36-year-old construction worker fell approximately 12 feet from scaffolding two hours ago, landing upright on his feet on packed dirt before crumpling onto his buttocks. He did not strike his head and had no loss of consciousness. He immediately felt severe pain at the junction of his mid and lower back, sharp and midline, worse with any attempt to sit up or log-roll, rated 8/10. He denies numbness, tingling, or weakness in his legs, and specifically denies saddle numbness, urinary retention or incontinence, and bowel incontinence. He has mild bilateral heel soreness. He was wearing a hard hat; no other workers were injured. Past medical history is significant for mild asthma with an albuterol inhaler as needed. No daily medications, no allergies. He smokes half a pack daily and drinks beer on weekends. He has never had spine problems or prior surgery. Family history is noncontributory. EMS transported him supine on a vacuum mattress with spinal precautions maintained; he received fentanyl 100 µg IV en route with partial relief.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man lying flat with spinal precautions. Marked midline tenderness at the thoracolumbar junction without palpable step-off or interspinous widening; no overlying ecchymosis. Motor strength 5/5 in hip flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion, EHL, and plantarflexion bilaterally. Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick in all lumbosacral dermatomes including perianal region. Patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantars downgoing. Normal rectal tone. Mild bilateral heel tenderness without deformity. Abdomen soft, pelvis stable, no other injuries on secondary survey.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging for this patient?
options: Standing plain radiographs of the lumbar spine
CT of the thoracolumbar spine with sagittal and coronal reformats, plus imaging of the calcanei
MRI of the lumbar spine as the sole initial study
No imaging — observe and mobilize as tolerated
Technetium bone scan
stages.explanation: A fall from height onto the feet with severe midline thoracolumbar tenderness is a classic axial-load story, and CT is the screening study of choice: it characterizes fracture morphology, canal retropulsion, and posterior element involvement far better than radiographs and quantifies what drives the management decision. The same mechanism transmits force through the calcanei, so heel pain warrants dedicated calcaneal imaging — the lover's fracture association. Standing films (A) would require upright loading of a potentially unstable spine before its anatomy is known. MRI (C) is an important adjunct for the posterior ligamentous complex and neural elements but is slower and inferior for bone detail as a first test. Skipping imaging (D) with this mechanism and exam would miss a major fracture. Bone scan (E) addresses occult or chronic lesions, not acute trauma.

stages.label: IMAGING RESULTS
stages.content:
CT thoracolumbar spine: burst fracture of L1 with comminution of the superior endplate
Retropulsed fragment narrowing the canal by approximately 40%
Anterior vertebral body height loss: 30%; segmental kyphosis: 12 degrees
Interspinous distance normal; facet joints aligned; no laminar fracture
MRI: posterior ligamentous complex intact — no T2/STIR signal in the interspinous ligaments or ligamentum flavum; conus normal, no epidural hematoma
Calcaneal radiographs: no fracture bilaterally
Bladder scan post-void residual: 30 mL

stages.label: CLASSIFICATION
stages.question: Using the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity (TLICS) system, how should this injury be scored and stratified?
options: TLICS 5 — surgery is indicated
TLICS 4 — either operative or nonoperative care is acceptable
Canal compromise of 40% mandates surgical decompression regardless of score
TLICS 2 (burst morphology 2 + intact PLC 0 + intact neurology 0) — nonoperative management is favored
stages.explanation: TLICS sums three domains: morphology (burst = 2), posterior ligamentous complex integrity (intact = 0; MRI showed no interspinous or flavum signal), and neurologic status (intact = 0). His total of 2 falls below the operative threshold — scores of 3 or less are managed nonoperatively, 4 is surgeon's choice, and 5 or more favor surgery. Option A inflates the score, which would require PLC disruption or deficit he does not have. Option B similarly miscounts. Option C reflects a common misconception: canal compromise percentage by itself is not an operative indication in a neurologically intact patient — retropulsed bone remodels and the canal re-expands substantially over months, and trials show equivalent outcomes with nonoperative care in intact patients regardless of initial retropulsion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Thoracolumbosacral orthosis (or extension bracing), analgesia, early ambulation, and standing radiographs to confirm stable alignment
Strict bed rest for six weeks followed by gradual mobilization
Percutaneous pedicle screw fixation T12-L2
Balloon kyphoplasty of L1
L1 corpectomy with cage reconstruction
stages.explanation: A neurologically intact patient with a TLICS 2 burst fracture and intact PLC does well with conservative care: analgesia, early mobilization in a TLSO or hyperextension brace, and upright radiographs to verify that alignment holds under physiologic load. Randomized data even suggest bracing itself may be optional, but early ambulation is the consistent theme — outcomes equal surgery with fewer complications. Prolonged bed rest (B) invites thromboembolism, deconditioning, and pneumonia and is obsolete. Percutaneous fixation (C) treats unstable patterns or TLICS 4+ injuries, adding operative risk without benefit here. Kyphoplasty (D) is for painful osteoporotic compression fractures, not acute traumatic bursts in a young man — cement near a retropulsed fragment risks canal extrusion. Corpectomy (E) is reserved for significant neurologic compression with deficit.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: He is fitted with a TLSO and walks in the ED with 9/10 pain improved to 5/10. At discharge counseling, which later development would most clearly change management to operative intervention?
options: Persistent midline ache at three weeks requiring scheduled NSAIDs
An additional 2 mm of anterior vertebral height loss on follow-up films
New lower-extremity weakness, saddle anesthesia, or urinary retention
Retropulsed fragment still visible on CT at six weeks
Discomfort and skin irritation from brace wear
stages.explanation: The development of a neurologic deficit — leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, or bowel dysfunction — converts a nonoperative burst fracture into a surgical one, because deficit changes the TLICS neurology score and signals active neural compression or progressive instability; patients must be explicitly taught these red flags before discharge. Progressive kyphosis with intractable pain is the other recognized conversion trigger. Residual aching at three weeks (A) is expected and managed medically. A couple millimeters of additional settling (B) is common and tolerated if alignment and neurology hold. Persistent retropulsion on CT (D) is irrelevant in an intact patient — the canal remodels over 12 or more months. Brace intolerance (E) prompts refitting or even weaning per modern evidence, not an operation.

cases.diagnosis: L1 burst fracture, neurologically intact, TLICS 2 with intact posterior ligamentous complex — managed nonoperatively with bracing and early mobilization
pearls: Fall from height onto the feet: think thoracolumbar burst plus calcaneal fractures — image both.
TLICS = morphology + PLC integrity + neurologic status; ≤3 nonoperative, 4 either, ≥5 operative.
Percent canal retropulsion alone is not a surgical indication in an intact patient — the canal remodels over time.
Counsel red flags at discharge: new weakness, saddle anesthesia, or urinary retention converts conservative care to surgery.

cases.title: Sports clearance for a boy who holds his neck stiffly
cases.chiefComplaint: Mother requests medical clearance for Special Olympics gymnastics and soccer, mentioning he sometimes holds his neck stiffly after tumbling class.
cases.history: A 10-year-old boy with trisomy 21 presents with his mother for a pre-participation evaluation before the regional Special Olympics season. He hopes to compete in gymnastics, soccer, and swimming. His mother reports he is generally thriving: he attends an integrated classroom, walks and runs without difficulty, and has had no falls. Over the past two months, however, she has twice noticed him holding his neck stiffly and rubbing it after tumbling practice, resolving within a day. She denies any weakness, gait change, clumsiness beyond his baseline, bladder or bowel accidents, or torticollis lasting more than a few hours. Past medical history includes an atrioventricular septal defect repaired in infancy with normal follow-up echocardiograms, hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 50 µg daily, and corrected hearing loss with tympanostomy tubes. He has no allergies. He has never had cervical spine imaging. Immunizations are current. He lives with both parents and a sister; there is no family history of skeletal dysplasia or early arthritis. He has never had surgery requiring intubation since infancy.
cases.exam: Cheerful, cooperative boy with features of trisomy 21. Neck is supple with full, pain-free active range of motion today; no torticollis or midline tenderness. Gait is steady, including tandem walking at his baseline. Motor strength is 5/5 in all four extremities; tone is mildly decreased diffusely, consistent with his baseline hypotonia. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetric without clonus; plantar responses are flexor; Hoffmann sign is absent. Sensation is grossly intact. No bowel or bladder concerns reported. Cardiac exam shows a well-healed sternotomy scar with regular rhythm and no murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial study to evaluate this boy's craniocervical junction before sports clearance?
options: MRI of the cervical spine as the first test
CT of the craniocervical junction
Lateral cervical spine radiographs in neutral, flexion, and extension
No imaging — clear him based on the normal neurologic examination
Somatosensory evoked potentials
stages.explanation: Children with Down syndrome have ligamentous laxity of the transverse atlantal ligament and odontoid anomalies, producing atlantoaxial instability in 10-15%. Screening begins with lateral cervical radiographs in neutral, flexion, and extension to measure the atlantodental interval under physiologic stress — flexion is when subluxation appears. His intermittent post-tumbling neck stiffness makes imaging clearly appropriate even though routine screening of fully asymptomatic children is debated, and Special Olympics participation in high-risk events traditionally prompts radiographic assessment. MRI first (A) is reserved for abnormal radiographs or neurologic signs. CT (B) adds radiation and images a static position only. Clearing without imaging (D) ignores his symptoms and the planned high-risk sports. Evoked potentials (E) are an adjunct, never the screening test.

stages.label: RADIOGRAPH RESULTS
stages.content:
Lateral cervical radiographs:
Atlantodental interval (neutral): 4 mm
Atlantodental interval (flexion): 7 mm
Atlantodental interval (extension): 3 mm
Space available for cord at C1: 16 mm
Odontoid: normally formed, no os odontoideum
No subaxial subluxation; no basilar invagination

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: How should the flexion atlantodental interval of 7 mm be interpreted?
options: Normal for a 10-year-old — children normally measure up to 8 mm
Atlantoaxial instability — the ADI exceeds the accepted pediatric threshold of about 4.5-5 mm
Diagnostic of os odontoideum
An artifact that requires CT confirmation before any conclusion
stages.explanation: In children the atlantodental interval should not exceed roughly 4.5-5 mm (adults 3 mm); a flexion ADI of 7 mm demonstrates incompetence of the transverse atlantal ligament and defines atlantoaxial instability, which in Down syndrome reflects the characteristic ligamentous laxity of the condition. The dynamic increase from 4 mm neutral to 7 mm in flexion is exactly the behavior screening flexion views are designed to expose. Importantly, his space available for the cord remains generous at 16 mm, and he has no myelopathic signs — so this is instability without current cord compromise. Option A overstates the pediatric normal range. Os odontoideum (C) was explicitly excluded by the normally formed dens; it is a separate cause of instability in Down syndrome. The measurement is reproducible across views and needs no CT to be believed (D).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given asymptomatic atlantoaxial instability with an ADI of 7 mm and a normal neurologic examination, what is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Proceed to C1-C2 posterior fusion now
Full clearance for all Special Olympics events, including gymnastics
Rigid cervical collar at all times until skeletal maturity
Restrict high-risk axial-load and neck-flexion activities, clear low-risk sports, and educate the family on myelopathy warning signs with neurologic follow-up
stages.explanation: Asymptomatic instability in the 5-10 mm ADI range with preserved space for the cord is managed with activity modification, not surgery: he should avoid gymnastics and tumbling, diving, the butterfly stroke and flip turns, trampoline, contact or collision sports, and similar activities that load or forcibly flex the neck, while low-risk events such as track or standard freestyle swimming remain open to him. Equally important is teaching the family the warning signs — new clumsiness, gait change, hand weakness, neck pain with torticollis, bladder changes — and arranging periodic neurologic reassessment. Fusion now (A) exposes an asymptomatic child to substantial operative morbidity without proven benefit. Full clearance including gymnastics (B) ignores a documented 7 mm ADI and his planned highest-risk sport. A constant rigid collar (C) is impractical, unnecessary, and does not protect against subluxation.

stages.label: SURGICAL THRESHOLD
stages.question: The mother asks what would change the plan toward surgery. Which of the following is the accepted indication for C1-C2 fusion in Down syndrome atlantoaxial instability?
options: A neurologic deficit or myelopathic signs, or an ADI of roughly 10 mm or more with reduced space available for the cord
Any ADI above 4.5 mm, even when asymptomatic
Completion of skeletal growth, regardless of measurements
An asymptomatic ADI of 6 mm before each sports season
stages.explanation: Fusion is reserved for symptomatic instability — myelopathy, progressive deficits, or persistent painful subluxation — or marked radiographic instability, conventionally an ADI of about 10 mm or more, particularly when the space available for the cord falls toward 13-14 mm or MRI shows cord compression or signal change. At that point the risk of cord injury from minor trauma justifies operative stabilization despite the higher complication rates of fusion in Down syndrome (nonunion, infection, junctional problems). Option B would commit 10-15% of the Down syndrome population, mostly never-symptomatic, to major surgery. Skeletal maturity (C) is not an indication by itself. A stable asymptomatic 6 mm ADI (D) calls for continued restriction and surveillance, not seasonal surgery — the trend over time and the neurologic exam are what matter.

cases.diagnosis: Asymptomatic atlantoaxial instability in Down syndrome (flexion ADI 7 mm) — managed with high-risk activity restriction and surveillance
pearls: Atlantoaxial instability occurs in 10-15% of children with Down syndrome from transverse ligament laxity; most are asymptomatic.
Screen with lateral flexion-extension radiographs: pediatric ADI >4.5-5 mm defines instability; flexion is the position that unmasks it.
ADI 5-10 mm and asymptomatic: restrict axial-load/neck-flexion sports (gymnastics, diving, butterfly, trampoline, contact) and teach myelopathy red flags.
Reserve C1-C2 fusion for neurologic symptoms or ADI ≥10 mm with diminished space available for the cord.

cases.title: Occipital headaches and clumsy hands in longstanding arthritis
cases.chiefComplaint: "For three months I've had terrible pain at the back of my head, and lately I keep dropping my coffee cup."
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman with 25 years of seropositive, erosive rheumatoid arthritis presents with three months of progressive occipital and upper neck pain radiating over the back of her scalp, worse with neck flexion such as reading. In the past six weeks she has noticed clumsiness buttoning blouses and dropping objects, and her husband says she walks "like she's on a boat." She describes brief electric sensations down her spine when she nods deeply. She denies bladder incontinence but reports new urinary urgency. Her RA is managed with methotrexate 20 mg weekly, folic acid, etanercept 50 mg weekly, and prednisone 5 mg daily; despite this she has chronic deformities of the hands and feet. She is scheduled for an elective right total knee arthroplasty in six weeks. Past history also includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg and osteoporosis on alendronate 70 mg weekly. No allergies. She is a never-smoker, drinks rarely, and lives with her husband. Family history is notable for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis.
cases.exam: Thin woman with ulnar deviation of the fingers, swan-neck deformities, and rheumatoid nodules at both elbows. Suboccipital tenderness with pain on gentle neck flexion; range of motion voluntarily limited. Motor: 4+/5 hand intrinsics and grip (limited partly by joint deformity), 5/5 proximally; lower extremities 5/5 but spastic. Hyperreflexia at biceps, triceps, knees, and ankles with three beats of ankle clonus; Hoffmann sign present bilaterally; plantar responses extensor. Gait is wide-based and spastic; tandem gait impossible. Romberg positive. Diminished vibration sense at the ankles. Cranial nerves intact; no nystagmus or lower cranial nerve dysfunction.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic strategy for this patient's symptoms?
options: Lateral cervical radiographs with flexion-extension views, followed by MRI of the craniocervical junction
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies for suspected carpal tunnel syndrome
Escalation of immunosuppression with re-evaluation in three months
Plain skull radiographs
MRI of the lumbar spine for her gait disturbance
stages.explanation: Occipital pain (C2 neuralgia from C1-C2 synovitis), Lhermitte phenomenon, hand clumsiness, spastic gait, and diffuse hyperreflexia in longstanding erosive RA point squarely at the craniocervical junction. Dynamic lateral radiographs quantify atlantoaxial subluxation — which appears or worsens in flexion — and measure both the anterior and posterior atlantodental intervals, while MRI defines pannus, cord compression, and myelomalacia. The two studies are complementary and both are needed. EMG for carpal tunnel (B), though common in RA, cannot explain leg spasticity, clonus, and extensor plantars. Intensifying DMARDs (C) without imaging delays diagnosis of a mechanical, surgically relevant lesion while myelopathy progresses. Skull films (D) are uninformative. Lumbar MRI (E) looks below the lesion — upper motor neuron signs in all four limbs localize to the cervical cord or higher.

stages.label: IMAGING RESULTS
stages.content:
Lateral cervical radiographs:
Anterior atlantodental interval (neutral): 5 mm
Anterior atlantodental interval (flexion): 9 mm, partially reducing in extension
Posterior atlantodental interval (PADI): 12 mm
Odontoid tip 5 mm above McGregor line (early cranial settling)
MRI craniocervical junction: periodontoid pannus with compression of the cervicomedullary cord at C1-C2
Cord: focal T2 hyperintensity at C1-C2
ESR 48 mm/h, CRP 2.9 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the full clinical and radiographic picture?
options: Carpal tunnel syndrome superimposed on lumbar spinal stenosis
Normal pressure hydrocephalus
Rheumatoid atlantoaxial subluxation with periodontoid pannus causing compressive cervical myelopathy, with early cranial settling
Multiple sclerosis
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
stages.explanation: Rheumatoid synovitis erodes the transverse ligament and odontoid, allowing C1 to slide forward on C2 — here a flexion ADI of 9 mm with a PADI of 12 mm — while inflammatory pannus behind the dens compounds the compression, producing the occipital neuralgia, Lhermitte sign, and four-limb upper motor neuron findings; the odontoid migrating above McGregor line marks early cranial settling from lateral mass collapse. The cord T2 signal confirms myelopathy. A peripheral-plus-lumbar combination (A) cannot produce Hoffmann signs, clonus, and extensor plantars together. NPH (B) features gait apraxia, incontinence, and cognitive change without neck pain or cord signal. MS (D) at 60 with a purely compressive lesion and pannus is untenable. ALS (E) is painless and has no sensory findings — her vibratory loss and Lhermitte exclude it.

stages.label: SURGICAL INDICATION
stages.question: Which feature most strongly establishes the indication for surgical stabilization in this patient?
options: Any anterior ADI above 3 mm in a rheumatoid patient
Surgery should wait until bowel or bladder incontinence develops
The T2 cord signal should first be observed for resolution on repeat imaging
A posterior atlantodental interval below 14 mm in a patient with objective myelopathy
stages.explanation: The posterior atlantodental interval — the space actually left for the cord — is the most powerful radiographic predictor in rheumatoid cervical disease: a PADI under 14 mm correlates with cord compression and with poor neurologic recovery if surgery is delayed, so a PADI of 12 mm plus objective myelopathy is a clear indication for posterior C1-C2 instrumented stabilization, extended to the occiput if cranial settling progresses. Anterior ADI alone (A) is unreliable late in disease, paradoxically shrinking as the dens settles upward; many patients with ADI above 3 mm never need surgery. Waiting for sphincter failure (B) forfeits recoverable function — outcomes track with preoperative Ranawat class. Watching the cord signal (C) ignores that myelomalacia reflects damage already occurring under ongoing compression.

stages.label: PERIOPERATIVE PLANNING
stages.question: Her surgeon also addresses the knee arthroplasty planned in six weeks. Regarding airway management for any surgery in this patient, what is the key consideration?
options: Standard direct laryngoscopy with full neck extension is acceptable once she is anesthetized
She needs cervical spine imaging reviewed before elective intubation, with awake fiberoptic intubation and manual in-line stabilization avoiding hyperextension
A supraglottic airway eliminates any cervical risk, so no imaging review is needed
Elective surgery is permanently contraindicated in rheumatoid patients with neck disease
stages.explanation: Rheumatoid patients facing anesthesia need their cervical spine status known before the airway is managed: forceful extension during direct laryngoscopy can acutely worsen atlantoaxial subluxation and precipitate cord injury in a patient whose ligaments are incompetent — a classic, preventable catastrophe. With documented unstable subluxation and myelopathy, the knee replacement should be deferred until the neck is stabilized, and any intubation performed awake with a fiberoptic scope and in-line stabilization, keeping the neck neutral. Option A describes precisely the dangerous maneuver. A supraglottic airway (C) reduces but does not eliminate positioning risk, and the imaging review is still mandatory; it is also unsuitable for many procedures. Option D is wrong — rheumatoid patients undergo elective surgery routinely once the cervical spine is assessed and, when necessary, stabilized first.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She asks whether the pannus behind the dens must be removed through her mouth. What is the most accurate response?
options: Yes — transoral odontoidectomy is always required before posterior fixation
Pannus responds only to higher doses of corticosteroids
Posterior stabilization alone typically leads to regression of the pannus, so anterior resection is rarely necessary
Radiation therapy is the standard treatment for pannus
The pannus is premalignant and requires en bloc resection
stages.explanation: Periodontoid pannus is reactive synovial tissue driven by pathologic motion at the atlantoaxial joint; once posterior instrumented fusion eliminates that motion, the pannus predictably involutes over months, with serial MRI documenting regression and indirect decompression of the cord. Transoral odontoidectomy (A) is reserved for the minority with fixed, irreducible ventral compression — typically advanced cranial settling that cannot be reduced with positioning or traction — not as a routine first stage. Corticosteroids (B) do not reliably shrink established pannus and she is already immunosuppressed. Radiation (D) has no role in this benign inflammatory tissue. The pannus is not premalignant (E); it is hypertrophic synovium, and treating the instability treats the pannus.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid atlantoaxial subluxation with periodontoid pannus, early cranial settling, and compressive cervical myelopathy (PADI 12 mm) requiring posterior stabilization before elective arthroplasty
pearls: In longstanding RA, occipital pain plus myelopathic signs means craniocervical disease until proven otherwise — image with dynamic radiographs and MRI.
PADI <14 mm, not the anterior ADI, is the key surgical trigger; ADI can paradoxically normalize as cranial settling progresses.
Every rheumatoid patient needs cervical spine assessment before elective intubation; hyperextension during laryngoscopy can cause acute cord injury.
Posterior C1-C2 (or occipitocervical) fusion stops the pathologic motion and pannus regresses — transoral odontoidectomy is rarely needed.

cases.title: A tackle, then arms and legs that wouldn't move
cases.chiefComplaint: Father reports the boy could not move his arms or legs for about twenty minutes after a football tackle, though he seems almost normal now.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought in by his father after a youth flag-football scrimmage that turned rough. About 90 minutes ago he was tackled from behind and his neck appeared to snap forward as he hit the ground. He lay on the field crying that he couldn't move his arms or legs, and teammates say his arms 'just flopped.' Over roughly twenty minutes movement returned, beginning in his legs; by EMS arrival he was moving everything but complained of burning in both hands and a 'sleepy' feeling in his fingers. EMS immobilized him in a pediatric collar. In the ED he says his neck is sore and his fingertips still tingle slightly. He has vomited once. He has no prior injuries or surgeries, takes no medications, and has no allergies. Birth and development have been normal; immunizations are current. He lives with both parents, is in first grade, and has no family history of skeletal dysplasia, connective tissue disease, or cervical anomalies. There was no head strike, loss of consciousness, or seizure activity.
cases.exam: Alert, tearful but cooperative boy in a pediatric rigid collar. Midline cervical tenderness at C3-C5 without step-off. Motor: 5/5 deltoids, biceps, triceps, and lower extremities; grip 4+/5 bilaterally. Sensory: subtle diminished pinprick in both palms; otherwise intact, including perianal region. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantars flexor; no clonus or Hoffmann. No priapism; normal rectal tone per nursing flowsheet. Cranial nerves intact. He moves spontaneously but guards his neck. The remainder of the trauma survey is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Cervical spine radiographs and a CT of the cervical spine are obtained and are entirely normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Remove the collar and discharge — imaging is normal and the examination is nearly back to baseline
Obtain flexion-extension radiographs now to prove stability
Begin a same-season graduated return-to-play protocol
Maintain rigid cervical immobilization and obtain an MRI of the cervical spine
stages.explanation: A child with a clear episode of transient quadriparesis after neck trauma has a presumed spinal cord injury regardless of normal radiographs and CT; the immature spine's elastic ligaments, shallow horizontal facets, and large head-to-body ratio let the column stretch and translate transiently, injuring the cord and then springing back into normal alignment. Immobilization must continue and MRI is mandatory to look for cord edema, hemorrhage, and occult disco-ligamentous injury. Discharging on the basis of normal bony imaging (A) is the classic error this entity punishes. Acute flexion-extension films (B) are unreliable with muscle spasm and potentially dangerous before the cord and ligaments are characterized. Any return-to-play discussion (C) is wildly premature with residual grip weakness and sensory change after a cord-level event.

stages.label: MRI RESULTS
stages.content:
MRI cervical spine: faint central T2 hyperintensity within the cord at C3-C4, consistent with cord edema
No intramedullary hemorrhage
Discs, anterior and posterior longitudinal ligaments, and posterior ligamentous complex: intact, no disruption or abnormal STIR signal
No epidural hematoma; no fracture or malalignment
Repeat neurologic exam: grip improved to 5-/5, palmar pinprick nearly normalized

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: SCIWORA — spinal cord injury without radiographic abnormality on plain films and CT
Bilateral brachial plexus stinger
Conversion disorder triggered by the on-field collision
Unstable ligamentous injury missed by all imaging
Vertebral artery dissection with transient ischemia
stages.explanation: Transient quadriparesis with burning hand paresthesias after cervical trauma, normal radiographs and CT, and cord T2 signal on MRI is the textbook presentation of SCIWORA, a predominantly pediatric entity (classically under age 8) explained by the elastic immature spinal column deforming beyond the cord's tolerance — the column can stretch about 5 cm while the cord tolerates only millimeters — then recoiling to normal alignment. A stinger (B) is a unilateral, single-limb brachial plexus event; bilateral arm plus leg involvement is cord until proven otherwise. Conversion disorder (C) cannot be invoked with objective cord edema on MRI. The MRI specifically demonstrated intact ligaments, excluding option D. Vertebral artery dissection (E) produces posterior circulation findings — vertigo, ataxia, cranial nerve signs — not a pure cord syndrome, and is not supported by this MRI.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management?
options: Halo vest application for three months
Posterior cervical instrumented fusion
Rigid cervical collar with strict activity restriction and close outpatient neurologic follow-up
Immediate return to play once the examination fully normalizes
48 hours of high-dose methylprednisolone
stages.explanation: SCIWORA with intact ligaments and improving deficits is managed nonoperatively: rigid cervical collar immobilization — commonly recommended for up to 12 weeks in classic teaching, though duration is debated and often shortened when MRI shows no instability — together with strict avoidance of sports and high-risk play, then re-examination and consideration of delayed dynamic films once spasm resolves. The rationale for immobilization and restriction is that the recently injured cord and stretched but intact soft tissues are vulnerable to a second hit. A halo (A) is disproportionate without any instability. Fusion (B) treats unstable disco-ligamentous injury, which MRI excluded. Immediate return to play (D) invites recurrent SCIWORA, which can be devastating. High-dose steroids (E) are not recommended in pediatric spinal cord injury — no proven benefit and real harms.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His parents ask when he can play football again. What is the best counseling?
options: He may return next week if his examination is normal

No contact sports for at least about three months, and only after a normal examination — with counseling that a repeat injury could be more severe and that some experts advise against returning to collision sports after a cord injury
A lifetime ban from all sports is mandatory in every SCIWORA case
He may return as soon as repeat X-rays are normal, since they define healing
stages.explanation: After SCIWORA, most protocols keep the child out of contact and collision sports for a minimum of roughly three months — covering the immobilization period plus graded reconditioning — and require complete symptom resolution, a normal neurologic examination, resolution of cord signal or at least clinical normalization, and often normal flexion-extension films before any clearance; families must also hear clearly that recurrent SCIWORA tends to be more severe than the first event, which is why many specialists counsel permanently against returning to collision sports after a documented cord injury, individualizing the decision. One week (A) is far inside the vulnerable window. An absolute lifetime ban in all cases (C) overstates consensus for a child with mild, resolving findings, though it is discussed. X-rays (D) were normal at the moment of injury — they measure nothing about cord healing.

cases.diagnosis: SCIWORA — spinal cord injury without radiographic abnormality (C3-C4 cord edema with normal X-rays, CT, and intact ligaments)
pearls: SCIWORA: real cord injury with normal X-rays and CT — the elastic pediatric column (especially under age 8) stretches beyond the cord's tolerance, then snaps back into alignment.
Normal bony imaging never clears a child with transient neurologic symptoms after neck trauma; keep the collar on and get MRI.
MRI findings stratify prognosis: normal cord or edema-only does well; intramedullary hemorrhage or transection does poorly.
Recurrent SCIWORA is typically worse than the first episode — restrict contact sports for months and counsel families about permanent avoidance of collision sports.

cases.title: Sudden mid-back pain after lifting groceries
cases.chiefComplaint: "I picked up a grocery bag four days ago and felt something give in my mid-back — it's hurt ever since."
cases.history: A 79-year-old retired teacher presents with four days of midline pain at the thoracolumbar junction that began abruptly while lifting a grocery bag from her car trunk. The pain is sharp with movement, rated 7/10 when standing or rolling in bed, and improves to 2/10 lying flat. It does not radiate into the legs. She denies numbness, weakness, gait change, saddle anesthesia, and bladder or bowel dysfunction. She has had no fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss, and no pain that wakes her from sleep apart from positional twinges. She has never been treated for osteoporosis despite a Colles fracture at age 71. She has lost about two inches of height since her fifties. Past history includes hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily and GERD on omeprazole 20 mg daily. No corticosteroid use. She has no allergies, never smoked, and drinks a glass of wine weekly. She has no personal or family history of cancer. She lives alone, walks daily, and is independent in all activities.
cases.exam: Thin woman with mild thoracic kyphosis who rises from the chair gingerly. Focal midline tenderness to percussion at the thoracolumbar junction; paraspinal muscles mildly tense. Pain reproduced with trunk flexion and with transitioning from lying to sitting. Motor strength 5/5 throughout the lower extremities; sensation intact in all lumbosacral dermatomes including perianal region. Patellar and Achilles reflexes 2+ and symmetric; plantar responses flexor. Negative straight-leg raise bilaterally. Gait is slow but steady without ataxia. No costovertebral angle tenderness; abdomen soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: MRI of the entire spine with contrast
Plain radiographs of the thoracic and lumbar spine with basic laboratory studies
Technetium bone scan
No imaging — treat as a muscle strain for six weeks
Urgent CT myelogram
stages.explanation: An elderly woman with abrupt focal midline pain after trivial loading, prior fragility fracture, and height loss almost certainly has a vertebral compression fracture, and plain radiographs confirm the diagnosis, define the level and degree of collapse, and reveal older fractures — all quickly and cheaply. Basic labs (CBC, ESR, calcium, alkaline phosphatase, with serum protein electrophoresis if anything raises suspicion) screen for the important mimics, myeloma and metastasis. Contrast MRI of the whole spine first (A) is reserved for red flags or diagnostic doubt; she has none — no cancer history, weight loss, fever, or neurologic deficit. Bone scan (C) is an adjunct for ambiguous or multiple lesions. Dismissing this as a strain (D) misses a fracture that mandates osteoporosis treatment. CT myelography (E) addresses neural compression she does not have.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Thoracolumbar radiographs: acute-appearing anterior wedge compression fracture of L1 with approximately 30% anterior height loss
Posterior vertebral wall preserved; no retropulsion; no pedicle erosion
Old mild wedge deformity at T8
CBC: WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL, Hgb 12.9 g/dL, platelets 240 ×10³/µL
ESR 14 mm/h; calcium 9.4 mg/dL; alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L; creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
DEXA (obtained later that week): femoral neck T-score −3.0

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Metastatic disease to the L1 vertebra
Multiple myeloma with pathologic fracture
Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture of L1
Unstable L1 burst fracture
Pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis
stages.explanation: Everything converges on an osteoporotic compression fracture: a low-energy mechanism in a thin elderly woman with a prior wrist fragility fracture, height loss, a T-score of −3.0, an anterior wedge with preserved posterior wall, an old asymptomatic wedge at another level, and entirely bland laboratory studies. Metastasis (A) is argued against by the absent cancer history, intact pedicles ('winking owl' sign absent), no constitutional symptoms, and normal alkaline phosphatase. Myeloma (B) typically brings anemia, elevated ESR, hypercalcemia, or renal impairment — all absent, though SPEP is reasonable if doubt persists or fractures recur. A burst fracture (D) requires posterior wall involvement, explicitly excluded. Osteomyelitis (E) is implausible without fever, elevated inflammatory markers, or disc-space involvement.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management plan?
options: Multimodal analgesia, early mobilization as tolerated, and treatment of the underlying osteoporosis with calcium, vitamin D, and an antiresorptive or anabolic agent
Strict bed rest for four weeks to let the fracture heal
Urgent same-week balloon kyphoplasty
Referral for instrumented spinal fusion
Scheduled long-acting opioids as the primary strategy
stages.explanation: First-line care is conservative: scheduled acetaminophen with cautious short-term opioids or other adjuncts if needed (calcitonin can help acute fracture pain short-term), early mobilization — which preserves bone and muscle and prevents thromboembolism and pneumonia — an optional brace for comfort, and, critically, treating the disease that caused the fracture: calcium, vitamin D, and pharmacotherapy such as a bisphosphonate, or an anabolic agent given that a fracture on a T-score of −3.0 defines very-high-risk osteoporosis. Most fractures become tolerable within 4-6 weeks. Bed rest (B) accelerates bone loss and deconditioning in exactly the wrong patient. Immediate kyphoplasty (C) skips the conservative trial that most patients pass. Fusion (D) has no role in a stable osteoporotic wedge. Opioid-first strategies (E) cause falls, constipation, and delirium in the elderly.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: At follow-up she asks about the 'cement procedure' her neighbor had. Which statement best reflects the role of vertebral augmentation (vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty)?
options: Augmentation is proven to reduce long-term mortality and should be offered to every patient
Augmentation is indicated immediately for all acute compression fractures
Augmentation is contraindicated in osteoporotic fractures

Augmentation is a reasonable option if disabling pain persists despite several weeks of optimal medical therapy, with MRI confirming the painful level is acutely fractured (marrow edema); the evidence is mixed, with sham-controlled vertebroplasty trials showing little benefit
