stages.explanation: This captures the honest state of the debate: sham-controlled vertebroplasty trials (INVEST, Buchbinder) found no significant benefit over placebo procedure, while open-label trials such as VERTOS II and the kyphoplasty FREE trial suggested benefit in selected patients with severe, refractory pain and MRI-confirmed acute edema at the symptomatic level. Guidelines therefore reserve augmentation for patients still disabled after a several-week trial of medical management, and MRI (or bone scan) targeting is essential because old healed fractures will not respond to cement. Mortality benefit (A) comes from observational registry data confounded by selection and is not established. Routine immediate augmentation (B) ignores that most patients improve without it and adds cement-leak and adjacent-fracture risks. Augmentation is designed for osteoporotic fractures, so option C inverts the truth.

cases.diagnosis: Acute osteoporotic L1 vertebral compression fracture — conservative management plus initiation of osteoporosis pharmacotherapy
pearls: A vertebral compression fracture after trivial load in an elderly patient is a sentinel osteoporosis event — always start bone-directed therapy, not just analgesia.
Red flags forcing MRI: cancer history, weight loss, fever, night pain, neurologic deficit, or pain refractory to appropriate care.
Early mobilization beats bed rest; immobility accelerates bone loss and causes the complications that kill elderly patients.
Vertebral augmentation is for MRI-confirmed acute fractures with disabling pain despite weeks of medical therapy — sham-controlled trials humbled the procedure.

cases.title: Months of back pain and a growing bump on the spine
cases.chiefComplaint: "My mid-back has ached for five months, I'm sweating through my sheets at night, and my wife says a bump is forming on my spine."
cases.history: A 47-year-old warehouse worker who emigrated from rural India three years ago presents with five months of progressively severe mid-thoracic back pain. The pain is constant, dull, worse at night, and no longer relieved by ibuprofen 400 mg, which he takes most days. Over the same period he has had drenching night sweats, intermittent low-grade fevers, fatigue, and a 6 kg unintentional weight loss. In the past month his wife noticed a hard prominence forming over his mid-back, and he reports new stiffness in his legs when walking long distances, without falls. He denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle numbness, cough, or hemoptysis. He has never been treated for tuberculosis; he recalls a childhood household contact with a chronic cough. He has no prior surgeries, takes no other medications, and has no allergies. He does not smoke or use drugs and drinks rarely. He lives with his wife and two children, all healthy. He has had no recent dental work and no injection drug use.
cases.exam: Thin, tired-appearing man. There is a palpable angular kyphotic prominence (gibbus) at the mid-thoracic spine with focal tenderness at T9-T10. Paraspinal fullness is appreciated on the right. Motor strength is 5/5 in the lower extremities, but tone is mildly increased; patellar reflexes are 3+ with two beats of unsustained ankle clonus; plantar responses are flexor. Sensation is intact, including the perianal region. Gait is normal at slow pace, mildly stiff when hurried. No cervical or axillary lymphadenopathy; lungs are clear; cardiac exam is normal; abdomen is soft without organomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup?
options: Plain thoracic radiographs alone, with NSAIDs and follow-up in two months
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed pyogenic osteomyelitis
Contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine plus inflammatory markers, chest imaging, an interferon-gamma release assay, and HIV testing
Immediate percutaneous biopsy without prior imaging
Physical therapy referral for mechanical back pain
stages.explanation: Subacute back pain with night pain, fevers, sweats, weight loss, a gibbus, and early upper motor neuron signs in a patient from a tuberculosis-endemic region demands urgent evaluation for spinal infection and malignancy: contrast MRI is the study of choice for defining vertebral destruction, abscess, and epidural disease; ESR/CRP gauge inflammation; chest imaging looks for pulmonary tuberculosis; an IGRA supports exposure; and HIV testing is mandatory because coinfection changes everything. Radiographs with a two-month NSAID trial (A) are inadequate with constitutional symptoms and deformity. Empiric antibacterials (B) before tissue diagnosis muddy cultures and miss mycobacteria entirely. Biopsy without imaging (D) is aimless — imaging targets the biopsy. Physical therapy (E) treats mechanical pain, which night sweats and a gibbus have already excluded.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
MRI thoracic spine with contrast: destruction of the anterior T9 and T10 vertebral bodies with 25-degree focal kyphosis
Relative preservation of the T9-T10 intervertebral disc
Large rim-enhancing bilateral paraspinal abscesses with subligamentous spread beneath the anterior longitudinal ligament to T8 and T11
Epidural extension with mild ventral cord compression; no cord T2 signal change
ESR 82 mm/h; CRP 6.8 mg/dL; WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL
Chest radiograph: right apical fibronodular scarring, no cavitation
Interferon-gamma release assay: positive; HIV antigen/antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits the clinical and imaging picture?
options: Tuberculous spondylitis (Pott disease)
Pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis and discitis
Metastatic carcinoma of the spine
Brucellar spondylitis
Lymphoma of the vertebral column
stages.explanation: The pattern is classic for spinal tuberculosis: anterior vertebral body destruction across adjacent levels, subligamentous spread beneath the anterior longitudinal ligament, large cold paraspinal abscesses, gibbus deformity, and — the key discriminator — relative sparing of the intervertebral disc, because Mycobacterium tuberculosis lacks the proteolytic enzymes that pyogenic organisms use to digest the avascular disc. Months of indolent constitutional symptoms, the endemic-region exposure, apical scarring, and positive IGRA complete the picture. Pyogenic osteomyelitis (B) is disc-centered from the start — the disc space is its epicenter — and runs a faster, more febrile course. Metastases (C) can spare discs too but rarely produce rim-enhancing abscesses or subligamentous tracking. Brucellosis (D) is possible but favors the lumbar spine with modest destruction and needs an exposure history. Lymphoma (E) infiltrates without this abscess pattern.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step before starting definitive therapy?
options: Begin empiric four-drug anti-tuberculous therapy without tissue confirmation
CT-guided core biopsy of the lesion for AFB smear and culture, molecular testing (NAAT/GeneXpert with rifampin resistance), and histopathology
Proceed directly to anterior corpectomy and reconstruction
Repeat the IGRA in four weeks to confirm
Serial ESR monitoring during an NSAID trial
stages.explanation: Microbiologic and histologic confirmation should precede months of multidrug therapy whenever the patient is stable enough to allow it: CT-guided core biopsy provides material for acid-fast smear, mycobacterial culture with drug-susceptibility testing, rapid nucleic acid amplification (GeneXpert MTB/RIF, which also flags rifampin resistance), and histopathology showing caseating granulomas — and it simultaneously excludes the mimics, malignancy chief among them. Drug resistance is a real concern in patients from endemic regions, making susceptibility data invaluable. Purely empiric therapy (A) is defensible only when biopsy is impossible or the patient is critically deteriorating. Upfront corpectomy (C) is not indicated without significant deficit or instability — and tissue can be obtained far less invasively. A repeat IGRA (D) adds nothing; it only marks infection at some point, not active disease. NSAIDs with ESR checks (E) postpones treatment of a destructive infection.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms caseating granulomas and GeneXpert detects M. tuberculosis without rifampin resistance. What is the cornerstone of treatment?
options: Two weeks of therapy, stopping if pain improves
Lifelong isoniazid monotherapy
Mandatory surgical debridement for all spinal tuberculosis
Four-drug anti-tuberculous therapy (isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for two months, then continuation-phase therapy, typically 9-12 months total for spinal disease
stages.explanation: Spinal tuberculosis is fundamentally a medical disease: standard therapy is the four-drug intensive phase (RIPE) for two months followed by isoniazid and rifampin in the continuation phase, with most guidelines extending total treatment to 9-12 months for spinal involvement (some accept 6 months with reliable response monitoring); the MRC trials established that chemotherapy alone achieves excellent outcomes in the majority, even with paraspinal abscesses, which usually resolve on drugs. Adherence support (directly observed therapy) and baseline visual acuity and hepatic monitoring accompany the regimen. Two weeks of therapy (A) guarantees relapse and breeds resistance. Isoniazid monotherapy (B) is for latent infection, never active destructive disease. Universal surgery (C) is outdated — operative treatment is reserved for specific failures of, or threats despite, medical therapy.

stages.label: SURGICAL INDICATION
stages.question: He begins therapy under direct observation. Which later development would most clearly indicate surgical intervention?
options: A persistently positive IGRA at three months
Progressive neurologic deficit or worsening kyphotic deformity despite appropriate chemotherapy
Any paraspinal abscess still visible on follow-up MRI
ESR remaining elevated at six weeks
Ongoing night sweats during the second week of treatment
stages.explanation: The accepted surgical indications in Pott disease are neurologic deficit that develops or progresses on adequate chemotherapy, spinal instability or progressive kyphosis (which in the thoracic spine threatens late paraplegia and cardiopulmonary compromise), failure of medical therapy with persistent destruction, and diagnostic uncertainty; surgery then combines anterior debridement and reconstruction with stabilization. His mild ventral compression and brisk reflexes make close serial examination essential. A positive IGRA at three months (A) is expected — it never reverts on treatment timelines and does not measure activity. Paraspinal abscesses (C) commonly persist radiographically for months while shrinking on drugs; size alone is not an indication. ESR (D) lags clinical response by weeks to months. Night sweats in week two (E) are simply the disease not yet controlled — constitutional symptoms typically fade over the first month or two.

cases.diagnosis: Pott disease — tuberculous spondylitis of T9-T10 with paraspinal abscesses and gibbus deformity, treated with prolonged anti-tuberculous chemotherapy and surgical surveillance
pearls: TB spondylitis destroys the anterior vertebral bodies and spreads subligamentously while relatively sparing the disc — pyogenic infection is disc-centered from the start.
Gibbus deformity plus constitutional symptoms in a patient from an endemic region is Pott disease until proven otherwise.
Biopsy before treating: cultures with susceptibilities and GeneXpert guide therapy and exclude malignancy; resistance is a real risk in immigrants from endemic areas.
Chemotherapy (RIPE, 9-12 months) cures most spinal TB; operate for progressive deficit, instability, or worsening kyphosis despite drugs.

cases.title: Worsening mid-back pain after a recent blood infection
cases.chiefComplaint: My back has been killing me and now it won't let up even when I lie down.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and end-stage kidney disease on hemodialysis through a tunneled catheter was hospitalized 3 weeks ago for methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) bloodstream infection and completed a course of IV antibiotics. Over the past 2 weeks he has had progressively worsening, well-localized lower thoracic back pain that is now constant, present at rest, and wakes him at night. It is not relieved by position change or acetaminophen. He reports intermittent low-grade fevers and night sweats but denies leg weakness, numbness, or bowel or bladder dysfunction. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, CKD on hemodialysis. Medications: insulin glargine, amlodipine, aspirin 81 mg. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker, no alcohol, denies IV drug use; lives with his wife. Family history: noncontributory. He has had no recent spinal procedures or trauma.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but non-toxic. Focal tenderness to percussion over the lower thoracic spine with paraspinal muscle spasm and markedly limited, painful flexion. Neurologic exam: strength 5/5 in all four extremities, sensation intact to light touch and pinprick, deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric, no clonus, plantar responses downgoing. No saddle anesthesia; rectal tone normal. Gait is cautious and antalgic due to pain. The hemodialysis catheter exit site is clean and dry without erythema or discharge. No costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has focal spinal tenderness and low-grade fever following a recent bloodstream infection. Which is the most appropriate initial diagnostic study?
options: MRI of the thoracolumbar spine with and without gadolinium
Plain radiographs of the spine alone
Technetium bone scan
Empiric IV vancomycin without further imaging
stages.explanation: MRI with and without contrast is the most sensitive and specific test for vertebral osteomyelitis, discitis, and epidural abscess; it detects marrow edema, disc involvement, and paraspinal or epidural collections early in the disease. Plain radiographs are insensitive during the first 2-3 weeks because bony changes lag behind infection. A technetium bone scan is sensitive but nonspecific and gives poor anatomic detail, so it is reserved for patients who cannot undergo MRI. Starting empiric antibiotics without imaging or a microbiologic diagnosis is premature in a stable patient and can lower the yield of a subsequent biopsy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.5 x10^3/uL
ESR 88 mm/hr
CRP 142 mg/L
Blood cultures (x2): no growth at 48 h
HbA1c 8.6%
MRI T-spine: T9-T10 disc with adjacent endplate erosion and vertebral marrow edema; T2/STIR hyperintensity across the disc space; small anterior paravertebral phlegmon; no epidural abscess and no cord compression

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On MRI, which feature most strongly favors pyogenic infection over a metastatic vertebral tumor?
options: Involvement limited to the posterior vertebral elements
A pathologic compression fracture of a single vertebral body
Destruction centered on the disc space with erosion of both adjacent endplates
Sparing of the intervertebral disc with multilevel vertebral body lesions
stages.explanation: Pyogenic spondylodiscitis characteristically centers on the disc and destroys the adjacent endplates: organisms seed the vascular endplate and then spread across the disc, so the disc space is involved early. Metastatic disease and most tumors spare the avascular disc and instead involve the vertebral body and posterior elements, frequently at multiple noncontiguous levels. Posterior-element-only disease points toward tumor. A pathologic compression fracture is nonspecific. Disc sparing with multilevel body lesions is the classic tumor pattern. Tuberculous (Pott) disease can also relatively spare the disc and produce a large paraspinal abscess, an important second consideration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is trending afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and neurologically intact, and current blood cultures show no growth. What is the best next step?
options: Start empiric vancomycin and cefepime immediately
CT-guided needle biopsy of the disc and vertebra before initiating antibiotics
Urgent surgical debridement with instrumented fusion
Begin a 6-week course of oral fluoroquinolone empirically
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stable patient without neurologic compromise and with negative blood cultures, IDSA guidelines recommend obtaining a microbiologic diagnosis via image-guided biopsy before starting antimicrobials, because targeted therapy improves cure rates and prior antibiotics reduce culture yield. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics are reserved for sepsis, hemodynamic instability, or progressive neurologic deficit, none of which are present. Surgery is not indicated without instability, a deficit, or an abscess causing compression. Empiric oral monotherapy without an identified organism risks undertreatment and resistance and would compromise the biopsy. Note that if blood cultures had grown S. aureus, antibiotics could reasonably start without biopsy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Biopsy grows MSSA and he starts targeted IV therapy. On hospital day 3 he develops new bilateral leg weakness and urinary retention. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue antibiotics and reassess in 48 hours
Add oral corticosteroids and prescribe bed rest
Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge with outpatient follow-up
Urgent repeat MRI and surgical decompression for likely epidural abscess with cord compression
stages.explanation: New, progressive neurologic deficit (paraparesis and urinary retention) signals an expanding epidural abscess with spinal cord compression, a surgical emergency requiring urgent MRI and decompression (laminectomy and debridement), often with stabilization. Continuing antibiotics and waiting risks irreversible cord injury. Corticosteroids do not relieve mechanical compression from pus and would delay definitive care. De-escalating to oral antibiotics and discharging is unsafe with an evolving deficit. Standard medical therapy is roughly 6 weeks of targeted IV antibiotics, but a new neurologic deficit overrides this and mandates immediate surgical evaluation. Serial neurologic checks during medical therapy are essential.

cases.diagnosis: Pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis with discitis (T9-T10) from hematogenous MSSA seeding
pearls: Pyogenic spondylodiscitis is disc-centered with endplate destruction; metastatic tumor and TB tend to spare the disc.
In stable patients without a deficit, obtain an image-guided biopsy BEFORE antibiotics to maximize culture yield.
S. aureus is the most common organism; sources include bacteremia, IV drug use, and vascular or dialysis catheters.
New or progressive neurologic deficit signals an epidural abscess and mandates urgent surgical decompression.

cases.title: Teen with forehead swelling, headache, and sudden one-sided weakness
cases.chiefComplaint: My head is pounding and now my right arm and leg feel weak.
cases.history: A previously healthy 14-year-old boy has had 2 to 3 weeks of a worsening upper respiratory illness with nasal congestion, purulent nasal discharge, and a dull frontal headache that was attributed to a cold and never evaluated or treated. Over the past several days a tender, boggy swelling developed over his forehead, his headache became severe and constant, and he developed fever, photophobia, and decreased oral intake with vomiting. This morning his parents noticed he was confused and that his right arm and leg were weak, and he had a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure en route to the hospital. He has no prior seizures, no head trauma, no immunodeficiency, and no recent travel. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Immunizations: up to date. Social: high-school freshman, lives with his parents, no tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing and postictal, drowsy but arousable, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6). A tender, fluctuant, warm swelling overlies the frontal bone with surrounding erythema, and there is purulent rhinorrhea. Neck is stiff with pain on flexion. There is a right hemiparesis (3/5 in the arm and leg), a right facial droop sparing the forehead, hyperreflexia on the right, and an upgoing right plantar response. Pupils are equal and reactive; fundoscopy shows early papilledema. Extraocular movements intact. No cerebellar signs. The left side is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a rapidly evolving focal deficit, seizure, fever, and a sinus source, what is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Lumbar puncture for CSF cell count and culture
Urgent non-contrast and contrast-enhanced CT of the head and paranasal sinuses
Outpatient ENT referral for sinusitis
EEG to characterize the seizure
stages.explanation: A rapidly progressive focal deficit with seizure, fever, and a sinus source demands emergent neuroimaging; CT is fastest and identifies sinus opacification, frontal osteomyelitis, an extra-axial collection, midline shift, and herniation risk. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with a focal deficit and signs of raised intracranial pressure because of herniation risk, and it rarely yields the organism since this is not primarily meningitis. Outpatient ENT referral dangerously underestimates an intracranial emergency. EEG does not address the acute structural and infectious threat. MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging is the most sensitive test and should follow once the patient is stabilized, but it must not delay emergent CT.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 x10^3/uL with left shift
CRP 210 mg/L
Na 134 mEq/L
Glucose 104 mg/dL
CT/MRI: opacified frontal sinuses with anterior table erosion (frontal osteomyelitis); crescentic extra-axial fluid collection over the left frontal convexity with peripheral rim enhancement and restricted diffusion on DWI; 6 mm midline shift; sulcal effacement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the rim-enhancing, diffusion-restricting crescentic collection in this clinical context?
options: Chronic subdural hematoma
Bacterial meningitis without a focal collection
Acute epidural hematoma from trauma
Subdural empyema complicating frontal sinusitis with a Pott puffy tumor
stages.explanation: A crescent-shaped extra-axial collection that crosses suture lines, with peripheral rim enhancement and restricted diffusion (like an abscess), in a child with frontal sinusitis and forehead swelling, is a subdural empyema. The forehead swelling is a Pott puffy tumor (frontal osteomyelitis with subperiosteal abscess); infection spreads intracranially through valveless diploic and emissary veins. A chronic subdural hematoma does not restrict diffusion or rim-enhance and lacks fever. Meningitis alone produces no focal collection or mass effect. An epidural hematoma is lentiform, does not cross sutures, and follows trauma. The infectious source plus DWI-bright pus are the key discriminators.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management of this condition?
options: Emergent surgical drainage (craniotomy or burr holes) plus IV antibiotics and sinus drainage
IV antibiotics alone for 6 weeks
Antibiotics plus high-dose corticosteroids without surgery
Endoscopic sinus surgery alone
stages.explanation: Subdural empyema is a neurosurgical emergency: pus in the subdural space spreads rapidly and causes thrombophlebitis, cortical infarction, seizures, and herniation. Treatment requires urgent surgical evacuation (a wide craniotomy is generally preferred over burr holes for thick, loculated pus) combined with prolonged IV antibiotics and source control of the infected sinus. Antibiotics alone cannot sterilize or decompress a loculated subdural collection and risk fatal progression. Corticosteroids may control edema as an adjunct but never replace drainage. Treating the sinus alone ignores the intracranial collection causing the deficit. Empiric antibiotics should cover streptococci (including the S. anginosus/milleri group), anaerobes, and staphylococci.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After drainage he is loaded with an antiepileptic. Why is seizure prophylaxis particularly important in subdural empyema?
options: Because the antibiotics used always lower the seizure threshold
Because subdural empyema has among the highest seizure rates of any intracranial infection due to cortical irritation and venous infarction
Because a seizure would prove the antibiotics have failed
Because prophylaxis prevents the empyema from re-accumulating
stages.explanation: Cortical irritation from subdural pus together with associated cortical venous thrombosis and infarction give subdural empyema among the highest seizure rates of any intracranial infection, so antiepileptic prophylaxis is standard perioperatively. Although some antibiotics (high-dose beta-lactams, carbapenems) can lower the seizure threshold, that is not the principal rationale here. Seizures reflect cortical irritation rather than necessarily antibiotic failure. Antiepileptics do not prevent re-accumulation of the empyema, which depends on adequate drainage, antibiotics, and source control. Close monitoring with repeat imaging is essential to detect re-collection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which additional study should be obtained to evaluate for a feared vascular complication?
options: Carotid duplex ultrasound
Transthoracic echocardiogram
MR or CT venography to assess for cortical and dural venous sinus thrombosis
Lower-extremity venous Doppler
stages.explanation: Subdural empyema and adjacent frontal sinusitis can propagate along cortical veins to cause cortical venous thrombosis and superior sagittal or cavernous sinus thrombosis, which worsens infarction, edema, and seizures. MR venography (or CT venography) evaluates the dural sinuses and informs decisions about anticoagulation. Carotid duplex assesses extracranial arterial disease, which is irrelevant here. Echocardiography screens for endocarditis or a cardiac source rather than the local venous complication. Lower-extremity Doppler evaluates DVT, not intracranial venous thrombosis. Recognizing and imaging for venous sinus thrombosis is essential because it changes prognosis and management significantly.

cases.diagnosis: Left frontal subdural empyema complicating frontal sinusitis with a Pott puffy tumor
pearls: A Pott puffy tumor (frontal osteomyelitis with subperiosteal abscess) signals intracranial spread; image the brain, not just the sinus.
Subdural empyema is a neurosurgical emergency requiring urgent surgical drainage plus IV antibiotics, not antibiotics alone.
Restricted diffusion on DWI distinguishes empyema or abscess pus from a sterile subdural hematoma or effusion.
Avoid LP with focal deficits or mass effect, and screen for venous sinus thrombosis with MR or CT venography.

cases.title: First-ever seizure in a healthy young man
cases.chiefComplaint: I've never had a seizure before, I just woke up on the floor and my tongue was bleeding.
cases.history: A 32-year-old man with no significant medical history is brought in after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure at work. Coworkers describe roughly two minutes of whole-body stiffening and shaking with tongue biting and urinary incontinence, followed by about 10 minutes of postictal confusion before he gradually returned to baseline. He has never had a seizure or any similar spell before. He immigrated 8 months ago from a rural region of Central America with limited sanitation and free-roaming pigs. Over the past few months he has had occasional mild headaches but no fevers, neck stiffness, weight loss, vision change, or focal weakness. He denies recent head trauma, alcohol, or drug use and takes no medications. He has had no recent febrile illness. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: works in construction, lives with relatives, no tobacco, alcohol, or recreational drugs. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Postictal initially, now fully alert and oriented with fluent speech. No meningismus or nuchal rigidity. Cranial nerves intact, visual fields full to confrontation, and no papilledema on fundoscopy. Strength 5/5 throughout with no pronator drift, sensation intact, deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric, plantar responses downgoing. Gait, coordination, and finger-to-nose testing are normal. A lateral tongue laceration is present. No subcutaneous nodules, no rash, and no lymphadenopathy. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams are unremarkable, and he appears nontoxic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has had a first unprovoked seizure. Beyond basic labs, what is the most important next step?
options: Reassure him and discharge; a first seizure needs no further workup
Start a lifelong antiepileptic without any imaging
Neuroimaging of the brain (CT or MRI) to identify a structural cause
EEG alone
stages.explanation: A first unprovoked seizure in an adult requires neuroimaging to find a structural lesion, and in a patient from a cysticercosis-endemic region this is essential. MRI is most sensitive; CT readily shows calcified cysts. Discharging without a workup misses a treatable and potentially dangerous cause. Reflexively starting a lifelong antiepileptic without identifying the etiology is inappropriate because the cause determines treatment and its duration. EEG helps classify the seizure type and estimate recurrence risk but does not reveal the structural lesion and is not the immediate priority. Basic labs (glucose, electrolytes, calcium, toxicology) should also be checked to exclude a provoked seizure.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
BMP, glucose, calcium: within normal limits
Toxicology screen: negative
CBC: normal, no eosinophilia
MRI brain: solitary 1.2 cm cystic lesion in the right parietal cortex with an eccentric hyperintense mural nodule (scolex) within it; minimal surrounding edema; faint rim enhancement

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cyst contains an eccentric mural nodule (the 'hole-with-dot' sign). What is the diagnosis?
options: Neurocysticercosis (vesicular/colloidal stage)
Pyogenic brain abscess
Glioblastoma
Cerebral toxoplasmosis
stages.explanation: A cystic lesion containing an eccentric scolex (the 'hole-with-dot' sign) is characteristic of neurocysticercosis, the larval stage of Taenia solium and the most common cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide. A pyogenic abscess shows a thick enhancing rim, marked edema, and restricted diffusion with systemic signs of infection. Glioblastoma is an irregular, heterogeneously enhancing infiltrative mass with substantial edema, not a thin cyst with a scolex. Toxoplasmosis produces multiple ring-enhancing lesions, typically in immunocompromised (HIV) patients, and lacks a scolex. Serology and stool studies can support the diagnosis, but the imaging here is essentially diagnostic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before prescribing albendazole, which evaluation is most critical to avoid precipitating harm?
options: Liver biopsy
Bone marrow examination
Transthoracic echocardiography
An ophthalmologic (fundoscopic) exam and review of imaging for intraventricular cysts and hydrocephalus
stages.explanation: Antiparasitic therapy triggers inflammation as cysts die. If a cyst lies in the eye, treatment can cause vision-threatening intraocular inflammation, so an ophthalmologic exam is mandatory first. If cysts are intraventricular or cause obstructive hydrocephalus, the inflammatory response can acutely worsen the hydrocephalus and raise intracranial pressure; these patients need CSF diversion or endoscopic cyst removal rather than, or before, antiparasitics. Liver function should be checked because albendazole is hepatotoxic, but a biopsy is unnecessary. Bone marrow examination and echocardiography are irrelevant. Corticosteroids and antiepileptics accompany antiparasitic therapy for parenchymal disease to blunt inflammation and control seizures.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If imaging had instead shown a cyst within the fourth ventricle with enlarged ventricles, what would be the most appropriate management?
options: High-dose albendazole alone as the first intervention
Endoscopic or surgical cyst removal and/or CSF diversion before, or instead of, upfront antiparasitics
Observation with no intervention
Lumbar puncture to drain CSF
stages.explanation: Intraventricular neurocysticercosis can obstruct CSF flow and cause acute obstructive hydrocephalus; killing the cyst with antiparasitics provokes ependymal inflammation that can acutely worsen obstruction and cause deterioration. The preferred approach is mechanical removal (often minimally invasive endoscopic excision) and/or CSF diversion (shunt or endoscopic third ventriculostomy) to relieve hydrocephalus. Albendazole alone upfront is dangerous in this setting. Observation is unsafe with obstructive hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated with obstructive hydrocephalus and a posterior fossa lesion because of herniation risk. This intraventricular trap is a classic, high-yield distinction from parenchymal disease.

cases.diagnosis: Neurocysticercosis (solitary parenchymal cyst, vesicular stage) presenting as a first seizure
pearls: Neurocysticercosis is the leading cause of acquired epilepsy worldwide; the cyst-with-scolex 'hole-with-dot' sign is characteristic.
Always check for ocular and intraventricular cysts before antiparasitics, because dying cysts inflame and can blind or obstruct CSF flow.
Intraventricular cysts with hydrocephalus need surgical removal or CSF diversion, not upfront albendazole.
Parenchymal disease is treated with albendazole plus corticosteroids and antiepileptics for seizure control.

cases.title: Recurring spells of a rising stomach sensation and lip-smacking
cases.chiefComplaint: I keep having spells where my stomach flips, then I go blank and people say I smack my lips.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman has had stereotyped spells for about 10 years. Each begins with a rising epigastric sensation and a feeling of deja vu, followed by behavioral arrest, a blank stare, oral automatisms (lip-smacking and chewing), and fidgeting hand movements lasting 1 to 2 minutes, with postictal confusion and word-finding difficulty for several minutes afterward. She is amnestic for the events and occasionally has secondary generalized convulsions, mostly with sleep deprivation. She had complex febrile seizures as a toddler. Despite adequate, tolerated trials of two appropriate antiseizure medications at therapeutic doses, she has 3 to 4 disabling seizures per month, which have cost her the ability to drive and now threaten her teaching job. She denies head trauma, CNS infection, or aura of fear. Medications: lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily, levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: lives with her partner, does not currently drive, rare alcohol, no tobacco. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Alert and fully oriented with normal, fluent speech and intact naming and repetition. No focal motor or sensory deficit and no pronator drift; visual fields full to confrontation; extraocular movements full and cranial nerves intact. Strength 5/5 throughout, deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric, plantar responses downgoing. Gait, tandem walking, and finger-to-nose coordination are normal. No neurocutaneous stigmata such as cafe-au-lait macules or hypopigmented patches. Bedside cognition and short-term memory are grossly intact. The interictal exam is entirely normal, which is typical for this condition.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She continues to have seizures despite adequate trials of two tolerated, appropriate antiseizure medications. What does this establish, and what is the next step?
options: She is not yet drug-resistant; simply try a third medication before any further workup
Drug-resistant epilepsy; refer for presurgical evaluation including video-EEG and high-resolution MRI
Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures; stop all antiseizure medications
Drug-resistant epilepsy; proceed directly to surgery without further testing
stages.explanation: Failure of two appropriately chosen, adequately dosed, tolerated antiseizure medications defines drug-resistant epilepsy by ILAE criteria. Adding a third drug yields a low probability (under about 10%) of seizure freedom, so she should be referred for presurgical evaluation. The first step is to localize the epileptogenic zone with video-EEG (capturing her habitual seizures) and a dedicated epilepsy-protocol MRI. Calling this psychogenic is unfounded given stereotyped semiology with automatisms and postictal confusion. Proceeding straight to surgery without confirming localization, concordance, and functional risk is unsafe. Temporal lobe epilepsy from mesial temporal sclerosis is the most surgically remediable epilepsy syndrome.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Video-EEG: stereotyped seizures with right anterior temporal rhythmic theta onset; interictal right anterior temporal spikes
MRI (epilepsy protocol): right hippocampal atrophy with increased FLAIR/T2 signal and loss of internal architecture
FDG-PET: right temporal hypometabolism
Neuropsychology: mild verbal-memory weakness; left-hemisphere language dominance suspected

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis do these concordant findings support?
options: Frontal lobe epilepsy
Right mesial temporal sclerosis with temporal lobe epilepsy
Idiopathic generalized epilepsy
Occipital lobe epilepsy
stages.explanation: Hippocampal atrophy with increased FLAIR/T2 signal and loss of internal architecture is the MRI hallmark of mesial temporal sclerosis. Concordant right anterior temporal EEG onset, right temporal PET hypometabolism, and the characteristic semiology (epigastric and deja vu aura, oral and manual automatisms, postictal confusion) localize a right temporal lobe epilepsy. Frontal lobe seizures are brief, often nocturnal, and hyperkinetic with rapid recovery. Idiopathic generalized epilepsy shows generalized spike-wave with a normal MRI. Occipital epilepsy features visual auras and occipital discharges. The convergence (concordance) of semiology, EEG, MRI, and PET to one region is exactly what drives a successful surgical plan.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She is left-hemisphere language dominant and surgery would target the right (nondominant) temporal lobe. What is the main purpose of Wada testing (or functional MRI) here?
options: To confirm the location of the seizure focus
To measure antiseizure drug levels in each hemisphere
To lateralize language and assess whether the contralateral hippocampus can support memory after resection
To screen for psychogenic seizures
stages.explanation: The intracarotid amobarbital (Wada) test, increasingly supplemented or replaced by functional MRI and memory fMRI, lateralizes language and estimates each hemisphere's memory capacity. Before temporal lobectomy, the key question is whether the unaffected contralateral hippocampus can sustain memory once the epileptogenic temporal lobe is removed, minimizing the risk of severe postoperative amnesia, while confirming language dominance to protect against aphasia. Wada does not localize the seizure focus (that is the role of EEG and MRI), does not measure drug levels, and does not diagnose psychogenic seizures. A patient with adequate contralateral memory reserve and concordant data is an excellent surgical candidate.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: All data are concordant and Wada testing is favorable. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment, and what outcome should she expect?
options: Anterior temporal lobectomy with amygdalohippocampectomy; roughly 60-70% chance of seizure freedom
Corpus callosotomy; nearly 100% seizure freedom
Vagus nerve stimulator; typically renders patients seizure-free
Continue medical therapy only; surgery is rarely better than drugs in this setting
stages.explanation: For drug-resistant mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with concordant data, anterior temporal lobectomy with amygdalohippocampectomy is the treatment of choice; randomized trials show roughly 60-70% of patients become seizure-free versus a few percent with continued medication. Corpus callosotomy is a palliative procedure for drop attacks and generalized seizures, not curative for focal temporal epilepsy. Vagus nerve stimulation is palliative, usually reducing rather than eliminating seizures, and is reserved for non-resective candidates. Continued medical therapy alone is inferior to surgery in this syndrome (Wiebe trial). Selective laser ablation (LITT) is an emerging, less invasive alternative with somewhat lower but meaningful seizure-freedom rates.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is a recognized risk specific to dominant-side temporal resections that guided the preoperative testing?
options: Contralateral hemiplegia
Verbal memory decline and naming or word-finding difficulty
Complete cortical blindness
Cerebellar ataxia
stages.explanation: Dominant (usually left) temporal lobe resection risks verbal memory decline and naming or word-finding (anomic) difficulty, which is why language lateralization and memory assessment (Wada/fMRI and neuropsychology) are done preoperatively. A superior quadrantanopia (a 'pie in the sky' field cut) from disruption of Meyer loop is also common but usually well tolerated. Contralateral hemiplegia is not expected from a standard temporal lobectomy. Complete cortical blindness would require bilateral occipital injury, not a temporal resection. Cerebellar ataxia is unrelated to temporal surgery. Careful preoperative mapping balances the seizure-freedom benefit against these neuropsychological risks.

cases.diagnosis: Right mesial temporal sclerosis causing drug-resistant temporal lobe epilepsy
pearls: Drug-resistant epilepsy is failure of two appropriate, tolerated antiseizure drugs; refer for surgical evaluation rather than just adding a third.
The MTS hallmark on MRI is hippocampal atrophy with increased FLAIR/T2 signal and lost internal architecture; childhood febrile seizures are a clue.
Successful surgery depends on concordance of semiology, video-EEG, MRI, and PET to one region.
Anterior temporal lobectomy renders about 60-70% of patients seizure-free; Wada and fMRI protect memory and language.

cases.title: Tremor and stiffness that wear off between pill doses
cases.chiefComplaint: My medications work great for a few hours, then I freeze up and shake until the next dose.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man has had Parkinson disease for 9 years. It began with a right-hand resting tremor and progressed to bradykinesia, rigidity, and micrographia, with an excellent and dramatic initial response to levodopa. Over the past 2 years he has developed increasingly troublesome motor fluctuations: predictable wearing-off about an hour before each dose with return of tremor, stiffness, and slowness, plus involuntary writhing movements (peak-dose dyskinesias) when his levodopa level is high. The off periods now occupy several hours of his day and interfere with eating and dressing. His cognition is intact, and he has no hallucinations, significant depression, early falls, or autonomic failure. He wants better control and asks about surgery. PMH: hypertension. Medications: carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 five times daily, pramipexole, entacapone. Allergies: none. Social: retired engineer, lives with his wife, no alcohol or tobacco. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Examined in the on state: mild right-greater-than-left resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia with decremental finger tapping, a mildly stooped posture, and reduced right arm swing. Intermittent choreiform dyskinesias are seen at peak dose. Gait is shuffling when off and improves when on. The pull test is normal in the on state. Vertical gaze is full (no supranuclear palsy). No cerebellar signs and no orthostatic hypotension on standing. MoCA 28/30.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is the single best predictor that he will benefit from deep brain stimulation?
options: His age of 63 years
A robust, sustained responsiveness of his motor symptoms to levodopa
The presence of dyskinesias alone
Disease duration greater than 5 years
stages.explanation: A strong, sustained response to levodopa is the best predictor of motor benefit from DBS: in general, symptoms that respond to levodopa improve with stimulation, and those that do not (such as levodopa-resistant gait freezing and postural instability) generally will not. Age is a consideration but not the primary predictor, and appropriately selected older patients can benefit. Dyskinesias indicate dopaminergic responsiveness and are an indication, but the broader determinant is overall levodopa response. Disease duration over 5 years helps confirm idiopathic PD and exclude mimics but does not itself predict benefit. Medication-refractory tremor is an exception that can respond to DBS even when less levodopa-responsive.

stages.label: WORKUP & LEVODOPA CHALLENGE
stages.content:
Levodopa challenge: MDS-UPDRS Part III motor score 48 OFF medication, improving to 14 ON (about 70% improvement)
MoCA: 28/30 (no significant cognitive impairment)
Depression screen (PHQ-9): 4 (no significant depression)
Brain MRI: mild generalized atrophy, no vascular or structural lesion, no findings of atypical parkinsonism
Orthostatic vitals: no significant drop

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding, if present, would most strongly argue AGAINST DBS by suggesting an atypical parkinsonian syndrome?
options: Early prominent dementia, vertical gaze palsy, or early autonomic failure with poor levodopa response
An asymmetric resting tremor
Peak-dose dyskinesias
A good response to levodopa
stages.explanation: Red flags such as early dementia, vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (progressive supranuclear palsy), early severe autonomic failure (multiple system atrophy), early falls, and a poor levodopa response point to atypical parkinsonism, which responds poorly to DBS and can worsen, so these are contraindications. An asymmetric resting tremor is typical of idiopathic PD and supports candidacy. Peak-dose dyskinesias confirm dopaminergic responsiveness and are actually an indication for DBS. A good levodopa response is the strongest favorable predictor. Cognitive screening, psychiatric assessment, and a levodopa challenge are part of the standard DBS workup precisely to exclude these red flags.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He proceeds to bilateral subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS. What is the most realistic expected benefit?
options: Cure of Parkinson disease and halting of neurodegeneration
Improvement in cognition and prevention of any future dementia
Elimination of the need for any caregiver in advanced disease
Reduced 'off' time, smoother motor control, decreased dyskinesias, and often a reduction in medication dose
stages.explanation: STN DBS reduces 'off' time, improves motor fluctuations and the tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia of the off state, and frequently allows a reduction in dopaminergic medication, which in turn lessens dyskinesias. It is symptomatic, not disease-modifying, and does not halt neurodegeneration or cure PD. It does not improve cognition; in fact cognitive decline and some psychiatric symptoms can worsen, which is why neuropsychiatric screening is essential. It does not reliably help levodopa-resistant axial symptoms such as freezing and balance, and it will not eliminate future care needs. Realistic counseling centers on quality-of-life and motor-fluctuation benefit.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: A different 63-year-old with the same diagnosis has disabling gait freezing and falls that do NOT improve with levodopa, plus early cognitive impairment. What is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Bilateral STN DBS will likely resolve his gait freezing and falls
DBS is unlikely to help his levodopa-resistant axial symptoms and may worsen cognition; optimize medical and supportive therapy instead
Proceed with DBS to treat his cognitive impairment
DBS is mandatory in all patients with disease duration over 5 years
stages.explanation: Levodopa-resistant axial features (gait freezing, postural instability, falls) typically do not improve with DBS because stimulation generally mirrors the levodopa response, and DBS can worsen cognition in patients with existing impairment. The better course is optimized medical therapy, physical and occupational therapy, fall prevention, and management of cognition; DBS is relatively contraindicated here. Claiming DBS will resolve his freezing and falls is false. DBS does not treat cognitive impairment and may aggravate it. DBS is elective and individualized, never mandatory based on disease duration. Patient selection is everything: levodopa-responsive symptoms, intact cognition, and no significant untreated psychiatric disease.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic Parkinson disease with motor fluctuations and dyskinesias, an appropriate candidate for STN deep brain stimulation
pearls: Levodopa responsiveness is the best predictor of DBS benefit; what responds to levodopa responds to DBS, with tremor as an exception.
DBS treats motor fluctuations and dyskinesias and reduces 'off' time; it is symptomatic, not disease-modifying.
Red flags (early dementia, vertical gaze palsy, early autonomic failure, poor levodopa response) suggest atypical parkinsonism; avoid DBS.
Levodopa-resistant gait freezing and postural instability usually do not improve with stimulation.

cases.title: Shaky hands that settle after a glass of wine
cases.chiefComplaint: My hands shake whenever I try to use them, and writing or holding a cup is getting impossible.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman reports several years of a slowly progressive hand tremor that appears when she uses her hands, such as writing, holding a cup, or eating soup, and is absent when her hands rest in her lap. The tremor has worsened gradually and is now interfering with her handwriting, applying makeup, and signing documents, causing her embarrassment in public. She has noticed it transiently improves after a glass of wine. She also has a mild head tremor and a slightly quivering voice. Her father had a similar tremor that ran in the family. She has tried propranolol, limited by fatigue and bradycardia with only partial benefit, and primidone, which made her too sedated; neither adequately controlled the tremor. She denies slowness, stiffness, falls, or trouble walking. PMH: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine; she has stopped propranolol and primidone. Allergies: none. Social: retired librarian, lives alone, occasional wine, no tobacco. Family history: father with action tremor.
cases.exam: Bilateral, roughly symmetric postural and kinetic tremor of the upper extremities, evident when the arms are held outstretched and on finger-to-nose testing, worsening as she approaches the target. A mild side-to-side head tremor is present. There is no resting tremor, no bradykinesia, and no cogwheel rigidity. Gait is normal with preserved arm swing, and the pull test is normal. An Archimedes spiral is tremulous and widened. Cranial nerves are intact, there is no dysmetria beyond the tremor, cognition is normal, and there are no Kayser-Fleischer rings.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes her condition from Parkinson disease?
options: A tremor present at rest that disappears with action
A pill-rolling quality with bradykinesia
An action and postural tremor that improves with alcohol and a positive family history
A unilateral tremor with cogwheel rigidity
stages.explanation: Essential tremor is a bilateral, largely symmetric action (postural and kinetic) tremor that characteristically improves transiently with alcohol and often runs in families in an autosomal dominant pattern. Parkinsonian tremor is a resting tremor (4-6 Hz, pill-rolling) that diminishes with action, is usually asymmetric, and is accompanied by bradykinesia and rigidity. A tremor present at rest that disappears with action describes Parkinson disease, not essential tremor. A pill-rolling tremor with bradykinesia and a unilateral cogwheel-rigid tremor are parkinsonian. The combination of action tremor, alcohol responsiveness, head and voice involvement, and family history is classic essential tremor; the absence of bradykinesia and rigidity is key.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal; excludes hyperthyroid tremor)
Serum ceruloplasmin and copper: normal
Medication review: no tremorgenic drugs (no beta-agonists, lithium, valproate, or stimulants)
DaTscan (when obtained for atypical cases): normal presynaptic dopamine uptake, supporting essential tremor over Parkinson disease

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which medications are considered first-line pharmacologic therapy for this condition?
options: Levodopa and a dopamine agonist
Carbamazepine and phenytoin
Levetiracetam and gabapentin as first-line agents
Propranolol and primidone
stages.explanation: First-line agents for essential tremor are the non-selective beta-blocker propranolol and the barbiturate primidone; either or both can substantially reduce tremor amplitude. Levodopa and dopamine agonists treat Parkinson disease and would not help. Carbamazepine and phenytoin are antiepileptics without a role in standard essential tremor treatment. Gabapentin and topiramate are reasonable second-line options, but levetiracetam is not effective for essential tremor, so that pairing is not first-line. This patient has already failed adequate, tolerated trials of both first-line drugs, limited by bradycardia and fatigue with one and sedation with the other, which sets up consideration of procedural therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her tremor remains disabling despite adequate trials of both first-line drugs. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for VIM thalamic DBS or MR-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy
Start levodopa
Reassure her that nothing more can be done
Begin lifelong benzodiazepines as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: For medically refractory, disabling essential tremor, the effective procedural options target the ventral intermediate (VIM) nucleus of the thalamus: deep brain stimulation (adjustable, reversible, and able to be done bilaterally) or MR-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy (incisionless and typically unilateral, with no hardware). Both markedly reduce contralateral hand tremor. Levodopa treats Parkinson disease and has no role here. Telling her nothing can be done is incorrect, because procedural therapy is highly effective. Chronic benzodiazepines are not definitive therapy and carry dependence, sedation, and fall risk, which is especially concerning at age 70. The choice between DBS and focused ultrasound depends on bilaterality, surgical risk, and patient preference.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She has primarily severe right-hand tremor, is anxious about implanted hardware and open surgery, and wants a single-session option. Which best fits?
options: Bilateral DBS is her only option
Unilateral MR-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy of the VIM
Repeat trials of propranolol indefinitely
Corpus callosotomy
stages.explanation: Unilateral MR-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) thalamotomy is an incisionless, single-session procedure that ablates the VIM to relieve contralateral (here, right) hand tremor without implanted hardware, which is ideal for a patient who wants one treatment and is averse to devices. DBS is excellent and is preferred when bilateral treatment is needed or when adjustability and reversibility are valued, but it requires implanted hardware and ongoing programming. Repeating a drug she has already failed will not help. Corpus callosotomy is an epilepsy palliation procedure, irrelevant to tremor. Bilateral lesioning is generally avoided because of speech and balance risks, whereas unilateral ablation has an acceptable safety profile.

cases.diagnosis: Essential tremor, refractory to first-line pharmacotherapy
pearls: Essential tremor is a bilateral action and postural tremor that improves with alcohol and often has a family history, with no bradykinesia or rigidity.
First-line therapy is propranolol and/or primidone; topiramate and gabapentin are second-line.
Refractory disabling tremor is treated with VIM-targeted DBS or MR-guided focused ultrasound thalamotomy.
Focused ultrasound is incisionless and usually unilateral; DBS is adjustable, reversible, and preferred for bilateral treatment.

cases.title: One-sided face twitching that started in the eyelid
cases.chiefComplaint: My left eye keeps twitching on its own, and now the twitching is pulling at my cheek and mouth.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman describes about a year of involuntary twitching of the left side of her face. It began as intermittent contractions of the lower left eyelid that she first dismissed as a stress-related eye twitch, and over months it has gradually spread to involve the cheek, the muscles around the mouth, and the platysma on the same side. Episodes now sometimes force the eye to close and pull the corner of her mouth. The twitches are involuntary, sometimes triggered by stress, fatigue, or talking, and she has noticed they continue even while she is asleep. She reports no pain, no facial numbness, no weakness, and no change in hearing or tinnitus. She has had no prior facial palsy or Bell palsy. The spasms have become socially embarrassing at her front-desk job. PMH: hypertension. Medications: amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: works as a receptionist, no tobacco or alcohol. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Intermittent, involuntary clonic and tonic contractions of the left hemiface involving the orbicularis oculi, zygomaticus, and platysma, producing brief eye closure with a simultaneous twitch of the mouth on the same side. Between spasms there is no facial weakness, and facial expression is symmetric. Facial sensation is intact (CN V normal), hearing is intact, and the remaining cranial nerves are normal. There is no bilateral eyelid involvement and no blepharospasm pattern. The rest of the neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes her condition from benign essential blepharospasm?
options: It is unilateral, begins in the orbicularis oculi and spreads down one side of the face, and persists during sleep
It is always bilateral and stops completely during sleep
It is voluntarily suppressible and preceded by a premonitory urge
It is accompanied by facial numbness
stages.explanation: Hemifacial spasm is unilateral, typically begins in the orbicularis oculi and spreads ipsilaterally to the lower facial muscles, and characteristically persists during sleep. Benign essential blepharospasm is bilateral, involves the eyelids symmetrically, and stops during sleep. A suppressible movement preceded by a premonitory urge describes a tic, not hemifacial spasm. Facial numbness implies trigeminal sensory involvement, which is not part of hemifacial spasm, a pure CN VII motor phenomenon. Recognizing the unilateral, sleep-persistent, spreading pattern is the key to the clinical diagnosis, which is usually made on history and examination.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate study to identify the underlying cause?
options: CT of the facial bones
Electromyography of the limbs
MRI/MRA of the brain focused on the cerebellopontine angle and facial nerve root exit zone
Carotid duplex ultrasound
stages.explanation: Hemifacial spasm is most often caused by a pulsatile vascular loop compressing the facial nerve at its root exit zone in the cerebellopontine angle, classically the anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA) but also the PICA or vertebral artery. High-resolution MRI with MRA best demonstrates this neurovascular conflict and also excludes secondary causes such as a cerebellopontine-angle tumor, cyst, or arteriovenous malformation. CT of the facial bones evaluates bony anatomy, not the nerve-vessel interface. Limb EMG is irrelevant to a focal facial movement disorder. Carotid duplex assesses extracranial carotid disease and would not show the posterior fossa vascular loop responsible.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI/MRA brain: an AICA vascular loop contacting and indenting the left facial nerve at the root exit zone
No cerebellopontine-angle mass
No demyelinating lesion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She wants treatment but prefers to avoid surgery initially. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Oral baclofen as a definitive cure
Botulinum toxin injections into the affected facial muscles
Immediate microvascular decompression
High-dose corticosteroids
stages.explanation: Botulinum toxin chemodenervation of the involved facial muscles is the first-line treatment for hemifacial spasm: it reliably reduces spasms for about 3-4 months per session and is repeated as needed, with a favorable safety profile. Oral agents such as carbamazepine and baclofen provide only modest, inconsistent relief and are not curative. Microvascular decompression is the definitive, potentially curative option but is reserved for patients who fail or decline botulinum toxin or who want a permanent cure, not as the initial step for someone wishing to avoid surgery. Corticosteroids have no role, because this is a compressive, mechanical phenomenon rather than an inflammatory neuropathy like Bell palsy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After several years she wants a permanent cure and chooses microvascular decompression. Which complication is most specifically associated with this operation?
options: Ipsilateral hearing loss from injury to the vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII)
Contralateral hemiplegia
Permanent diabetes insipidus
Lumbar radiculopathy
stages.explanation: Microvascular decompression places a Teflon pad between the offending vessel and the facial nerve and is curative in the majority of cases. Because the operation is in the cerebellopontine angle adjacent to the vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII), ipsilateral hearing loss or tinnitus is the most characteristic specific risk, along with facial weakness and, less commonly, a CSF leak. Contralateral hemiplegia is not a typical complication of this surgery. Diabetes insipidus relates to hypothalamic-pituitary surgery, not the posterior fossa. Lumbar radiculopathy is unrelated to intracranial surgery. Intraoperative brainstem auditory evoked potential monitoring is used specifically to reduce the risk of hearing loss.

cases.diagnosis: Left hemifacial spasm due to AICA neurovascular compression of the facial nerve
pearls: Hemifacial spasm is unilateral, starts in the orbicularis oculi, spreads down the face, and persists during sleep, unlike bilateral blepharospasm.
The cause is usually a vascular loop (AICA, PICA, or vertebral) compressing CN VII at the root exit zone; image with MRI/MRA.
Botulinum toxin is first-line; microvascular decompression is the definitive cure.
The signature MVD risk is ipsilateral hearing loss from CN VIII injury, monitored with intraoperative BAERs.

cases.title: Newborn not moving one arm after a difficult delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: The baby isn't moving his right arm after birth.
cases.history: A term newborn boy was delivered vaginally after a prolonged second stage complicated by shoulder dystocia that required obstetric maneuvers to free the anterior shoulder. He is macrosomic at 4.4 kg, born to a mother with gestational diabetes who previously delivered a large infant. Immediately after birth the right arm lies limp at his side while he moves the left arm and both legs normally and cries vigorously. Apgar scores were 8 and 9 at 1 and 5 minutes. He has no respiratory distress, is pink and well perfused, and is latching and feeding well. The nursing staff noted reduced spontaneous movement of the right upper limb on the initial newborn exam. Maternal history: gestational diabetes managed with diet, and a prior macrosomic infant; the pregnancy was otherwise uncomplicated and the delivery was not instrumented or breech. No family history of neuromuscular disease or birth injury.
cases.exam: The right arm is adducted and internally rotated at the shoulder, extended at the elbow, with the forearm pronated and the wrist flexed: the classic 'waiter's tip' posture. The Moro reflex is absent on the right but the grasp reflex is intact on the right (the fingers move). The left arm has a normal Moro and spontaneous movement. The biceps reflex is absent on the right. There is no ptosis or miosis (no Horner syndrome). Chest rise is symmetric with no respiratory distress, suggesting intact phrenic function. The clavicles have no palpable step-off or crepitus, and the hands are warm and well perfused.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The posture and preserved hand grasp localize the injury to which part of the brachial plexus?
options: Lower trunk (C8-T1)
Upper trunk (C5-C6), an Erb-Duchenne palsy
The entire plexus (C5-T1)
The median nerve at the wrist
stages.explanation: The 'waiter's tip' posture (arm adducted and internally rotated, elbow extended, forearm pronated, wrist flexed) with an absent Moro but preserved hand grasp localizes to the upper trunk (C5-C6), the classic Erb-Duchenne palsy seen after shoulder dystocia. A lower trunk (C8-T1) injury (Klumpke palsy) produces a claw hand with weak intrinsic hand muscles and often a Horner syndrome, the opposite pattern; here grasp is intact. A total plexus injury (C5-T1) would render the entire arm and hand flaccid. A median nerve injury at the wrist would not explain the proximal shoulder and elbow posture or the absent Moro reflex.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which associated injury should be specifically evaluated in this newborn?
options: Clavicle or humerus fracture and diaphragmatic (phrenic nerve) function
Intraventricular hemorrhage by routine head CT
Developmental hip dysplasia by forced abduction of the hips
Congenital heart disease by cardiac catheterization
stages.explanation: Shoulder dystocia injuries frequently coexist: clavicle and humeral fractures can accompany or mimic a brachial plexus palsy, so they should be sought by palpation and radiographs if there is crepitus or asymmetry. An upper-trunk injury extending toward C4 or a phrenic nerve injury can cause diaphragmatic paralysis and respiratory distress, so diaphragm function should be assessed (with a chest film or ultrasound if there is concern). Routine head CT for intraventricular hemorrhage is not indicated without specific signs and carries radiation risk. Forced hip abduction is not the correct dysplasia screen and risks harm. Cardiac catheterization is invasive and unrelated. Crucially, also examine for Horner syndrome, which signals a more severe lower-trunk or preganglionic lesion.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Right upper-extremity radiograph: no clavicle or humerus fracture
Chest radiograph: normal, symmetric diaphragm position (no phrenic nerve involvement)
Exam: no Horner syndrome; hand grasp present and symmetric
Pulse oximetry: 98% on room air with no respiratory distress

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate surgical exploration and nerve grafting within the first week
Rigid splinting of the arm in full extension
Gentle range-of-motion physical and occupational therapy with observation for spontaneous recovery
Botulinum toxin injection into the deltoid
stages.explanation: Most neonatal brachial plexus palsies, especially upper-trunk (Erb) injuries from stretch or neurapraxia, recover spontaneously. Initial management is supportive: gentle range-of-motion exercises (often begun after the first 1-2 weeks) guided by physical and occupational therapy to prevent contractures, with close serial examinations to track recovery. Immediate surgery in the first week is not indicated because many infants improve on their own and the recovery trajectory cannot yet be judged. Rigid splinting in extension risks contracture and is not standard. Botulinum toxin is not a primary treatment for the acute palsy, though it is sometimes used later for co-contraction or contracture. The timing of recovery, particularly of biceps function, guides whether surgery becomes necessary.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: By what point does persistent lack of recovery, notably absent biceps and elbow flexion, generally prompt referral for microsurgical nerve repair?
options: By 24 hours of life
By around 3-6 months of age
Only after 3 years of age
Surgery is never indicated for this condition
stages.explanation: If meaningful recovery, particularly return of biceps function and antigravity elbow flexion, has not occurred by roughly 3-6 months of age, the infant should be referred to a specialized center for consideration of microsurgical reconstruction (neurolysis, nerve grafting, or nerve transfers). Delaying beyond this window risks irreversible motor endplate loss and poorer functional outcomes, so waiting until 3 years is too long. Surgery within the first 24 hours is unnecessary because most infants recover and the injury cannot yet be characterized. Saying surgery is never indicated is wrong, because a subset with persistent deficits benefit substantially. Serial exams and tools such as the Active Movement Scale track the recovery that drives this decision.

cases.diagnosis: Neonatal upper-trunk (C5-C6) brachial plexus palsy, an Erb-Duchenne palsy after shoulder dystocia
pearls: Erb palsy (C5-C6) causes the 'waiter's tip' posture with an absent Moro but preserved hand grasp.
Risk factors include macrosomia, maternal gestational diabetes, and shoulder dystocia; check for clavicle fracture and phrenic/diaphragm involvement.
Klumpke (C8-T1) palsy gives a claw hand with possible Horner syndrome; Horner suggests a more severe lesion.
Most cases recover spontaneously with physical therapy; refer for microsurgery if biceps function has not returned by about 3-6 months.

cases.title: Limp, useless arm after a motorcycle crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Since my motorcycle accident a few weeks ago, my whole right arm is dead and I can't move it at all.
cases.history: A 24-year-old right-hand-dominant man was in a high-speed motorcycle crash 4 weeks ago, landing on his right shoulder and head with forceful depression of the shoulder and lateral neck flexion away from it, the classic traction mechanism. Since then his entire right arm has been flail, with no shoulder, elbow, wrist, or hand movement, complete numbness of the limb, and constant severe burning and electric-shock pain that disrupts his sleep. He has also noticed a drooping right eyelid and a smaller pupil on that side. There has been no recovery in the affected arm over the past month. His other injuries, including a healed clavicle fracture and rib fractures, have improved. PMH: none. Medications: gabapentin for neuropathic pain. Allergies: none. Social: construction worker, current smoker, occasional alcohol; he is anxious about whether he can return to manual work. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: The right upper extremity is flail with 0/5 strength across all myotomes (shoulder abduction, elbow flexion and extension, wrist, and hand intrinsics) and global anesthesia of the limb. There is right-sided ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis (Horner syndrome). Medial winging of the right scapula is present, and the right hemidiaphragm appears elevated on chest exam. The right arm is areflexic. A Tinel sign over the supraclavicular plexus is absent. The left arm and both legs are normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of findings most strongly suggests a preganglionic root avulsion rather than a postganglionic rupture?
options: A distal Tinel sign that advances over weeks
Isolated weakness in a single peripheral nerve distribution
Intact sensation with normal strength
Horner syndrome, an elevated hemidiaphragm, and medial (rhomboid and serratus) scapular winging
stages.explanation: Preganglionic (root avulsion) injuries detach the nerve root from the spinal cord proximal to the dorsal root ganglion and proximal to the take-off of very proximal branches. Clues include Horner syndrome (T1 sympathetic disruption causing ptosis, miosis, and anhidrosis), phrenic nerve palsy with an elevated hemidiaphragm (C3-5 roots), and winging from dorsal scapular (rhomboids) or long thoracic (serratus anterior) involvement, branches that arise at the root level. These features indicate avulsion, which cannot be repaired by direct grafting. A progressing distal Tinel sign suggests a regenerating postganglionic injury. Single-nerve weakness or a normal exam does not fit a severe plexus avulsion. Distinguishing preganglionic from postganglionic injury fundamentally changes the surgical strategy.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT myelogram: pseudomeningoceles at right C7, C8, and T1 with absent nerve rootlets
Nerve conduction: ABSENT motor responses in the right arm; PRESERVED sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) in the anesthetic limb
EMG (at 4 weeks): denervation (fibrillations) in affected muscles, including rhomboids and serratus anterior
Chest radiograph: elevated right hemidiaphragm

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why are sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) preserved in a limb that is completely numb in this type of injury?
options: The sensory nerves are uninjured, so sensation should actually be normal
In a preganglionic avulsion the dorsal root ganglion and its peripheral axon remain intact (so SNAPs persist) even though the sensory root is disconnected from the cord, producing clinical anesthesia
SNAPs are always absent in any brachial plexus injury
The findings indicate a purely muscular, not neurologic, problem
stages.explanation: This classic paradox localizes the lesion. In a preganglionic avulsion the tear is proximal to the dorsal root ganglion. The ganglion cell bodies and their peripheral sensory axons stay alive and connected, so peripheral SNAPs are preserved, yet the sensory signal never reaches the cord because the root is avulsed, producing clinical anesthesia. Preserved SNAPs in an anesthetic limb therefore indicate a preganglionic, non-graftable injury. It does not mean sensation is normal or that the nerves are uninjured. SNAPs are not universally absent in plexus injuries; their presence versus absence is exactly what differentiates preganglionic from postganglionic lesions. The pseudomeningoceles corroborate root avulsion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given confirmed multilevel root avulsions, what is the appropriate reconstructive strategy?
options: Nerve transfers (neurotization), such as spinal accessory-to-suprascapular and an Oberlin ulnar-fascicle-to-biceps transfer
Direct nerve grafting from the avulsed roots to the distal plexus
Indefinite observation, since avulsions recover spontaneously
Immediate shoulder arthrodesis as the first procedure
stages.explanation: Avulsed roots have no proximal stump attached to the cord, so they cannot serve as a donor for conventional grafting. The strategy is nerve transfer (neurotization): a functioning, expendable donor nerve is rerouted to power a critical target, for example the spinal accessory nerve to the suprascapular nerve for shoulder abduction and external rotation, and an Oberlin transfer (a redundant ulnar fascicle to the biceps motor branch) for elbow flexion. Direct grafting from an avulsed root is impossible because the proximal connection to the spinal cord is gone. Root avulsions do not recover spontaneously. Shoulder arthrodesis and other secondary reconstructions (tendon transfers, free functioning muscle transfer) are considered later, not as the first move.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the optimal timing for nerve-transfer surgery in this patient?
options: There is no time limit; surgery is equally effective at any point
Wait at least 3 years to allow maximal spontaneous recovery
Generally within about 3-6 months (and before roughly 12 months), before irreversible motor endplate degeneration
Operate within the first 24 hours in all closed traction injuries
stages.explanation: Reinnervation must reach muscle before the motor endplates degenerate irreversibly, generally a window of roughly 12-18 months from injury, so nerve transfers for avulsion are best performed within about 3-6 months once avulsion is confirmed. Waiting 3 years guarantees failure because the target muscles become unreceptive to reinnervation. There is definitely a time limit, contrary to the first option. Emergent surgery within 24 hours is reserved for sharp or penetrating lacerations (clean transections) and certain open injuries; closed traction injuries are observed briefly, with EMG at about 3-4 weeks, to allow recovery of repairable lesions, then operated within the window if avulsion or no recovery is confirmed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He has severe burning pain in the anesthetic hand. What is the most likely mechanism?
options: Acute compartment syndrome
Deafferentation (neuropathic) pain from the avulsed dorsal roots
Complex regional pain syndrome from an occult wrist fracture
Referred cardiac pain
stages.explanation: Severe burning, often crushing or electric pain in an insensate limb after root avulsion is deafferentation pain, arising from loss of sensory input and hyperactivity of deafferented dorsal horn neurons. It is treated with neuropathic agents (gabapentinoids, tricyclics), and in refractory cases dorsal root entry zone (DREZ) lesioning can be highly effective. Acute compartment syndrome causes pain with a tense, swollen compartment and is an emergency unrelated to this chronic anesthetic state. Complex regional pain syndrome typically follows a distal injury with autonomic and trophic skin changes and does not fit an avulsion-anesthetic limb. Referred cardiac pain does not produce full dermatomal anesthesia of one arm. Recognizing deafferentation pain guides both medical therapy and the option of DREZ surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic preganglionic brachial plexus injury with multilevel cervical root avulsions (C7-T1)
pearls: Horner syndrome, an elevated hemidiaphragm, and medial scapular winging point to a preganglionic root avulsion.
Preserved SNAPs in an anesthetic limb is the hallmark of preganglionic injury: the dorsal root ganglion stays intact while the root is torn from the cord.
Pseudomeningoceles on CT myelogram or MRI confirm avulsion; avulsed roots cannot be grafted, so use nerve transfers (Oberlin, spinal accessory to suprascapular).
Operate within about 3-6 months (before roughly 12-18 months) to beat motor endplate degeneration; DREZ lesioning treats refractory deafferentation pain.

cases.title: Tripping over my own foot after losing weight
cases.chiefComplaint: My right foot slaps the ground and I keep catching my toe and tripping.
