cases.history: A 56-year-old man reports that over the past 2 weeks his right foot has begun to slap the floor and he catches his toe on stairs and curbs, tripping repeatedly and nearly falling twice. He recently lost about 20 kg over a few months through intentional dieting and increased exercise, and he notes that he habitually crosses his legs at the knee while sitting at his desk for long stretches each day. He has tingling and mild numbness over the top of his foot and the outer part of his lower leg but no pain in the leg. He denies back pain, radiating buttock or leg pain, saddle numbness, and any bowel or bladder symptoms. He has no diabetes or prior neuropathy. PMH: hyperlipidemia. Medications: atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: desk job, frequent leg crossing, social alcohol, no tobacco. Family history: noncontributory.
cases.exam: Right foot drop with weak ankle dorsiflexion (tibialis anterior 2/5) and weak great-toe extension, plus weak foot eversion (3/5). Foot inversion (tibialis posterior) and plantarflexion (gastrocnemius) are preserved at 5/5. There is sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and the lateral lower leg, with the sole spared. Gait shows a right steppage pattern. A Tinel sign is positive over the right fibular (peroneal) neck. Knee and ankle reflexes are 2+ and symmetric. Straight-leg raise is negative, hip abduction (gluteus medius) strength is normal, and there is no spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding best localizes the lesion to the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head rather than an L5 radiculopathy?
options: Weakness of foot dorsiflexion
Numbness over the dorsum of the foot
Preserved foot inversion and preserved hip abduction with weakness limited to dorsiflexion and eversion
A steppage gait
stages.explanation: Both common peroneal neuropathy and L5 radiculopathy cause foot drop with weak dorsiflexion and dorsal foot numbness, so those findings do not localize. The discriminator is foot inversion: the tibialis posterior (ankle inversion) is supplied by the tibial nerve via the L5 root, so it is weak in an L5 radiculopathy but PRESERVED in a peroneal lesion at the fibular head. Likewise, hip abduction (gluteus medius, an L5 muscle) is weak in L5 radiculopathy but normal in a peroneal palsy. The common peroneal nerve supplies dorsiflexion (deep branch) and eversion (superficial branch) only, so isolated weakness there with preserved inversion points to the fibular head. A steppage gait occurs with any foot drop.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Nerve conduction studies: focal conduction block and slowing of the common peroneal nerve ACROSS the fibular head, with normal conduction below
EMG: denervation in tibialis anterior and peroneus longus; NORMAL tibialis posterior, NORMAL short head of biceps femoris, NORMAL gluteus medius and lumbar paraspinals
MRI lumbar spine: no nerve-root compression

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: EMG shows a normal short head of the biceps femoris and normal paraspinals. What does this indicate?
options: The lesion must be an L5 radiculopathy
The lesion is a sciatic neuropathy in the thigh
The lesion is at or distal to the fibular head, sparing the short head of biceps femoris and the L5 root
The findings indicate a primary myopathy
stages.explanation: The short head of the biceps femoris is the only muscle supplied by the common peroneal division of the sciatic nerve above the knee, so if it is normal while distal peroneal-innervated muscles are denervated, the lesion is at or below the fibular head, not in the thigh (sciatic) or at the root. Normal lumbar paraspinals and normal L5 muscles (tibialis posterior, gluteus medius) exclude an L5 radiculopathy, in which paraspinal denervation and weakness of those muscles would be expected. A high sciatic lesion would also denervate the short head of biceps femoris and the hamstrings. A myopathy does not fit a focal, sensory-involving mononeuropathy with conduction block at the fibular neck.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a demyelinating conduction block from external compression at the fibular head, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Urgent surgical decompression of the peroneal nerve
Avoid leg crossing and external pressure, provide an ankle-foot orthosis, and start physical therapy while awaiting recovery
Lumbar laminectomy
High-dose corticosteroids and immunosuppression
stages.explanation: Most common peroneal neuropathies from compression at the fibular head, here a 'slimmer's palsy' from weight loss plus habitual leg crossing, are demyelinating with conduction block and recover spontaneously over weeks to months once the offending pressure is removed. Management is conservative: eliminate leg crossing and external compression, protect the nerve, use an ankle-foot orthosis to prevent tripping and improve gait, and provide physical therapy. Surgery is reserved for a structural lesion (ganglion, mass, entrapment) or failure to recover with ongoing axon loss. Lumbar laminectomy treats a radiculopathy, which has been excluded. Corticosteroids and immunosuppression target inflammatory neuropathies, not a focal compressive palsy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which scenario would most appropriately prompt referral for surgical exploration?
options: Rapid full recovery within two weeks of stopping leg crossing
A purely demyelinating conduction block with no axon loss
Imaging revealing an intraneural ganglion cyst or mass at the fibular head, or a progressive deficit with severe axonal loss and no recovery
Mild residual dorsal foot numbness with otherwise normal strength
stages.explanation: Surgery is indicated when there is a structural, correctable lesion such as an intraneural ganglion cyst, a nerve tumor, or fibrous entrapment at the fibular head, or when there is severe axonal loss with a progressive deficit and no signs of recovery on serial exams and EMG. In those cases decompression, with cyst excision or grafting, can restore function. Rapid recovery after removing compression needs no surgery. A pure demyelinating block without axon loss carries an excellent prognosis and recovers on its own. Mild residual numbness with normal strength is not a surgical indication. Serial clinical and electrodiagnostic follow-up determines whether the expected spontaneous recovery is occurring.

cases.diagnosis: Common peroneal (fibular) neuropathy at the fibular head ('slimmer's palsy') from weight loss and habitual leg crossing
pearls: Peroneal palsy at the fibular head causes foot drop with weak dorsiflexion and eversion but PRESERVED inversion, unlike L5 radiculopathy.
Weak L5 muscles beyond the peroneal territory (tibialis posterior, gluteus medius) and paraspinal denervation point to radiculopathy, not a fibular lesion.
A normal short head of the biceps femoris localizes the lesion at or below the fibular head rather than in the sciatic nerve.
'Slimmer's palsy' from weight loss and leg crossing is usually demyelinating and recovers with pressure avoidance, an AFO, and therapy.
